question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the planned kitchen schemes for Housing Executive properties in (i) Woodburn; and (ii) Whitehead.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail when he will answer AQW 43247/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) an update on sewage pollution at Belfast Lough; and (ii) any actions his Department is currently taking to address this pollution.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any consideration he has given to subsidising blister pack dispensing in community pharmacies.
To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any consideration she has given to introducing Jade's Law.
To ask the Minister of Health whether there are any plans to introduce the TAP device being used within some NHS Trusts in England.
answer_text: My Department continues to have a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which specific types of guidance are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. However, there is no relevant NICE guidance supporting the routine use of this device. MedTech Innovation Briefings (MIBs) do not have any formal status in Northern Ireland and the MIB programme was discontinued by NICE in 2023. These briefings provide objective information on medical technologies but do not contain formal NICE guidance or recommendations for adoption.My Department has noted the MIB published by NICE on the use of needle-free arterial non-injectable connectors (of which TAP is one such device). The briefing asserts that one of the key uncertainties identified in the available evidence relates to whether the use of this technology would deliver cost savings or improved outcomes for HSC organisations.In the absence of available evidence demonstrating clear clinical and cost effectiveness, there are currently no plans to introduce this device across HSC services. However, my Department will continue to monitor emerging evidence and developments in relation to this device.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the provision of timely multiple sclerosis infusions, whether he would consider implementing a system to allow patients in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to be referred to alternative Trusts to avoid delays in treatment.
To ask the Minister of Health whether any assessment has been made on the level of missed appointments due to Health and Social Care Trusts being unable to organise ambulances.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to urgently increase infusion capacity within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust to ensure that patients with multiple sclerosis receive their prescribed treatments at clinically recommended six-monthly intervals.
answer_text: Services for people with multiple sclerosis (MS) are provided as part of wider neurology services in each Trust area. There has been detailed engagement with the clinical teams in each Trust over the past number of years with a view to improving the care and treatment locally available for people with this condition. As part of this engagement, a Service Specification was developed, consistent with NICE Clinical Guidelines, which sets out the requirements for delivering a multi-disciplinary service consistently across the region. The priorities that were identified from the Service Specification were for all Trusts to have dedicated clinics for people with MS, for each Trust to have dedicated MS nursing staff and for the capacity to be available in each Trust to allow infusion delivered disease modifying treatments (DMTs) to be available locally.Previously, patients requiring infusion delivered therapies would have received these in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust. However, infusion capacity is now available in all five Trust areas with increasing numbers of patients benefitting from these treatments each year.The Northern Health and Social Care Trust is working to urgently review accommodation capacity for provision of drug infusions for people with MS and a range of other conditions and is taking opportunities to increase this by exploring new treatment areas such as ambulatory areas on the Antrim Hospital site. My Department works closely with Trusts each year to consider the requirements associated with the increasing number of people with MS who are on DMTs, including infusion delivered therapies. Over £20m is invested across the region in supporting the provision of approved drug treatments to eligible patients. Proposals for the expansion of infusion capacity including any requirement for additional infusion staff to support the delivery of new and/or existing therapies in line with clinically determined timelines, will be considered as part of this ongoing engagement and supported, subject to available funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children who applied for a funded pre-school place; (ii) the success rate at the end of stage 1; (iii) the number of children engaging in stage 2; and (iv) the number of available places remaining, in Carrickfergus
answer_text: In Stage One of the pre-school admissions process a total of 328 target age children in the Carrickfergus area applied for a funded pre-school education place.  Of these 92.38% were offered a funded place at close of Stage One on 23 March.Parents of 15 children that did not receive an offer of a funded place in Stage One remained with the admissions process and submitted further preferences in Stage Two.  The outcome of these applications will be communicated to parents on 19 May.  Stage Two applications closed on 1 April and the EA has advised that for the 15 children that submitted further preferences, there is currently capacity to increase provision within their listed Stage Two preference(s) to ensure a funded pre-school place is available. Provision levels will also continue to be reviewed throughout Stage Two and the EA will provide further additional funded pre-school places if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether, in setting the cap on incontinence products, his Department has undertaken an equality impact assessment that considered the needs of females who menstruate, including how those needs are reflected in the current allocation.
To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the PSNI holiday pay business case.
answer_text: The Department of Finance has not received a business case relating to the PSNI holiday pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the PSNI holiday pay business case.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the PSNI Holiday Pay business case which must satisfy the requisite approvals process, including approval by the Department of Finance. While I cannot give a definitive timeframe at this stage, I want to reassure you that my officials are working closely and constructively with the PSNI to progress the Holiday Pay business case as quickly as possible.  Holiday Pay is an exceptional pressure that is wholly unaffordable under any circumstances within existing or future PSNI and DoJ baseline budget. Furthermore, Holiday Pay is an issue that has an impact across all departments and, therefore, a collective solution at Block level will be required.Further engagement will be required with the Executive and the Department of Finance to explore potential funding options when the liability crystallises.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any guidance that has been issued to primary schools regarding the funding of SEAG transfer test preparation materials; and (ii) whether his Department will provide additional funding to ensure that pupils are not required to pay for practice papers as part of their preparation.
answer_text: The SEAG transfer test is used by some postprimary schools as part of their admissions arrangements and is privately administered. As it does not form part of the Department’s endorsed or statutory assessment system, the Department has no plans to provide additional funding for SEAG practice papers.No guidance has issued to primary schools regarding the funding of SEAG transfer test preparation materials.  Guidance for schools on the overarching transfer process is available on the Department’s website which sets out the key role of primary schools to meet statutory obligations to deliver the primary curriculum.  It further notes that schools are free to exercise the right, in line with the wishes of parents and pupils, to facilitate entrance test arrangements by supplying support materials, carrying out any preparation for tests during core teaching hours, coaching in exam technique, providing a location for testing where mutually agreed with the test provider, and/or providing familiarisation with a testing environment, consistent with statutory obligations.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities pursuant to AQW 35478/22-27, for an update on the lighting referenced.
answer_text: My Department and Mid and East Antrim Borough Council continue to engage on how lighting can be installed in this area. The Department has recently become aware of additional structural works that may be required at the area of the harbour, which falls under Departmental ownership. Given these emerging infrastructure needs, the proposed lighting cannot be progressed in isolation, and any scheme must now be considered within the wider programme of works required at the harbour. My officials remain in active discussions to explore the infrastructure requirements at this location in partnership with Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the cancellation of special schools’ summer schemes for 2026, to detail (i) any risk assessments undertaken that informed the decision that schemes could not proceed safely; (ii) any engagement that took place with the Department of Health and relevant health trusts regarding the provision of nursing and healthcare support; (iii) the dates on which his Department and the Education Authority were advised that sufficient healthcare provision would not be available; (iv) whether any options were explored with the Department of Health to enable sche
answer_text: (i) Following engagement with the Special School Strategic Leadership Group and the Public Health Agency as part of its review of nursing provision in special schools, the Education Authority (EA) determined that there were  significant risks relating to identified healthcare needs of some children and young people attending the summer schemes, and made the decision, based on safety grounds that summer schemes would not be able to proceed in the absence of appropriate nursing provision being made available on-site. (ii) The EA, Department of Health (DoH) and my department engaged regularly at senior official levels prior to any decisions being taken. (iii) The DoH confirmed on 24 March 2026 that the required nursing cover would not be available. (iv) Following engagement, the EA made the decision, based on safety grounds that summer schemes would not be able to proceed in the absence of appropriate nursing provision being made available on-site.  Partial schemes were not considered viable. (v) My department, the EA and DoH will review future provision under the auspices of the Joint Education and Health Oversight Group which meets regularly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how his Department monitors housing associations' compliance with their duties with respect to maintenance of properties.
answer_text: The Department’s Housing Regulation Branch (HRB) undertakes annual regulatory assessments of each Registered Housing Association (RHA) to determine their compliance with the standards set out in the Regulatory Framework for Registered Housing Associations in Northern Ireland.Maintenance of properties is a key component of Consumer Standard 2 within this Framework. In applying this standard, HRB assesses RHAs against a range of desired outcomes and expectations. These include:performance against response maintenance targetsthe existence and periodic review of relevant policies and procedures, including asset management strategiesthe adequacy of maintenance planning and performance against budgetsstock condition survey arrangementscompliance with the Decent Homes Standard and statutory requirementscustomer satisfaction other relevant performance metrics Following these annual assessments, the Department publishes Regulatory Judgments and Key Performance Reports (KPRs) for each RHA. A sector-wide KPR is also published. These provide transparency on RHA performance, including maintenance activity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any powers his Department has to investigate malicious oil dumps.
answer_text: My Department has powers to investigate the unauthorised dumping of controlled waste (including waste oil) under The Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997.		Any incidents of suspected unauthorised waste activity should be reported to NIEA Environment Crime Unit using the below email:          EnvironmentalCrime@daera-ni.gov.uk
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any reason why the planned increase in the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme from 15 per cent to 20 per cent, as indicated in the Childcare Strategy, will not be implemented from 1 April 2026; and (ii) any financial, budgetary, or policy constraints that have led to this decision, given the impact on working families.
answer_text: In the absence of agreed Executive Budgets for 2026-27 no funding has been allocated to my Department for the Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.  However, recognising the importance of the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme (NICSS) for working families, I have extended the scheme beyond 1 April 2026 at the current subsidy contribution of 15%.  A decision to increase the NICSS in line with the proposals set out in the draft Early Learning and Childcare Strategy can only be considered in the context of an appropriate allocation of earmarked funding by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any steps being taken to progress the regional agreement required for approval of funding for non-prescription continuous glucose monitoring devices for hybrid closed loop systems; (ii) the anticipated timeframe for its completion; and (iii) when the multidisciplinary team case conference process will begin for patients awaiting assessment.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the number of prosecutions his Department has undertaken in relation to malicious oil dumps under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997, in the last five years.
answer_text: There has been one prosecution undertaken by my Department in relation to malicious oil dumps under the Waste and Contaminated Land (Northern Ireland) Order 1997 in the last five years. This prosecution occurred in July 2024.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on nursery provision at Sunnylands Primary School, including (i) details on the current status of the proposal; (ii) any progress made to date; and (iii) the expected timeline for a decision or implementation.
answer_text: The establishment of a statutory nursery class at Sunnylands Primary School would require the publication and approval of a statutory Development Proposal (DP).  Detailed guidance on this process is available on my Department’s website here. I understand that the school has made initial contact with the EA to discuss the possibility of publishing a DP to establish nursery provision.  If a DP is published, I will consider it in line with the processes set out in the above guidance.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the inconsiderate parking secondary legislation, announced in November 2025.
answer_text: Following my announcement to introduce a ban across the North on parking a vehicle wholly on the footway, parking across dropped kerbs and double parking, my officials are currently taking forward the necessary work to develop the secondary legislation to introduce these new measures. It is my aim to introduce these changes in the latter part of 2026, subject to the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Dilapidation Bill (NIA 21/22-27).
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 35988/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer (i) AQW 41219/22-27; (ii) AQW 41220/22-27; and (iii) AQW 41221/22-27.

To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any assessment that has made of the availability of funded pre-school places in Carrickfergus, given reports that there are no remaining places locally following Stage 1 of the admissions process, with places only available in neighbouring towns for stage 2 options; (ii) any urgent actions that will be taken to increase pre-school capacity in Carrickfergus during Stage 2; and (iii) whether additional or temporary pre-school places will be created within Carrickfergus to meet local demand.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for ensuring that sufficient funded pre-school education provision is available in all areas to provide a funded place for very target age child whose parents want it.  The EA has advised that there are currently funded pre-school places available in one setting in Carrickfergus and further places available in settings in neighbouring areas within the five-mile radius.  The EA has also further advised that in an effort to provide additional places in this area it has undertaken the following actions:approached all statutory nursery settings to consider Temporary Flexibility requests; contacted non-statutory settings who had imposed caps on their own numbers to request that these be reconsidered to help address local demand; and engaged with the Northern HSCT to explore any additional non-statutory settings that may be registered to provide funded pre-school education.The availability of additional funded places will depend on the decisions made by individual providers and the demand evidenced at the deadline for applications in Stage Two on 1st April.  Levels of provision and demand in the area will continue to be closely monitored and at the close of Stage Two applications the EA will undertake a further assessment of demand and availability to identify any continuing pressure and determine whether any further actions are required. I would remind all parents that staying with the admissions process to the end and providing additional preferences when invited to do so provides their child with the best chance of securing the offer of a place in a setting of their preference.  Doing so does not disadvantage children in any way, as, should places become available in a higher preference setting, these will be offered to children on setting’s waiting lists in rank order.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any regulatory framework currently in place for property management companies; and (ii) whether he intends to review or strengthen regulations to ensure appropriate standards of transparency, accountability, and service delivery.
answer_text: Other than general aspects of consumer law and company law, there is no specific regulatory framework for property management companies. A recent scoping exercise conducted by my Department and published in October 2025 outlined the issues and problems associated with shared property management.I have indicated that legislative reform is required and the resources necessary to undertake further work are being finalised for the forthcoming financial year. A small team with relevant expertise is in the process of being identified, and once in place will undertake further policy development that should lead to legislative reform in the early part of the next mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in relation to employment tribunals, to detail (i) the number of deposit orders requested by parties; (ii) the number of deposit orders issued by employment tribunal judges; (iii) the number of deposit orders complied with; (iv) the number of cases struck out for non payment of deposit orders; (v) the percentage of employment tribunal cases upheld at final hearing; and (vi) the percentage of employment tribunal cases upheld at final hearing, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: I am able to provide the information below:  		Deposit Orders	2021	2022	2023	2024	2025	Number of Orders Issued		7	6	4	2	3	Number of Orders complied with		2	3	0	0	0	Number of cases struck out for non payment of Orders		1	1	0	0	0			Information in relation to the number of deposit orders requested by parties, and the percentage of employment tribunal cases upheld at final hearing cannot be extracted from the Office of Industrial Tribunals and the Fair Employment Tribunals’ case management system.  Work has commenced on upgrading the case management system to reduce the administration and to provide enhanced reporting capability in future.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) an update on the current planning for the proposed co-location of youth services between the Education Authority and Monkstown Boxing Club; (ii) any outstanding steps; and (iii) the expected timelines.
answer_text: A Partnership Brief has been developed and agreed, framed around the opportunity for collaboration between the Education Authority and Monkstown Boxing Club. The completion of feasibility studies and planning for the proposed site will be taken forward in consultation with focus groups. My Department continues to face significant financial pressures with capital resources prioritised to keep schools open and safe, and there remains no funding available to progress any capital youth schemes. I wrote to the Finance Minister, John O’Dowd MLA, in May 2025, requesting a ringfenced youth capital fund to progress capital projects, but regrettably this was not supported by the Finance Minister.I will continue to make the case for additional and substantial capital investment in our educational infrastructure including youth focused settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any policy her Department has in place, to ensure effective coordination of road works carried out by her Department, local authorities and utility companies; and (ii) any steps her Department is taking to prevent multiple road works being scheduled in close proximity, where this results in significant traffic delays for residents and commuters.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (“the Order”) forms the legislative framework for street works activities. The Department has issued a number of codes of practice under the Order which were prepared jointly by the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) and the Department.The Order places a duty on my Department to use best endeavours in the co-ordination of street works with a parallel duty on the undertakers of these works to co-operate with the department.The Code of Practice for the Co-ordination of Street Works and Works for Road Purposes and Related Matters defines the co-ordination function, with the purpose of minimising inconvenience and to protect the street structure and the apparatus within it.The Northern Ireland Street Works Register and Notification System (NISRANS) is a vital tool in the co-ordination and communication of all such street works.There are already a number of groups set up for street works activities including NIRAUC and four Divisional Road and Utility Committees, DRAUC. Officials in my department are also in daily contact with utility providers to co-ordinate works and balance the potentially conflicting interests of road users and utility customers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment that has been made of the emotional and financial burden placed on respondents involved in employment tribunal cases that extend over multiple years before being struck out.
answer_text: My Department has not made a specific assessment of any emotional and financial burden which may be placed on respondents involved in employment tribunal cases in the circumstances outlined, but it is widely recognised that involvement in any tribunal case or employment dispute is likely to be a stressful experience.		A number of developments in recent years have been aimed at promoting early resolution of employment disputes by alternative means.  New, less complex, tribunal rules and procedures were introduced in 2020.  		This gave rise to the addition of the over-riding objective to deal with cases fairly and justly, more robust case management and greater promotion of alternative dispute resolution.  A number of measures were introduced, designed to assist the parties to come to a mutually agreeable position, and avoid stressful and potentially expensive legal proceedings. 		Early Conciliation was introduced, aimed at attempting to resolve workplace disputes in a legally binding way without the need to take a case to the tribunals. This requires the claimant to make contact with the Labour Relations Agency in the first instance to consider the potential for early conciliation, but even if a case does proceed to the tribunal following this step, conciliation remains an option at any stage of the process.  		In April 2023, the Tribunals also introduced a Judicial Mediation scheme.  This is a voluntary process for resolving claims in which a judge, acting as a mediator, facilitates the parties to find an acceptable solution without having to go to a full hearing. Almost 80% of mediations are successful, delivering real personal and human benefits to the parties in reaching a confidential, consensual resolution, avoiding an adversarial tribunal hearing and preserving employment relationships.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any practical supports currently available to schools to implement TransformED NI; and (ii) the expected timeline for schools to receive delivery of the resources, training, and funding mechanisms set out in the strategy and Delivery Plan.
answer_text: As set out in both the TransformED Delivery Plan and the recently published Implementation Roadmap, my Department is progressing the implementation of TransformED NI in a planned and wellsupported way. I am committed to ensuring that all reforms are phased, sequenced and manageable, with extensive support, training and resources being provided to schools. A wide range of practical support and funding is already available. Investment in teacher professional learning is central to TransformED and a wide range of additional training and investment has been provided. Notably, schools now have access to the £31 million Teacher Professional Learning Fund, alongside new Science of Learning online professional learning platforms, researchinformed conferences, and ongoing updates through the teacher development section of the TransformED hub. The new online TransformED hub brings together all guidance, policies and resources in one place giving schools clarity about what is changing and when.I have also provided schools with three annual TransformED Development Days, providing protected noncontact time to engage with priority areas such as curriculum, assessment and qualifications, reform. These days are supported by high-quality resource packs, including video inputs, structured materials and reflection questions.I am committed to ensuring that every school has the time, tools and professional learning needed to implement TransformED confidently and successfully, so that reform strengthens teaching and learning in every classroom.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions being taken to ensure adequate staffing levels in the Education Welfare Service, with particular reference to the northern locality.
answer_text: There are currently 30 Education Welfare Officers and 3 Team Leads in the Northern Locality.  There are no vacant posts or unfilled posts in the Northern Locality of Education Welfare Service (EWS).As there currently are no vacancies within EWS in the Northern Locality no actions are being taken in relation to staffing levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of Education Welfare Officers currently employed in the northern locality of the Education Authority; and (ii) the number of vacant or unfilled posts.
answer_text: There are currently 30 Education Welfare Officers and 3 Team Leads in the Northern Locality.  There are no vacant posts or unfilled posts in the Northern Locality of Education Welfare Service (EWS).As there currently are no vacancies within EWS in the Northern Locality no actions are being taken in relation to staffing levels.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools currently operating (i) within their allocated budget; (ii) at, or below budget; and (iii) over budget.
answer_text: Details at 31 March 2025 are outlined in the table below:			CategoryNumber of controlled and maintained schoolsNumber of voluntary grammar and grant-maintained integrated schoolsTotal number of schoolsi) within allocated budget*, being controlled and maintained schools with in-year surplus or voluntary grammar (VG) / grant-maintained integrated (GMI) schools not granted emergency grant funding by the Education Authority (EA)21078288iii) over budget i.e. controlled / maintained schools with in-year deficit or VG / GMI schools that were granted emergency grant funding by the EA75411765*  Items i) and ii) are interpreted to be the same for the purpose of answering this question.  Data as at 31 March 2026 will not be available until after the close of the current financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on the Specialist Provision in Mainstream Schools planned for Sunnylands Primary School in Carrickfergus.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that:A Site Assessment for Specialist Provision was carried out at Sunnylands Primary School on 18 February 2026.  This has been completed and is now going through a technical assessment to determine if there are any available solutions.Renewal of an existing licence agreement between EA and Surestart is on-going (Surestart occupy part of the school).The Education Authority is committed to working with schools to explore all viable options for the establishment of specialist provision, and I welcome the opportunity to update the Assembly on progress in relation to Sunnylands Primary School.An initial visit to the school was completed by EA in December 2025, at which point a site and accommodation assessment was requested. A subsequent site visit involving relevant officers took place in February 2026 to assess the available accommodation options in detail.Following that assessment, two potential internal accommodation spaces have been identified as possible locations for specialist provision. One of these spaces represents spare capacity within the school. The second is currently occupied by an external organisation which has been using a significant amount of accommodation within the school for approximately 19 years. EA are actively progressing this work and seeking an update on the lease position so that the school can be kept fully informed. I recognise that timelines in cases such as this can be affected by the complexity of existing arrangements, however the EA remains committed to working collaboratively with the school to explore all viable solutions to support our children and young people with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that every school in Northern Ireland has now been equipped with a defibrillator, to detail (i) whether any assessment has been made of the number of schools who have registered their devices on The Circuit, the national defibrillator network; and (ii) any steps is the Department taking to ensure all school based defibrillators are fully registered, and therefore visible in an emergency.
answer_text: As part of the recent rollout of Automatic External Defibrillators (AEDs), recipient schools were encouraged to register their devices on The Circuit.The next stage of the rollout will involve liaison with the British Heart Foundation and schools to identify the sites where a school’s AED can most usefully be placed in an external cabinet, to further facilitate use by the wider community.Officials will also contact schools directly, to highlight the importance of having their AED properly registered on The Circuit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 40387/22 27, to detail the number of mental health staff who have completed the training referenced.
answer_text: At present, it is not possible to provide a breakdown of the number of health and social care staff working specifically within mental health services who have completed the new autism and neurodiversity awareness training. Completion data is currently reported to the Department in monthly aggregate form by HSC Trust, with figures grouped by staff and professional type rather than by service area. As of 1st January 2026, 365 health and social care staff have completed the autism and neurodiversity awareness training across Northern Ireland since launch.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 39126/22 27, to detail on the mental health specific training referred to, including: (i) the nature and content of the proposed training; (ii) when and how it will be rolled out across mental health settings; and (iii) the approach his Department will take to promote uptake among staff.
answer_text: My Department will work closely with HSC Trusts to promote uptake of the training. The training will be hosted on the LearnHSCNI platform and will be supported by targeted communications out to mental health teams, professional leads and service managers to encourage staff engagement. The training will be formally launched across all Trusts and will be supported by a robust communications plan to raise awareness among staff and ensure managers understand its relevance and encourage staff participation. Promotion will also take place through existing regional mental health networks, and my Department will monitor uptake across Trusts to identify where further support or encouragement may be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children who were diagnosed with type 1 diabetes after presenting in diabetic ketoacidosis, in each of the last five years; and (ii) any steps that are being taken to reduce late diagnosis.

To ask the Minister of Health, following the debate on Lyla’s Law in Westminster on 9 March 2026, whether his Department will review whether systems are sufficient to ensure early detection of type 1 diabetes in children.
To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department is currently considering any support packages for residents affected by the increase in oil prices.
answer_text: Many of our households and businesses are already under pressure from the cost-of-living crisis, and this new energy shock, caused by conflict in the Middle East, is compounding those pressures.When the conflict in the Middle East, began, the reduction in electricity bills of around £30 per year had already been announced and delivery was being progressed by my Department.The £17 million announced by the British government is clearly inadequate, therefore, it is important that whatever support is available, or becomes available going forward, is targeted at those in most need. I am committed to engaging with my Executive colleagues to ensure that support is delivered as quickly as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has carried out an assessment of the applicability of Sir Brian Leveson's Independent Review of the Criminal Courts, to the Northern Ireland criminal justice system.
answer_text: I am currently taking time to consider the detail of the proposals contained within both parts 1 and part 2 of the review, as well as their potential implications for the operation of the criminal justice system in Northern Ireland.The review will be helpful in providing further insights to inform the Speeding Up Justice Programme and as part of this, will include consideration of whether any of the proposals could be adapted for, or applied within, the Northern Ireland justice system, having regard to our distinct legal and institutional arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has any plans to withdraw funding for the TinyStart Programme.
answer_text: The Budgets for 2026-27 or subsequent periods have not yet been formally set by the Executive. However, the Finance Minister has published his proposals in respect of a draft budget for 2026-2029/30, and a consultation exercise is currently underway.  Current indications are that the Department will have a resource funding gap of c£0.7bn increasing to over £1 billion in 2028-29.I am therefore not presently in a position to provide any commitments to funding.  Any decision regarding future funding will be considered in the context of my Department’s 2026-27 budget allocation, the allocation of earmarked funding for the Executive Early Learning and Childcare Strategy and wider financial pressures.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to commission Dexcom G7.

To ask the Minister of Finance, pursuant to AQW 39321/22-27, to detail when he anticipates the Baby Loss Certificate scheme will go live for applications, following the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill receiving Royal Assent on 25 February 2026.
answer_text: The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss (NI) Act 2026 received Royal Assent on 25 February 2026, making it possible for my department to establish the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme by secondary legislation. My officials are working at pace to finalise the draft Baby Loss Certificate Scheme Regulations. I intend to provide these to the Assembly prior to Easter Recess.  Should these regulations proceed through the Finance Committee scrutiny and approval process without delay, I am hopeful that they can be debated by the Assembly at the beginning of May 2026. If the regulations are agreed my officials will then be able to finalise the policies and technical infrastructure to make the scheme operational in June 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, following the recent report by the Northern Ireland Audit Office on her Department’s delivery of active travel, whether she will adopt and implement its recommendations, within the next three months.
answer_text: I welcome the publication of the NIAO report on active travel which recognised that the introduction of the Climate Change Act (NI) 2022 significantly changed the active travel landscape in the North. My Department has accepted all recommendations contained within the report and the NIAO were formally notified of this on 27 November 2025. A copy of the Department’s response to each of the recommendations, including target implementation dates in 2026/27, has been deposited in the Assembly library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action he plans to take to ensure the My Care app does not release sensitive test results or serious diagnoses to patients, before they are able to discuss these with their doctor.
answer_text: Patient safety and the appropriate communication of clinical information are central priorities for encompass and Trusts.  As part of design of My Care, clinical and patient representative input was sought to ensure results were released in a safe and clinically appropriate manner.Under the agreed approach routine normal blood results are automatically released to My Care, however, abnormal test results are systematically held for 15 days to allow clinicians the opportunity to review and discuss these in advance, where appropriate. In addition to this, more complex or potentially sensitive results such as Radiology and Pathology results are not automatically released to My Care. Along with letters these are only shared when a Clinician makes a decision to do so. These safeguards are in place to minimise the risk of patients receiving a potentially distressing diagnosis through the My Care service without prior discussion with their clinician first.  Governance structures are in place and review of these processes continue to ensure the functionality supports clinical practice and meets the needs of service users and this will be monitored to ensure protection remains effective.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to introduce a community-based audiology service for adults to improve access to assessment and treatment.
answer_text: At present the level of demand for audiology services exceeds the capacity available in the HSC leading to unacceptable waiting times for some patients.  A number of factors have contributed to this, including funding pressures and missed opportunities in the past to modernise how health and social care services are delivered.As demand, in the form of referrals into Audiology Services in Northern Ireland has increased, the number of audiologists available within HSC has reduced restricting capacity. The reasons for this are complex but officials in my Department have been working closely with stakeholders to identify how rising demand can be met in the short, medium and longer term.This engagement has included discussions with HSC Trusts, workforce leads, the British Association of Audiologists, and community and primary care providers. While some of these discussions have not resolved the challenges as yet, they have been constructive and are helping to shape thinking around a more sustainable model of care. A key consideration is maintaining the right balance between hospitalbased audiology services and those that may be safely and effectively delivered in community settings, while also ensuring workforce capacity is protected.Officials are currently exploring the development of a pilot proposal for an alternative communitybased audiology model to complement existing HSC services. This pilot would allow a small group of patients to be seen in a new way in the short term, with the intention of gathering evidence to inform a longerterm solution.  Further work on this model of care will be taken forward later this month to fully scope the opportunities available.  Should the pilot commence and be positively evaluated, any further roll-out of such an initiative will need underpinned by an extensive tendering exercise and additional funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he plans to reopen the Access and Inclusion Programme for new applications.
answer_text: The Access and Inclusion Programme has improved accessibility at arts, cultural, and active recreation venues across both urban and rural areas for people with disabilities. The Programme has been previously administered through local Councils and Northern Ireland Museums Council with a funding contribution from DAERA. My officials, in partnership with colleagues in DAERA, are currently considering options regarding future delivery.I have also asked DfC officials to examine opportunities to enhance DfC-owned assets for access and inclusion purposes. These assets include libraries and museum buildings, the Armagh Observatory and Planetarium, the Public Record Office NI, and a range of State Care Monuments across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the availability of cataract surgery services; and (ii) the Health and Social Care Trusts, including hospital sites, that are delivering these services.
answer_text: Cataract surgery services in Northern Ireland are delivered through a mix of dedicated Regional Elective Care Centres, Trust hospitals and independent sector providers. To address high demand and long waiting times, my Department established regional day surgery hubs focusing specifically on cataracts.In order to increase productivity, efficiency and activity around low-complexity cataract cases, the majority of cataract operations are carried out within the dedicated Cataract Day Procedure Centre (DPC) network. As such, only complex surgeries are now undertaken within Belfast Health and Social Care Trusts (BHSCT) and Western Health and Social Care Trusts (WHSCT) locations, these being the two regional ophthalmology provider Trusts. The majority being delivered within the three regional Cataract DPCs, Mid-Ulster, Downe and South Tyrone HospitalsThose remaining in BHSCT and WHSCT acute hospital settings will have more complex surgical, social, and anaesthetic needs, including the need for general anaesthesia.The network of three Cataract Day Procedure Centres was established in 2018/19 to manage lists regionally, to equalise and reduce waits across Northern Ireland and to avoid clinic and theatre short notice cancellations that may happen in larger more acute hospital settings due to emergencies. These measures have seen regional urgent surgical cataract waiting lists fall from over 3,700 in March 2021 to close to zero today.The following HSC Trusts and hospital sites are primary providers of cataract surgery in NI:South Eastern Health & Social Care Trust: Downe Hospital: A designated Regional Cataract Centre providing a high-volume, daily service.Northern Health & Social Care Trust: Mid-Ulster Hospital (Magherafelt): A designated Regional Cataract Centre.Southern Health & Social Care Trust: South Tyrone Hospital (Dungannon): A designated Regional Cataract Centre.Belfast Health & Social Care Trust: Royal Victoria Hospital: Provides ophthalmology services, with the trust managing high-volume, year-round treatment.Western Health & Social Care Trust: Altnagelvin Area Hospital and Omagh Hospital and Primary Care Complex: Involved in the elective care transformation programme. On occasion in an effort to manage waiting lists, the Department use independent sector providers for, in some cases, weekend, or "in-reach" capacity.  There are a number of private providers of cataract surgery in NI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the low level of female applications to the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS), to detail (i) any measures in place to support the retention of female firefighters; (ii) any policies in place regarding (a) maternity leave; (b) flexible working arrangements; and (c) return-to-work support for firefighters in operational posts; and (iii) whether any assessment has been made of whether these measures are sufficient to improve both recruitment and long-term retention of women in the NIFRS.
answer_text: Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) female operational workforce has increased from 4.65% in April 2020 to 8.45% in January 2026. In the last Wholetime Firefighter campaign, applications from females increased from 12% to 16%.  Notwithstanding that, NIFRS aim to increase the number of female firefighters year on year.Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) support the retention of female firefighters through an inclusive workplace culture, staff training, the Women’s Staff Network, Human Resource guidance, suitable uniforms and equipment, and womenfocused development programmes. Flexible shift patterns and the option to request flexible working also help support work–life balance.(a) NIFRS maternity policy offers enhanced protections for operational female staff, requiring immediate removal from operational duties once pregnancy is known or suspected, followed by mandatory risk assessments that are regularly reviewed throughout pregnancy. Pregnant staff are also placed on modified duties and often carry out administrative duties to support either their own watch or another department. This ensures health and safety compliance and reduces attrition risk by providing structured support and appropriate adjustments for pregnant firefighters.  Furthermore, the NIFRS maternity policy provides up to 52 weeks’ leave, antenatal care time, statutory and enhanced pay, up to 10 Keeping in Touch days, enabling female operational staff to attend training, maintain skills, and prepare for a phased return to work without affecting maternity entitlements. This measure supports confidence, operational readiness, and continuity, factors known to improve post-maternity retention.(b) NIFRS flexible working policy allows all employees to request changes to working hours, working patterns, start and finish times, or job-share arrangements, with all requests considered under statutory requirements and refusals permitted only on recognised business grounds.(c) All returning staff are supported through existing maternity, shared parental leave, occupational health and flexible working provisions.NIFRS regularly assess the effectiveness of its measures through statutory monitoring and Article 55 reporting, which informs targets to increase female representation.  NIFRS also evaluates positive action initiatives, such as inclusive advertising, womenfocused programmes and “Come and Try” days, alongside outreach and application trends to identify progress and areas for further improvement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking in relation to the ongoing issues surrounding the Radar Tower in Carrickfergus, given its B2 listed status and current state of dereliction, including (i) whether his Department has engaged with Mid and East Antrim Borough Council regarding the unresolved title issues associated with the site; (ii) any protections or enforcement actions available to ensure the preservation of this listed structure; (iii) whether any funding streams or regeneration schemes could be made available to support the restoration or adapt
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department plans to take to replace the diabetic nurse and restore diabetic clinics at Castle Practice, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: My Department has contacted the Castle Practice on this occasion and they have confirmed that all the diabetic patients at the practice are regularly reviewed and have other opportunities to be reviewed on request at any time.  Patients with a diabetes diagnosis are also called several times a year for vaccinations.The practice has also advised that they have never employed a Diabetic Nurse, however they do employ a Practice Nurse who has skills in diabetes treatment, asthma treatment, and many other areas of Primary health care.The Diabetic clinics run weekly for Castle Practice patients who are called and recalled in advance of their appointment to have bloods taken, face to face with the Healthcare Assistant at the practice.  This is then followed up with a face to face consultation with the Practice Nurse who will have access to the most up to date diabetic blood results.As GPs are independent contractors, my Department does not make decisions on how GPs manage the day to day running of their practices, including human resources management, the use of technology, telephony and management of their appointment system. This also includes clinical decisions and related clinics offered within the practice.  These decisions are made locally.The practice has asked that any patients who are concerned about their diabetes or their review should, in the first instance, contact the practice manager to discuss.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the Disability Discrimination Act audit on accessibility in Parliament Buildings.

To ask the Minister of Health to detail the (i) commissioning status of abiraterone acetate within the health service for the treatment of advanced prostate cancer; and (ii) rationale as to why it is not routinely available in the same way as in other UK jurisdictions.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. This link has ensured that Northern Ireland has access to up-to-date, independent, professional, evidence-based guidance on the value of health care interventions. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland. Where NICE has recommended the use of abiraterone (Zytiga®) in the treatment of prostate cancer, this medicine is available to eligible patients in Northern Ireland, in line with the current NICE guidelines. Further information on this treatment is available at the following links:https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta259https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta387https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta951https://www.nice.org.uk/guidance/ta1110Whilst there is a process to check the guidance for legal and policy applicability here, there is no Departmental reassessment of the clinical or cost evidence used by NICE in coming to its decisions and forming its advice.In circumstances where NICE is yet to complete a full appraisal, or does not recommend a particular drug, my Department does not routinely commission that therapy for use in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland does not have its own health technology assessment body or equivalent to NICE, Scottish Medicines Consortium or All Wales Medicine Strategy Group, that could make a bespoke determination for Northern Ireland patients in the absence of NICE guidance.My Department has recently published a review of HSC medicines access arrangements which recognises the need to take action to ensure continued access to clinically and cost-effective medicines for the Northern Ireland population on an equitable basis, including in cases where NICE have not made a determination, but use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Informed by the review’s recommendations, my Department plans to review the existing workforce infrastructure underpinning medicines access pathways to identify potential resource requirements needed to strengthen existing access arrangements, including for use of off-label and off-patent medicines where use is supported by clinical evidence and best practice. Any additional resource requirements for medicines access infrastructure, and additional costs associated with provision of access for off-label or off-patent medicines, will need to be considered within the context of my Department’s challenging financial position and competing priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any assessment he has made of the impact of the forthcoming UK Government changes to Making Tax Digital for Income Tax Self-Assessment, including the removal of the 10 per cent wear-and-tear allowance for childminders, on the financial sustainability of childminding provision; and (ii) any engagement he has had with HM Treasury, and HMRC, regarding the potential impact for childcare capacity here.
answer_text: I refer the member to the answer to AQO 3176/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the status of the rollout of hybrid closed loop systems for people living with type 1 diabetes, including (i) the planned implementation timeline in each Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) the eligibility criteria being applied; and (iii) any funding allocated to support equitable access.

To ask the Minister of Health, given the importance of structured education in improving health outcomes for people with diabetes, to detail (i) the funding status for programmes such as DESMOND and DAFNE; and (ii) any assessment his Department has made of the current uptake and capacity for these programmes.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Diabetes Network, within my Department, commissions structured diabetes education within Northern Ireland.  A suite of quality assured Structured Diabetes Education have been agreed in response to the implementation of the Diabetes Strategic Framework, supporting self-management for people living with diabetes.DAFNE and DESMOND programmes have been regionally agreed as the two adult education programmes which are consistent with NICE criteria.  Both these programmes are quality assured.CHOICE programme has been regionally agreed as the paediatric and young person programme for Northern Ireland and achieved Quality Institute for Self-Management Education and Training (QISMET) accreditation in June 2023.All three programmes are available in all five HSC Trusts in Northern Ireland, delivered by trained programme educators.Uptake of these programmes have continued to increase post COVID, however there are local variation in terms of access, accessibility and delivery.  The Northern Ireland Diabetes Network has been working with stakeholders to develop a Regional Structured Diabetes Education Coordination Service model which will provide equity, reduce variability and improve patient education across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment made by his Department or the Health and Social Care Board regarding the clinical and cost effectiveness of abiraterone acetate; and (ii) when a final decision on routine commissioning is expected.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here, and where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 38766/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any property owned by her Department in the vicinity of the Shore Road and Station Road, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: My Department owns a property at 110 Shore Road, Greenisland, which is currently being progressed for disposal.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any actions being taken to address the illicit breeding and importing of dogs from the UK mainland and Ireland for the purposes of dog fighting.
answer_text: It is an offence under the Welfare of Animals (Northern Ireland) Act 2011 to organise, attend, participate in, or promote animal fighting and enforcement responsibilities sit with the PSNI. Councils retain responsibility for enforcing dog breeding rules and my Department liaises regularly with both bodies to ensure animal welfare laws are maintained.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the current suppliers of all products provided by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Continence Service.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail all products currently available through the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Continence Service.
answer_text: The Northern Health and Social Care Trust continence service provides treatment and management options for people who experience bladder and bowel dysfunction. There are a range of products currently available via the Northern Health and Social Care Trust adult continence service, these are;Tena men level 2Tena comfort mini superTena comfort normal, plus compact, extra super maxiTena fit premium small, medium, large, extra-large and XX largeTena slip plus X small, small, medium, largeTena slip super medium, largeTena slip Bariatric XXLTena slip maxi small, medium, largeTena slip active fit plus medium, largeTea slip active fit maxi medium, largeTena flex plus small, medium, large, X largeTena flex super small, medium, large, X largeTena flex maxi small, medium, large, x largeTena pants plus x small, medium, large, x largeTena pants super small, medium, largeThese products are currently supplied by Tena (Essity). As this service is to provide treatment and management options for people who experience bladder and bowel dysfunction, products for the management of menstruation are not provided by this service.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether any products supplied by the Northern Health and Social Care Trust Continence Service are clinically suitable for the management of menstruation.
To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for an update on the delays in the family court system; and (ii) to detail any actions her Department is taking to address these delays.
answer_text: The management of individual cases in the family courts is a matter for the independent judiciary. However, the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995, in Article 3(2), provides that, in any proceedings in which any question with respect to the upbringing of a child arises, the court must have regard to the general principle that any delay in determining the question is likely to prejudice the welfare of the child. This is often referred to as the ‘no delay principle’.    The performance standards published in the business plan for the Northern Ireland Courts and Tribunals Service (NICTS) reflect the performance target of listing 90% of Children Order applications for first direction within six weeks of receipt of correct information. The NICTS Annual Report 2024/25 demonstrates that the actual performance achieved for the reporting period was 92%. The full report can be found here.I am aware that a judicially led “Early Resolution Court Pilot” is commencing in Laganside Family Care Centre (County Court), Belfast on 4 March 2026 for an initial period of twelve months subject to review. The aim of the pilot is to improve outcomes for children by reducing delay, encouraging early agreement, and narrowing issues at the earliest stage through focused judicial case management.More generally, in regard to work being undertaken by my Department, my Enabling Access to Justice Programme includes policy projects that will consider the circumstances in which legal aid can best be used to enhance access to justice and support positive outcomes for families through the family courts, and the circumstances in which alternative support mechanisms may be more appropriate, in particular through consultation processes relating to the means and merits tests for legal aid.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 39206/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail her Department's current expected response and repair times for reported street light faults, with specific reference to the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Street lighting outages are generally being repaired within the required timeframe of five working days from receipt of the initial report. However, underground cable faults, where excavation works are necessary, may take longer to locate and repair.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment his Department has made of the financial barriers faced by university students in accessing NHS dental services; and (ii) whether he will consider introducing free NHS dental care for university students.
answer_text: The Department of Health currently provides assistance with health costs, for individuals who qualify through income-related benefits or through the Low-Income Scheme. Eligibility for help with health costs is determined by the Department for Communities. Depending on their personal circumstances, part-time and some full-time university students may qualify for income-related benefits or the Low-Income Scheme and may therefore receive help with health costs including health service dental treatment. Students and prospective students can seek advice on their eligibility from their local Jobs and Benefits Office or student adviser. There is also support on the nidirect website at the following links: https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/benefits-higher-education-students.Help with health costs | nidirectThere are currently no plans to expand free dental treatment to additional population groups. Any such proposal would require detailed consideration of eligibility and affordability, alongside the significant financial pressures already facing the health budget. The Low-Income Scheme remains the primary route for means-tested assistance for dental treatment.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any assessment her Department has made on the safety of the roundabout at Carrickfergus Castle, given police data on collisions over the last five years.
answer_text: The Department receives annual information from the PSNI on personal injury road traffic collisions, which is considered alongside a range of other factors as part of an evidenceled approach to assessing road safety risk and informing decisions on where interventions may be most appropriate, within the context of  finite resources.My Department undertook a review of collision cluster sites in the Mid and East Antrim area in 2025, including the roundabout at Carrickfergus Castle.  Collision data at this location did not indicate a common causation factor and on the basis of this information, this location is not identified as a priority for additional measures at this time; however, it will continue to be kept under review as further data and information become available.Concerns regarding driver behaviour can also be reported to the PSNI using their non-emergency 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions schools are required to take under the Special Educational Needs Framework when a child has been screened and indicators of dyslexia have been identified, but a formal diagnosis has not yet been obtained.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) advises that schools follow the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice (available at Special Educational Needs (SEN) | Education Authority Northern Ireland) to identify and support students with literacy difficulties.The Graduated Response Framework (available at Graduated Response Framework | SEND Plan) guides schools through a six-step IPAMER cycle - identify, plan, action, monitor, evaluate, review - documented in an Individual or Personal Learning Plan (IEP/PLP).  Pupil progress is regularly reviewed, and support levels are adjusted in consultation with parents and pupils using the IEP/PLP process.EA Local Impact Teams provide a range of support, resources and training or targeted support for severe and persistent literacy needs.  SEN Co-ordinators can request support via the Request For Involvement portal, without the need for involvement with the EA Educational Psychology service.Schools have access to professional development EA Learning Portal : EA Bookings, comprehensive resources such as the Literacy Inclusion Toolkit | SEND Plan and Early Assessment for Reading and Literacy Intervention (EARLI) . Parallel resources and information are available for parents/carers via Supporting Literacy at Home – A Toolkit for Parents and Carers | SEND Plan

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the part-time 20mph speed limit scheme at schools.
answer_text: Road Safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph speed zones at 40 across the North focussing on rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies. This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools and represents a significant intervention to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis. Works to implement the latest tranche of schools is progressing well.  My officials are continuing to progress the required legislation and procurement to facilitate the implementation of the 40 schemes. Delivery has commenced at a number of sites and will continue to roll out over the months ahead.  In addition to the above, I recently launched a public consultation on speed limits that will close on 22 April 2026. The consultation seeks views on the wider use of permanent 20mph speed limits, the lowering of national speed limit on rural single and dual carriageways, and alignment of HGV speed limits with other regions. Further consideration on the use of part-time 20mph speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any guidance that has been issued to schools regarding progression through the Special Educational Needs Staged Approach, where dyslexia is suspected but not formally diagnosed.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) advises that schools follow the Special Educational Needs (SEN) Code of Practice (available at Special Educational Needs (SEN) | Education Authority Northern Ireland) to identify and support students with literacy difficulties.The Graduated Response Framework (available at Graduated Response Framework | SEND Plan) guides schools through a six-step IPAMER cycle - identify, plan, action, monitor, evaluate, review - documented in an Individual or Personal Learning Plan (IEP/PLP).  Pupil progress is regularly reviewed, and support levels are adjusted in consultation with parents and pupils using the IEP/PLP process.EA Local Impact Teams provide a range of support, resources and training or targeted support for severe and persistent literacy needs.  SEN Co-ordinators can request support via the Request For Involvement portal, without the need for involvement with the EA Educational Psychology service.Schools have access to professional development EA Learning Portal : EA Bookings, comprehensive resources such as the Literacy Inclusion Toolkit | SEND Plan and Early Assessment for Reading and Literacy Intervention (EARLI) . Parallel resources and information are available for parents/carers via Supporting Literacy at Home – A Toolkit for Parents and Carers | SEND Plan

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure full funding and implementation of continuous glucose monitoring, and TA943 technology, so that all patients with type one diabetes have access to devices, in line with National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidance.
answer_text: There are many Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices available to support people living with diabetes.  The most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.Funding for Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices which are not listed on the NI Drug Tariff would need to come directly from the Trusts or privately funded by the patient. Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, work has been completed in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support. Funding is required for devices, in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  I have supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan. Similar to other nations, a phased 5-year implementation plan is in the final stages of development, in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation. Funding has been secured to support the implementation of this plan through the Regional Insulin Pump service.  This will support equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department will take to ensure deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals are not disadvantaged when utilising emergency services.
answer_text: The Department of Health commission a Regional Communication Support Service (RCSS), provided by Sign Language Interactions (SLI), Sorenson.  The RCSS is a service for people who are Deaf, Deafblind, or hard of hearing and require an interpreter when accessing health and social care services.  This service was introduced in April 2023 and is currently available as required in all Emergency Departments in hospitals across the region.  It is recognised that at times of an emergency, it is not always possible to access a face-to-face interpreter immediately.  The RCSS also provides a remote service, whereby an interpreter can be accessed on demand via video link, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.  The DoH along with key partners from SLI, HSC Trusts and community and voluntary sector organisations, are currently focused on a number of initiatives to improve these services further.  This includes developments to ensure reliable connectivity in EDs, exploration of remote access interpreting during ambulance conveyancing to hospital and provision of education and increased awareness of the service, for both service users and HSC Staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail how the Sign Language Bill will ensure that deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals will not be disadvantaged when utilising emergency services.
answer_text: The Sign Language Bill will place duties on those public bodies listed as prescribed organisations.  This will include ensuring that information and services provided by the organisation are as accessible to individuals in the deaf community, who normally rely on British Sign Language (BSL) or Irish Sign Language (ISL), as they are to individuals who are not in the deaf community.The Department of Health has oversight for the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS) which is a listed HSC Trust and the Northern Ireland Fire Service (NIFRS). The Department of Justice has oversight for the PSNI and has advised that matters relating to how the Police Service of Northern Ireland supports deaf and hard-of-hearing individuals when utilising emergency services fall under the operational responsibility of the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.Although there is current accessibility to emergency services through BSL and some through ISL, it is my intention that NIAS, NIFRS, PSNI and the NI Policing Board will be listed, alongside every Executive department, as prescribed organisations for the purposes of the Sign Language Bill to ensure access to all their emergency services for BSL and ISL users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of pothole-related compensation claims, relating to roads in East Antrim, which are currently outstanding.
answer_text: My Department maintains information relating to roads related claims by financial year based on road stretch within local Section Office boundaries. Section Offices broadly align with Council areas. The East Antrim constituency falls within the Mid and East Antrim section office.I can confirm that, as of 3 March 2026, there were 46 roadsrelated claims that are yet to conclude, arising from incidents within the Mid and East Antrim Section Office where the cause of the injury or damage is currently recorded on my Department’s Claims Management System as a pothole.The list below details the top ten road stretches within the Mid and East Antrim section office with the cumulative highest number of claims received during the last full five financial years (i.e. from 2020/2021 to 2024/2025).
