question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 37818/22-27, whether any staff from the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service have completed the autism and neurodiversity awareness training since its launch.
answer_text: Autism and neurodiversity awareness training is available for all NIAS staff to complete as part of their Continuous Personal Development plan. However, at present this training is not mandatory and is an optional module available to staff.My Department is unable to provide definitive data on the number of NIAS staff who have completed this training.  As the training is optional, completion rates are not routinely recorded by NIAS.  In addition, Mencap deliver training to NIAS operational staff on ASD, neurodiversity and learning disability as part of core education.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 39208/22-27, whether she (i) has considered the provisions of the Victims and Courts Bill, including the introduction of Prohibited Steps Orders; and (ii) will assess how such orders could be used, or strengthened, to safeguard children in cases where a parent has been convicted of serious child sexual offences, including offences against children other than their own.
answer_text: ANSWERAs noted in my response to AQW 39208/22-27, judges in this jurisdiction can suspend or in certain cases completely remove parental responsibility where the parent has committed a serious criminal offence, including child sexual abuse. My officials continue to review our legislation on parental responsibility taking account of any relevant developments in other jurisdictions, including the provisions of the Victims and Courts Bill. However, given existing legislative commitments and resource constraints, it would not be possible to initiate new legislation in this area during the current Assembly mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale for maintaining five Health and Social Care Trusts rather than a single, unified Trust.
answer_text: I am focused firmly on the need to maintain service and system stability, so that the key elements of the HSC Reset plan can be delivered effectively and at pace.Whilst I can understand the appeal, undertaking a major structural reorganisation would require substantial attention, administrative effort and transitional planning. This would risk diverting attention and resources away from key priorities including prevention initiatives, waiting list reduction and working to make our system financially sustainable. In the present operational and financial climate, and already operating within a reduced Assembly term, reforming internal structures would not be the most effective use of limited capacity.There is also evidence which demonstrates that structural reform is likely to deliver limited financial benefit at this time. Most of the Health and Social Care expenditure relates to staffing and frontline service delivery. Even under a single Trust model many of these core costs would remain. I have been clear that HSC Trusts should operate as much as possible as one system. A range of structures have been established to promote this. The Senior Leadership Group was established in June 2025. Its purpose is to provide strategic direction to the Health and Social Care system and to work collaboratively to identify, manage and resolve system issues and exploit synergies and opportunities for efficiency. Additionally,  HSC Trusts’ Committees in Common was established in late 2025 to support HSC organisations in adopting a whole-system approach, to tackle regional challenges, strengthen shared decision-making and leadership. A system financial management group has also been established to promote a one system approach to addressing the unprecedented financial challenges and promote efficiency and cost improvement across all aspects of our system.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) to detail any (a) financial support; (b) wage subsidies; and (c) training grants, currently available to small hairdressing salons that employ trainees; and (ii) whether she plans to introduce any additional measures to ensure that training requirements do not act as a barrier to small businesses taking on apprentices or trainees.
answer_text: My Department provides funding for the directed, off-the-job training element of apprenticeships, allocating approximately £48 million annually across both the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes. This financial support is specifically designed to ensure that employers do not bear any financial responsibility for the delivery of apprenticeship training.Apprentices participating on the ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeships programmes are employees. As employees earning a wage, they do not receive extra financial support from the Department. Employers of apprentices must pay at least the appropriate National Minimum Wage rate, as determined by HMRC.Businesses may qualify for an employer incentive payment following the successful achievement of a Level 2 or Level 3 apprenticeship framework by their apprentice. For Hairdressing frameworks, the incentive is set at £670. The incentive is intended to encourage employers to support apprentices through to full achievement of their apprenticeship.  In addition, employers in the North are exempt from Class 1 National Insurance contributions for apprentices under 25, if specific conditions are met.Finally, the Department’s Apprenticeship Action Plan sets out a range of actions intended to support the continued growth of the apprenticeships system including piloting new Apprenticeship Navigators to help support businesses employ their first apprentice.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the number of staff, currently employed within her Department, with the responsibility for inspecting the performance of utility companies in relation to the quality of road reinstatements, following road openings; and (ii) for her assessment of whether the current level of staffing is adequate to ensure compliance with statutory inspection requirements, given the Northern Ireland Audit Office finding that her Department is not completing the required number of inspections of road openings.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.Undertakers remain responsible for the reinstatement for a minimum period of 2 years, 3 years for a deep excavation, after registration of the permanent reinstatement.After the end of the warranty period there is no further liability on the statutory undertaker for the reinstatement unless it could be proved that the original reinstatement did not comply with the prescribed specification.Road defects identified during routine maintenance inspections will be checked if it is thought the defect may be because of previous undertaker works to see if it can be notified to the undertaker for repair if within the warranty period. There are 21 staff, currently employed within my Department, with the responsibility for inspecting the performance of utility companies in relation to the quality of road reinstatements, following road openings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of instances that have required her Department to fund repairs, due to reinstatement defects emerging, after the expiry of the utility warranty period, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 (the Order), forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers. Undertakers remain responsible for the reinstatement for a minimum period of 2 years, 3 years for a deep excavation, after registration of the permanent reinstatement.After the end of the warranty period there is no further liability on the statutory undertaker for the reinstatement unless it could be proved that the original reinstatement did not comply with the prescribed specification.Road defects identified during routine maintenance inspections will be checked if it is thought the defect may be because of previous undertaker works to see if it can be notified to the undertaker for repair if within the warranty period. The Department does not hold the information requested as road defects are not recorded by causation factors.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment made of the impact of changes to the State Pension age on women, in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Department for Communities has not undertaken a standalone, Northern Ireland specific assessment of the impact of changes to the State Pension age on women.		Factors specific to Northern Ireland residents are considered in the analysis underpinning UK-wide State Pension age reviews. Local stakeholders, including the Commissioner for Older People for Northern Ireland and Advice NI, have provided evidence highlighting how changes to the State Pension age may disproportionately affect groups within Northern Ireland.		Wider analysis of the impact of State Pension age changes—particularly on women born in the 1950s—has been carried out at UK-wide level, with the Parliamentary and Health Service Ombudsman’s report highlighting communication failings around the accelerated pension age increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, under the present regulatory framework, to detail (i) any enforcement actions that are taken by her Department when a utility company fails to properly reinstate a road, including details of the warranty periods applied to reinstatement works, two years or three years, depending on excavation depth; and (ii) her Department’s recourse when defects arise outside the warranty period.
answer_text: The Street Works (Northern Ireland) Order 1995 forms the legislative framework for street works by statutory undertakers and requires the undertaker to comply with the Specification for the Reinstatement of Openings in Roads.My Department applies the procedures for inspection of reinstatements and for dealing with reinstatement failures as set out in the Northern Ireland Road Authority and Utilities Committee (NIRAUC) Code of Practice for Inspections.  Where a statutory undertaker fails to carry out reinstatement to an acceptable standard, the undertaker is duly notified to carry out remedial works to bring the reinstatement to the required standard.Undertakers remain responsible for the reinstatement for a minimum period of 2 years, 3 years for a deep excavation, after registration of the permanent reinstatement.After the end of the warranty period there is no further liability on the statutory undertaker for the reinstatement unless it could be proved that the original reinstatement did not comply with the prescribed specification.Road defects identified during routine maintenance inspections will be checked if it is thought the defect may be because of previous undertaker works to see if it can be notified to the undertaker for repair if within the warranty period. There are 21 staff, currently employed within my Department, with the responsibility for inspecting the performance of utility companies in relation to the quality of road reinstatements, following road openings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Mid and East Antrim Local Economic Partnership.
answer_text: The Mid and East Antrim Local Economic Partnership (LEP) has submitted an action plan for this year, covering staffing costs associated with the establishment of the LEP and development of a multi-year Action Plan. The LEP is making positive progress on finalising its multi-year Action Plan, which I expect to be submitted for approval in the coming weeks. My officials and Invest NI are continuing to work closely with the LEP to support this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 39610/22-27, whether any consideration has been given to National Crime Agency staff based in Northern Ireland, who, under National Police Force proposals, would transition from civil servants to police staff.
answer_text: Implementation of the National Crime Agency’s (NCA) inclusion into the National Police Service, including staff based in Northern Ireland, will be the responsibility of the UK Government. While the Home Office White Paper relating to policing reform proposals applies to England and Wales, I have already engaged with the Director General of the NCA to highlight that any indirect implications for Northern Ireland will need to be identified at an early stage.  At this point, I am not aware of any decisions relating to NCA staff based in Northern Ireland.  Any future proposals which emerge that have potential implications for Northern Ireland, will be considered carefully, in line with devolved arrangements and in consultation with relevant stakeholders.  I will of course engage with Executive and Assembly colleagues at the appropriate junctures.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any engagement her Department has had with the Department for Communities, to ensure that the roll-out of smart electricity meters complements the delivery of the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy and maximises benefits for households, struggling with high energy costs.
answer_text: The Department has been working closely with officials in the Department for Communities to ensure that the Warm Healthy Homes Strategy aligns with the principles set out in the Energy Strategy.A public consultation on the draft Smart Metering Design Plan was launched in October 2024. A central principle of the draft plan is that consumers will be at the heart of smart metering. Access to a smart meter will enable all consumers to participate more actively in the energy market, supporting better energy management and helping households save money through new products and services.The rollout of smart meters will be designed to meet the needs of all consumers, including groups with specific access requirements and other vulnerabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that Olderfleet Primary School currently provides a Key Stage One specialist unit, but has no Key Stage Two provision, to detail any steps being taken by (i) his Department; and (ii) the Education Authority, to establish a Key Stage Two specialist unit at the school.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has been working closely with Olderfleet PS in relation to the establishment of a Key Stage 2 specialist provision class within the school. Despite several site visits, due to an increase in mainstream pupil numbers, previous solutions to provide the Key Stage 2 class are no longer feasible. The EA remains committed to working actively with the school, to consider any future solutions that may support the development of this much needed provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any assessment he has made of the adequacy of the current rate of payment for foster parents; and (ii) whether he has any plans to review or increase this rate.
answer_text: The allowances paid to foster carers in Northern Ireland (NI) are for costs associated with day to day care and are outlined within the annual published Fostering Model Scheme. The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis in respect of the decision for an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. The current published allowance rates are 2024-25 rates, and these include the annual uplift increase of 3% which was applied to foster care allowance rates for the 2024-25 financial year. The current allowance rates are published on the Department of Health website and are available to view at Foster care allowances | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). Consideration of an annual uplift to apply to allowance rates payable for 2025-26 is currently under review, and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift to rates for 2025-26 will be backdated to April 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for her assessment of whether the current policy guidance on roadside hedge cutting, during the closed season of 1 March to 31 August, is sufficiently (i) clear; (ii) robust; and (iii) enforceable, to prevent routine, non-essential cutting that may impact nesting birds and biodiversity.
answer_text: My Department carries out hedge and tree maintenance only where it is necessary for road safety in order to protect biodiversity and the policy position is clear, that hedge cutting is not permitted between 1 March and 31 August, as this is the recognised bird nesting season in the North. This closed period is set out in DAERA’s cross-compliance rules, which exist specifically to protect birds, nests and eggs and to conserve wildlife habitats.  There is also a single, clearly defined exception, hedge cutting during this period may only take place where there is a demonstrable health and safety issue, such as where visibility is obstructed for drivers or pedestrians. In these circumstances, only the road facing side should be cut, and only the minimum amount required to address the safety risk.  The Department’s hedge management approach operates within:The Roads (NI) Order 1993, which places a duty on my Department to maintain the road network, andDAERA’s cross-compliance rules, which prohibit hedge cutting during the closed season and provide for financial penalties for claimants of DAERA scheme payments where breaches occur.  In addition, the Wildlife (NI) Order 1985 provides legal protection for nesting birds, making it a criminal offence to disturb or destroy nests, eggs or young birds. Cutting hedges during the nesting season therefore carries a clear risk of harming wildlife and potentially committing an offence.Enforcement of the current policy guidance is supported through both environmental and road safety mediums. The PSNI has powers under wildlife legislation, and my Department can require action where hedges obstruct roads, footways, driver visibility, street lighting, or traffic signs. While enforcement can be resource dependent in practice, the policy itself is clear, legally grounded, and designed to prevent routine, non-essential cutting during the closed season.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 38537/22-27, for his assessment of whether the number of dentists listed in the answer is adequate to serve the population of East Antrim.
answer_text: At a UK-wide level, relative to its population size, Northern Ireland has the highest number of dentists—60 dentists per 100,000, compared with 43 in England, 59 in Scotland, and 46 in Wales.Within East Antrim specifically, as of 31 December 2025, there were 15 practices offering Health Service care, a reduction of one since 2020/21. More significantly, the number of dentists working in these practices has increased from 45 to 57 over the same period. This growth represents a substantial expansion in local clinical capacity.Patient registration data is not reported by Northern Ireland Assembly area, rather the geographical unit used in the official statistics is Local Government District (LGD). There are 11 LGDs in Northern Ireland and 2024-25 figures show that the Mid and East Antrim LGD has 54% of its population registered with a dentist. This is the same as the figure for the Northern Ireland average.Based on the above information, it is my assessment that the number of dentists in East Antrim is adequate to serve the population of East Antrim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail in what circumstances a patient would be charged for a referral letter.
answer_text: A GP in Northern Ireland can charge for certain referral letters, but it depends on the type of referral.A GP may charge for referral letters which are not related to Health & Social Care (HSC) ie NHS services, such as referral letters for private medical care, or other letters requested by the patient, such as letters for housing, work, insurance, travel, etc.As independent contractors, GPs provide services to their registered patients under the General Medical Services Contracts Regulations and as such have the authority to decide what letters they complete. Anything outside the contract — including private referral documentation — can be charged for.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any eligibility criteria for Welfare Supplementary Payments when waiting for an appeal to an independent tribunal, following an unsuccessful reassessment form Disability Living Allowance to Personal Independence Payment; including where applicants can find information on this.
answer_text: When reassessment from Disability Living Allowance (DLA) to Personal Independence Payment (PIP) results in a disallowance or a reduction in benefit of at least £10 per week, a Welfare Supplementary Payment (WSP) can be awarded if the person was in receipt of DLA on the date the invitation to claim PIP was issued or the date a PIP claim was made if no invitation was issued. In the case of a PIP disallowance the person is also required to lodge an appeal before an award can be made.If the claimant’s initial appeal is unsuccessful and leave to appeal to the Social Security Commissioner is granted, WSP will become payable again from the date the leave to appeal is granted.Claimants do not need to apply for a WSP as potential eligibility is identified by the Department for consideration of a payment.  A letter issues to the claimant explains the reason for the WSP award. Additional information can be found by contacting the Supplementary Payments Team helpline or on the NI Direct website at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/support-if-youre-affected-welfare-changes

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36857/22–27, to detail (i) the number of risk assessments currently awaiting completion; and (ii) the average timescale for completing a risk assessment.
answer_text: The Education Authority has advised me that as of 9 February 2026, there are 18 risk assessments relating to home to school transport awaiting completion and the average timescale for completing a risk assessment is six to seven weeks. These timescales can vary depending on the complexity of each case as some assessments may require the coordination of multidisciplinary meetings and/or consultation with equipment manufacturers.Once a risk assessment has been completed, additional time may be required to introduce the recommended control measures or to review their implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 16117/22-27, to detail any further plans to enhance Carrickfergus Grammar School's sports provision.
answer_text: School Enhancement ProgrammeCarrickfergus Grammar School was announced in May 2020 to advance in design under the second call of the School Enhancement Programme (SEP).  The proposal includes provision of a new sports centre to include a sports hall, changing facilities, fitness suite and Physical Education classroom.The project is being managed by the Education Authority (EA) who are working with Carrickfergus Grammar School to identify options for inclusion in the business case which are most suitable for the school.  The preferred option to be taken forward will be identified by the approved business case.I can confirm that funding continues to be available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender for a contractor will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.Curriculum-Led Investment ProgrammeI launched the Curriculum-Led Investment Programme on 3 December 2024.  This programme aims to ensure that every post-primary school in Northern Ireland has access to high-quality synthetic sports facilities.I wrote to the Principal of Carrickfergus Grammar School on 2 February 2026 to advise that the school’s application to the programme has been successful and they have been selected to receive a synthetic pitch as part of this initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 38129/22-27, to detail (i) any actions the Housing Executive is taking, under the contractual performance framework, in response to Greenview’s failure to deliver the Bathroom/Kitchen/Rewire Scheme in the Ransevyn Park area of Whitehead; and (ii) any ways in which these actions will prevent further delays, and ensure affected tenants receive the promised works within a clear and acceptable timeframe.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Housing Executive has implemented a range of contractual performance measures in response to Greenview’s delivery of the CT0136 Bathroom/Kitchen/Rewire contract, which includes the scheme at Ransevyn Park, Whitehead.The Housing Executive has maintained ongoing engagement with Greenview through regular contract performance meetings. These have included the requirement for the submission of Remedial Improvement Plans and the monitoring of progress against agreed actions. A key issue identified has been Greenview’s ability to secure and retain sufficient subcontractor resources to deliver the programme, notwithstanding previous assurances provided.In response, the Housing Executive has set a further deadline for Greenview to submit the required subcontractor documentation and to provide assurance regarding their capacity and resourcing arrangements for the delivery of the remaining programme.Our priority is to minimise further delays and to ensure that works proceed only where sufficient resources are in place, in order to limit disruption to tenants. We will continue to apply the full range of contractual mechanisms available, in line with the terms of the contract, including enforcement of programme requirements, the application of contractual damages where appropriate, and, where necessary, consideration of contract termination.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any engagement she has had with the Home Office, in relation to the white paper, From Local to National: A New Model for Policing.
answer_text: To date, there has been one officiallevel meeting with the Home Office in October 2025.  I was also updated by the Minister for Policing, Sarah Jones MP, on 26 January, on the White Paper proposals on police reform. I have agreed that officials may continue engagement on an information-sharing basis.  In doing so, I have emphasised the importance of utilising existing Northern Ireland policing and governance structures and respecting devolved responsibilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the Home Office white paper, From Local to National: A New Model for Policing; and (ii) the implications of this for staff in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The Home Office White Paper relates to policing reform proposals in England and Wales.  Policing and justice are devolved matters in Northern Ireland, delivered through established legislative, governance and accountability arrangements. I recently discussed the White Paper proposals with the Minister for Policing, Sarah Jones MP, and have confirmed that officials may continue engagement on an information-sharing basis.  In doing so, I have emphasised the importance of utilising existing Northern Ireland policing and governance structures and respecting devolved responsibilities.No decisions have been taken that would affect police officers or staff in Northern Ireland. Should any future proposals emerge that have potential implications for Northern Ireland, these would be considered carefully, in line with devolved arrangements and in consultation with relevant stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the introduction of the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill.
answer_text: The Employment Rights Bill will be introduced to the Assembly once it has been approved by the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) when the next tranche of 20mph speed limit zones at schools, will be released; and (ii) whether Islandmagee Primary School will be considered.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.The introduction of part-time 20mph zones at schools across the North is a significant intervention that has helped to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis and in recognition of this intervention I announced a third tranche of 40 schools bringing the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This latest tranche focused on rural schools that are located on roads where the national speed limit applies, however, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas that would benefit from a reduction in the speed limit, and I am currently undertaking a consultation on Speed Limits which includes the introduction of permanent 20mph zones.Further consideration on the use of permanent 20mph zones will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any assessment her Department has made of the availability and reliability of mobile phone coverage in rural and coastal areas, specifically in Portmuck.
answer_text: Mobile infrastructure and associated services are currently provided on a wholly commercial basis by Mobile Network Operators in the North. Any issues regarding poor levels of service should be directed to these service providers in the first instance.My Department does not monitor availability or reliability of mobile phone coverage.  This is the responsibility of Ofcom, the independent regulator for communications services. I recognise the importance of mobile connectivity, and my department has therefore been leading on a number of initiatives which will help improve  connectivity. These include the Mobile Action Plan and the Barrier Busting Taskforce which identified the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and has provided solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity. This work remains ongoing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the agencies responsible for assessing risk and approving accommodation when convicted sex offenders are rehoused in the community.
To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the Carrickfergus Academy new build.
answer_text: The review of the feasibility report submitted by the Integrated Consultant Team, which detailed feasible options to provide suitable accommodation for the school, was concluded by the Department’s technical advisers on 6 November 2025.  Departmental economists are currently drafting the Business Case which will determine the preferred option to be progressed in design.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail the next steps in the process of the roll out of smart meters, since the consultation has now closed.
answer_text: Officials have now completed a comprehensive analysis of the consultation responses on the draft Design Plan for the rollout of smart electricity meters, reflecting the views provided by a broad range of organisations and individuals.The final Design Plan will be published in the coming weeks. It will set out the roles and responsibilities of the key organisations involved in delivering the rollout, alongside detailed arrangements for consumer protection and engagement. It will also outline the foundational and technical requirements, as well as the overall rollout strategy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any steps his Department will take to ensure (i) children at Acorn Integrated School, Carrickfergus, are not placed at risk; and (ii) road safety provision continues during the interim period before a new school crossing patrol is in post.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for the Home to School Transport Policy and the Education Authority (EA) for the delivery of school transport which includes the operation of the School Crossing Patrol (SCP) scheme. The aim of the SCP scheme is to assist with the prevention of accidents, however parents remain primarily responsible for ensuring their child’s safety, even when an SCP is operating. While the safety of all children is a key priority, my Department has no remit for road safety. You may wish to raise any issues or concerns with the Minister for Infrastructure who has responsibility for road safety policy, or the PSNI with regards to enforcement.   The EA informs me that an application was recently received from Acorn Integrated primary school for a replacement SCP as the post will become vacant in the near future. Where a school requests a replacement SCP, the EA must carry out a site assessment to determine if the site still meets the criteria of the SCP scheme. In the interim, schools are advised to ensure that parents are informed that there is no SCP in operation and are encouraged to share road safety information which is available on NI Direct: Road safety resources | nidirect.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether there are any plans to revise the Checklist for Principals when considering opening or closure of school; and (ii) how any updates will be communicated to schools and Principals.
answer_text: The Checklist for Principals is reviewed each time the Exceptional Closure Circular is issued. The latest circular is DE Circular 2024/12 issued in August 2024.My Department continues to keep all guidance related to Exceptional Closures under review and will write to schools with any updates to the existing processes if required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to Graduated Driver Licensing, to detail (i) the rationale for extending the R plate restriction period from 12 months to 24 months; and (ii) any purpose the R plate will now serve during this extended period, considering the removal of the main restriction of the 45 miles per hour speed limit.
answer_text: The main rationale for introducing a two year post-test ‘new driver period’ is, as several studies have shown, the heightened collision risk for new drivers only levels out after the first two years of solo driving. 	It also aligns with the two year probationary period prescribed in the Road Traffic (New Drivers) (Northern Ireland) Order 1998. Although GDL does not impose any speed restrictions on newly qualified drivers, a specific distinguishing plate will help inform enforcement agencies that the driver is within the passenger restriction period during their first six month post-test. A further distinguishing plate must be displayed for the remaining 18 months of the probationary period. Displaying a distinguishing plate should help deter high risk behaviour and inform other road users of the relative inexperience of the driver. It should also be noted that the New Drivers Order provides for the revocation of a driving licence during the probationary period if a new driver receives an accumulation of six or more penalty points.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to Graduated Driver Licensing, to detail whether the proposed removal of the 45 miles per hour speed limit for new drivers will apply to both L and R plate stages.
answer_text: Learners whose provisional licence entitlement to drive a category B vehicle commences on or after the introduction of GDL on 1st October 2026 will not be subject to a 45mph speed limit. Neither will such learners be subject to a 45mph speed limit when they pass their test.  In addition, any learner who passes a category B driving test on or after 1st April 2027, ie following the six-month transitional period, will not be subject to that speed limit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail when the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme will go live for applications.
answer_text: I am committed to opening the new Baby Loss Certificate Scheme as soon as possible. This will first require the Deaths, Still-births and Baby Loss Bill to be given Royal Assent. It will also require the passage through the Assembly of regulations to set out the details of the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme, which will allow my officials to put in place policies and procedures to make the scheme operational. While we wait for Royal Assent, my officials are working to prepare the required regulations. They are also putting in place the technical systems required to support applications to the Baby Loss Certificate scheme and ensuring that they have the right resources in place to meet demand once the scheme goes live. These preparations will enable us to bring the Scheme into operation as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in relation to Graduated Driver Licensing, to detail whether the removal of the 45 miles per hour speed limit for new drivers will apply to (i) all roads; or (ii) just motorways.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 provides for the removal of the speed limit of 45mph for both learner and newly qualified drivers. As such, it will allow for lessons to be taken up to the posted speed limits on all roads including, where appropriate, on a motorway.  New subordinate legislation is required to mandate that while on a motorway, the learner must be accompanied by either an Approved Driving Instructor or an Approved Motorcycle Instructor.  The proposed Regulations will further restrict Approved Motorcycle Instructors from supervising more than one rider whilst on the motorway.All newly qualified drivers may drive on any road up to the posted speed limit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any actions her Department will take, following the passing of the fireworks motion in the Assembly on 18 November 2025.
answer_text: The motion passed on the 18 November 2025 agreed to the consideration of the appropriate level of penalties for fireworks offences as part of the upcoming sentencing review.On 14 January 2026, I issued a Written Statement to the Northern Ireland Assembly alongside an accompanying press release.  This written statement “Launch of the review of Sentencing Policy” outlined the plans and steps which will be taken as part of this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) any reason for the continued use of Net Annual Values calculations, which are primarily turnover-based, when operating costs across the hospitality sector have risen sharply, and are not adequately reflected in the valuation mode; (ii) any consideration that has been given to changes in trading conditions, since the last valuation, including softening occupancy and room rates during 2025; (iii) his assessment of the extent to which turnover remains an appropriate measure for valuing hospitality businesses, in a post-Covid, high cost environment.
answer_text: (i) The continued use of Net Annual Value (NAV) calculations, including the turnoverbased Receipts & Expenditure (R&E) methodNondomestic rating valuations here are carried out under the Rates (Northern Ireland) Order 1977, which requires all properties to be assessed at the rental value they could reasonably be expected to command at the statutory valuation date. For many hospitality properties, openmarket rental evidence is limited. In these circumstances, valuers use the UKstandard Receipts and Expenditure (R&E) method, which determines rental value by reference to the Fair Maintainable Trade (FMT) of the property and deducts typical operating costs, including energy and staffing costs, before deriving the divisible balance and ultimately the rental value. FMT therefore inherently reflects the property’s trading potential after taking account of typical costs of sales and working expenses relevant to a reasonably efficient operator. (ii) Consideration of changes in trading conditions since the last valuationThe statutory framework requires valuers to base assessments on market conditions as they existed at the fixed valuation date (AVD), not on conditions arising after that date. This means that factors such as softening occupancy or room rates during 2025 cannot be taken into account when determining NAVs for a valuation list whose AVD predates those conditions. Any departure from this principle would be inconsistent with the requirements of Schedule 12 of the Rates Order.  (iii) Assessment of whether turnover remains an appropriate measure for hospitality valuationTurnover remains the accepted basis for assessing rental value in the hospitality sector across the UK where rental evidence is limited. Rents for pubs, hotels and similar premises are typically set in the open market as a percentage of turnover because turnover is the clearest indicator of trading potential and therefore the amount a hypothetical tenant could reasonably afford to pay in rent. The shortened R&E method used here applies fixed percentages to FMT, informed by industry practice and historic analysis. These percentages are reviewed at the start of each revaluation cycle, but any change must be supported by robust empirical evidence from the sector. LPS has sought such evidence from representative bodies, but the level of detailed data required to justify a change has not been provided.  As the Member will be aware, I took the decision to stop the Reval 2026 process on 29 January, I refer you to my statement to the Assembly.Finance Minister Oral Statement - Reval 2026 | Department of Finance

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of waiting times and accessibility for Disabled Facilities Grants for families requiring urgent home adaptations.
answer_text: I have been clear of the need to reduce processing times and improve accessibility for Disabled Facilities Grants, and my Department is working with the Housing Executive and the Department of Health, as part of the wider review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants, to do so.  While progress has been made, work continues. I will put out proposals for public consultation this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of reports that some NHS Trusts in England continue to use fax machines, whether any Health and Social Care Trusts are still using fax machines for clinical or administrative purposes.
answer_text: All Trusts were asked to confirm their current operational use of fax machines. The responses indicate that none of the five HSC Trusts use fax machines for routine clinical or administrative work, though limited businesscontinuity provisions exist in some cases. The responses demonstrate continued progress in the digital modernisation of HSC communications systems, aligning with wider UKwide efforts to eliminate legacy technologies such as fax in favour of secure digital alternatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether she (i) has reviewed Northern Ireland’s legislation on parental responsibility, in cases where a parent has been convicted of serious child sexual offences; and (ii) will consider introducing automatic removal or suspension of parental responsibility in such cases, including where the offender abused a child, other than their own.
answer_text: Parental responsibility is defined under Article 6 of the Children (Northern Ireland) Order 1995  to include the rights, duties, powers, and responsibilities that a parent has in relation to a child. The threshold for removal of parental responsibility is high. A court may only remove parental responsibility where it is satisfied, having considered all the circumstances of the case, that doing so is in the best interests of the child. The welfare of the child is the court’s paramount consideration.Judges in this jurisdiction already have the power to remove or suspend parental responsibility in cases involving:serious criminal offending, including child sexual abuse;behaviour causing significant harm or posing a serious risk to the child;situations where retaining parental responsibility would undermine the child’s welfare, safety, or stability.These powers are exercised on a case-by-case basis, reflecting the principle that family law decisions must respond to the specific circumstances of the child concerned.The current statutory framework therefore provides the courts with the ability to remove or suspend parental responsibility in the most serious and exceptional circumstances, including those involving sexual offences against children.My officials will continue to keep the legislation, and emerging approaches in other jurisdictions under ongoing review to ensure the framework remains robust and responsive to the most serious safeguarding concerns.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the (i) prevalence; and (ii) impact, of delayed or missed diagnosis of endometriosis in children and young people.
answer_text: Although the exact prevalence of endometriosis among children and young people is difficult to determine, it is known that it is one of the most common gynaecological conditions which affects around 1 in 10 women globally, and that symptoms can start from a young age. It is also known that the devastating impact that endometriosis can have on a person’s education, career, relationships, sexual relationships, fertility and quality of life are exacerbated by delayed diagnosis and treatment.This points to a clear need for greater public understanding and awareness around endometriosis, improved diagnostic pathways as well as age-appropriate menstrual health education and healthcare. I welcomed the focus brought to this condition during the Assembly motion in September 2025, which highlighted these important aspects. My Department is working with Health and Social Care Trusts to improve waiting times for diagnosis and treatment of gynaecological conditions including endometriosis, and to improve awareness and education to help ensure that women and girls are referred earlier to appropriate services and treatment pathways. This includes support for gynaecology pathways in primary care where appropriate, providing access to NICE approved treatments for endometriosis on a cost per cost basis, and, subject to resources, planning for the establishment of accredited endometriosis specialist centers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether existing specialist endometriosis services have sufficient capacity to meet patient demand.
answer_text: My Department is committed to ensuring that we have the right model of care, in the right place, at the right time. In this context, we have secured and delivered a Getting It Right First Time report on gynaecology and are progressing the implementation of its recommendations.A Regional Gynaecology Forum has been established to take this work forward, with an Endometriosis Task and Finish Group operating as part of this. This regional group is developing services along the patient pathway, from initial presentation through to diagnostic assessment and onto treatment either locally or in a tertiary centreAll HSC Trusts provide care for endometriosis, with women referred under the care of a consultant gynaecologist. Gynaecology services are delivered through multidisciplinary teams to ensure holistic, responsive and appropriate care throughout the patient journey. Each team member, whether a consultant, nurse, or allied health professional functions as a specialist within their discipline.The diagnostic pathway includes symptom assessment, investigations to determine severity and progression, and planning appropriate next steps. Management options range from ongoing monitoring to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention, with consideration of fertility needs throughout.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is working with the Gynaecology Collaborative to invest in all five Trusts, enhancing capacity for diagnostics, additional laparoscopy sessions and clinical appointments. Work is also underway to develop two regional Endometriosis Centres within the Western and Belfast Trusts. These centres will provide complex surgical care for women who require it, supported by multidisciplinary teams of surgeons, including colorectal and urological consultants.It is clear capacity does not meet demand in this area, so the development of endometriosis services remains one of my key priorities for improvements in women’s health and my officials are expecting the submission of the relevant business cases before March 2026. However, there is insufficient funding to ensure that all of the capacity required for endometriosis services will be secured with the funding currently available and the expansion of this service will need to be phased as further funding is identified.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of current waiting times for the diagnosis of endometriosis within the health service.
answer_text: Endometriosis services are currently delivered as part of general gynaecology services in Northern Ireland, and therefore it is not possible to provide the current waiting times for the diagnosis of endometriosis.  The diagnosis of endometriosis cannot be confirmed until after the assessment has been undertaken.The diagnostic pathway includes symptom assessment, investigations to determine severity and progression, and planning next steps. Management options range from ongoing monitoring to hormonal therapies and/or surgical intervention, with consideration of fertility throughout.The Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) is working with the Gynaecology Collaborative to invest in all five Trusts, enhancing capacity for additional laparoscopy sessions and clinical appointments. Work is also underway to develop two regional Endometriosis Centres—in the Western Trust and Belfast Trust—which will provide complex surgical care for women who require it.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of access to GP and primary care support for children with severe learning disabilities and autism.
answer_text: Children with severe learning disabilities, who have co-occurring autism and acute behavioural / mental health needs are fully entitled to access GP support when required through the same pathway as for all children.  In addition, access to all other health and social care services that young people may be referred to is on the basis of eligibility criteria and professionally assessed need.With regard to access to respite care support – an assessment of need by the relevant Health and Social Care Trust would be completed using the Understanding the Needs of Children in Northern Ireland (UNOCINI) framework. The UNOCINI is the standardised assessment model that is used within children’s services to capture assessed need for children and families, this includes assessed need in terms of support required, and also for services such as respite support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of whether current paediatric and child psychology services have sufficient capacity to meet demand, particularly for children with complex needs.
answer_text: Collaborative approaches to paediatric service planning ensure that children and their families receive the best care possible.  The current paediatric psychology teams in the Trusts provide a specialist psychological service to children and young people where psychological issues relate to acute and chronic physical illness or traumatic injury.  Their integrated management means they work jointly with other professionals and agencies to provide support and consultation for a wide range of psychological issues.  In light of this, Transformation funding for the paediatric strategy identified funding for over £320,000 for paediatric psychology support for chronic disease to be split in an equitable manner between the Trusts. As well as these investments a psychologist is a key member of the Regional Paediatric Palliative Care Network ensuring that psychological support is considered for those children and young people with chronic and palliative conditions.  Across NI, paediatric psychological need is increasing, driven by rising medical complexity, higher prevalence of longterm conditions, increased emotional distress linked to health needs, growth in Looked After Children populations and demand for neurodevelopmental assessment and specialist intervention. SPPG reached out to Trusts and the following response was provided.BHSCTBHSCT hosts the regional Paediatric Psychology Service within the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children, supporting multiple regionally commissioned conditions (oncology, haematology, cystic fibrosis, cardiology, renal, epilepsy surgery, neurodisability, gastroenterology).NHSCT NHSCT provides psychology for neonates, epilepsy, diabetes, and complex health conditions, staffed by 2.3 WTE psychologists.SEHSCT SEHSCT’s Children’s Psychology Service provides community-based care only.SHSCTThe SHSCT Paediatric Psychology Service, established via Transformation funding in 2019, covers four chronic condition areas (diabetes, epilepsy, allergies, asthma).WHSCTWHSCT has 2.4 wte staff and reports the most extreme waits across children’s psychology services, reflecting longstanding undercapacity relative to need.Across all Trusts, the paediatric psychology system is characterised by a demand–capacity imbalance. The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the introduction of the prohibition of right-hand turn legislation, at the junction of Glenville Road and A2 Shore Road, Whiteabbey.
answer_text: I can advise that the legislation process is ongoing with advertisement expected later this year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of (i) the recent US dietary guidelines emphasising increased protein, red meat, and full-fat dairy; and (ii) what impact this guidance will have on current UK and Northern Ireland dietary advice.
answer_text: I am aware of the recent publication of the new US dietary guidelines. The Scientific Advisory Committee on Nutrition (SACN) provides independent scientific advice and risk assessments on nutrition and related health issues to the four UK health departments and other government departments and agencies. SACN has not conducted a formal review of the US dietary guidelines.In the UK, the Eatwell Guide provides the current government recommendations on healthy eating and achieving a balanced diet. The Food Standards Agency (FSA) leads on this policy area for Northern Ireland. The Eatwell Guide is underpinned by evidence-based advice from SACN. Based on the latest National Diet and Nutrition Survey, the Eatwell Guide recommendations are still relevant for the Northern Ireland population and there are no immediate plans to review the Eatwell Guide

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the waiting times for paediatric Ear, Nose and Throat (ENT) services in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust; (ii) any recent actions his Department and the Health and Social Care Trusts have taken to reduce waiting times, including specific measures targeted at children with recurrent ENT issues; and (iii) what additional support is available to families whilst children remain on paediatric ENT waiting lists.
answer_text: (i)Outpatient Waiting TimesWaiting times for patients less than 18 years old for a first consultant-led outpatient appointment under the Ear, Nose and Throat (ENT) specialty in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, at quarter ending September 2025, are in Table 1.Table 1. Numbers of, and waiting times for, patients less than 18 years old for first consultant-led outpatient Ear, Nose and Throat appointments at quarter end September 2025, in Northern HSC Trust.Time band (weeks)Numbers of patients waiting 1, 2, 30 to 6203>6 to 956>9 to 1286>12 to 15113>15 to 18117>18 to 26274>26 to 39433>39 to 52303>52 to 65173>65 to 78215>78 to 91234>91 to 104165>1045060 to >1042,878Source: Department of Health Hospital Waits Information Branch, Outpatient Waiting Times Dataset.Notes:Inpatient and Day Case Waiting TimesWaiting times for patients less than 18 years old for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the Ear, Nose and Throat (ENT) specialty in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust, at quarter ending September 2025, are in Table 2.Table 2. Numbers of, and waiting times for, patients less than 18 years old for admission for inpatient or day case treatment under the Ear, Nose and Throat specialty at quarter end September 2025, in Northern HSC Trust.Time band (weeks)Numbers of patients waiting 1, 2, 30 to 656>6 to 1346>13 to 2147>21 to 2625>26 to 52136>52 to 6585>65 to 7855>78 to 9159>91 to 10435>1044220 to >104966Source: Department of Health Hospital Waits Information Branch, Outpatient Waiting Times Dataset. (ii) The service has undertaken additional day-case lists at weekends with a focus on those paediatric patients waiting >4 years for tonsillectomy procedure.  By end March 2026, 102 paediatric patients will have had their procedure carried out.   	QR validation has been completed for paediatric patients who are on waiting lists for a tonsillectomy procedure >52 weeks, this was to determine the number of episodes of tonsillitis that each individual had in the previous 12 months, clinicians then reviewed this against NICE guidelines.  From this 38% of those who were waiting were discharged.Those paediatric patients who have had surgery for vent insertion are placed on a patient initiated follow up pathway which gives them direct access back into the service within a 12-month period following surgery, the patients have one nurse led review post operatively. (iii) GP’s can send in a further referral to have a patient’s priority reconsidered. A clinical review will take place to determine if patient remains on correct priority waiting list.Notes:1 Data sourced from the encompass system. The encompass specialty classification differs from, and is not directly comparable to, legacy (pre-encompass) data sources.2 The figures above refer to the number of patients waiting for a first consultantled outpatient appointment under the Ear, Nose and Throat (ENT) s

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will undertake a review of speed enforcement infrastructure, including fixed, mobile and average speed cameras, to determine whether the current provision is sufficient to address road safety priorities.
answer_text: The policy regarding any review of speed detection cameras is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I suggest that you contact the Partnership directly for this information.Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfast BT6 9LD

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will publish a more accessible and user friendly version of its grit box and salt bin policy, to help residents clearly understand the criteria used to determine why their area does or does not qualify for a grit box.
answer_text: Salt bins are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis on public roads which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule, providing the necessary criteria are met.Residents and proprietors can make an application to the Department to request the installation of a salt bin in their area however, provision of new salt boxes is not automatic and may be refused if the location in question is not on the publicly maintained road network; the gradient of the road in question is less than 5%; or if reasonable alternative routes are available.When considering requests for the provision of a salt bin, my officials carry out an evaluation using a criteria-based system, which takes into account various factors including topography, traffic volume, road gradient/geometry, alternative routes as well as other factors such as residential usage and community facilities including schools, hospitals and care homes for the elderly.This approach is documented in detail in my Department’s Winter Service Policy with further advice available on the NI Direct website.   There are no plans to publish an alternative version.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on his work to improve animal welfare, specifically in relation to puppy farming and Lucy’s Law.
answer_text: My Department does not hold information on all currently live initiatives across the Executive Departments. These strategies, programmes and action plans are the responsibility of the relevant individual Departments.Once it has been agreed by the Executive, it is intended that the Anti-Poverty Strategy, which my Department is leading on, will provide a framework for departments’ future work to address poverty across Northern Ireland.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department intends to increase the number of fixed speed-enforcement cameras, given the reliance on mobile enforcement and the low number of fixed sites, compared with other UK regions.
answer_text: The policy regarding the management of the cameras in speed detection cameras is the responsibility of the Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership, and I suggest that you contact the Partnership directly for this information.Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfast BT6 9LD

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 30874/22-27, to detail (i) any further preparatory work her Department has undertaken in relation to Graduated Driver Licencing (GDL), and (ii) when she anticipates GDL will be introduced in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: In my response to your earlier question in September 2025, I had indicated that I expected to be able to announce the anticipated date that GDL would be introduced shortly.  I subsequently announced on 27 January that GDL will be introduced on 1 October 2026. Work is ongoing to ensure operational readiness and that the subordinate legislation required, which will be subject to scrutiny by the Infrastructure Committee, is in place before the October commencement date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 34277/22-27, to detail (i) any work that has been undertaken by the Child Health Partnership in relation to Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID); (ii) when the multidisciplinary group was established; and (iii) any wider work carried out by his Department to date in addressing ARFID.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an an update on the introduction of secondary legislation to tackle pavement parking.
answer_text: Following my announcement to introduce a ban across the North on parking a vehicle wholly on the footway, parking across dropped kerbs and double parking, my officials are currently taking forward the necessary work to develop the secondary legislation to introduce these new measures. It is my aim to introduce these changes in the latter part of 2026, subject to the legislative process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive, in the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 38656/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of NHS dental practices that are accepting new NHS patients, in East Antrim.
answer_text: Dental practices manage their own workloads, so no information is held centrally in relation to whether practices are currently registering new health service patients, or how many they are able to register.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the availability of NHS dentistry places in East Antrim.
answer_text: Dental practices do not have a number of health service places allocated to them. Under the current contract, dental practitioners have the right to register or not to register a patient as a Health Service patient.The number of Health Service dental practices, including orthodontic, with a practice address in the East Antrim Assembly Area at 30 September 2025 (the most recent publicly available statistics) was 15.Dental practices manage their own workloads, so no information is held centrally in relation to whether practices are currently registering new health service patients, or how many they are able to register.The table below details the Health Service dental practices in the East Antrim Assembly Area at 30 September 2025: Surgery NameSurgery AddressABBEY DENTAL CLINIC620 SHORE ROAD, NEWTOWNABBEY, BT37 0STCLEAR DENTAL14 MARINE PARADE, WHITEHEAD, BT38 9QPCLEAR DENTAL11 VICTORIA ROAD, LARNE, BT40 1RTCLEMENTS DENTAL CARE22 WEST STREET, CARRICKFERGUS, BT38 7ARTHE GLENS DENTAL PRACTICE2 GORTACLEE ROAD, CUSHENDALL, BT44 0TEGREENISLAND DENTAL PRACTICE50 STATION ROAD, GREENISLAND, BT38 8URCLEAR DENTAL11 VICTORIA ROAD, LARNE, BT40 1RTLARNE DENTAL CENTRE9/11 GLENARM ROAD, LARNE, BT40 1BNMONKSTOWN DENTAL105 MONKSTOWN ROAD, MONKSTOWN, NEWTOWNABBEY, BT37 0LGMORRISON FAMILY DENTAL CARE11 THORNDALE AVENUE, THE RODDENS, LARNE, BT40 1QXMOYLE DENTAL CARE137 OLD GLENARM ROAD, LARNE, BT40 1NHRADIANCE ORAL HEALTHCARE19 ST BRIDES STREET, CARRICKFERGUS, BT38 8AFTHE DE COURCEY DENTAL PRACTICEUNIT 3 LANCASTRIAN STREET, CARRICKFERGUS, BT38 7ABVICTORIA DENTAL CARE51 VICTORIA ROAD, CARRICKFERGUS, BT38 7JJHARPER DENTAL CARE22 NORTH STREET, CARRICKFERGUS, BT38 7AQ(Source: Business Services Organisation, Information & Analysis Directorate, Department of Health, NI )As at 30 September 2025, these practices had, between them, 37,267 registered health service patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of (i) adults; and (ii) under-18's, who were registered for NHS dental care in East Antrim, in each of the last three years.
answer_text: The number of child registrations (aged under 18) and adult registrations (18+ years) for health service dentistry for those living in the East Antrim constituency area in each of the last three financial years is shown in the table below, along with the percentage of the population that is registered (registration rate).Table 1: Child, Adult, Total Registrations and Registration Rates for Health Service Dentistry in the East Antrim Assembly Area by Financial YearFinancial YearChild RegistrationsChild Registration Rate2Adult RegistrationsAdult Registration Rate2Total RegistrationsTotal Registration Rate22022-23113,79276.6%46,61965.1%60,41167.4%2023-24113,42574.6%44,17261.7%57,59764.3%2024-25112,22767.9%34,28547.9%46,51251.9%1 At 31st March of the financial year2 2022 NISRA mid-year population estimate for East Antrim Assembly Area used to calculate registration rate

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any support her Department provides to businesses at risk of flooding, in East Antrim.
answer_text: My Department continues to work with multi-agency partners, including Local Government, to provide support to local communities affected by flooding across the North through the work of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG). At present the group is working with 50 communities to help improve their preparedness and community resilience to severe weather including groups in Carrickfergus and Cushendall in the East Antrim constituency. Our preparations for a response to severe weather, including flooding, is an ongoing process throughout the year. The unpredictable nature of weather events means heavy rainfall can occur at any time and in preparation we operate an on-call Duty and Flooding Officer Rota providing support 24 hours a day, 365 days a year. To support the reporting of flooding incidents during periods of severe weather my Department has established the Flooding Incident Line, or FIL, which is a single telephone number to report a flood. This number, ?0300 2000 100, is available 24 hours a day, seven days a week and is the route that all emergency flood events should be reported. Reports are triaged and issued to the respective drainage authority for response.   	My Rivers Directorate will continue its’ inspection and maintenance regime in relation to designated watercourses and their associated infrastructure within the East Antrim area. If an inspection determines that there is a blockage significantly impeding the free flow of water within a watercourse or that drainage infrastructure is in an unsatisfactory condition, then works will be programmed.		Rivers Directorate is also progressing a number of feasibility studies for Areas of Potential Significant Flood Risk, as identified in the 2nd cycle of the Northern Ireland Flood Risk Management Plan, including for Carrickfergus, Kilroot, Larne and Newtownabbey. These studies will establish the extent of fluvial (river) and tidal flood risk and identify if there are any viable practical measures that could be developed to reduce these risks.		In recent years my Department has been working with the Department for Economy led cross-departmental group to explore options for a future framework to support flood recovery and resilience. Establishing a flood support framework would remove the need for bespoke schemes to be designed at pace in response to future flooding incidents, reducing the risks of ineffective use of limited public resources and providing more certainty to those impacted by flooding.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any support currently available to community and voluntary organisations, in East Antrim, that are at risk of flooding, including (a) emergency financial assistance; (b) resilience or preparedness support; and (c) funding for repairs, mitigation measures or flood prevention works; and (ii) whether the Department has plans to introduce additional support for community facilities affected by flooding.
answer_text: Emergency Financial Assistance - A Scheme of Emergency Financial Assistance (SEFA) was introduced by the Executive in 2007.  It provides immediate financial support with grants of £1k to domestic properties impacted by flooding. The SEFA does not provide support to businesses, sports clubs or other organisations, including community and voluntary organisations.Resilience/Preparedness support – My Department provides funding of c£900k to the Regional Resilience Team who play a key role working across central and local government on emergency planning and response. This team is part of the Regional Community Resilience Group (RCRG) formed in 2013 to help communities prepare for and respond to weather related emergencies. It brings together multi-agency partners organisations from government, utilities, and the voluntary sector to work for and with Communities at Risk of Severe Weather. Repair & Mitigation funding - In November 2023, following severe flooding mostly in Newcastle and Downpatrick, the Secretary of State made funds available to assist businesses in their recovery.  This was delivered by councils on behalf of Department for Economy (DfE).  Eligible businesses received a payment of £7,500 and received 100% rates relief until March 2024 along with temporary accommodation. Further funding was announced in December 2023 to assist small and medium sized businesses.  The support was linked to costs incurred by businesses in relation to replacement of damaged or destroyed equipment, refitting of flooded property and repair of building damage. Up to £100,000 per business was available.  A number of eligibility criteria were set for businesses to qualify for this support.  This once again was administered by local councils on behalf of DfE.Plans for Additional Support -  The Department’s policy consultation on the draft Voluntary & Community Sector Infrastructure Support Framework, issued in November 2023, refers to the scope for infrastructure support to enable “Sector-led partnerships that support community resilience and the NI Civil Contingencies Framework”.  This reference sits within a broad range of potential provision under the ‘Partnership and Collaboration’ outcome and there is no funding currently to support this specific area of work
