question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the adequacy of aftercare provision for patients with a tracheostomy.
answer_text: According to the Intensive Care Society Tracheostomy Care Guide, last reviewed in August 2023, the patient population in NHS hospitals that are managed with a tracheostomy has evolved significantly over the last 20 years. Although most tracheostomies are temporary, critical care is often part of the journey for the vast majority of these patients. The Health and Social Care Clinical Education Centre provide regular training for any Registered Nurse or Allied Health Professional working with a patient with a Tracheostomy.  This programme aims to improve the knowledge and skills of practitioners in person centred care and management of tracheostomies. The theory is supported by supervised practice to consolidate learning and application to practice.One learning outcome of the programme relates to the National Tracheostomy Safety Project guidelines for the management of an adult with a tracheostomy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 38018/22-27, given the lengthy timescale of the Carrickfergus City Deal process, to detail any consideration he has given to introducing interim measures with the Council, to sensitively illuminate Carrickfergus Castle, to ensure this important heritage asset is showcased while the project progresses.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has for (i) upgrading traffic lights; and (ii) wider road safety measures; on the A2 Belfast Road, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: Details of the road improvement schemes completed in 2024/25 and planned for the 2025/26 financial year are included in my Department’s Annual Report to Mid & East Antrim Borough Council.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when he will receive the Autism Reviewers’ annual report; and (ii) when this will be presented to the Assembly.
answer_text: It is the intention for the first annual report of the Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland to be published in April 2026. This first report will detail the work of the Reviewer from the date of appointment in September 2024.My Department will lay the report before the Assembly as soon as practicable upon its receipt.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) why the Housing Executive's improvement scheme in Whitehead has faced delays, and (ii) any action being taken to address the ongoing issues associated with Greenview’s contractual performance, to ensure residents receive the works and support within an acceptable timeframe.
answer_text: This is an operational matter for the Housing Executive.	I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘(i) The Northern Ireland Housing Executive’s Bathroom/Kitchen/Rewire (BKR) Scheme in Whitehead has experienced delays primarily due to capacity challenges faced by the appointed contractor. To date the contractor has not been able to secure the personnel or a subcontractor to complete this scheme. While works commenced on 25 August 2025, progress on-site has been limited, with only two dwellings completed to date.(ii) The Housing Executive remains committed to ensuring residents receive the works and support within an acceptable timeframe and will continue to take all necessary actions to achieve this. From the beginning of the contract, the Housing Executive has been actively engaging with Greenview to address these issues through regular performance meetings, however, given the lack of progress on the scheme to date, the matter has been escalated to senior management level within the NIHE who are currently determining next steps within the contractual performance framework.’

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, due to the importance of Carrickfergus Castle as a major tourist attraction, to detail whether he will explore opportunities to sensitively illuminate the castle, to further highlight this vital heritage asset.
answer_text: Opportunities to illuminate Carrickfergus Castle are being considered by Mid and East Antrim Borough Council as part of the Carrickfergus City Deal Project. The Royal Institute of British Architects (RIBA) Stage 2 proposals and the project lighting strategy envisage illuminating the Castle and Town Walls with a Walk of Light. Detail will be finalised as the project progresses through RIBA stages 3 & 4.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any assessment that has been carried out by her Department, regarding the subsidence on the Belfast Road, Carrickfergus; (ii) any measures in place, to ensure the safety of motorists and pedestrians; and (iii) the expected timescale for repair and reopening of the affected lane.
answer_text: My Department has inspected the revetment along the Belfast Road, Carrickfergus regularly since damage was caused following Storm Bram on Tuesday 9 December 2025. I can confirm that repair work commenced on Monday 12 January 2026.  Unfortunately, due to the tidal nature of the work it is difficult to provide a detailed timescale for its completion.  However, it is hoped that the work will be concluded by the end of January 2026.To ensure safety of the travelling public and construction workers, a footway and lane closure will be operational during the works.  When work is paused, the footway closure will remain in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of the ADHD needs assessment conducted by his Department; and (ii) to detail any actions that will be taken to address the identified needs.
answer_text: The ADHD needs assessment was commissioned in February 2025 to identify levels of demand to inform decisions on a future commissioned ADHD service, subject to budget availability. The assessment has considered future demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The issue of shared care arrangements has also been considered as part of the work, albeit it is recognised that this issue is wider than ADHD. The needs assessment was received by the Department on 30 November 2025. I and my officials are now considering next steps and potential options for such a service, subject to funding becoming available. It is anticipated that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of the condition and effectiveness of the sea defences in the Whitehead area, in particular the section adjacent to the Glasgow Rangers Supporters Club; and (ii) to outline any actions her Department will take to improve coastal protection for nearby homes and businesses.
answer_text: Ownership of the sea defences, in the location you refer to, are registered to Mid & East Antrim Borough Council.  Therefore, my Department does not inspect the condition of this structure, nor has any plans to improve coastal protection in this area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of people who have completed the new autism and neurodiversity awareness training for health and social care staff, from its launch, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: As of 1st January 2026, the number of people who have completed the new autism and neurodiversity awareness training for health and social care staff is as follows:Number of HSC staff completions of Neurodiversity Awareness training since launch, broken down by HSC TrustHSC TrustHSC staff who have completed all 3 modulesBHSCT40NHSCT117SEHSCT76SHSCT35WHSCT95Total365 Full completion of this training takes almost 3 hours and is designed to be completed in a modular approach to allow staff the opportunity to complete it over a period of time. The training meets CPD requirements with a certificate of completion only available after all modules have been completed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when he expects to conclude his consideration of the recommendations arising from the regional ADHD needs assessment; (ii) whether those recommendations will be published; and (iii) any actions, including in relation to shared care arrangements, he intends to take to address unmet need and regional inconsistency in ADHD services.
answer_text: The ADHD needs assessment was commissioned in February 2025 to identify levels of demand to inform decisions on a future commissioned ADHD service, subject to budget availability. The assessment has considered future demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. The issue of shared care arrangements has also been considered as part of the work, albeit it is recognised that this issue is wider than ADHD. The needs assessment was received by the Department on 30 November 2025. I and my officials are now considering next steps and potential options for such a service, subject to funding becoming available. It is anticipated that the report will be published in early 2026.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, in light of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls: Experiences and attitudes of adults in Northern Ireland in 2024 report, which showed only 27 per cent of women feel safe in parks or open spaces after dark compared with 68 per cent of men, to outline the steps their Department plans to take to make public spaces safe for women and address this disparity.
answer_text: The focus of the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls Strategic Framework is on prevention, stopping the violence before it starts.  We are working with partners across sectors to align efforts and support initiatives which aim to ensure women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhere.Key to this work is our commitment to community investment.Through the Change Fund, we are raising awareness and equipping everyone to take action. A powerful example comes from Youth Action, where young people mapped safety experiences in their communities. Momentum funding is supporting Councils to embed actions in their service delivery, developing signposting and resources for communities, specialised staff training and winter safety events for running.  Last year we partnered with Queen’s University Belfast on research into violence against women and girls in public spaces. Their report, Safer Streets, Shared Voices will inform our work going forward.  We are also working together with partners including PSNI, Translink and Hospitality Ulster through the Safer Socialising Steering Group to improve safety outcomes for women and girls. We will continue to do everything we can to ensure that women and girls are safe and feel safe everywhere.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for her assessment of her Department's handling of the recent snow and icy conditions; and (ii) to detail any steps she will take to ensure her Department is better prepared for its winter service in the future.
answer_text: Prior to the start of each winter service season, my Department carries out a significant amount of pre-planning to ensure a state of readiness. Staffing arrangements are finalised, pre-season checks on winter service equipment are complete and our salt barns are filled to capacity.My Department targets the limited resources available on the busier main through routes, as per the current winter service policy. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted, and provides action where it is most needed and most-effective.In addition to gritting this 28% Primary Salting Network, salt bins and grit piles are provided for use by the public, on a self-help basis, on other routes adopted or maintained by my Department, but which do not qualify for inclusion on the gritting schedule. Where possible, once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is secured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion. The full policy can be viewed on the Department’s website below:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022	It must, however, be emphasised that the service provided cannot eliminate the risks – even on roads that are gritted, ice free conditions cannot be guaranteed, and I would urge motorists to drive with caution, take the ever-changing weather conditions into account and leave extra time for their journey. During this recent cold spell, staff across all our roads divisions have been working tirelessly day and night in some very difficult conditions, to keep traffic moving safely. We owe a huge debt of gratitude to our gritter drivers and snow blowers, who are often the first traffic on many roads, leaving their own families behind to ensure we can travel safely. Their hard work and persistence ensured tangible results for our road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current grit stock levels; (ii) any contingency arrangements in place, following the depletion of grit supplies; (iii) how routes for gritting are being prioritised; and (iv) when grit supplies are expected to be replenished.
answer_text: As the delivery of Winter Service is ongoing, the grit levels are constantly fluctuating with use and replenishment, so it would not be possible to give an accurate level of current stocks held.I can confirm that our salt reserves were filled to capacity over the summer months in preparation for the beginning of Winter Service, with additional re-orders available throughout the winter as required. Re-orders are set to 90% of full capacity to ensure stock levels are maintained.The roads selected for salting and the schedule for gritted routes on the network have been generated through the application of a specifically designed set of criteria to determine if a section of public road should be included.This Primary Salting Network is made up of higher-trafficked main through-routes, such as Motorways and Trunk Roads, main routes carrying more than 1,500 vehicles per day, and busy routes carrying 1,000 -1,500 vehicles per day with special difficulties such as railway crossings, and excessive gradients. The application of this policy means that 28% of the total road network, which carries around 80% of traffic, is salted.Once the condition of the Primary Salting Network is assured, secondary salting may be undertaken on other, more rural roads, particularly during prolonged periods of hazardous weather. These decisions are taken by experienced engineers who review all available data and Met office expert opinion.The full Winter Service Policy is available to view on my Department’s website at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/winter-service-rsppg-e022.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 36662/22-27, to detail (i) whether a submission has been made to the Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group; (ii) the expected timeframe for a decision; and (iii) any actions his Department can take, in conjunction with the Housing Executive, to increase the provision of supported living for those with learning difficulties, in the East Antrim area.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘(i) whether a submission has been made to the Housing Executive’s Development Programme Group; (ii) the expected timeframe for a decisionThe Housing Executives Development Programme Group has yet to receive an Application for Project Approval from Choice Housing in respect of the proposed 6-unit scheme for the extension of the Supported Housing facility at Kilcreggan, Carrickfergus. A timeframe for a decision cannot be confirmed until an Application for Project Approval submission has been made. (iii) any actions his Department can take, in conjunction with the Housing Executive, to increase the provision of supported living for those with learning difficulties, in the East Antrim area.           The Supporting People (SP) Programme, which is administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, provides grant funding towards the provision of housing support services, to enable vulnerable people to live independently in the community. The Programme is focused on four thematic areas; working with people who are experiencing homelessness, young people, older people and people with a disability (including mental health and learning disabilities).We are currently undertaking a Public Consultation (open until Friday 23 January 2026) to seek feedback on the Supporting People Strategic Intent 2026–2031. The aim of this consultation is to gather views that will help shape the direction of the programme over the next five years and inform our operational plans for delivering the Strategic Intent and its associated priorities.’	This year, I allocated an additional £1 million for pipeline schemes in 2025/26 to fund new and extended Supporting People services.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 34781/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether NI Water will release the wastewater analysis study, carried out by Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute and Imperial College London, to help give an understanding of the prevalence of drug use in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The report referred to was commissioned by the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute (AFBI) in collaboration with the Imperial College London; therefore, I would direct the member to contact these organisations to request access to the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, given the number of men presenting as victims of domestic abuse, to detail any plans his Department has to establish a dedicated refuge, or safe accommodation provision, for men.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Proposals for services grant funded through the Supporting People programme are required to come through the associated Supporting People approval processes. No proposals specifically for male victims of domestic violence have been submitted to Supporting People for consideration.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) any actions his Department will take to strengthen sea-defence infrastructure in the area around Sloefield Road and Belfast Road, Carrickfergus, including any plans for coastal walls, improved drainage, or long-term flood-mitigation works; and (ii) any assessments carried out to determine the most effective protections for this part of the coastline.
answer_text: My Department has no immediate plans to strengthen the sea defences along the A2 Belfast Road, Carrickfergus.  However, officials have commissioned a feasibility study to assess the fluvial and coastal flood risk within the Carrickfergus Area of Potential Significant Flood Risk, to investigate if there are any viable options to help reduce the impacts of flooding from rivers and the sea.  Work on the feasibility study is ongoing and is expected to be complete late in 2026.I would also highlight that a section of sea wall further along the coast within Rhanbuoy Park, Carrickfergus suffered a partial collapse.  My Department has carried out temporary repairs, thereby reducing the risk of flooding in the interim until this section of sea wall is rebuilt.  In parallel with the rebuilding of this wall, officials will commission an assessment to ensure it is structurally sound.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 36435/22-27, to detail (i) the timeframe for approval of child restraints and car seats by the Education Authority; and (ii) whether this is arranged in advance of a child being allocated a private transfer.
answer_text: The criteria on the use of car seats in private operator vehicles delivering school transport are set out in the operator’s contract with the Education Authority (EA) and no separate approval is required.Before a pupil’s private transport service is allocated, individual risk assessments and/or wheelchair assessments are carried out and any restraint systems required are organised. Sometimes safety concerns only become apparent when transport arrangements commence, for example, if a child repeatedly takes off their seatbelt then additional equipment may be required to keep them safe on their journey to school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, given that only four Stalking Prevention Orders have been issued since October 2023, to detail any consideration she has given to addressing the challenges associated with these orders, to improve the process for the victims.
answer_text: The Protection from Stalking Act is a key piece of legislation that I brought forward in 2022 to protect victims of stalking.  The Act included provision for Stalking Protection Orders, which became operational by police in October 2023. My officials and other groups, including PSNI, met regularly to work towards the development and implementation of Stalking Protection Orders (SPOs), which were conceived as a tool to disrupt stalking behaviours at an early stage before they escalated and to offer protection to victims right from the start of an investigation.PSNI has responsibility for the application and operation of these orders, and for the training of its officers. Whilst these are operational issues and therefore independent of my Department, I am very happy to receive details from the PSNI if they are having any problems with the legislation.You may wish to know that my Department is currently working on stalking provisions contained in the Westminster Crime and Policing Bill to ensure they are extended to Northern Ireland. The provisions include giving courts the power to impose SPOs directly at the end of criminal proceedings on conviction or acquittal, thereby widening the opportunity for their application which should see an increase in the number of SPOs granted.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail whether, under current Education Authority policy, parents are legally required to apply to the school closest to their home, in any area.
answer_text: There is no legal requirement for parents to apply to the school closest to their home.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on the July 2023 business case for the Choice Housing Kilcreggan project in Carrickfergus.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:‘The business case was revised following engagement with Kilcreggan in June 2025. While the preferred option in the business case is revenue neutral from a SP perspective, capital funding will be required in the form of Housing Association Grant. This will require a formal submission to the NIHE’s Development Programme Group by Choice Housing Association and will be subject to funding.’ Please note SP refers to the Supporting People Programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any support, or funding, available through his Department to assist families in creating a sensory room for a child, or adult, with complex sensory needs; and (ii) any steps being taken to factor in (a) the waiting times for occupational therapy assessments required to access the Disabled Facilities Grant; and (b) the threshold currently applied for such adaptations to be deemed necessary and appropriate for grant approval.
answer_text: (i) My Department does not provide specific funding or support in relation to the creation of sensory rooms for children or adults with complex sensory needs. If a clinical need for such a space is included in recommendations from an Occupational Therapist for an adaptation, funding may be available. For privately owned properties, the Housing Executive administer Disabled Facilities grant-aid on behalf of my Department. Funding for adaptations of Housing Association properties is provided via the Social Housing Development Programme. The Housing Executive is responsible for adaptations for its own properties. I have provided £2m following the December Monitoring Round for adaptations for NIHE properties for tenants living with a disability which will allow for delivery of priority improvements works. (ii) (a) Waiting times for Occupational Therapy assessments are the responsibility of the Department of Health (DoH) and the Health and Social Care Trusts.  My Department is leading a review of NIHE Administered Private Sector Grants, with the Department for Health and the Housing Executive. The outcomes of the review are to make grant funding quicker to access, create a more user-friendly customer journey and ensure that lived experience informs service improvements.(b) For Disabled Facilities Grants, administered by the Housing Executive,  I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Health and Social Care Trust Occupational Therapists assess the clinical need of a disabled person to decide what works to their property are necessary and appropriate, in line with agreed best practice guidance for Northern Ireland. The Housing Executive’s Private Sector Grants technical team decides if those works are reasonable and practical in Disabled Facilities Grants cases.”

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the current status of the (i) PSNI pay award; and (ii) PSNI holiday pay redress, including (a) the funding source; (b) the timeline for payment; and (c) details of any engagement with the Department of Finance and HM Treasury to ensure the expected delivery at the end of the year.
answer_text: Police PayI am committed to implementing the Police Remuneration Review Body recommendations in respect of the 2025 Police Pay award as soon as possible. However, public sector pay awards must meet the affordability test before they can be realised.I have been consistent in advocating for police officer pay to be placed on an equal footing with other public sector workers. As a result, the Executive recently agreed that my Department will have first call in the December Monitoring Round of up to £6.7 million for police pay.  I will continue to work with the Chief Constable to explore options to allow PSNI to demonstrate affordability for the pay review body recommendations.The successful implementation of the police officer pay award remains a priority for me, as Minister of Justice and, I am confident the award can be realised by the end of this year.  Holiday PayThese costs are truly inescapable and are not affordable from within my Department’s budget under any circumstance without additional funding from the Executive. The costs arising from the holiday pay ruling are a matter for the Executive to take decisions on, rather than the Treasury, and my officials continue to engage with their Department of Finance counterparts to identify and secure the necessary future funding that will enable us to bring these matters to a close.My Department has bid for additional funding to meet these costs, as part of the Budget 2026-29 information gathering process, as these exceptional pressures are not affordable within my Department’s baseline. These bids are being considered by the Finance Minister and it is my understanding that the Executive will be making decisions on draft Budget allocations in the coming weeks. I should hope to see these cases brought to a conclusion without undue delay and in this context, I welcome the fact that there are dates scheduled for next year with the Industrial Tribunals in relation to holiday pay case hearings which should provide time to progress this issue.Engagement is ongoing between officials from my Department and the Department of Finance in relation to progressing the relevant business cases.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any current guidance from his Department regarding the use of appropriate child restraints, or car seats by private providers of home-to-school transport, for those with special educational needs or disabilities; and (ii) any steps being taken to ensure the safety of children using such transport.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the operation and delivery of school transport and issues comprehensive guidance to all private operators providing home to school transport which include a Private Operator Driver’s Instruction Manual and a Private Operator Escort’s Instruction Manual.To ensure the safety of pupils with special educational needs or disabilities these manuals provide detailed instruction on the use of child restraints and car seats, advising drivers not to use any additional equipment unless it is approved by the EA and that where a special seat is required for a pupil, it must be fitted securely and used for all journeys, with no exceptions.The manuals also include direction on transporting pupils in wheelchairs, the appropriate use of seat belts as well as advice on handling passengers who repeatedly remove their seatbelts or shoulder straps.To ensure the safety of children the EA employs Locality Transport Inspectors who conduct risk assessments for pupils requiring wheelchairs or additional safety measures. These inspections cover all EA home-to-school transport services, including those delivered by private hire providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department collects data on the number of individuals previously in receipt of Disability Living Allowance who, following reassessment for Personal Independence Payment (PIP), (i) have been awarded zero points; or (ii) have not met the eligibility threshold for the standard rate of PIP.
answer_text: I can confirm that my Department collects the data specified. From September 2020 to 31 August 2025, the number of individuals previously in receipt of Disability Living Allowance who, following reassessment for Personal Independence Payment (PIP), were awarded zero points was 1,660. Similarly, for the same period the number of individuals previously in receipt of Disability Living Allowance who, following reassessment for PIP, did not meet the eligibility threshold for the standard rate of either the Daily Living or Mobility component of PIP was 890.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the July 2023 business case for the Choice Housing Kilcreggan project in Carrickfergus, given the significant need for accommodation for adults with complex learning disabilities.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that a crossing island was installed and subsequently removed outside Ballycarry Primary School, Carrickfergus, to detail what further measures her Department will consider to improve road safety in the area and to ensure that children can cross safely at the school.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.Ballycarry Primary School has enhanced safer routes to school signage in place and is currently within a 30mph speed limit zone.  My Department met with school representatives in 2022 and agreed to carry out improvement works to the junction of the Island and Hillhead Roads.  Works commenced in October 2023, and it became evident before the installation of the traffic island was completed, that large vehicles such as lorries and buses could not navigate the junction without mounting the island.  As the topography of the road would not facilitate the repositioning of the island, it was removed for safety reasons.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North which will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. My officials are carrying out a review on a number of speed related matters including options for permanent 20mph zones in residential areas and near schools and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise in the coming weeks as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: The Dilapidation Bill is currently in Committee Stage, which has now been extended to 15 May 2026 to allow the AERA Committee adequate time to scrutinise the Bill.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of postgraduate medical students at Queens University Belfast (QUB) in their final year of study being excluded from his Department's bursary funding, which is offered to undergraduate students at QUB and postgraduate students at Ulster University.
answer_text: My Department currently provides a bursary package for eligible medical students from year five of study onwards, including non-repayable tuition fee support and a means-tested bursary. This complements the standard student finance support available in earlier years. Postgraduate-entry students have traditionally been expected to self-fund tuition fees, with limited support available through Student Finance NI. This approach evolved in order to target the limited funds available at primary degree level, to ensure that as many individuals as possible could undertake a university degree.I recognise that recent announcements introducing tuition fee loans for postgraduate medical students entering Ulster University’s Graduate Entry Medicine programme from 2025/26 have raised questions about parity. These loans were introduced as part of a wider Executive commitment to expand medical training capacity and address workforce needs and reflect the current policy position that second degrees do not generally qualify for tuition fee loan support. This position was confirmed by the Minister for the Economy in response to an Assembly Question in October 2024, which also noted that support for second degrees more broadly will be considered as part of a review of higher education funding. My Department is not responsible for the funding underpinning this expanded arrangement, nor for the policy decisions behind it. My officials are conducting an ongoing review of the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) whether the Treasury will meet the costs arising from the holiday pay ruling; (ii) any assessment her Department has been made of the risks posed by ongoing delays in the redress process, and (iii) any steps her Department is taking, in conjunction with the Department of Finance, to ensure that affected staff receive timely and fully funded redress.
answer_text: The costs arising from the holiday pay ruling are a matter for the Executive to take decisions on rather than the Treasury.My Department has bid for funds to meet these costs, as part of the Budget 2026-29 information gathering process, as these exceptional pressures are not affordable within my Department’s baseline.These bids are being considered by the Finance Minister and it is my understanding that the Executive will be making decisions on draft Budget allocations in the coming weeks.  As this is a cross-Executive issue there are a number of business cases completed in this space which have sought, amongst other things, to appraise the risks involved in these cases, including on delay.  I should hope to see these cases brought to a conclusion without undue delay and in this context, I welcome the fact that there are dates scheduled for next year with the Industrial Tribunals in relation to holiday pay case hearings which should provide time to progress this issue.Engagement is ongoing between officials from my Department and the Department of Finance in relation to progressing the relevant business cases, and the budget setting process for multi-year budgets. Furthermore, Northern Ireland Prison Service and Department of Finance have been proactive in seeking to engage with staff representatives on the retrospective settlement and have made an offer to settle cases.   Discussions are ongoing with solicitors acting on behalf of trade unions representing staff.  The value of any settlement has to be balanced against the constraints of the available budget that covers all public services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any financial support available from (a) his Department; and (b) its arm’s-length bodies, to support the development and growth of women’s football; and (ii) any plans in place to increase investment and opportunities for female (a) players; (b) coaches; and (c) clubs.
answer_text: My Department does not have any programmes specifically aimed at the development and growth of women’s football.The Chief Executive of Sport NI, an Arms Length Body of my Department, has advised me that it has a number of programmes which have supported and continue to support women’s football and increase opportunities for female players, coaches and clubs.  Due to the volume of information involved, I have arranged for this to be placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why postgraduate medical students at Queen’s University, Belfast are not eligible for his Department's bursary funding in their final year; and (ii) any steps planned to ensure they have equitable access to financial support comparable to undergraduate medical students and postgraduate students at other Northern Ireland universities.
answer_text: The Education (Student Support) (No.2) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2009 preclude students who already hold a primary degree awarded by an institution in Northern Ireland or Great Britain, from obtaining fee loans or maintenance grants for a second primary degree. This approach is based on the principle that the limited financial resources available should be targeted at primary degree level, thereby allowing a greater number of students to experience third level study. My officials are conducting an ongoing review of the bursary funding arrangements for pre-registration health and social care students. The review is intended to inform options for a public consultation. I am unable to provide any further detail until the review is finalised and options developed for consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) why routine diabetes reviews have reduced from twice yearly in childhood to less than once annually for adults; and (ii) any actions he is taking to restore timely access to specialist care.
answer_text: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines, states timeframes for routine diabetes reviews. For adults, it is recommended that a jointly agreed individual care plan with the adult with type 1 diabetes should be reviewed annually and amended as needed, considering changes in the person's wishes, circumstances and medical findings. Children and young people living with type 1 diabetes should be encouraged to attend clinic 4 times a year, with regular contact with the diabetes team to help them maintain optimal blood glucose levels.Overall, service delivery is provided in line with this guidance, which was last updated in 2015 and presents a different recommended appointment frequency for adults and children. It is recognised that there are service pressures and workforce issues can impact timeframe on occasions, but HSCTs work hard to minimise this impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps he is taking to ensure that all people with type 1 diabetes, receive the annual comprehensive review, required under National Institute for Health and Care Excellence guidelines.
answer_text: The NI Diabetes Network was established in 2016 to support the implementation of the Department of Health’s (DoH) Diabetes Strategic Framework. The work of the NI Diabetes Network is taken forward through workstream subgroups which have clinical, service user and Diabetes UK involvement. Many of these actions have been completed, however, as with any ambitious programme of work, there are still some actions to be fully implemented. Many of these are resource dependant and impacted by other factors such as workforce, technology and finance.  The NI Diabetes Network are working with Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland colleagues and Diabetes UK to review and consider the information that can be collated from across the Health and Social Care system to support participation in the National Diabetes Audit.  This audit will document the number of people living with type 1 diabetes who receive the annual comprehensive review and identify areas of further service development required.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 34164/22-27, to detail how her Department is ensuring effective cross-engagement between the teams responsible for carrying out the ongoing reviews and both Translink and Community Transport providers, given the alignment and potential operational crossover between the two sectors.
answer_text: The review of Ulsterbus services is being led by Translink, while the review into Community Transport services is being led by my Department. These are two separate reviews considering different aspects of public transport provision. Nevertheless, any emerging findings of the Ulsterbus review will be considered and pertinent points captured within the Community Transport review.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of hospitals that have Changing Places facilities; and (ii) whether he has any plans to provide additional Changing Places facilities in hospitals.
answer_text: Below are the details of the number and location of Changing Places facilities provided by each Health and Social Care Trust.    Northern Health and Social Care Trust  The NHSCT has two Changing Places facilities with one located at Antrim Hospital and one within the Ballymena Health & Care Centre.     South Eastern Health and Care Trust  The SEHSCT has one Changing Places facility within the Lisburn Primary and Community Care Centre located on the Lagan Valley Hospital Site.      Southern Health and Social Care Trust  The SHSCT has two fully compliant Changing Places facilities with one located at the Banbridge CTCC site and one located at the Blossom Paediatrics building at Craigavon Area Hospital.     Belfast Health and Social Care Trust  The BHSCT does not yet have a Changing Place facility in any of its acute hospital sites.     Western Health and Social Care Trust   No response received.  In light of the severe financial challenges facing the Northern Ireland Health & Social Care (HSC) sector, as well as the competing demands on the capital budget, the department has no plans to centrally fund the provision of Changing Places facilities in any existing hospitals or health centres where currently not provided. HSC Trusts are however provided with general capital funding monies and may consider the provision of these facilities from this allocation based on their own assessment of need and options available.  Since June 2022 amendments to building regulations require that all new build hospitals and primary care buildings and those whole hospital or primary care buildings formed by a material change of use of existing facilities should provide at least one changing places facility.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the installation of a sensory room at the Royal Victoria Hospital, following the Adjournment Debate on this in the Assembly on 4 November 2025.
answer_text: I have sought an update from the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust to obtain an update on the installation of a sensory room following the Adjournment Debate that took place on 4 November 2025.  The Trust have advised as follows:“Improving facilities for our patients with sensory processing challenges has been the topic of recent ongoing discussions in the Royal Belfast Hospital for Sick Children (RBHSC).  As access to suitable, adaptable space within the building is particularly challenging, it was agreed that the most suitable option available was for the Psychology Service to try to progress a charitable funds application for the purchase of a Cubbie. It is anticipated that there will be agreement to progress with this purchase in December 2025. The Cubbie is an innovative, multisensory moveable hub (the equivalent of a small sensory room) designed to support individuals of all ages in managing anxiety, reducing sensory overwhelm, and increasing engagement.  It delivers a tailor-made experience of sound, light and vision, matching individualised sensory needs. It has been proven to reduce distress and helps individuals regulate their sensory and emotional state - before, during, or after appointments - enabling them to engage more comfortably with medical staff, treatment, and care.  The Cubbie has a daily capacity of between 20-30 users.  It has already been installed in many other Children’s hospitals (including Temple Street, Dublin), helping healthcare professionals create calmer, more inclusive spaces for all patients.  The service recognises that the current Bhive location is not appropriate for children and young people attending ED.  The Cubbie will therefore be positioned in an accessible area for patients attending both ED and the outpatients department, most likely the mall. It is also fully wheelchair accessible and offers different seating options (swing/soft sofa cushioning).  The team feel that the Cubbie will be a beneficial addition to RBHSC and its ability to be individualised will offer more than generic sensory rooms. As a Trust we are looking at key environments in which to review how to support the needs of children, young people and families with neuro-diverse needs. We will be reviewing the overall RBHSC ED environment, involving Autistic service users and staff alongside the Trust Neurodiversity Consultant Psychologist Lead. This will also include updating staff training to best support the needs of children, young people and families in this area. We will be happy to share outcomes of these initiatives over the next months.” I welcome the update received from the Trust and would encourage them to expedite the delivery and installation of the Cubbie to allow mobilisation as soon as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail whether his Department plans to introduce legislation to ban no-fault evictions, similar to the Renters’ Rights Act, which abolishes Section 21 evictions from 1 May 2026.
answer_text: I currently have no plans to introduce legislation to ban no-fault evictions. I am taking forward work to introduce much longer notice to quit periods, as mandated by the Assembly in the Private Tenancies Act (NI) 2022. This change will greatly improve security of tenure for individuals and families in the private rented sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any steps his Department is taking to improve (i) regulation; and (ii) oversight, of letting agents in Northern Ireland, in light of the stronger regulatory framework operating in England.
answer_text: I have decided not to progress letting agent regulation and oversight in this mandate so I can focus resources on those areas of reform which, I believe, will have a greater impact in tackling the complex challenges of the private rented sector. This includes introducing minimum standards for fire and electrical safety, increasing notice to quit periods and enhancing the Landlord Registration Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department intends to bring forward legislation to strengthen protections for private renters, including measures relating to (i) eviction grounds; (ii) rent increases; and (iii) property standards.
answer_text: My programme of PRS reform addresses the key issues of security, affordability, and standards and is covered by the provisions of the Private Tenancies Act (Northern Ireland) 2022. This includes:Regulations to introduce much longer notice to quit periods, as mandated by the Assembly;Commencing legislation to restrict the frequency of rent increases to once in any 12 months, with a three-month written notice given; andRegulations on fire and electrical safety and amending the Landlord Registration Scheme to link it more explicitly to standards. Officials are also exploring Minimum Energy Efficiency Standards for the Private Rented Sector.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any assessment his Department has made of the differences in renter protections between Northern Ireland and England, following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act.
answer_text: My Department has not undertaken any formal assessment of the differences in renter protections between Northern Ireland and England following the introduction of the Renters’ Rights Act.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the correspondence sent to the world angling governing body, Confédération Internationale de la Pêche Sportive, regarding the recognition of Northern Ireland as a competing entity in international angling competitions.
answer_text: I last wrote to the Confédération Internationale de la Pêche Sportive in September 2024 expressing my support for the recognition of Northern Ireland as a competing entity in international angling competitions.  I have not yet received a formal response andit is disappointing that no progress appears to have been made concerning the recognition of anglers from Northern Ireland having the right to compete for their country in international competition.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of concerns regarding the safety of women and girls, and the lack of lighting in land owned by his Department at the Harbour in Carrickfergus, to detail any plans his Department has to install lighting to improve visibility and safety in this area.
answer_text: My Department is working closely with Mid and East Antrim Borough Council (MEABC) through the Carrickfergus City Deal project, which is considering this area as part of a wider £42 million regeneration initiative. This project aims to enhance the public realm and visitor experience across the town and includes a proposal to install lighting along this area of the harbour.Since becoming aware of concerns regarding the lack of lighting in this area, my officials have been in discussions with MEABC to consider if the planned lighting in this area could be installed sooner than the current City Deal programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any (i) steps being taken to address the variation in waiting times for children awaiting autism assessments across Health and Social Care Trusts; and (ii) actions being implemented to reduce the backlog within the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Department of Health (NI) has a formal link with NICE under which NICE Technology Appraisals are reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability in Northern Ireland. Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within HSC organisations. In practice this means that treatments that have been recommended by NICE for routine use in the NHS in England are also routinely available in Northern Ireland.		In the guidance published by NICE on 6th August 2025 (TA1086), Ribociclib (Kisqali®) was recommended with an aromatase inhibitor as an option for the adjuvant treatment of hormone receptor-positive, HER2-negative, early breast cancer at high risk of recurrence. This guidance has been formally endorsed by the Department and was approved for use within the HSC under the Managed Entry of New Medicines process on 18th September 2025. The drug is available for use under interim commissioning (cost-per-case) arrangements. Details of the HSC Managed Entry decision are available at https://niformulary.hscni.net/managed-entry/managed-entry-decisions/Officers within my Strategic Planning and Performance Group have been working with colleagues from HSC Trusts to confirm the number of eligible patients and to agree a proposed service model to deliver this drug therapy. In the discussions to consider the resource implementation needs for TA1086 (Ribociclib), the Trusts have advised that given the numbers of expected patients and existing system capacity, there is a need to prospectively agree the additional staffing and testing infrastructure.  This will require an agreed approach to secure and train the additional staffing. It is proposed that an interim service model be established on a non-recurrent basis before transitioning to the recurrent model when the additional staffing required are available.SPPG officers during discussions with the Trusts have been advised that other UK Nations are facing similar challenges when considering similar models of implementation for this particular NICE TA.SPPG officers continue to work at pace to secure agreement on the implementation of arrangements of an interim model for NI. While this model is being put in place eligible patients have access to the medicine under interim commissioning (cost-per-case) arrangements where existing capacity allows. I hope that this information provides assurance of the significant efforts being made to allow introduction of Ribociclib on a planned and effective basis, and at the earliest possible opportunity for eligible patients in Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of children awaiting autism assessments, in each of the last five years, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust (HSCT); and (ii) any measures being taken to ensure equitable and timely access to diagnostic services across all HSCTs, particularly in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust where waiting lists are highest.
answer_text: The Autism information provided below is management information sourced from Trusts' manual returns, and not official statistics.  The implementation of Encompass from 2024 has impacted on both the returning of data and confidence in its accuracy. In light of this HSC Trusts are currently completing validation exercises to assess the robustness of their data returns.Total number of children waiting for an autism diagnosis at year end, by HSC Trust Mar-21Mar-22Mar-23Mar-24Mar-25Belfast1866228030664023 Northern1880275836994269 South Eastern70106160  Southern 17922157511601610Western11291295157720002686Total512466609077114524296(ii) All Health and Social Care Trusts follow the Integrated Elective Access Protocol (IEAP), to manage autism referrals. IEAP is a regional guidance document issued by the Department of Health (Sept 2025). IEAP guidelines encourage the timeliness, equity and appropriateness of treatment for all patients/clients. Referrals for autism assessment are defined as routine, and therefore under IEAP are managed in chronological order by referral date. Specific actions within NHSCTWithin the Northern HSC Trust, robust recovery measures, service redevelopments & innovative practices have been systematically introduced across the years. However, given the continuing significant increase in demand, the total numbers waiting continue to increase as demand significantly exceeds capacity. The greatest demand is on the assessment & diagnostic service, however, demand for intervention and support also continues to rise year on year.The diagnostic service has developed diagnostic referral criteria & referral forms aligned to other services in NI as a region, which allows for robust triage to ensure an autism assessment is most appropriate for that child/young person and to allow appropriate triage into the correct assessment care pathway and to reduce duplication.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department plans to take to ensure that frontline healthcare staff with early or late shift patterns are not disadvantaged or placed at personal risk by the new Belfast Health and Social Care Trust parking scheme, particularly those who rely on private transport due to the lack of suitable public transport options.
answer_text: The Belfast Health and Social Care (HSC) Trust have confirmed that the new criteria-based parking permit application process has been paused and all staff who have previously held a car parking permit will retain their permit.The Trust will now consider the feasibility of increasing the number of available permits, the potential to provide alternative transport solutions, and their ability to reduce demand at the site. A range of supports are in place to help encourage a reduction in the demand for onsite staff car parking including free off-site staff parking spaces with free staff shuttle buses to and from work, electric car pools, cycle to work and car share schemes, cycle shelters and showering facilities onsite to encourage cycling, running or walking to work, free evening and weekend parking  passes for parking out of hours, as well as the provision of bespoke Personal Travel Plans for staff to encourage a travel modal shift.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has given consideration to extending eligibility for accessible parking spaces to families of disabled children under the age of three, who are currently excluded as they cannot receive the higher rate mobility component of Disability Living Allowance, due to age.
answer_text: Children under the age of three may qualify for a Blue Badge if they have a disability because of a medical condition and need to travel with bulky equipment or be close to a vehicle for emergency medical treatment.Provision of a blue badge is a key component of the assessment of an accessible parking bay application. My Department currently administers the provision of accessible parking bays using the policy document RSPPG E056 – ‘Provision of Parking Places’. Provision of parking places RSPPG E056Currently there are no plans to change or amend this policy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) how often her Department (a) reviews; or (b) retenders, contractors of weed control on the roads and footpaths; and (ii) the performance metrics used.
answer_text: Weed control is carried out through my Department’s Term Contract for Environmental Maintenance. There are currently 9 live Contracts carrying out weed control across the North that have a minimum term of 3 years up to a maximum 5 years. These contracts are retendered in two phases, bringing together alternate areas across our four Divisions. The tenders for each contract are managed through my Department’s CoPE, that is focused on delivering value for money through contract procurement. Each contract uses a performance specification to ensure an effective weed spraying treatment is provided.  Officials monitor and carry out checks to ensure the weed spraying is completed in accordance with the contract performance requirements. Performance is regularly monitored using key performance indicators and reviewed at six monthly meetings. Officials, through regular routine inspections, can also note areas where weed control has not been carried out in accordance with the specifications, for referral to the contractor.  Ad hoc inspections can also be undertaken due to complaints on weed issues at specific locations, and again, if appropriate, the matter would be referred to the contractor for resolution.In addition, I would advise that my Department has no specific statutory obligation to remove weeds, other than in relation to the control of noxious weeds and invasive species.  We also undertake weed control aimed at the safety of road users and preventing the deterioration of the road pavement. It is practically impossible to eliminate weed growth and consequently we spray footway surfaces, kerb edges and drainage channels on an annual basis. In rural areas, the most effective method of weed control is by cutting grass before the weeds take a firm hold.  Weed spraying is carried out by trained personnel normally during the months of April through to October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department monitors the performance of external contractors, responsible for weed control on roads and footpaths, to ensure value for money and effectiveness.
answer_text: Weed control is carried out through my Department’s Term Contract for Environmental Maintenance. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has reviewed the performance of contractors, responsible for weed control on roads and footpaths, in light of the growing number of complaints.

In rural areas, the most effective method of weed control is by cutting grass before the weeds take a firm hold.  Weed spraying is carried out by trained personnel normally during the months of April through to October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider expanding the proposed 20 mph speed limit zone at Jordanstown School, to include (i) Rosstulla Special School; and (ii) Thornfield House Special School.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer for everyone. I am acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads.My Department is currently taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North listed on my Department’s website; https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/20mph-speed-limit-schools-tranche-3. This will bring the total delivered across the North since 2020/21 to 273 schools.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.While the Tranche 3 programme focuses on schools on rural roads where the national speed limit applies, I am aware that there are schools in urban and residential areas which would also benefit from a reduction in speed limits. Officials are exploring options for permanent 20mph zones particularly near schools, in predominately residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking and cycling.My Department is also carrying out a review on a number of other speed related matters and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise as part of this review.  Further consideration on the use of part time speed limits at schools will be taken on completion of the consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any powers available to Environmental Health Officers to address situations where homeowners have installed solar panels without appropriate pigeon-proofing or netting, leading to birds nesting underneath and causing a nuisance to neighbouring properties; and (ii) whether the provisions of the Dilapidation Bill will provide any additional means to address such issues.
answer_text: District Council Environmental Health Officers have powers under Part 7 of the Clean Neighbourhoods and Environment Act (NI) 2011, to investigate, and if appropriate, take action, in relation to matters which constitute a statutory nuisance. 		The Dilapidation Bill will not provide any additional means to address such issues.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) any (a) charges; (b) fees; or (c) contributions, required of women staying in refuge hostels; (ii) how these charges are determined; (iii) any provisions that exist to support women who cannot afford these charges, including (a) waivers; (b) subsidies; or (c) emergency funding; (iv) whether his Department has assessed if the cost of refuge accommodation deters women from leaving abusive households; (v) any consideration that has been given to introducing additional funding mechanisms, or best practice models, to allow women in employment to acc
answer_text: Administration of the Supporting People Programme and Housing Benefit are operational matters for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Supporting People (SP) programme, which is administered by the Housing Executive on behalf of the Department for Communities, provides grant funding to organisations towards the provision of housing-related support. This includes provision for women at risk of domestic violence where support is provided through short-term accommodation-based refuge services as well as floating support delivered in the community. Women staying in refuge accommodation in Northern Ireland are generally also asked to pay rent and service charges, these costs cover accommodation and shared facilities. Housing Benefit may cover all or part of these charges.The organisations funded through the SP programme are independent organisations and set these other charges themselves.The Housing Executive also administers the Housing Benefit scheme in Northern Ireland on behalf of the Department for Communities, in line with the Housing Benefit Regulations (NI) 2006.Entitlement to Housing Benefit is subject to a means test, the eligible rent is assessed against a claimant’s income and capital to determine the amount of Housing Benefit they are entitled to, including this type of supported accommodation.There is no charge to service users for housing related support delivered through floating support.”My Department provided the Housing Executive with an additional ringfenced allocation £2.5m for strategic homelessness prevention in 2025/26. The Housing Executive’s prevention priorities include:Woman vulnerable to and experiencing chronic homelessnessIndividuals experiencing or having experienced domestic abuseFinancial Inclusion servicesMy Department also currently contributes £136k per annum for the 24-Hour Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline service, jointly funded by the Departments of Justice and Health, which provides a critical, life-saving service for victims and survivors of domestic and sexual abuse. It offers immediate, confidential emotional support, practical information, and referral pathways to specialist services.My Department has not assessed if the cost of refuge accommodation deters women from leaving abusive households.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on any progress of the School Enhancement Programme, in the East Antrim area.
answer_text: The table below provides an update on the progress of School Enhancement Programme (SEP) projects in the East Antrim area. 	School Name	RIBA Stage	Carrickfergus GS	Stage 1 – Technical Feasibility	Greenisland PS	Stage 1 – Technical Feasibility	Roddensvale School, Larne	Stage 1 – Technical Feasibility	Thornfield House, Newtownabbey	Stage 2 – Scheme Design	I have made funding available to progress SEP projects through the design stages to pre-tender.  A decision to release projects to tender will be taken on a case-by-case basis, dependent on budget availability at that time.I will continue to make the case for additional capital funding for education through the Executive.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) whether the recently updated Education Authority handbook on school sports facilities will be reviewed to ensure that sports hall size requirements do not restrict the competitive playing of sports such as (a) netball; (b) volleyball; and (c) basketball; (ii) whether his Department plans to update the handbook to ensure sports halls can safely accommodate these sports; (iii) any consultation that has taken place with (a) schools; and (b) sports governing bodies, on the impact of current size restrictions; (iv) any guidance or support available to
answer_text: The Department of Education Building Handbook guidance for sports halls has been developed collaboratively between the Education and Training Inspectorate,  Education Advisory Branch and the Education Authority for delivery of the  Northern Ireland Curriculum Programme of Study for Physical Education. A review of the Department’s Building Handbook is ongoing which will include the consideration of sports facilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions being taken to (i) improve the recruitment, retention, and career progression of female staff within the emergency services, including the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service and the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service, and (ii) address barriers such as work-life balance, flexible working, and workplace culture.
answer_text: NIAS and NIFRS have advised the following actions are being taken to improve recruitment, retention and career progression of female staff.  NIASNIAS applies merit-based decisions for recruitment to all posts across the service and ensures attendance at post-primary careers events so the service is represented as a career option for all female students considering a graduate career in paramedicine.  There are also other non-graduate entry routes into NIAS our ACA and EMT roles and increasingly there are specialist clinical roles within paramedicine all of which are open to female staff or applicants.  NIAS ensures that all advertisements and marketing materials that include a diverse visual representation of staff including that of our female colleagues.  NIAS offers a range of internal and external training opportunities for staff to progress with female staff having equal opportunity to apply for career progression.  This includes supporting staff to obtain places to the graduate paramedic graduate programme. NIAS has a low attrition rate amongst female staff, however, it makes every effort to reduce attrition, increase retention and create a culture where female staff achieve career progression across the service. NIAS has a comprehensive Organisational Culture programme which is overseen by the Trust Board and Chief Executive.  The objectives of the programme align with the corporate plan of ‘promoting a culture where all staff feel valued’.  The plan incorporates a range of strategies including the Open, Just and Learning Organisation, the Sexual Safety Strategy, the Being Human Framework for Safety Culture and the Collective Leadership approach for HSC.  This programme is designed to ensure that NIAS promotes a safe, equitable and just culture for all staff including our female colleagues.  In addition, NIAS offers a range of flexible working options through the flexible working application process and a range of special leave options.  Requests for applications are considered and a range of flexible options are available for staff with elder or child (or other) caring responsibilities.  This includes part-time working and flexible working as well as transfers within the service.  Flexibilities can be offered on either a temporary or permanent basis to ensure female staff with caring responsibilities can balance their work and family life.NIFRSNorthern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) actively engages with young women through career fairs and visits to schools to raise awareness of firefighting as a viable and rewarding career option and to build early interest among future candidates. Recruitment campaigns feature targeted outreach in schools and interactive ‘Come and Try’ days at fire stations across Northern Ireland.  These offer practical insight and opportunities to engage with serving female firefighters.  The NIFRS application process has been refined, with clear fitness guidance and tailored preparation programmes.  Once empl

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the number of times that NI Water has discharged into Belfast Lough in the last year; and (ii) any improvements that have been made to reduce this.
answer_text: The management of public wastewater infrastructure is an operational matter for NI Water. However, I have sought the requested information from NI Water and have been advised as follows:During the 12 months of 2024, NI Water’s infrastructure discharged into Belfast Lough on 1,475 occasions. NI Water is carrying out a programme of additional maintenance to ensure continued effective operation of its wastewater assets which discharge into Belfast Lough until such times as a budget is available to undertake any more substantial upgrades that may be required. Those upgrade schemes, which have been identified as part of the Living with Water in Belfast Plan, will be taken forward as normal business through the Price Control.I will also continue with my three-pronged approach to address wastewater issues. Firstly, I continue to work with Executive colleagues to ensure appropriate investment for NI Water, secondly, I am bringing forward my new Water, Sustainable Drainage and Flood Management Bill, that will make provision for future regulations in relation to sustainable drainage systems (SuDS), which help to store stormwater so that it releases slower into the wastewater network and thirdly, I have consulted on proposals for developer contributions. That consultation has now closed and a consultation response paper will issue in the coming weeks.This year, my Department will provide NI Water with over £500m of public money to enable it to deliver essential water and sewerage services for all people across the North.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact NI Water directly, through the Elected Representatives Hotline on 0345 300 6461 or through waterline@niwater.com. Making direct contact with NI Water will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

answer_text: NIAS& (ii) NIAS data is set out in the table belowYearFemale Staff Retention RateFemale Staff Turnover RateNumber of Female Staff Recruited (Headcount)Percentage of Female Staff Recruited2020/2188.73% 2.29% 102 56.35%2021/2293.37% 2.95% 58 50.43% 2022/2395.56% 3.91% 25 36.23% 2023/2494.83% 6.06% 30 50.00% 2024/2590.49% 5.72% 83 52.20% Data extracted from HRPTS system.
