question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 30875/22-27, for an update on when Agenda for Change staff will receive their pay rise in line with colleagues in England and Wales.
answer_text: Following a meeting held on the 6th November, the Executive agreed to the implementation of the recommended pay awards for HSC staff for 2025/26. This will restore pay parity for healthcare staff here with those in England. The recommended pay uplifts will backdated to 1 April 2025. However, as with any pay award, there is a process in place that must be followed before healthcare staff will receive their monies. The Department of Finance must approve an official pay remit prior to the issuing of a formal pay circular and BSO will then require time to request, test and implement the required IT changes on the HSC payroll system. I would expect our HSC workforce to receive their uplift and backpay in their February 2026 pay packets.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs what plans his Department have to improve the water quality in Belfast Lough along the East Antrim coast.
answer_text: I recognise the critical importance of safeguarding water quality in Belfast Lough and affirm my strong commitment to implementing comprehensive measures to improving water quality across Northern Ireland.Wastewater is a significant pressure in the Belfast Lough catchment. Although wastewater infrastructure falls within the remit of Department for Infrastructure (DfI), my Department is engaging with both DfI and Northern Ireland Water to enhance the regulation, monitoring and treatment of wastewater. I am progressing plans to identify inner Belfast Lough as a sensitive area for shellfish waters under the Urban Waste Water Treatment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007, subject to Executive approval. Additional wastewater treatment will be required as a result of the identification. I am aware of the need to progress that important area as a matter of urgency. Above all, addressing the issue depends on proper investment. I will continue to support the need for investment in wastewater infrastructure in Northern Ireland and to take the tough decisions that we need to take to ensure that we have sustained long-term investment.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 33935/22-27, whether he will consider introducing guidance for GPs to allow flexibility in providing post-operative or follow-up care in exceptional circumstances for patients who have received treatment privately, particularly where (i) NHS waiting times have effectively removed patient choice; (ii) the patient is unable to access private aftercare due to mobility or transport limitations; and (iii) a refusal to provide basic wound or recovery care would pose a risk to patient health or safety.
answer_text: As advised in my response to AQW 33935/22-27, individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care (HSC) system.  In these circumstances, there is no obligation on GPs to provide pre and/or post treatment support.  In December 2024, my Department wrote to all GP practices to provide clarification regarding pre and post treatment support for individuals wishing to avail of private healthcare.  This specifically concerned the provision of such support within primary care by general practitioners who deliver general medical services and was not a substitute for their professional clinical judgement and consideration.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the rationale as to why enhanced rate payments for additional shifts by Emergency Department nurses have been withdrawn, while agency nurses and locum doctors continue to receive enhanced rates.
answer_text: Internal enhanced rates are only applicable to nurses and are a matter for individual HSC Trusts as the employers. The enhanced rate had been used in Emergency Departments across HSC when all other options of filling gaps in the roster with bank, overtime and the use of agency staff had been exhausted. The Western HSC Trust has not withdrawn enhanced rates in their Emergency Department and the Northern HSC Trust currently has a protocol in place to offer enhanced rates for Emergency Departments. The Southern HSC Trust ceased enhanced rates for all nurses in late 2023 including Emergency Departments. The South Eastern HSC Trust ceased the use of enhanced rates, including in Emergency Departments, in November 2025. Belfast HSC Trust have never had a process for enhanced rates of pay that steps outside of AFC process, with the exception being the regional Covid enhanced pay during the pandemic.A Regional Agency Reduction Implementation and Planning Group has been established to co-ordinate the improvement of staff utilisation, with the aim of reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend. The main function of the Group is to develop and implement a plan, with local and regional commitment and coordination, to safely facilitate, and manage the service impact of, reducing unsustainable agency and locum spend.In addition, with the aim of stabilising the HSC workforce our strategic approach to workforce development, supported by sustained increased investment in pre-registration education and training, has facilitated a 18.7% (+10,629) increase in whole time equivalent staff in post across the HSC in Northern Ireland between March 2018 (56,803) and September 2025 (67,432).   This includes a 31.7% (+1,331 wte) increase in medical and dental staff, a 19.0% (+2,875 wte) increase in registered nursing and midwifery staff and a 25.9% (+2,104 wte) increase in professional and technical staff in post.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the progress of the SEN Capital Investment Programme.
answer_text: Good progress has been made on the four strands of my SEN Capital Investment Programme (CIP) over the last 18 months:Strand 1 Annual Maintenance & Equipment Programme - £6m has been provided in furniture and equipment grants to all new specialist provision classes which have been established and a £2m programme of works has/is being delivered both last year and this year to facilitate and improve access to the outdoors in special schools and in mainstream schools to support specialist provision;Strand 2 New Specialist Provision - 242 new specialist provision classes have been created in mainstream primary and post-primary schools and an additional 96 classrooms in special schools;Strand 3 Extension and Refurbishment of Special Schools: two School Enhancement Projects (SEPs) at specials schools are now in construction and a third will commence early in 2026.  Five of the existing 10 SEPs in special schools have now been expanded in scope;Strand 4 New Special Schools: the business case is currently going through the approvals process for the first of the new Belfast special schools.  The ‘Plans of Action for Special Schools’ were published on 16 September outlining the five-campus Belfast model and new school/campuses across Northern Ireland.My department has invested £110m from its capital budget over the last two years on the SEN CIP with the cost to deliver the currently announced SEN CIP in the region of £430m over the next 10 years.  To continue to expand specialist provision in mainstream and deliver on the proposals in the Plans of Action, an estimated £1.7 billion is required over the next decade.The current levels of my department’s capital budget cannot absorb this commitment.  The only way forward to put investment in SEN infrastructure on a sustainable footing is for it to be recognised as a strategic cross-government priority and secure Executive commitment to funding over the next decade. On 9 September I circulated a draft Executive paper to Executive colleagues outlining my proposals for an Executive-led, and funded, SEN capital investment programme which would sit outside the conventional Education capital budget. Without a commitment to funding I cannot deliver on the proposals in the Plans of Action.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any cross-departmental work being undertaken to address antisocial behaviour and public safety concerns, associated with the misuse of fireworks.
answer_text: No cross departmental work is currently being undertaken specifically regarding the misuse of fireworks although my Department works closely with local councils who may monitor planned fireworks displays to ensure compliance with the licence.Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by my Department and the NI Policing Board, play a key role in the Department’s operational response to community safety concerns at a local level.Tackling antisocial behaviour is a key strategic priority for PCSPs, which engage with local communities, statutory agencies and the community and voluntary sector to develop planned, purposeful, structured interventions to address the issue in each Council area.Although we are not aware of any specific fireworks interventions by PCSPs , they support partner messaging on social media, such as the PSNI Halloween safety campaign.Some PCSP initiatives include on street youth intervention and community safety wardens who assist in addressing the issue of ASB in local areas.Northern Ireland has among the most robust firework regulations in these islands. I recognise that this is an area of concern for many members of the public, however, and have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether she has considered reviewing the current licensing and enforcement framework for fireworks, to ensure it remains effective and proportionate.
answer_text: I have no current plans the review the existing fireworks licensing arrangements.  The existing regulations provide a robust framework that seeks to ensure fireworks are sold and bought responsibly for use by the public.The licensing system aids partners, including PSNI, to identify unlicensed and illegal fireworks use.  Offences committed under the firework regulations may result in a fine of up to £5,000, or a prison sentence, or both.Any concerns about the misuse of fireworks should be reported to the PSNI.In recognition of the public concern around the misuse of fireworks, I have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any assessment her Department has made of the potential for introducing low noise or silent fireworks, as seen in other jurisdictions.
answer_text: I continue to raise awareness of the availability of low-noise fireworks in my annual messaging, highlighting that by making a simple change, it could help make displays more enjoyable for neighbours and reduce the distress to animals.  Information on fireworks, including the availability of low noise fireworks, is available all year round on the government services webpage NI Direct. I am aware of a number of events this year that have used low noise fireworks or alternatives to fireworks.The setting of decibel limits for fireworks in the UK remains a reserved matter.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any avenues available to private owners of former Housing Executive flats to challenge the quality, or cost, of work undertaken by the Housing Executive, where they have been required to contribute to such schemes.
answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 34651/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of reports that PSNI officers have been asked to remove firearms before meeting Sinn Féin representatives.
answer_text: Policies and procedures relating to firearms are an operational matter for the PSNI which is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board, of which you are a Member. I understand from reports that you had the opportunity to raise this matter directly with the Chief Constable, who set out his assessment and position unequivocally. As the facts of these reports are now in dispute, the Policing Board is the most appropriate forum in which to investigate further and establish their veracity or otherwise.That fact notwithstanding, I believe any request for PSNI officers to surrender their personal protection weapons while on duty would not only be entirely inappropriate but also pose a significant risk to the personal safety of officers and public safety more widely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the rationale for the permanent removal of street lighting along the Ballystrudder Road between Middleton Park and Middle Road, Islandmagee; (ii) whether any assessment was undertaken of road safety implications, including pedestrian safety, prior to the decision being made; and (iii) whether her Department will review this decision, given the increased pedestrian use linked to nearby amenities, and the ongoing concerns regarding speeding in the area.
answer_text: When a street lighting system in an area reaches the end of its serviceable life, a survey is carried out at the location and replacement lighting is installed only in those areas which meet my Department’s policy for the provision of road lighting, as outlined in Street lighting RSPPG E072 .Following an inspection, the existing street lighting on this stretch of Ballystrudder Road was deemed to have reached the end of its serviceable life. An assessment found that this location did not meet the criteria set out in my Department’s policy and consequently, the existing lighting was removed and not replaced.This policy helps to ensure that lighting is provided where it is most needed, while also balancing considerations such as maintenance costs, energy reduction and environmental impact.Road safety remains a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and address the needs of all road users. In this instance, my officials have advised that PSNI statistics indicate that there have been no recorded collisions at this location.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps her Department is taking to support the PSNI in tackling the illegal sale and use of fireworks, particularly during the lead-up to Halloween and New Year.
answer_text: My Department works closely with the PSNI.  If officials are made aware of any concerns about the illegal sale or use of fireworks, they will share information with the PSNI.  I encourage anyone with information about illegal activity to contact PSNI directly.PSNI are provided with details of current registered retailers and stores, and officials will provide relevant information to support test purchases, including statements and court attendance if required.Fireworks retailers are required to keep a record of fireworks sales, and submit the record to my Department annually, usually by the end of November.  Records are checked against licences issued, and any discrepancies are investigated and details shared with the PSNI.Northern Ireland has among the most robust firework regulations in these islands. I recognise that this is an area of concern for many members of the public, however, and have instructed my officials to examine penalties and enforcement concerning the illegal use or sale or fireworks in the pending review of sentencing issues.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department will review the environmental impact of its current weed control methods, given that a reduction in weed killer strength may result in more frequent treatments or increased weed growth.
answer_text: My Department uses external contractors to carry out weed control on a performance specification basis, until an effective treatment is provided. The choice of control method, strength, and type of weed killer is a matter for the individual contractor.My Department ensures that all pesticides are authorised under the Plant Protection Product Regulation (NI) 2011 and follow the requirements for the safe use of Plant Protection Products (PPPs) (Pesticides) as directed by the Sustainable Use Regulation 2012.Only herbicides suitable for use on hard surfaces, which are on the Health & Safety Executive’s approved list are permitted.  All weed spraying operations must be carried out by suitably experience operators either holding, or under the direct supervision of someone holding, a Certificate of Competence recognised by DAERA.  Furthermore, to mitigate the risks associated with this activity, weed spraying operations must not take place when weather conditions dictate that spray drift is likely to cause damage; herbicides should not be applied over drains or in drainage channels, gullies or similar structures; and care must be exercised when spraying near water courses, catchment areas and reservoirs only utilising approved products at such locationsAll chemical, herbicide and pesticide products are supported by an accompanying Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (COSHH) assessment and subsequent COSHH safety controls. Officials are exploring the use of alternatives to herbicides on the road network that support All-Ireland Pollinator Plan actions, such as:Use of mechanical weed control, i.e. mowing, strimming, or cutting machines to manage roadside verges.Use of organic mulches to suppress weeds.Use of biological controls i.e., using natural predators or biological agents such as beneficial insect/fungi to control pests e.g. nematodes.Use of manual removal i.e., manual weed pulling or cutting in small, sensitive areas where machinery or other methods are not suitable. Replace grass-heavy verges with native wildflower meadows that supress invasive weeds and support pollinators.My Department continues to work with environmental charities such as Ulster Wildlife, and the Department of Transport to assess the environmental impact of weed control operations and discuss feasible alternatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether her Department has changed the strength or type of weed killer, used for weed control on roads and footpaths in recent years.
answer_text: My Department uses external contractors to carry out weed control on a performance specification basis, until an effective treatment is provided. The choice of control method, strength, and type of weed killer is a matter for the individual contractor.My Department ensures that all pesticides are authorised under the Plant Protection Product Regulation (NI) 2011 and follow the requirements for the safe use of Plant Protection Products (PPPs) (Pesticides) as directed by the Sustainable Use Regulation 2012.All chemical, herbicide and pesticide products are supported by an accompanying Control of Substances Hazardous to Health (COSHH) assessment and subsequent COSHH safety controls.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of complaints her Department has received from the public regarding weeds on the roads and footpaths, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: Complaints in relation to weeds on roads and footpaths are categorised as “Overgrown Weeds/Grass” on my department’s defect management system.The table below provides details of the numbers of complaints received in this category in each of the last five years.YearNumber of Complaints2020/21           1,381 2021/22           2,535 2022/23           2,829 2023/24           3,132 2024/25           3,909

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has to increase investment and job creation in the Mid and East Antrim area, particularly in Carrickfergus and Larne, which appear to have received lower levels of support, compared to Ballymena, over the last three years.
answer_text: My department and Invest NI are firmly committed to delivering greater levels of investment in all parts of the North.  In line with this commitment, my Sub-Regional Economic Plan has been created as a framework to improve sub regional economic performance.  Invest NI published its new strategy last year titled “Our Future in Focus” (Invest NI Business Strategy 2024-2027 (PDF)). This strategy is directly aligned with my department’s commitment to balanced regional growth. Invest NI is implementing a new regional approach that is seeing it increase the capacity and capability across its regional office network, assisting it to seek out, identify, and engage with more businesses than ever before. Alongside this, with support from Invest NI and my department, all 11 Councils have established Local Economic Partnerships. Mid & East Antrim has been allocated £4.509m of funding in support of its Local Economic Partnership delivery, from total Regional Balance Funding of £45m. Invest NI and my Officials are providing expertise to the Local Economic Partnership in Mid & East Antrim with the aim of helping this group make the greatest impact across the entirety of the council area, while supporting ground-up design and decision making.  Regional Business Advice Clinics, planned for Carrickfergus and Larne in November, and will be delivered alongside local partners including council, colleges and InterTradeIreland are an example of increased outreach and actions being undertaken by Invest NI to engage with businesses and increase the number of investments being made in these areas. Invest NI has also increased delivery of its support through online application calls. Initiatives such as the Business Innovation Grant (BIG) and Ambition to Grow can be applied for through the Invest NI website leading to greater accessibility for businesses including those in Mid and East Antrim.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any action that can be taken to assist residents who are affected by Japanese knotweed.
answer_text: Japanese knotweed has been established in Northern Ireland for many decades.With regards to individual property owners, in both national and EU legislation, DAERA’s policy context towards all invasive non- native species is one to assist to protect the ecology of an area as opposed to safeguarding property or other commercial assets. The Department carries out focused work to address Japanese knotweed, prioritising designated and high value biodiversity sites, via conditioning its removal through planning applications, invasives management in NIEA country parks, and through the Environmental Farming Scheme. More generally, invasive species control is also conditioned on all funding streams, such as the Environment and Challenge Funds, and Water Quality Improvement Grants. Departmental officials frequently provide advice to homeowners about Japanese knotweed. Removal of invasive non – native plant species such as Japanese knotweed must be part of any land or homeowners’ general maintenance responsibilities. DAERA also provide a wide range of advice and guidance on how to manage invasive plants on their website – https://www.invasivespeciesni.co.uk.In August 2025 Japanese knotweed was listed by the EU as a Species of Union Concern meaning landowners have a legal obligation to prevent this species spreading from their land ‘into the environment’.DAERA is also now required to implement  Article 19 of EU Regulation 1143/2014 (Widely Spread Species) within 18 months of Japanese Knotweed being listed on the EU list.  This will mean that home and landowners will be required to put management measures in place to stop its spread and ultimately facilitate its removal.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any outreach activity Invest NI is undertaking to engage small and medium enterprises, located in Mid and East Antrim, that may not be aware of the support available to help them grow and expand.
answer_text: Invest NI carries out a wide range of activities to engage with businesses, especially small and medium sized enterprises, across the Mid & East Antrim area to make them aware of the support available to help them grow and expand. Invest NI’s regional teams work closely with local partners and stakeholders to promote the range of support available and help identify new businesses that may be eligible for support. Since 1 April 2024 Invest NI has held 27 events in Mid & East Antrim covering topics such as Supply Chains, Operational Excellence, Training Needs Analysis, Access to Finance, R&D and Exporting. Invest NI is also currently delivering a series of free business advice clinics in partnership with councils, further education colleges and InterTradeIreland in Mid & East Antrim, a clinic took place at Ballymena Business Centre on 22 October. Invest NI has specific solutions aimed primarily at businesses that are not yet Invest NI clients. 21 businesses in the Mid & East Antrim area have been supported to become clients through Ambition to Grow since January 2023. And four SMEs from the Mid & East Antrim area have received support through the new Business Innovation Grant (BIG), launched last November.Invest NI also promotes its support through a range of channels, including its weekly email newsletter ‘2 minute update’ which is delivered to 16,000 subscribers. Its corporate social media channels, which publish an average of 30 posts per week, have over 200,000 subscribers. Invest NI also offers free resources through its online business information service, NIBusinessInfo.co.uk. The website has over 5,500 pages of guidance available covering key topics, and its monthly newsletter is circulated to 12,215 email addresses.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline the progress made in developing a stocking protocol for public angling sites such as Woodburn Dams, Carrickfergus.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail how her Department prioritises the implementation of (i) local transport; and (ii) safety measures, including traffic calming and safer routes at schools in East Antrim.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department, and we are committed to working proactively to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users. We are acutely aware that children and young people are amongst the most vulnerable groups using our roads, particularly around schools.However, my Department has been operating in a difficult financial environment for a number of years due to underfunding and austerity by the British Government and limited budget resources mean we must prioritise our interventions to those locations deemed most in need.Within the constraints of the budget for 2025/26, my officials have provided an equal allocation of the funding available in Northern Division to each of the three councils.  My department holds priority lists of schemes in each council area.  The schemes on these priority lists have been determined in accordance with the published policies (available at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/articles/dfi-roads-policy-statements) with funding allocated to those with the highest priority and where the greatest impact will be provided andMy Department is also taking forward a third tranche of part-time 20mph zones at 40 schools across the North.  This will bring the total delivered since 2020/21 to 273 schools with an investment of £5m from by my department. The third tranche includes Abbey Community College and Jordanstown School, both within the East Antrim constituency.This initiative is a significant intervention that will help to ensure that children, parents and staff feel safer as they travel to and from school on a daily basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the amount of funding her Department has allocated for the (i) treatment; and (ii) control, of Japanese Knotweed, found on (a) roads; and (b) land owned by her Department, in each year, since 2019.
answer_text: As a responsible landowner, my Department has a legal obligation under The Wildlife (Northern Ireland) Order 1985, to prevent invasive non-native weeds, such Japanese Knotweed, from spreading into the wild or adjacent private lands.  The treatment and control of noxious and invasive weeds is best managed at local level to prevent spread.  Funding allocations are not specific to the control/treatment of Japanese Knotweed.  My Department’s funding allocation is for grass cutting, tree maintenance, and all weed treatment and control which includes Japanese Knotweed. It would also not be possible to distinguish between roadbed and land owned by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 21487/22-27 and AQW 12966/22-27, to provide an update on progress made by his Department in addressing Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder.
answer_text: Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) will form part of a wider review of clinical pathways that are discussed and agreed by the Child Health Partnership.  This will be discussed at a meeting of the Partnership in the coming weeks where the clinical leads will decide, based on the range of clinical priorities facing the service, when this review will take place.The Child Health Partnership has not established this multi-disciplinary group to date, as they have been dealing with a number of other clinical priorities and there has been capacity issues amongst relevant staff.  Adult Mental Health Services are not commissioned to treat ARFID

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of the Equality Commission’s recently published review of the Ulster Boxing Council.

To ask the Minister of Justice (i) for her assessment on the recent hate crime incidents at both Rasharkin and Dunloy Orange Halls; and (ii) to detail any support available to protect Orange Halls that suffer incidents of sectarian hate crime.
answer_text: ANSWERThere must be no place in our society for sectarian hatred of any kind, and I condemn without reservation the attacks on Rasharkin and Dunloy Orange Halls.  I also recognise the impact of attacks on key building on the wider community, including distress and disruption caused by resulting damage.My Department, in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, provides funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) to work in partnership with local communities and other key partners, including local statutory bodies/agencies and the voluntary and community sector, to develop actions to deal with, and reduce, the impact of issues such as Anti-Social Behaviour (ASB) and hate incidents. Although there are currently no projects being delivered which are specific to Orange Halls, PCSPs generally act swiftly to address local issues as and when they arise.A PSNI control strategy for attacks on symbolic buildings is in place across all Districts. PSNI continue to work in partnership with the Orange Order and other community organisations to prevent such attacks and, where they occur, to exploit all evidential opportunities to bring those responsible to justice. I would encourage anyone concerned about attacks on Orange Halls to access guidance from the PSNI’s Crime Prevention Officers (CPOs), who can provide advice on security management in respect of symbolic buildings to help prevent any future incidents.In the last mandate, I considered the potential development of a Places of Worship/Key Buildings Security Scheme for Northern Ireland following discussions with interested groups. My officials continue to monitor evidence-based information to assess if a scheme for Northern Ireland is required; however, given the unfunded pressures facing my Department, any decision would require a decision on additional resourcing at Executive level.I remain committed to working across government, councils and stakeholders, to tackle drivers of sectarianism, reduce levels of offending, and prevent people coming into contact with the criminal justice system.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether the Folate Receptor Autoantibody Test has ever been commissioned in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Folate Receptor Autoantibody Test is not routinely commissioned in Northern Ireland or elsewhere in the NHS UK.  DOH understand that it may have been used in certain clinical trials exploring its association with autism-related conditions. Additionally, there may be a small number of private laboratories in the UK offering the test.Northern Ireland will continue to work collaboratively with our NHS counterparts across the UK to assess the clinical evidence base for the test.  In line with NICE recommendations, we will keep the use of this test under review to ensure decisions remain in the best interests of our population.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans her Department has to restore or enhance weekend and off-peak public transport services in rural areas, such as Islandmagee, where residents, particularly the elderly and disabled, are being isolated as a result of limited service.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink continually monitors the transport network to look for opportunities to better serve the population and generate modal shift to sustainable transport. Whilst Translink recognises the vital role that public transport can have in rural communities, given the current funding climate there are no immediate plans to enhance services that are not economically sustainable; this includes services around Islandmagee. In addition, my department provides funding for the Dial-a-Lift service which operates in rural areas for those who find it difficult or impossible to access public transport.  This member only service operates during the day, Monday to Friday enhancing access to local goods and services and enabling people to remain connected to their communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider commissioning a review into rural transport provision in areas where there is no weekend bus service.
answer_text: The provision of, and timetabling of services is a matter for Translink. I can, however, advise that there are reviews underway of both Ulsterbus services and Community Transport services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the enforcement mechanisms in place to ensure developers meet road adoption standards in new housing developments; and (ii) any support available to residents affected by unadopted streets.
answer_text: Earlier this year I gave a commitment to the Assembly to provide updated Private Streets Enforcement Guidance to staff in an effort to reduce the risk of developments entering the Department’s backlog of unadopted private streets sites.On the 25th of September I announced the introduction of the new Private Streets Enforcement Guidance. The guidance establishes a threshold for the initiation of enforcement action of 2 years following 80% occupation of houses in a development.My department has also published new developer information which amends the bond release thresholds. This change will enable my department to retain a larger proportion of the bond which provide the funds required to bring new developments roads infrastructure to an adoptable standard should enforcement action be necessary.The new guidance, implemented alongside the changes to the bond release thresholds, will protect homeowners by greatly reducing the risk of new developments remaining unadopted.The department may consider enforcement when a developer has defaulted through the issuing of a Notice under Article 11 of the Private Streets (NI) Order 1980, outlining the outstanding work, and allowing the developer to take action towards completion of the site within 28 days The developer has a right to appeal under Article 12.In instances where there is non-compliance with the Article 11 Notice, my Department may take forward the necessary works and recover the costs under the provisions of Article 15 and adopt the road on completion of the work.If the Developer does not pay costs as detailed in Department's request or the developer is no longer trading, the Article 15 demand is served to the appropriate Bank or Insurance Company acting as surety for payment of costs incurred in bringing the development to an adoptable standard.If a site is not brought to an adoptable standard by the developer, and the funds in the bond are insufficient to complete the infrastructure to an adoptable standard, my department are not funded to carry out such works. My department continue to communicate with and encourage developers to have sites brought to an adoptable standard at the earliest date.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps that will be taken to assess and address the social and recreational impact on East Antrim constituents caused by the non-stocking of Carrickfergus Dams.
answer_text: Carrickfergus Dams, Marshallstown, Middle South Woodburn and Killylane Public Angling Estate (PAE) waters were stocked earlier this year with Brown trout.  Stocking of fish to the PAE Waters over the Autumn and Winter period is planned when conditions allow compliance with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985 with respect to preventing the escape of Zebra Mussels, an invasive species. There are 89 public angling estate waters throughout Northern Ireland.  Only 27 of these fisheries are stocked and despite the suspension or reduced stocking, these waters still contain wild fish that are suitable for angling. Therefore, a wide variety of wild fisheries and angling opportunities are available to permit holders.  In addition, purchasing a permit for the PAE does not guarantee any level of stocking within the PAE; it only provides access to fish the waters
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to address delays in the provision of community mobility equipment, including powered wheelchair components, due to stock and supply issues.
answer_text: To ensure continuity of supply of Community Equipment, BSO identified any potential impacted lines supplied by Nottingham Rehab Services Ltd (NRS) and took steps in July 2025 to buffer additional stock, eventually switching to alternative suppliers on the Framework.  This proactive decision ensured that there were no delays to community equipment due to stock and supply issues.In relation to the Community Wheelchair Service, NRS were placed into liquidation 1 August 2025.  During this time the Official Receiver continued to provide services under its contract with HSCNI Trusts, working alongside PwC as Special Managers to transition the service without interruption.  The Regional Disablement Service (RDS) within BHSCT worked with suppliers to ensure the ongoing supply of parts for manual and powered wheelchairs.  This enabled the service to respond as normal to requests for repairs and modification to wheelchairs on loan to clients across HSCNI.My Department, in agreement with the key stakeholders from HSCNI, opted to bring the service in-house under the Business Services Organisation (BSO) and the new Community Wheelchair Service (CWS) subsequently transitioned to BSO on 18 September 2025.  This transition was facilitated under a commissioning Service Level Agreement (SLA).To ensure ongoing continuity of service, BSO now directly employ the staff who were previously working under NRS.  This has enabled the knowledge and expertise held within this staff group to transfer over to the new CWS.  BSO have also entered into new lease agreements to secure appropriate premises and fleet and have enabled a new IT infrastructure to support the operational delivery.  My Department is overseeing a planned approach which will work to stabilise the new CWS under BSO throughout October 2025, this will include ensuring recovery on any backlog resulting from the NRS liquidation.  In an effort to improve the service going forward, BSO is in discussion with the main suppliers to establish a local stock holding of high-volume parts during November 2025 to improve stock availability, visibility and lead times for repairs and modifications.  This will ensure a more robust service delivery model, with improved performance in respect of operational responsivity to service user need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any guidance in place for GP's regarding the provision of aftercare for patients who have received private treatment, particularly in situations where the patient is unable to access private follow-up care due to mobility, transport, or health reasons; and (ii) whether his Department will consider reviewing current policy to ensure patients are not left without essential aftercare, regardless of how their initial treatment was delivered.
answer_text: As independent contractors and clinical professionals, GPs are responsible for making appropriate decisions about the treatment, care, and management of their individual patients.Individuals choosing to procure healthcare privately are effectively stepping outside of the health and social care (HSC) system.  In these circumstances, there is no obligation on GPs to provide pre and/or post treatment support.  Therefore, it remains important for individuals wishing to avail of private healthcare to ensure that all pre and post treatment support costs are included in the overall package of care procured from the private healthcare provider. In instances where any ongoing treatment and medication is recommended, but is not provided, by the private provider as part of the private service then individuals should be prepared to meet any associated costs.   Where a GP is made aware of an individual’s plans to procure private healthcare in a private capacity, it is recommended that: the individual is encouraged to ensure all pre and post treatment costs are included in the overall package.the individual is informed that they may have to pay for any ongoing treatment or medications which are recommended.GPs remain responsible for clinical decisions regarding the care of individual patients and as such they should exercise their clinical judgement prior to providing any pre and/or post treatment support for individuals availing of private healthcare.  Whilst there is no obligation on GPs to provide such support, in instances where a GP considers that the support being requested is clinically appropriate, falls within their area of competence, does not breach the terms of their GMS contract or indemnity cover, and is in keeping with the General Medical Council (GMC) standards on prescribing and Good Medical Practice then they may choose to provide this support or continue to provide this support.In line with normal practice, individuals procuring healthcare privately will continue to be able to access emergency care where necessary.In circumstances where a patient is receiving care in the Independent Sector as an HSC patient, pre and post treatment care will be provided by the HSC as normal.There are currently no plans to review the Department’s position.  The principle in which individuals procuring private healthcare are effectively stepping outside the HSC remains appropriate.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail a breakdown of the funding provided through the Regional Change Fund for Ending Violence Against Women and Girls, in East Antrim.
answer_text: The Regional Change Fund of £1.2m is allocated to eight community and voluntary sector organisations operating regionally to support schools, communities and workplaces in preventing violence against women and girls. These organisations are Women’s Resource and Development Agency (WRDA), Youth Action, Nexus, Barnardo’s NI, NSPCC, White Ribbon, Women’s Aid NI and Relate NI.In the first six months of the Regional Change Fund opening, these organisations have delivered over 400 events, reaching 5,140 participants across the region. It is not possible to identify specific spend by constituency as the data is not available in that format.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the Communities in Transition projects funded in East Antrim, in each of the last 2 years.
To ask the Minister for the Economy for a breakdown of Invest NI support in the Mid and East Antrim local council area, broken down in the areas of (i) Ballymena; (ii) Larne; and (iii) Carrickfergus.
answer_text: Invest NI proactively publishes a full breakdown of support offered to businesses over the past 5 years on the NI Open Data online portal. The portal contains the following information which will facilitate access to the data requested for Mid and East Antrim local council area:Portal | Invest NI Total Tables 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025Provides a complete picture of Invest NI support through summary tables that aggregate both published and non-published data (including indirect support, like advice and guidance, and offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues). This information is broken down into several different variables, including council and constituency areas.Portal | Invest NI Financial Offers of Assistance (Detail)Provides detail on individual offers of direct financial assistance issued by Invest NI to businesses between 1 April 2020 and 31 March 2025. It does not include indirect support, like advice and guidance, or offers that cannot be released due to data protection issues.Portal | Invest NI Open Data GuidanceProvides guidance on the data contained in 1 and 2 above. question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of Mid and East Antrim recording one of the lowest number of applications for Invest NI support in Northern Ireland, to outline the measures her Department is taking to ensure that this area is adequately supported to attract investment and avail of economic development opportunities.
answer_text: Between 2020–25, Invest NI received close to 11,000 applications, of which 5% were from businesses within the Mid & East Antrim area. This performance reflects that the area has the third lowest number of businesses and is home to just 3% of our region’s exporters. The Sub Regional Economic Plan identifies disparities in export intensity as a barrier to regional balance. To deliver against this my department has established new Local Economic Partnerships supported by a dedicated fund of £45m, of which approximately £4.5m has been made available to the Mid & East Antrim partnership. The plan also commits Invest NI to a new regional approach that is seeing the agency increase the capacity and capability of its regional offices. It has already increased the number of staff in its Eastern regional office, located in Ballymena, providing more resources to attract investment and seek out more businesses from across the borough that can apply for its support which, over the long term, will drive better regional outcomes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any work she has undertaken with the PSNI to (i) streamline recruitment for former police officers seeking to return to the Police Service, without repeating basic training; and (ii) permit the transfer of police officers from other UK jurisdictions, to address current PSNI officer shortages.
answer_text: The ability for former officers to return to the PSNI, and for officers to transfer from other UK forces to the PSNI is currently under consideration. My Department is currently exploring with PSNI how these provisions can be achieved. Any legislative change, either primary or secondary, will be subject to a statutory consultation process and will be impacted by capacity within the current legislative mandate.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, given the concerns regarding pollution in Belfast Lough, whether he will commit to expanding formally identified bathing water sites to include areas such as Carrickfergus, where open water swimming is becoming increasingly popular.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) any mechanisms in place to monitor the implementation of the Armed Forces Act 2021 in schools; and (ii) how the effectiveness of these measures is evaluated.
answer_text: I recognise that the children of service personnel often face distinct and additional challenges as a result of the demands and circumstances associated with their parents’ or guardians’ service. Children of services personnel can be considered vulnerable due to the disruptions they face in daily life, which can lead to emotional and wellbeing challenges. These issues often stem from parental deployment, extended absences during tours of duty or frequent home moves, which can cause anxiety and uncertainty for affected children and young people.With regard to education, the Armed Forces Act 2021 placed a legal duty on the Education Authority and the Boards of Governors of grant-aided schools to consider the unique sacrifices of the Armed Forces, address disadvantages which arise from service, and give due consideration to the provision of appropriate support measures that mitigate these disadvantages.To ensure that all relevant bodies were fully informed of their statutory responsibilities in supporting children of services personnel, the Department disseminated the UK Statutory Guidance on the Armed Forces Covenant Duty to all Northern Ireland schools, EOTAS centres, and the Education Authority in November 2022.In order to enable schools to support the children of services personnel, my Department supplies a funding premium to schools on a per pupil basis for children of services personnel pupils. For primary schools, the allocation per pupil is £618.82 and for post-primary schools, the allocation per pupil is £622.57. While my Department issued the Statutory Guidance to all relevant bodies in order to support the implementation of appropriate interventions, there are no prescribed requirements governing how schools allocate or utilise the additional funding premium. Aside from the additional funding provided to schools, the Education Authority provides support which is available to children of services personnel. For instance, the Education Authority provides transport assistance to all eligible pupils, and appropriate arrangements for transport assistance are currently provided for pupils residing at Ministry of Defence sites in Aldergrove, Lisburn and Holywood. Additionally, although the Armed Forces Covenant does not change the Admissions Procedure for application to a school, the legislation ensures that a child of services personnel is not discriminated against. The Education Authority’s Primary and Post Primary Admissions offices have dealt with queries from schools regarding the Armed Forces Covenant and schools have been advised appropriately.  There have also been communications with the Children’s Education Support Officer for Northern Ireland who looks after Services children in Northern Ireland on Education matters. Recognising the importance of effectively supporting children of services personnel, I instructed my officials to undertake a review of current practices and to identify opportunities for further enhancement. To that end,

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for an update on the Go Succeed service.
answer_text: Go Succeed is the Council led business start-up programme for the North of Ireland. It was launched in November 2023 and is majority funded through the British Government’s Shared Prosperity Fund (SPF). Go Succeed plays a crucial role in helping entrepreneurs across the North start and grow their own businesses.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail (i) any engagement their Department has had in relation to the digital ID scheme announced by the Prime Minister; and (ii) any actions they will take to oppose the introduction of the scheme.
answer_text: We were briefed by Chief Secretary to the Prime Minister prior to the announcement.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for an update on the business case being developed to enable mediation and the issuing of outstanding holiday pay to retired police officers, including the expected timeline for approval and funding.
answer_text: My officials are currently considering the PSNI Holiday Pay business case which must satisfy the requisite approvals process, including approval by the Department of Finance. Holiday Pay is an exceptional pressure that is wholly unaffordable within existing or future PSNI and DoJ baseline budget. Further discussions will be needed with the Executive and the Department of Finance (DoF) to consider options for funding this exceptional pressure.Furthermore, Holiday Pay is an issue that has an impact across all departments and, therefore, a solution at Block level will be required.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the measures in place to ensure that children with type 1 diabetes have equal and safe access to after-school classes, including the availability of trained staff to support their medial needs, so these pupils are not excluded from opportunities due to their condition.
answer_text: In accordance with the Special Educational Needs and Disability (Northern Ireland) Order 2005 and the Disability Discrimination Act 1995, schools have a duty to make reasonable adjustments in order to ensure that disabled pupils are not at a substantial disadvantage compared to other non-disabled pupils.  This duty is both an anticipatory duty and a reactive duty and also applies to non-education services provided such as after school clubs and after school activities. After school provision is a matter for schools with the policies and procedures in operation during the school day also applicable for after school activities.  Schools have a responsibility to follow the departmental guidance ‘Supporting pupils with Medication Needs’ and make reasonable adjustments to ensure that that the needs of pupils with medical needs, including diabetes, are met. First Aid and Administration of Medication policies should be in place.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions he is taking to ensure patients with a red flag referral for suspected breast malignancy are seen within 14 days, per National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Guideline NG12.
answer_text: To address longstanding inequalities in red flag waiting times, a single regional waiting list for breast cancer assessments was introduced on 8 May 2025.  This system, ensures patients are offered the earliest available appointment, regardless of home Trust.  This change has led to improvements in waiting times in some Trusts while others have experienced delay. However, it has successfully eliminated disparities in access, helping to ensure that every patient is treated fairly and consistently across the region.  I fully acknowledge that the regional approach, while promoting fairness in access does not yet meet the 14-day target for red flag breast assessment services.  Our current focus is now on reducing overall waiting times so that every patient across Northern Ireland can be seen within the recommended timeframe. Achieving this requires a broader, coordinated effort to address the gap between demand and available capacity. To support this, £5 million in recurrent funding has been identified through my Department’s Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation Plan.  My Department is working closely with Trusts to increase capacity.  In the short-term waits will be addressed using additional waiting list initiatives clinics and increased use of independent sector capacityI acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast assessment patients remain lengthy and of deep concern for patients across Northern Ireland.  This situation reflects a combination of funding shortfalls of objectively assessed need and missed opportunities for system reform.   However, I want to assure you that improving access to breast cancer assessment services remains a key priority for the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of current waiting times for patients with a red flag referral for suspected breast malignancy.
answer_text: To address longstanding inequalities in red flag waiting times, a single regional waiting list for breast cancer assessments was introduced on 8 May 2025.  This system, ensures patients are offered the earliest available appointment, regardless of home Trust.  This change has led to improvements in waiting times in some Trusts while others have experienced delay. However, it has successfully eliminated disparities in access, helping to ensure that every patient is treated fairly and consistently across the region.  I fully acknowledge that the regional approach, while promoting fairness in access does not yet meet the 14-day target for red flag breast assessment services.  Our current focus is now on reducing overall waiting times so that every patient across Northern Ireland can be seen within the recommended timeframe. Achieving this requires a broader, coordinated effort to address the gap between demand and available capacity. To support this, £5 million in recurrent funding has been identified through my Department’s Elective Care Framework (ECF) Implementation Plan.  My Department is working closely with Trusts to increase capacity.  In the short-term waits will be addressed using additional waiting list initiatives clinics and increased use of independent sector capacityI acknowledge that the current waiting times for red flag breast assessment patients remain lengthy and of deep concern for patients across Northern Ireland.  This situation reflects a combination of funding shortfalls of objectively assessed need and missed opportunities for system reform.   However, I want to assure you that improving access to breast cancer assessment services remains a key priority for the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide an update on Carrickfergus Academy’s new build project.
answer_text: A technical feasibility report which explored options and costs for the provision of adequate accommodation for Carrickfergus Academy was submitted to the Department for consideration on 18 July 2025.Once the Department’s technical advisers have concluded their review of the report a Business Case will be completed, which following approval, will identify the preferred option to be taken forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what options are available to children with sensory needs who require emergency dental treatment, particularly those requiring the use of anaesthetic.
answer_text: Children may receive dental care across a range of settings in Northern Ireland: General Dental Services (GDS), Community Dental Services (CDS), and the Hospital Dental Services (HDS). Potentially, all dental services have a role to play in the provision of emergency care to a child with additional needs, dependent on the particular dental treatment needs of that child.Early registration with a family dentist (GDS) is encouraged for all children but especially those with additional needs such as sensory processing issues as this can help with the implementation of preventive measures as well as confidence building and desensitization to the sensory challenges within the dental environment.The CDS specialises in providing routine dental care to patients who, for various reasons, are not suitable for treatment in the primary care setting. CDS dental staff are experienced in managing children with additional needs such as sensory processing issues/ neurodiversity and can work together with the children and their carers on patient centred strategies to make the process easier to accept.For young children, those with additional needs or particularly complex cases, it may be necessary on occasion for extractions and/or restorative treatment to take place under General Anaesthetic (GA).  It should be noted that patients who require all or most of their dental care under GA still require reviews and preventive measures at regular dental appointments. Waiting lists for comprehensive restorative care are separate from the paediatric extraction waiting lists. Children with additional needs may be placed on either list depending on their individual needs.All five Health and Social Care (HSC) Trusts Community Dental Services offer dental treatment under GA to children with additional needs.In 2024-25, the CDS saw over 3,500 children for extractions under GA. To ensure that the most urgent cases are treated as quickly as possible, CDS teams within HSC Trusts across Northern Ireland operate a strict triage and prioritisation process which is based on clinical need.For those children with the most complex needs, a GA day case in a district hospital may not be suitable. Therefore, for both patient safety and complexity of care reasons, a referral to the regional Paediatric Consultant led service in BHSCT may be required.Last year, my Department consulted upon the Children's Oral Health Improvement Plan. This document made a number of strategic recommendations to prevent dental decay among children. In May of this year, I announced an extension of the Happy Smiles Pre-school Toothbrushing programme to P1, P2 and P3 children attending schools in the 20% most deprived wards.  With the proven effectiveness of the existing Happy Smiles programme in pre-school children, the expectation is that the expanded programme will produce even greater oral health gains and a reduction in the need for children to receive dental treatment under GA.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department can better support private tutors who wish to meet high professional and safeguarding standards, but currently face barriers such as accessing AccessNI checks.
answer_text: The Department is currently liaising with the Home Office and Ministry of Justice to explore what needs to be done in order to extend eligibility of Enhanced checks to self-employed individuals such as private tutors.The AccessNI disclosure process is primarily arranged to help employers and voluntary organisations to make safer recruitment decisions.  The long-standing position is that self-employed people cannot obtain an Enhanced check as they have no-one to be responsible to for any information that might be disclosed on their certificate as they are in effect the employer.  The legislation requires that an employer or regulator must be able to ask an exempted question (in accordance with provisions within the Rehabilitation of Offenders (Exceptions) Order (NI) 1979) and is required to view the disclosure certificate and to make an assessment on the appropriateness of the individual to undertake the specific role.  For the self-employed there is no such employer / decision maker.Anyone, including Tutors, can apply to AccessNI for a Basic Check.  A Basic Check shows all unspent convictions or will state that no convictions were found.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how he will ensure that marching bands are recognised and celebrated as part of Northern Ireland’s cultural heritage.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work has been carried out by his Department to improve access to Direct Payments.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail what urgent steps his Department is taking to ensure that home oxygen deliveries are made on time for patients who rely on them daily.
answer_text: A procured Oxygen Service is currently provided by BOC Homecare Service for patients who require home treatment with oxygen in Northern Ireland. HSC Trusts and GPs access this same service. 				To ensure there are adequate supplies and to ensure the correct patients get oxygen therapy in line with their needs there is an agreed prioritisation process with Trust respiratory leads.				SPPG, together with colleagues from each of the 5 Trust areas, meet with BOC on a bi-monthly basis to discuss the contract performance in relation to the KPIs specified within the procured oxygen contract. 		 SPPG have contacted BOC in response to your query and I can confirm there are no current shortages in oxygen or delays in delivery of home oxygen.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 25299/22-27, to detail (i) an update on the preparatory work on Graduated Driver Licencing; and (ii) a timeline for the introduction of same.
answer_text: Work is ongoing on the operational planning and subordinate legislation changes that are required before GDL can be implemented. That preparatory work is advancing well, and I expect to be able to announce the anticipated date that GDL will be introduced shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when Agenda for Change staff will receive their pay rise in line with colleagues in England and Wales.
answer_text: As you will be aware, on the 28 May, in a written statement to the Assembly, I confirmed my decision to issue a Ministerial direction to implement the pay recommendations for 2025/26. I informed my Department’s Permanent Secretary that I wanted the current recommended increases to be approved without delay.At the heart of this decision is my recognition of the immense dedication and professionalism of our HSC workforce.  Staff deserve to be valued and fairly remunerated for their vital work.  Timely pay awards are an important part of that.You will also be aware that my decision to issue a Ministerial Direction to implement the 25/26 Pay Award has been referred to the Executive for consideration and decision.  I have on, 2 occasions, raised the issue at Executive meetings without any progress or decision being taken.  The HSC Pay Award was again discussed at the meeting on 04 September and, while there was another robust discussion, regrettably the Executive has still not taken decision on the issue, and that is preventing the Department from proceeding with implementing a 25/26 Pay Award for HSC staff. I will continue to engage with the Executive to try to convince them of the need to move forward in delivering a pay award that fully recognises the valuable services provided by our HSC workforce.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, given that coastal erosion intends to be added as an orphan risk through the Civil Contingencies Framework, to detail (i) when coastal erosion and associated management through Shoreline Management Plans will be added to the Programme for Government; and (ii) which Department will take the lead on delivery.
answer_text: Coastal erosion is recognised on the Risk Register, and preparedness for this risk is led by the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA), with support from the Department for Infrastructure (DfI), in line with the principle of subsidiarity.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline how her joint work with the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, through the Coastal Forum, will discuss and implement the role of nature-based solutions to enact areas of the Blue Carbon Action Plan and provide alternative solutions, where feasible, to hard infrastructure, such as sea walls.
answer_text: Minister Muir and I will be co-chairing the meeting of the Coastal Forum on 19 November and I have been advised by Minister Muir that DAERA will be providing an update on the potential role of blue carbon habitats as a nature-based solution for climate change adaptation at this meeting.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the public angling estate.
answer_text: My Department actively manages the 89 waters within the Public Angling Estate (PAE) through ongoing maintenance and development of those sites. Collaboration continues with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a stocking protocol to address the presence of zebra mussels at Movanagher Fish Farm which is compliant with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985. An interim protocol allowed limited stocking earlier in the year. A co-design process has commenced to develop options for the future of the PAE.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any review which has been carried out of the Education Authority’s standardised school meals provision in order to ensure it adequately meets the needs of children with autism and other special educational needs, particularly those with (i) restricted diets or (ii) sensory-related food preferences.
answer_text: In 2022-2023 the Education Authority (EA) Catering Service undertook a review of its school meals offering in consultation with children and young people, parents, school principals and catering staff. The review included nursery, primary, special and post-primary schools.  Menus offered by the EA accommodates those children and young people with special dietary requirements for cultural, religious and medical needs, including those with sensory-related food preferences. Following a parent making the EA aware of a special dietary need, they will inform the school principal and the EA special diets process will commence. If appropriate, input from a relevant medical professional may be sought. As part of this process, the parent(s), principal and EA catering manager will work in partnership in order that the individual needs of the child are met. This includes the opportunity for parents to record foods that should be avoided, potential substitute foods and also detail any changes required to food texture.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he will (i) review if specific towns or rural parts of East Antrim are experiencing shortages of school places, even when the wider area appears to have a surplus; and (ii) take any action to address these imbalances.
answer_text: My Department continues to monitor the availability of school places across all areas of Northern Ireland, including East Antrim. While headline figures may suggest a surplus of places across a wider area, I recognise that this does not always reflect the pressures faced in particular towns or rural communities, where parental preference and local demographics can result in an uneven distribution of places. My officials engage with the Education Authority (EA), the Council for Catholic Maintained Schools (CCMS) and other sectoral support bodies on a regular basis to ensure that such localised pressures are identified and understood.(ii) 	Where evidence shows that an imbalance exists between the distribution of school places and local demand, schools and managing authorities working in partnership, can seek to bring forward appropriate measures. These may include temporary variations in admissions, consideration of area planning proposals, or the allocation of additional places to better meet need. My Department will continue to support this process to ensure that children in East Antrim, and across Northern Ireland, have equitable access to a suitable and sustainable school place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail why some works have been classified as R4; (ii) to detail what specific criteria were used to assign this lower-priority status; and (iii) to outline how her Department intends to ensure that such re-classified works do not compromise safety or service standards for affected communities.
answer_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail why some works have been classified as R4; (ii) to detail what specific criteria were used to assign this lower-priority status; and (iii) to outline how her Department intends to ensure that such re-classified works do not compromise safety or service standards for affected communities.ANSWER My Department has in place established procedures for the inspection and repair of the road network. These are contained in our policy and procedure guide, the Roads Maintenance Standards for Safety, which specifies the classification of and response times for the repair of various defects https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/drd/road-maintenance-standards-for-safety-rsppg-e019.pdfRoad safety is a key priority for my Department and given the current constrained financial environment, my Department will continue to prioritise its road maintenance activities to ensure that the safety of road users is not compromised.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the new Pupil Allocation Number review will improve transparency for parents in East Antrim, particularly those who struggled to secure a pre-school place last year despite an overall surplus of places regionally.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Programme is a highly successful programme and in each of the last twelve years, including the 2025/26 admissions process, at least 99.8% of children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school education place, and remained with the process to the end, received an offer of a funded pre-school place in a setting of their parent’s preference.  The pre-school admissions process is preference based, however, it is not possible for all children to receive an offer of a funded pre-school place in their first preference setting, or at stage one of the admissions process.The EA continually reviews the levels of pre-school provision in all areas to ensure that sufficient provision is available to meet demand for funded places.  The EA has advised that at the outset of the 2025/26 pre-school admissions process, 650 funded pre-school places were available within the Mid and East Antrim council area, with a total of 584 first preference applications received during stage one of the process. All children whose parents stayed with the admissions process to the end received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parent’s preference and places remained available in the area at the end of stage two of the process. The operational processes regarding pre-school admissions and the allocation of funded pre-school education places to non-statutory settings are a matter for the Education Authority (EA).  In April 2025, I asked the EA to carry out a review of the current systems and processes and propose potential options for change and improvement including for the allocation of Pupil Admission Numbers (PAN) to settings.  The EA has completed this review and a copy of their report can be found here.  The report contains a number of actions to bring about improvement, including (i) providing data in the published admissions criteria for all funded pre-school providers  including application and admission figures; (ii) publishing provisional PAN allocations within admissions criteria; and (iii) publishing the detail of the PAN allocation process before the commencement of the pre-school admissions process.  I am satisfied that the actions within the report will bring about improvement to the EA processes and provide better transparency for both parents and settings.  My officials will monitor the EA’s implementation of the actions set out in the report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail what steps he is taking to ensure that headline regional figures translate into accessible local provision for parents, given that while Education Authority data shows more pre-school places than first-preference applications in East Antrim, many families still reported difficulty securing places.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how the Department will ensure that lessons are learned from this year’s admissions difficulties in East Antrim, in order to inform Pupil Allocation Numbers for 2026–27 and beyond.
answer_text: The Pre-School Education Programme is a highly successful programme and in each of the last twelve years, including the 2025/26 admissions process, at least 99.8% of children whose parents applied for a funded pre-school education place, and remained with the process to the end, received an offer of a funded pre-school place in a setting of their parent’s preference. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of patients having rapid response care packages removed upon admittance to hospital, even if that admittance is for one night.
answer_text: The Southern and Western Trusts do not operate a Rapid Response service. The other three Trusts do.Rapid Response is a short-term Domiciliary Care Service with the primary aim of providing a rapid discharge service from an acute hospital setting.  This is where someone with reablement potential, requires a new homecare package to facilitate discharge.  No one is expected to be on this service for longer than 6 weeks.  Should an individual require a hospital admission, the three Trusts vary in how long they will hold this service for, it could be 1, 2 or 3 days.   Rapid Response can then recommence at point of discharge if the individual’s assessed needs remain the same.  Due to the demand for reablement services, and hospital pressures the Trusts are obliged to continue to recycle service capacity to maximise service availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how his Department is ensuring that children with special educational needs are not subjected to excessive travel times on buses or in taxis; and (ii) any maximum journey time standard being applied.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it delivers home to school transport assistance for nearly 15,000 children with special educational needs (SEN). Transport arrangements for SEN pupils with additional transport needs are organised individually. The EA plans routes and services to minimise journey time where possible, however it does not currently have a system which measures the length of time that a child spends travelling on a bus or in a taxi.The journey to and from school for pupils who have additional needs can sometimes take longer than expected. This can be due to longer travel distances, the need to collect and drop off pupils at their home addresses or other designated points and the time required for safe boarding and alighting of the vehicle.There is no set maximum journey time for home-to-school transport, however efforts are made to keep travel times as short as possible.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail how her Department is supporting the training and development of childcare professionals to equip them with the skills necessary to care for children with additional needs during extended hours.
