answer_text: My Department currently facilitates a range of training and qualification pathways to assist childcare professionals who care for children with additional needs. Under my Department’s ApprenticeshipsNI programme, apprentices can pursue Level 2 and 3 qualifications in Children’s Care, Learning and Development, which include units focused on supporting children with disabilities and/or special needs. Similarly, at Level 5, the Higher Level Apprenticeship programme offers a more advanced qualification, incorporating modules designed to equip apprentices with the skills and competencies needed to care for children with additional needs. Both programmes provide fully funded off-the-job training to support the childcare sector. Under my Department’s Skills for Life and Work and Traineeship programmes, participants can pursue qualifications in Childcare from Entry Level to Level 2, including units focused on supporting children with additional needs.  I have also allocated over £350,000 from my Skills Fund to support FE Colleges to develop a series of online accredited micro courses, focused on supporting individuals working with children with additional needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what financial incentives, or support mechanisms, are available to childcare providers to encourage them to offer services during non-traditional hours, such as evenings and weekends, for children with special educational needs.
answer_text: The Department of Health (DOH) administer Early Years Development Funding (EYDF) which is used to support all registered childcare providers through the Childcare Partnership.The Childcare Partnerships were first established in April 2000 as a result of the Northern Ireland Childcare Strategy, “Children First” (1999). In 2023 DoH moved to establish one regional Childcare Partnership (CCP) which includes around 40 members from across the statutory, community and voluntary sector to support the roll out of EYDF funding.The CCP Training Programme supports all registered childcare providers to meet the Minimum Standards for Childminding and Day Care for Children Under Age 12 (DHSS&PS 2012).The core/mandatory training courses include access to Full and Refresher Safeguarding Children/Child Protection, Designated Child Protection Officer, Full and Refresher Paediatric First Aid, Fire Safety, Level 2 Food Safety, Health & Safety for Childminders, Health & Safety, Risk Assessment and Manual Handling.14,073 registered childcare providers attended the CCP core/mandatory training via this programme from April 2019-March 2024.  From April 2024-March 2025 the CCP delivered 225 Core training courses to 3,286 childcare practitioners.The CCP also commissions additional learning opportunities and continuing professional development (CPD) courses for staff working within the childcare sector which includes training to support the inclusion of children with additional needs in registered childcare provision.13,350 registered childcare providers attended the CCP Continuous Professional Development (CPD) Training Programme over the period April 2019-March 2024. From April 2024- 2025 the CCP delivered 149 fully funded CPD courses to 2,259 childcare practitioners.The Childcare Partnership team provide grant awards to a range of member organisations and registered childcare providers annually to improve the overall quality of childcare provision funded from the DOH, EYDF.The DOH provides additional funding in addition to the EYDF contribution to facilitate the Fair Play grant award scheme which aims to ensure children with a range of additional needs are included in early years services and can access quality early years experiences.Fair Play funds are administered by the academic year September to June.The Bright Start Disability Holiday Grant Scheme aims to offer financial support to childcare providers to provide additional childcare (i.e. an extension to the normal service) to facilitate children with a disability accessing services during holiday periods such as Summer, Halloween, Christmas and Half-term. Funding for this scheme is provided to the CCP by the DE.Additional DE funding was secured by the CCP to provide a grant Award to NICMA in 2024 and 2025 to facilitate further development of the Approved Home Childcarer (AHC) model as part of the Executives Early Learning and Childcare Strategy.The AHC model is a childcare option that allows parents, to

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on cancer screening for firefighters.
answer_text: Screening policy across the UK is informed by the recommendations from the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC), an independent scientific committee which advises the four UK countries on screening matters.  Currently, there is no recommendation by the UK NSC for a population screening programme, or targeted screening programme, based on occupational exposure such as for firefighters.  The UK NSC received two submissions in 2024, regarding a cancer screening programme for firefighters, as part of the UK NSC annual call process. The submissions were considered by the Annual Call Evaluation Group (which includes the UK NSC Chair, the Chairs of the Committee’s expert Fetal, Maternal and Child Health Reference Group and Adult Reference Group, Public and Patient Voice representatives, and the UK NSC evidence team). Both the submissions were rejected on the grounds that screening for fire fighters would fall under the remit of occupational health rather than being delivered by the health service and therefore were not within the scope of the UK NSC.The ‘Control of Substances Hazardous to Health Regulations 2002 (COSHH),’ requires all employers to assess the risk to their employees in relation to substances that are hazardous and "to prevent or adequately control" those risks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when the Rural Micro Capital Grants Scheme will open for applications.
answer_text: My officials have developed the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme for 2025 and I will be making an announcement on the opening of the scheme shortly.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what training and awareness initiatives are being rolled out for GPs and frontline clinicians in East Antrim to improve early recognition and referral of endometriosis.
answer_text: My Department do not hold lists or information regarding GPs and their training.  As independent contractors, and in order to provide the services that they are contracting for, GPs will be required to meet the required training and competency standards.All doctors wishing to become GPs undertake training covering a wide range of conditions including endometriosis.GPs are ultimately responsible for ensuring that they maintain their own continuous professional development (CPD) in order to keep their knowledge, skills and training up to date. This includes participation in annual appraisal and in Revalidation, the requirements of which are set by the General Medical Council (GMC). Every licensed doctor must revalidate to show that they are fit to practise and to maintain their licence to work in the UK.My Department have endorsed the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) guidelines on the diagnosis and management of endometriosis.   Last year the National Confidential Enquiry into Patient Outcome and Death (NCEPOD) published A Long and Painful Road, a review of the quality of care provided to adult patients diagnosed with endometriosis and this was shared with all GP practices.I have a number of initiatives which are designed to ensure that GPs and Practices can access training readily and at a time that is convenient.GPNI was established during the Covid-19 pandemic to ensure effective communication was maintained, with all those working within GP Primary Care. This service is commissioned from the Eastern GP Federation Support Unit and allocation for this service for 2025/26 is £51.8k. The GPNI website and webinars are now widely accepted as a key regional communications platform designed to improve dissemination of, and access to, information for Primary Care practitioners.Practice Based Learning (PBL) is a programme of learning sessions for GP’s and the wider General Practice staff.  The aim of PBL is to allow dedicated time to be set aside for educational sessions to contribute to the continued professional development of GP’s and their Practice Staff. Provision of PBL was previously developed by the then Health & Social Care Board (HSCB) for all GP Practices, however from March 2020 the Strategic Planning & Performance Group (SPPG) commissions the provision of PBL from the Federations for their member Practices.  PBL is facilitated by local GP Federation Support Units and £178k has been allocated for PBL for the 24/25 year.  They have provided a cost-effective programme of ongoing learning for their GPs, nurses and wider Practice staff.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether Health and Social Care Trusts are piloting shared care models or alternative pathways for ADHD diagnosis and treatment; and (ii) whether these could be scaled regionally.
answer_text: There is currently no commissioned service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) in Northern Ireland. ADHD provision has evolved across Trusts in response to population demand and expertise within HSC teams. Currently there is no piloting of shared care models or alternative pathways for ADHD diagnosis or treatment. There is some provision available across the Health and Social Care system, but there are inconsistencies and lengthy waiting lists across Trusts. In response, the Department commissioned a needs assessment on ADHD in early February 2025, the findings of which will help inform options for a commissioned ADHD service across Trusts, subject to future budget availability.  The needs assessment, which covers both adults and children, will aim to determine the level of demand for a commissioned service, as well as recommendations for what such a service might look like, including potential implementation and staffing costs. While the initial estimated completion date was end June 2025, due to a desire and demand for further stakeholder engagement, information gathering and analysis, it is likely that the work will now be completed by November 2025. Given the importance of this work in informing future ADHD service provision, it is important that sufficient time is given to ensure a robust evidence base and proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail what plans are in place to accelerate the delivery of affordable housing in East Antrim.
answer_text: I want to increase supply and affordable options across all housing tenures to meet need and demand, including continuing to help people into home ownership. That is why on the 20th March this year I announced a further £153M in funding for Co-Ownership to support 4,000 people to buy a home over the next four years through its Co-Own product.My Department has engaged with the housing sector and key stakeholders to explore how current issues and pressures associated with the existing new build social housing model might be addressed to maximise delivery in areas of acute housing need, particularly in the current challenging budget environment.As a follow up to the two-day SHDP Strategic Insights Lab in June 2024, I chaired a Housing Roundtable meeting in January 2025, which brought together key stakeholders to share knowledge and experience on how to increase social housing in the current challenging environment. Officials are currently taking forward a programme of work with the NIHE and the wider sector regarding a number of proposals aimed at increasing, improving or removing barriers to the delivery of the SHDP in future years.The Housing Supply Strategy sets out a range of policy areas of responsibility for the Executive as a whole. The corresponding action plan is due in December and will contribute towards the delivery of more affordable housing across all of Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline the steps the Delivery Unit is taking to ensure delivery of the Executive's key priorities.
answer_text: This Executive continues to deliver against the priorities outlined in the Programme for Government. The Childcare Subsidy Scheme has saved eligible working parents approximately £8million since it launched in September 2024. In Health, almost 59,000 additional outpatient, diagnostic and inpatient procedures have been delivered – which is well on track to meet the PfG target of 70,000.The first ever Executive agreed strategy for housing has been published, and implementation of the agreed Lough Neagh Action Plan and the Ending Violence Against Women and Girls programme are progressing well.	While much has been done, we know there is more to be done, both in the short term and in the medium to long term, to make sure that progress is maintained. This is why we have established a Delivery Unit – to keep a relentless focus on delivery against our priorities. The Unit will work across the wider Executive to understand the various challenges, review current plans, monitor progress and identify specific areas where we can support delivery.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail how schools were selected for inclusion in the 20 mph speed limits; and (ii) whether Islandmagee Primary School was considered for inclusion.
answer_text: I was pleased to announce the next phase of part-time 20mph speed limit schemes for schools across the North. A full list of schools included in the next tranche is available on my department’s website 20mph speed limit at schools - Tranche 3 | Department for InfrastructureThe programme of schools to be treated this financial year was developed, based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies.  Our approach to selecting schools is outlined in our interim policy document DEM 194/25 ‘Part-time 20mph Speed Limits at Schools – Tranche 3 2025/26’ which is publicly available at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/2025-05/part-time-20mph-speed-limits-at-schools-tranche3-2025-26-dem-19425.pdf Regrettably, Islandmagee Primary school did not score as highly as other locations and was not included in this tranche.  However, the school will be considered in any future extensions of the programme.I would however like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) his assessment of the East Antrim Band Forum's Exhibition; and (ii) how can his Department support initiatives such as this moving forward, to highlight the positive impact of the marching band scene.
answer_text: It was a privilege my officials and I to attend the East Antrim Band Forum’s exhibition which amplified the importance of music and bands, and how they contribute to and enrich our cultural experience.I am committed to increasing the quality of music-making and supporting greater participation more generally across the arts.  I am very open to supporting initiatives such as this, and my officials are discussing how the current exhibition can be built on.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail (i) what progress has been made on assessing drug-driving thresholds; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to the cost implications to the PSNI.
answer_text: A review of drug driving is one of the key strategic interventions in the Road Safety Strategy to 2030 and my Department is currently in the process of exploring legislative options to address the issue of drug driving.  These options, along with the related impact assessments, will be subject to a full consultation in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline any work her Department is undertaking with Mid and East Antrim Borough Council as part of the Belfast Region City Deal Carrickfergus Regeneration project to build upon tourism in the town.
answer_text: Carrickfergus Regeneration Project is a DfC led project within the Belfast Region City Deal. Tourism NI continues to work closely with MEABC on the tourism elements of the project and is an active member of the Project Board and the Castle & Walls and Transport & Infrastructure working groups. During the early design phase, Tourism NI facilitated a bespoke workshop with the MEABC project team and their design teams to advise on consumer insights and how best to optimise the overall visitor experience.In March 2025, Tourism NI facilitated a learning opportunity for MEABC to visitor attractions in Limerick, Malahide and County Clare to research potential operating models for the project. Tourism NI will continue to provide support to Council during the development phase and has committed to work with Tourism Ireland and other partners to maximise the marketing and promotion of the project to the travel trade and consumers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the review into speeding.
answer_text: Road safety is a high priority for my Department and I am committed to working proactively with partners to reduce death and serious injuries on our roads, to make our roads safer and to address the needs of all road users.  I have asked my officials to prepare a paper on a number of speed related matters including the national speed limit on rural roads, HGV speed limits and 20mph speed limits in general.  Work on this is underway and, and I plan to undertake a public consultation exercise on this important issue later this year. One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour, particularly to speeding. Drivers need to view speed limits as a limit, not a target, and drive at speed appropriate to the road and surrounding conditions.  My Department’s public information campaigns repeat the message that speeding is not acceptable.  As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail what work his Department can do to reduce the waiting times for MRI scans, particularly for gynaecology patients.
answer_text: The recently published Elective Care Framework Implementation and Funding Plan sets out clear proposals for how we will systematically tackle the backlog of patients waiting longer than Ministerial standards and how we will transform services to allow us to meet the population’s demands in the future.As part of the Implementation and Funding Plan, £12m has been secured to expand diagnostic imaging capacity across the region.  This not only includes increasing capacity for MRI but will include areas such as CT and ultrasound.  The initial focus will be ensuring that scanning sessions, not already commissioned, are recurrently funded Monday to Friday. Further work will then be undertaken to look at evening and weekend working.Plans are also in place for two additional MRI scanners in the Rapid Diagnosis Centres in the Southern Trust and Northern Trusts for regional use, which will be operational in 2025/26.  These will be used to reduce MRI backlogs across Northern Ireland.  In addition, plans are in place within the Department’s 15-year Capital Plan for a significant investment over the next 10 years, to expand the equipment base, which includes an additional MRI scanner. This will be subject to funding approval. Additional capacity is for all specialities including gynaecology. Patients are prioritised according to clinical need and appointed in chronological order.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail why there has been a delay in implementing Graduated Driver Licensing.
answer_text: Implementation of the Graduated Driver Licensing scheme entails significant amendment of current driver training, testing and post testing arrangements. It requires further subordinate legislation as well as significant operational planning activity and development of the online digital solution required to support delivery of the Programme of Training and Logbook. Once operational planning and the work my officials are doing on the legislative changes has been sufficiently progressed, I will be able to provide an anticipated date for the introduction of GDL.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of the amount spent on providing legal advice to asylum seekers, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The table below provides a breakdown of authorised expenditure, by Supplier Type, for legal advice where the primary matter is defined as Immigration, in each of the last five years. The annual totals for each year correspond with the information provided in response to AQW 28361/22-27.As outlined previously, these figures include both asylum and non-asylum immigration cases, as it is not possible to isolate asylum-related services within this matter.Table 1: Legal Aid Authorised Expenditure for Advice on Immigration cases by Supplier Type, 2020/21 to 2024/25Supplier TypeAdvice and Assistance2020/21Solicitor Firm£283,081Third Party Payee£37,600Total£320,6812021/22Solicitor Firm£366,232Third Party Payee£102,519Total£468,7512022/23Solicitor Firm£406,020Third Party Payee£205,448Total£611,4682023/24Solicitor Firm£529,023Third Party Payee£193,945Total£722,9682024/25Solicitor Firm£1,022,413Third Party Payee£277,386Total£1,299,799Source: Legal Services Agency (Official Statistics and Management Information)Payments to Solicitor Firms will include profit costs, VAT (where appropriate) and a small amount of disbursements covering expert reports as well as travel and mileage. Payments to Third Party Payees relate to the provision of translation and interpreter services.  You will note there are no payments to Barristers in these cases.This information has been sourced from the Legal Services Agency’s case management system (LAMS).  Figures covering the latest year (2024/25) constitute management information at this stage and may be subject to revision during preparation for the Agency’s 2024/25 Official Statistics release in Autumn 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when the road marking for Minorca Drive, Carrickfergus, will be carried out.
answer_text: The road markings, at the junction of Minorca Drive and Ellis Street, have been instructed for renewal.  It is anticipated that these works will be completed over the summer period.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister how their Department will ensure that digital transformation will not exclude vulnerable groups, such as older adults, people with disabilities or victims of abuse, who may struggle with digital access and literacy.
To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any further action her Department can take to support victims of domestic abuse.
To ask the Assembly Commission for an update on the Disability Discrimination Act audit on accessibility in Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the diabetes audit.
answer_text: The Diabetes Strategic Framework (2016) included an action to participate in National Diabetes Audit, similar to the Diabetes Audit used in England and Wales.There have been significant challenges in progressing this recommendation, as the required infrastructure is not currently in place to participate fully in the audit. Encompass has been rolled out across all Health and Social Care Trusts.  Once fully embedded, Encompass will enable the provision of regional data, that is both available and extractable. By working with Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland colleagues, information can be collated from across the Health and Social Care system to support participation in the National Diabetes Audit.My Department remains committed to working towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail how data required for the diabetes audit will be collected from primary care.
answer_text: Audits were undertaken at a regional, local Trust and general practice level in the development of the Diabetes Strategic Framework, published in 2016.Available data on diabetes treatment and care has been considered in the development of the Regional Integrated pathway for Diabetes, which has been co-produced with stakeholders over the past three years.  This has included data recorded and analysed from Primary Care, including data from the General Practitioner Intelligence Platform.By working with Digital Health and Care Northern Ireland colleagues, General Practitioner Intelligence Platform can provide the mechanism to routinely extract confidential, non-identifiable data from GP clinical information systems.  This in turn will support my Department’s commitment to work towards participation in the National Diabetes Audit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the amount spent on giving legal advice to asylum seekers in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The information you have requested has been provided within a response to a similar question (AQW 27258 22-27) which was answered on 28th May 2025. You will note from that answer that the figures provided include both asylum and non-asylum immigration cases, as it is not possible to isolate asylum-related services. A copy of this response, together with supporting contextual information, can be viewed via the official record on the Assembly website: https://aims.niassembly.gov.uk/questions/printquestionsummary.aspx?docid=439812

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department is aware of any concerns from users of Omnipod 5 and its compatibility and usability with Abbott FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus; and (ii) whether his Department has any plans to commission Dexcom G7.
answer_text: In Northern Ireland, continuous glucose monitors currently listed on the NI Drug Tariff can be provided to patients, with the most appropriate system determined in consultation between the clinician and the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  The Omnipod 5 is a hybrid closed loop system that can integrate with the Abbott FreeStyle Libre 2 Plus continuous glucose monitor sensors.Dexcom G7 senor is a type of complex Continuous Glucose monitoring device that is not currently listed on the NI Drug tariff.  Some patients have been self-funding these devices as they enable closed loop technology, although it may not be the most appropriate system for some patients.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any plans his Department has to introduce funding streams to improve access to (i) community buildings; and (ii) places of worship,  for those with a disability.
answer_text: My officials are currently developing a programme of support for small scale capital refurbishment and improvement projects for community facilities and buildings which deliver benefits to the wider community.Eligibility criteria are currently under consideration as part of the programme development. I plan to make an announcement on this programme next month.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the new mental health facility for Carrickfergus that has recently received planning permission from Mid and East Antrim Borough Council.
answer_text: The new Mental Health facility located within the grounds of Carrickfergus Health Centre will accommodate the Carrickfergus Community Mental Health Team.  The new facility will be known as Archway Mental Health Resource Centre.The building is due to complete in Autumn 2025. At present, there are no known issues/concerns that could have potential to impact on this timeline. This facility, once established, will realise a positive impact on services users who utilise the Community Mental Health Service by ensuring accessible services in their hometown, whilst maintaining consistency in care provision.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure due to concerns raised regarding the new fencing along the Shore Road, Whiteabbey, to detail (i) what actions her Department has taken to address the concerns; and (ii) whether any consideration has been given to remove the mesh wire.
answer_text: I am aware of the concerns raised and whilst it is regrettable that the view of the lough has been restricted, my Department’s primary concern is road safety; therefore, a parapet must be provided which meets the required standards.Wire mesh is also a requirement, to prevent climbing, as prescribed within design standard ‘CD377 - Requirements for road restraint systems’.  Whilst its complete removal is not permitted under this standard, my Department is considering the removal of the wire mesh between the two uppermost rails.  This would ensure that the views to the lough are opened up whilst still providing the required safety intervention for cyclists.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, given the recent guidelines from the American College of Obstetricians and Gynecologists in relation to Intrauterine Device (IUD) insertion pain and appropriate pain management, whether his Department has plans to review and update its current guidelines for IUD procedures in line with these guidelines.
answer_text: Clinicians within the Northern Ireland Health and Social Care system follow IUD insertion pain and appropriate pain management guidance produced and promoted by the Royal College of Obstetricians and Gynaecologists (RCOG) and the Faculty of Sexual and Reproductive Health (FSRH).My Department is not responsible for issuing guidelines in relation to IUD insertion pain and appropriate pain management.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail whether her Department has any plans to improve traffic calming and road safety measures for school children from the junction at Circular Road, through Whiteabbey Village to the traffic lights at A2 Shore Road.
answer_text: My Department currently has no plans to improve traffic calming and road safety measures for school children or other pedestrians from the junction at Circular Road, through Whiteabbey Village to the traffic lights at A2 Shore Road.The road between Circular Road and the A2 Shore Road is subject to a 30mph speed limit, and has an excellent safety record with no recorded collisions in the last 3 years.I would however like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how the development of day opportunities is aligned with skills gaps to ensure that individuals with special educational needs and disabilities are supported in accessing employment opportunities.
answer_text: The specific alignment of day opportunities with skills gaps to support individuals with special educational needs and disabilities (SEN/D) in accessing employment, primarily falls under the purview of the Department for the Economy and the Department for Communities. However, I can provide information on how health-related services integrate with these efforts.The Department for the Economy, in collaboration with the Department for Communities, is actively involved in developing strategies and programs that aim to improve employment outcomes for individuals with disabilities, including those with SEN/D. This includes the development of the Disability and Work Strategy, which encompasses various aspects such as supporting individuals not currently active in the labour market, those already in work, and particularly young disabled people transitioning out of educational settings.Furthermore, the Education Authority's 14+ Transitions Service works closely with various bodies and organisations to provide post-16 services for individuals diagnosed with SEN, including day opportunities and skills programs. These services are designed to support pathway planning for each young person leaving education at 16, which indirectly contributes to addressing skills gaps and enhancing employment opportunities.My Department is also finalising the Learning Disability Service Model, which aims to provide a consistent and early-stage support framework for individuals with Learning Disabilities across the region.  In line with this draft model, Day Services have been expanding regionally. The development of Day Opportunities offers a more diverse range of services in local communities, to support individuals with more complex needs to access community-based opportunities. Opportunities include social farming, performing arts and further education and supported employment opportunities from a wider range of community and voluntary organisations. While my Department focuses on health aspects, the integration with employment and skills development is crucial and is supported through multi-departmental collaboration. Collaboration ensures that health services align with educational and employment support to provide comprehensive care and opportunities for individuals with SEN/D.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail how many providers in East Antrim applied to the Pathway Fund in 2024/25.
answer_text: 2024-25 was the second year of a two-year funding cycle with applications submitted to the Pathway Fund in December 2022.  There was not a separate application process for 2024-25 and settings which were successful in 2023-24 continued to be supported in 2024-25.  Four providers within East Antrim applied to the Pathway Fund in the 2023-24/ 2024-25 funding cycle; all four applicants received a funding award.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the variance in the application to become an approved driving instructor compared to that of a light goods vehicle Instructor.
answer_text: The Driver & Vehicle Agency (DVA) regulates and maintains the Register of Approved Driving Instructors (ADIs) on behalf of the Department under the Road Traffic (Northern Ireland) Order 2007.  To become an ADI, an individual must meet the legal requirements and successfully apply for and pass three tests (theory, driving ability and instructional ability) within a two-year timeframe.  Upon successful completion of the tests the candidate must apply to have their name entered onto the ADI Register and AMI Register.  Enhanced security checks are required at the entry point of the qualifying process, and when applying to register or renew an ADI licence.The DVA does not regulate the Large Goods Vehicle (LGV) instructor sector.  There is no requirement to take formal training or be registered to instruct someone to drive a LGV.  However, there is provision for the supervision of provisional licence holders in the driving of LGVs, provided it is done by a qualified driver, under the Motor Vehicles (Driving Licences) Regulations (Northern Ireland) 1996.  Regulation 11 defines a qualified driver as a person who is: 21 years of age or overholds a relevant driving licence, i.e. a full licence authorising the driving of vehicles of the same class as the vehicle being driven by the provisional licence holder; andhas the relevant driving experience.  A person is deemed to have relevant driving experience if they have held the relevant licence for a continuous period of not less than three years or for periods amounting in aggregate to not less than three years.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: I have now received confirmation that the Dilapidation Bill is within the legislative competence of the Assembly and shall be seeking approval for the introduction of the Bill at the earliest available meeting of the Executive.   Once Executive approval is received, I will seek to have the Bill introduced, with the Speaker’s agreement, as soon as possible.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide an update on the Waste Management Strategy.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Resources & Waste Management Strategy is currently under development, with a view to launching a consultation later this year.A date for publication will be set following the consultation and once responses have been analysed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) how many children in East Antrim have yet to receive a place for pre-school provision; and (ii) how the figures compare to the previous 5 years.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is responsible for the pre-school admissions process.  The process consists of two stages and is designed so that children can receive an offer at either stage. The EA has advised that at the end of Stage one, 34 children (5%) in East Antrim had not yet received the offer of a funded place in a pre-school setting of their parents’ preference. All were invited to remain with the admissions process and provide further preferences.Since the end of Stage one, of these 34 children, 13 have been offered a place in a preferred setting via the recall process and 12 have nominated further preferences in Stage two and will receive the outcome of these on 10 June.  Nine have chosen not to engage further with the admissions process at this time but can reengage at the end of Stage two.  At 13 May 2025, the EA confirmed there were 52 funded places available in the area.The EA has advised that figures are not available by the East Antrim parliamentary constituency area for each of the last five years.   Data for three years as indicated below is available for the Mid & East Antrim Council area.YearCouncil AreaParliamentary ConstituencyNot yet placed as at End Stage One2024/2025 East Antrim62023/2024 East Antrim142022/2023Mid & East Antrim 192021/2022Mid & East Antrim 462020/2021Mid & East Antrim 49

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether autism training being rolled out to Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service staff will include how to adapt mental health interventions, such as cognitive behavioural therapy, for people with autism.
To ask the Minister for Communities when he intends to open the fund for community halls.
answer_text: I have asked my officials to develop a programme to provide funding for small scale capital refurbishment and improvement projects for community facilities.This work is under way, and I plan to make an announcement on a new programme next month.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given that the Consumer Council no longer provides advice on private parking Penalty Charge Notices (PCN), to detail (i) any support currently available for individuals who have received a private parking fine; (ii) whether there are any plans to regulate private companies that issue PCNs; and (iii) whether any consideration has been given to the excessive amounts that some private companies are charging in fines.
answer_text: The Consumer Council continues to provide information on private parking charges on its website, outlining the different options available to consumers who have received a Penalty Charge Notice (PCN). If a consumer has received a PCN due to mitigating circumstances, for example, a blue badge has fallen off the dashboard, then The Consumer Council can provide more detailed advice on the options available and assist them with their appeal if required.It cannot, however, assist with any PCNs issued due to non-mitigating circumstances and consumers in these instances will be directed to its website for advice and information on the options available.Whilst my Department is responsible for on-street parking enforcement it has no responsibility for the regulation of private car parks and currently has no plans to regulate the business practices of private car parking companies. However, any organisation that operates parking on private land and requests keeper details from the DVLA must be approved by an Accredited Trade Association such as the Approved Operator Scheme (AOS) managed by the British Parking Association (BPA). Members of this scheme must adhere to the BPA AOS Code of Practice for the control and enforcement of parking on private land and unregulated public car parks.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether she will consider conducting a survey to assess the feasibility of implementing a 20 mph speed limit zone outside Ballycarry Primary School, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: Officials have advised that Ballycarry Primary School  benefits from Safer Route to Schools signage and there have been no recorded accidents in the last 3 years.  A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and is available on my Department’s internet site https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schools . The review recommends the provision of a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding. The report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work to update our policy document is underway.   I have asked officials to prepare the programme of schools to be treated in the next financial year based on the existing scored listing, but with preference given to rural schools which are located on roads where the national speed limit applies. I would also like to see what can be done to improve attitudes to speeding, not only for road safety purposes, but to make places cleaner and greener.  I have therefore asked my officials to prepare a paper on the use of permanent 20mph speed limits particularly near schools, in predominantly residential areas and places such as town centres where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister for their assessment of any risks associated with the use of Artificial Intelligence as a solution in the public sector.
answer_text: As set out in the PfG we have established an Office of AI & Digital to be led, in the interim, by the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser (CSTA). The CSTA chairs a Task-and-Finish Group comprising over 40 representatives from government departments, industry and academia to scope out the work of this office. Recognising AI’s transformative power and the need for responsible stewardship, the Group will provide leadership, technical insights, and cross-sector coordination across a number of workstreams including governance, infrastructure, data, skills; and Public Sector Transformation. Work has also started on the development of an AI Action Plan to deliver tangible benefits for our economy and society.Potential risks will be addressed across all of the workstreams and will be considered in the AI Action Plan.  The establishment of the Office of AI and Digital aims to provide a central hub and therefore potential risks and opportunities can be shared across Departments.There is significant potential for AI, but it is not a universal solution. A balanced, evidence-based approach is essential to ensure AI is deployed responsibly, with appropriate governance and transparency.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the Rural Business Development Grant Scheme 2025-26 will reopen.
answer_text: My department is considering the implications of the UK Supreme Court judgement and awaits further guidance from the Equality Commission for Northern Ireland which they anticipate will be published in June 2025.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the Belfast Road, Carrickfergus, meets the established criteria for the installation of a fixed speed camera, (i) to detail the reasons preventing the implementation of a fixed camera at this location; and (ii) for an update on the expected timeline for the completion of the Road Safety Partnership’s review of fixed camera sites across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: The policy regarding the management of these cameras is the responsibility of the N IRoad Safety Partnership..Their contact details are:Northern Ireland Road Safety Partnership42 Montgomery RoadBelfast BT6 9LDPhone: 028 9092 2751Email: zNIRSP@psni.police.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any further action his Department can take to improve the availability of the Folate Receptor Autoantibody Test through the NHS for conditions such as cerebral folate deficiency.
answer_text: My Department has a formal link with the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) under which NICE Guidance is reviewed locally for their legal and policy applicability here.  Where found to be applicable, they are endorsed for implementation within Health and Social Care (HSC) organisations.The Folate Receptor Autoantibody Test (FRAT) is not routinely commissioned in Northern Ireland.  As the Department is not aware of any specific endorsement by NICE, there are currently no plans to formally commission the test at this time.It is, however, recommended that individuals consult with a medical professional or specialist in Northern Ireland to discuss testing options and potential access to FRAT testing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education (i) to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that all target-aged children in Carrickfergus, who applied for a pre-school place, receive a suitable placement; (ii) , given that a number of children have not yet been allocated a place, when parents can expect to be advised on the next steps; and (iii) what specific challenges have contributed to this shortfall in provision.
answer_text: The Education Authority Pre-School Education Group (EA PEG) is responsible for ensuring that sufficient pre-school provision is available in all areas to provide a funded pre-school education place for every target age child whose parents want it.The pre-school admissions process has two stages and the EA has advised that at this point, 97% of target aged children whose parents applied for a pre-school place have already received an offer of a funded place in a setting of their preference.The EA has advised that there are currently 2,316 funded places available across Northern Ireland to accommodate the 620 children who have not yet received the offer of a funded place in a setting of their parents’ preference.Within the Mid and East Antrim council area, 650 funded pre-school places were available for 2025/26 with a total of 584 first preference applications received during Stage One of the admissions process.  The EA has advised that 550 target age children received an offer of a funded place on 3 April (not all first preference).  A further 59 children have yet to receive an offer of a place in a preferred setting.  However, the EA has advised that sufficient provision remains within the Mid and East Antrim council area with 104 funded places still available. Parents whose children have not yet received the offer of a funded place in one of their preferred settings have already received advice on the next steps and have been invited to submit further preferences from Monday 7 April.  A list of providers with funded places still available has been posted on the EA website.I would encourage parents to engage with the admissions process to the end and provide further preferences when invited to do so in order to maximise the chances of their child being offered a place in one of their preferred settings.  Should additional funded places be required in local areas, the EA will adjust allocations to settings where necessary. Parents will be advised of Stage Two application outcomes on 10th June.I have written to the EA to request a review of the PAN allocation process, so that opportunities for improvement can be identified and explored.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what work has been undertaken to identify the opportunities and risks from Artificial Intelligence, particularly for the public sector.
answer_text: As set out in Programme for Government (PfG) we have tasked the Chief Scientific and Technology Adviser with chairing a Task-and-Finish Group to scope out the role of an AI and Digital Office. The group will include representatives from government departments, industry and academia. This will involve consideration of a number of areas with a focus on public sector reform; governance; skills; data and infrastructure.A significant part of the work will look at how we integrate and showcase the expertise we already have, and how we progress the areas that need to be developed. The Group will identify the opportunities and risks and how these can be successfully managed by the NICS and the wider public sector through the creation of an AI Workplan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15954/22-27, to detail any further work undertaken in relation to the implementation of the Graduated Driver Licensing Monitoring Report recommendations.
answer_text: As indicated in the response to AQW 15954/22-27, the Graduated Driver Licensing Monitoring Report does not make recommendations in relation to GDL.  It provides a statistical baseline so that when GDL is introduced its impact and effectiveness can be monitored and assessed.That said, I can advise that my officials have resumed work on the operational planning and subordinate legislation changes that will be required in advance of the implementation of GDL. I should emphasise that this is a major project, entailing very significant amendment of current driver training, testing and post-testing arrangements. Once the preparatory work has progressed sufficiently, I will be able to provide an update on an anticipated date for introduction.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail when letters of offer will be issued to all community transport providers for the 2025-26 financial year, including (i) Rural Community Transport Partnerships; (ii) the Disability Action Transport Scheme; and (iii) Bridge Transport, Londonderry.
answer_text: I will consider responses to the ongoing Budget Equality Impact Consultation before I make decisions on final budget allocations across my Department. However, in advance of the final budget decision, I have agreed to provide funding for three months to those organisations who applied for grant from the Rural Transport Fund or the Transport Programme for People with Disabilities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether the baby loss certificate scheme will be backdated to allow parents who have lost a baby before the scheme is introduced to apply.
answer_text: ANSWERThe Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill, which will enable my Department to establish a Baby Loss Certificate scheme through regulations, completed its First Stage in the Assembly on 25 March 2025.  This will be the starting point for the Baby Loss Certificate Scheme: details of how the scheme will operate will be set out in secondary legislation, which I also expect to be considered by the Assembly.It is very important to me that the Bably Loss Certificate Scheme meets the needs of families here, and I intend to consult on the question of the backdating of the scheme and a range of other matters.  The General Register Office continues to work with colleagues in the Department of Health to plan for this and the introduction of the Scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the total amount of funding requested by community transport providers to meet their local needs as part of departmental budget submissions for (i) the Rural Community Transport Partnerships; (ii) the Disability Action Transport Scheme; and (iii) Bridge Transport, Londonderry.
answer_text: The Rural Community Transport Partnerships have requested funding from the Rural Transport Fund of £3,070,309 to support delivery of the rural Dial-a-Lift transport scheme in 2025/26.Disability Action has requested funding from the Transport Programme for People with Disabilities of £2,358,337 to support delivery of the urban Disability Action Transport Scheme in 2025/26 including across Derry.Bridge Accessible Transport who delivers the Disability Action Transport Scheme in the Derry area on behalf of Disability Action, is not directly funded by my Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities how voluntary and community sector organisations can access funding through his Department's Voluntary and Community Division and Community Empowerment Division.
answer_text: A number of programmes are currently administered through my Department's Voluntary and Community Division and Community Empowerment Division, including Neighbourhood Renewal; Areas at Risk and Small Pockets of Deprivation programmes; and Voluntary and Community Sector Infrastructure Support programmes. These programmes are currently closed for applications.  Work is ongoing in both Divisions to refresh policies which underpin some of my Department’s support to Voluntary and Community sector organisations including the development of a Voluntary and Community Sector Infrastructure Support Policy Framework. It is anticipated that implementation of this Policy Framework will provide some opportunity for the Sector to apply to support future delivery.  Communication and engagement will continue with Voluntary and Community organisations to inform them of opportunities and to support them through application processes.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any support available for homeowners who experience coastal erosion.
answer_text: Under the existing policy and legislative framework, my Department is not responsible for providing support to homeowners impacted by coastal erosion.The current approach to managing the coast is outlined by the Bateman Formula, 1967. This is essentially a coast protection policy, stating that Departments will protect their own assets and take responsibility for dealing with the impacts and potential impacts of coastal erosion as these impinge on statutory responsibilities.  Where private individuals or organisations are affected by coastal erosion, then responsibility and liability rests with the owner of the property concerned.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to detail the number of Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme applications have not progressed due to the lack of a Medical Factual Report.
answer_text: As of week commencing 31 March 2025, almost 11,000 applications have been submitted to the Scheme.  Of that total number, there are currently 582 applications awaiting a Medical Factual Report (MFR) within the medical assessment space.  There are processes in place to monitor the progress of the return rate of MFRs and to follow up with relevant partners where necessary. Cases can proceed to determination stage in the absence of an MFR with other medical evidence used to complete the assessment.Almost £85m has been paid to eligible applicants since the Scheme opened for applications

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to detail any child protection and safeguarding guidance it provides to MLAs who are hosting work experience students who are under 18 years of age.
answer_text: The Assembly Commission’s Human Resources (HR) Office is currently developing Safeguarding Guidance for Members and this will be published on the Members Portal in the near future. The Guidance will include advice to Members on the management of work placement students.    Should an MLA decide to accept a work placement student, it is their responsibility to ensure the safety and supervision of the student at all times.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail (i) whether Northern Ireland is currently in line with the rest of the United Kingdom in terms of flexible working; (ii) any differences between Northern Ireland and the rest of the United Kingdom on flexible working; and (iii) any plans she has to bring Northern Ireland into line with the rest of the United Kingdom in relation to flexible working.
answer_text: The Employment Relations (Flexible Working) Act 2023, which came into effect on 6 April 2024, introduced a number of changes to the right to request flexible working arrangements in Britain.The recent Good Jobs consultation sought advice on a broad range of policy proposals to inform a future Good Jobs Bill, which I intend to bring through the Assembly in this mandate to ensure the employment law framework remains fit for purpose and operates effectively for both businesses and workers. These policy proposals include flexible working which would enhance the current rights and entitlements for employees by:removing the current 26-week qualifying period before a flexible working request can be made, thereby making this a right available to both new and existing employees;allowing an employee to make two statutory requests in any 12-month period (the current entitlement being one such request); andremoving the requirement that the employee must explain as part of the statutory request what effect the change would have on the employer and how that might be dealt with.These changes would bring rights and entitlements here into line with how they presently operate in Britain.In October 2024, as part of its ‘Make Work Pay’ Agenda, the British Government launched a series of policy proposals, including a Bill, to update the employment rights framework in Britain. This includes proposals to make further changes to statutory flexible working rights. My officials are engaging with counterparts in the Department of Business and Trade regarding the British Employment Rights Bill.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the work of the Independent Autism Reviewer.
answer_text: Following a public appointment process in August 2024 I formally appointed the first Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland. Ema Cubitt took up office on the 2 September 2024.Since her appointment, the Reviewer has undertaken a wide range of early engagements with key stakeholders, including both statutory and non-statutory organisations and bodies, as well as individuals, including autistic people and their families and carers. This included the recent attendance at a joint meeting of the Education and Health Committees to brief members on her role.The Reviewer has also provided responses to calls for evidence in relation to specific committee inquiries and produced a number of reports, as well as working to contribute to the broader public discourse on the care, services, and support provided to autistic people, their carers, and families.With the recent appointment of a Head of Office for the Reviewer, work is underway to further support the establishment of her office and identify key priorities for the year ahead.The work of the Reviewer will be in line with the statutory functions of the role and will be relevant to the responsibilities and remits of Departments across the Northern Ireland Executive. The Reviewer is not subject to the direction or control of my department or any Northern Ireland department.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the mandatory autism training for Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service staff.
answer_text: Department officials are working with colleagues from Middletown Autism Centre and Health and Social Care Leadership Centre to design and provide Health and Social Care staff with three training modules that are research and practice/strengths based. Each module will be delivered in two half hour sessions and will be available through the Health and Social Care Learning platform.  I indicated my expectation that this training will be mandatory for mental health professionals and officials are in dialogue with Trusts to explore what process can be followed to ensure these modules are mandatory Continuous Professional Development training for all exitsing CAMHS staff and a mandatory element of induction training for all new staff. The three modules will improve Health and Social Care staff understanding as they will focus on the following:i.	Autistic differences - insight into how autistic people experience the world and an opportunity to reflect on how an autistic person might experience the service / environment that you work in; ii.	Providing supportive environments - insight into how the environment can impact on the autistic service user.  An analysis of the difficulties experienced by the autistic person across a series of likely environments and interfaces. Supports and materials to provide and create more supportive spaces; andiii.	Promoting a neuro-affirming culture - How to integrate neuro-affirming practices into your work environment; from the language used to the culture in the workplace both for your service users and team members.The first module is on target to be available for launch in April 2025 with the following modules (two and three) delivered by June 2025

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the current waiting time to receive results for a cervical smear, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) any actions his Department can take to improve this.
answer_text: The waiting time for cervical smear laboratory results to issue to GPs is currently up to 12 weeks. All laboratory services for cervical screening in Northern Ireland moved to the Belfast Health and Social Care Trusts from 1 November 2024. As this service is now provided by a single regional laboratory, a breakdown of current waiting times by Health and Social Care Trust is not applicable. The time to receive a result is dependent on the result of the HPV test and whether additional cytology reporting is required. Until recently there were delays in the authorisation of completed test results within the laboratory as the process was manual. An electronic auto-authorisation system was introduced on 12 March 2025 and the Public Health Agency has confirmed that the backlog in the authorisation of HPV negative results in the laboratory has now been cleared. This will markedly improve results ‘turnaround times’ for the majority of women going forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any funding streams available to Health and Social Care Trusts to improve their environments for neurodiverse patients.
answer_text: I am aware of the importance of ensuring neurodiverse patients can access services in an environment that meets their needs. Funding streams for improvements to Trusts environments would be part of an overall funding stream for facilities improvement works or capital redevelopment works rather than patient group specific. Whilst there is not a specific funding stream, I can provide assurance that this is taken into consideration when designing and funding new capital builds through equality impact assessments and service user engagement and when improving existing facilities. HSC Trusts are mindful of the diverse range of service user’s needs, and this is reflected as they continually make improvements to the patient environment.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department has any plans to introduce sensory rooms in Emergency Departments in the Belfast Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: The Belfast HSC Trust has advised that there are currently no plans in development to introduce sensory rooms in Emergency Departments in either the Royal Victoria Hospital or the Mater Hospital.  The Trust have advised however that it is sensitive to the experience of patients who find a highly stimulating environment such as the Emergency Department particularly challenging.  ED staff readily recognise this in their interactions with individual patients and carers and endeavour to make reasonable adjustments to place patients in more suitable areas as operating conditions allow them to.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of children on the waiting list for Education Welfare support in East Antrim; and (ii) any steps he is taking to increase the number of Education Welfare Officers.
answer_text: Office AreaTeamTotal casesBallymena Central (Ballymena/Antrim)88Northern (Coleraine)67Southern (Loughview)108Total263Recruitment in this area occurred on 23rd Jan 2025. The Education Welfare Service offered three posts to successful applicants and all accepted, however, two of the three subsequently declined the offer. The Education Welfare Service is commencing a further recruitment exercise week beginning 17th March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline how any lessons learned from the first year of implementing the Autism Strategy 2023-2028 will inform the next phase.
To ask the Minister of Health to detail any actions his Department will take to increase capacity and access to NHS dental services in East Antrim.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the current provision of NHS dental services in East Antrim.
answer_text: Currently, the demand for Health Service dentistry across Northern Ireland significantly exceeds availability and this is resulting in reduced access, especially for unregistered patients. This is likely to the case for some time.A number of dental access initiatives were announced for the 2024/2025 financial year. These investments aim to bolster support for dental practices and protect public access to Health Service dental care across Northern Ireland, including your constituency of East Antrim.The investment included £1m to fund an Enhanced Child Examination Scheme, £4.3m to fund a 30% enhancement to fees paid to dentists for priority treatments, and £3.9m to fund a Dental Access Scheme (DAS) for the treatment of high priority unregistered patients. The Northern Ireland-wide DAS began on 1 August 2024, aimed at improving access (during the working week) for unregistered patients experiencing an emergency, urgent or pressing dental condition. There are 29 practices participating in the scheme, 6 of which are located in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust area. Currently, DAS is providing access to dental care for around 500 patients each week.In addition, a 6% pay uplift for dentists was backdated to April 2024 and is in line with the recommendation from the Doctors’ and Dentists’ Review Body. This uplift comes alongside a 2.8% increase to non-staffexpenses for 2024/25, representing investment of approximately £6.45m per annum, based on current activity levels.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the Urban Villages Initiative.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any financial support available to homeowners to strengthen coastal defences.
answer_text: Although no financial support is specifically available to homeowners in relation to strengthening coastal defences, my Department’s does manage the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme which remains open to new applications, including those from coastal areas. The grant scheme covers 90% of the costs, up to a maximum of £10,000, of the total survey and estimated installation costs. The homeowner is required to make a contribution of 10% of the survey and estimated installation costs and any additional cost that may be incurred above £10,000. Further information on the Homeowner Flood Protection Grant Scheme is available on my Department’s website.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 20019/22-27, to detail a timescale for publication.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.I have recently supported the establishment of a clinical working group to work with relevant stakeholders across Health and Social Care to develop an implementation plan which outlines an appropriate timescale for rollout. This plan should provide equitable, sustainable and affordable access to this technology, which is targeted to optimise outcomes for those groups most likely to benefit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the introduction of a baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this initiative continue to be taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. Primary legislation is currently being drafted which will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. The Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill will provide us with a power to make regulations to establish such a scheme. The Bill is nearing finalisation, and I look forward to bringing it to the Assembly in the coming weeks.The General Register Office is in regular discussions with the Department of Health (DoH) on the development of the baby loss scheme and the detail of what shape it might take. There are a number of options to be considered, and I intend to consult on the detail of the regulations that will set up the scheme to ensure that it meets the needs and expectations of bereaved families. My officials continue to progress this initiative at pace, and a more detailed schedule will be made available when the legislation has been considered by the Assembly, including the secondary legislation that will set out the scheme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline any plans her Department has to improve the efficiency of the Access NI system to prevent unnecessary delays for job applicants and volunteers.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 13 – AQO 1612/22-27		ANSWERIt is expected that over 180,000 disclosure applications will have been processed by AccessNI during 2024/25, with 82% of disclosure certificates issued within 2 days of receipt.  In this context, I consider that AccessNI is currently offering a strong level of performance.  Work is ongoing to deliver process and system enhancements that will bring further increased service improvements, including:-Firstly, obtaining access to the recently enhanced Police National Database – doing so will deliver an improvement in the quality of case referrals to police forces, which should help speed up police response times.  It is hoped that accessing this database will provide the gateway for portability of AccessNI checks.Secondly, onboarding to NIDA Level of Assurance 2 accounts for all AccessNI applications.  The Northern Ireland Identity Assurance (NIDA) service provides for the verification of identity for those using government services online; andFinally, introducing a requirement for applicants to upload identity documents along with their disclosure application.The last two of these are expected to be in place in spring 2025 and will greatly speed up the pre-liminary checking process that employers and voluntary groups are required to undertake before disclosure applications are submitted to AccessNI.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the amount of funding received by schools from the Minor Works scheme in the last five years, broken down by school.
answer_text: The amount spent on minor works in each of the last five financial years, broken down by school, is set out in a table which I have deposited in the Assembly Library.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps being taken to expand access to nursing degrees for healthcare support workers who wish to progress in their careers while remaining in the workforce.
answer_text: The Department commissions pre-registration training places from the Open University, which enables Healthcare Support Workers employed within the HSC Trusts, who wish to pursue a career within nursing and complete their nursing degree, whilst remaining in the workforce. This pathway for career development is promoted widely throughout the Trusts via staff engagement events.The Chief Nursing Officer has commissioned a “Widening Access to Nursing, Midwifery and Allied Health Professions” project.  The project aims to identify, explore and appraise current access routes and widening access initiatives available within Northern Ireland, and to identify any gaps in current provision for those aspiring to pursue a career as a nurse, midwife or AHP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQO 1190/22-27, (i) for a breakdown of the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme budget in the last five years; and (ii) to detail any cross-departmental mitigation measures put in place to ensure the protection of rural dwellers' transport needs.
answer_text: The Assisted Rural Travel Scheme (ARTS) provides discounted travel to SmartPass holders using the Dial-a-Lift (DAL) rural transport service, which is funded by my Department. As such, it is demand-led, and payments are made to Rural Community Transport Partnerships (RCTPs) to cover the fares foregone when members use their SmartPass for travel. The payments made to RCTPs as reimbursement for fares foregone over the last five years are as follows.2019/202020/212021/222022/232023/24£709,672£720,707£601,497£589,389£581,388ARTS is a good example of a cross-departmental initiative designed to address rural dwellers’ transport needs. The Scheme has previously been funded by Department of Agriculture, Environment, and Rural Affairs (DAERA) but administered through my Department and delivered through the DAL service.My predecessor initiated a cross-departmental working group to explore options to best meet the needs of users and the potential for community transport to support the work and objectives of other departments.  My officials continue to engage with colleagues in Department of Health and DAERA to explore potential options for community transport to support other departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme funding, transferred to her Department, will be safeguarded and ring-fenced within her Department's baseline budget from 2025-26.
answer_text: I am aware that the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme funding is due to be baselined in my Department’s budget from 2025-26, and my officials continue to engage with the Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs on this. I have not yet taken any decisions on the draft 2025-26 budget outcome for my Department, however I fully understand the importance of community transport to those who rely on these services to access goods and services, retain a sense of independence, and remain connected to their communities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) whether the rationale for the decision to transfer the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme budget from the Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to her Department, ahead of the conclusion of the Strategic Review of the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Programme Framework; and (ii) whether her Department engaged with the Rural Community Transport Partnerships to discuss the rationale and implications of the budget transfer, given the impact on vulnerable people who rely on the service for access to essential services.
answer_text: I understand the importance of community transport to those who rely on these services to access goods and services, retain a sense of independence, and remain connected to their communities.The Assisted Rural Travel Scheme has been funded through a budget transfer between Department for Agriculture, Environment, and Rural Affairs to my Department since the Scheme was initiated in December 2009. In previous years this has been facilitated through a technical transfer of budget as part of the in-year monitoring round. This year, the intention is to facilitate a baseline transfer of £560k as part of the opening Budget 2025/26 outcome to streamline this process. This is a largely procedural decision which will, in itself, have no impact on ARTS and, as such, there was no engagement with any external parties.The Strategic Review of the Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation Programme Framework is a matter for Minister Muir and played no part in this procedural decision.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether the decision to transfer the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme (ARTS) budget was subject to (a) an Equality Impact Assessment; and (b) a Rural Needs Assessment; and (ii) whether this decision marks the end of the ARTS Programme within his Department.
answer_text: The Department for Infrastructure (DfI) as the policy lead department for rural transport provides grant aid from the Rural Transport Fund budget to the 11 Rural Community Transport Partnerships across rural Northern Ireland to deliver the Dial-a-Lift Scheme.Through the TRPSI Framework, DAERA has worked with the Department for Infrastructure to develop the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme (ARTS) which permits rural dwellers entitled to a SmartPass to use it on rural community transport vehicles. This intervention brought equivalency to services that urban dwellers were able to avail off and provided free or half fare travel on trips provided through the Dial-a-Lift scheme. Since the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme was introduced in 2011-2012 my Department has provided funding of just over £8m to the Department for Infrastructure to support its delivery. In recent years, my Department has provided £560k annually to DfI. To streamline this funding process, it is intended that the £560k will be transferred to DfI as part of the opening 2025/2026 budget outcome and included in that Department’s baseline for future years.
