As the same amount of funding is still due to go to the Assisted Rural Travel Scheme, there is not a requirement for DAERA to undertake an Equality Impact Assessment nor a Rural Needs Impact Assessment.My officials continue to meet with their counterparts in the Department for Infrastructure to ensure the needs of rural dwellers are adequately considered. A Working Group has been established which includes officials from the Department for Infrastructure, the Department of Health and my Department.   The Working Group, which has met twice to date, is considering whether transport in the community can provide a more cost effective, value for money service delivery model to meet the needs of rural users. It is also investigating the potential for community transport providers to be part of a collaborative group of transport options and pathways that can be accessed by the population.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on day opportunity contracts for people with a learning disability.
answer_text: Health and Social Care Trusts are responsible for the delivery of Day Opportunities. Day opportunities support adults with Learning Disabilities to engage in vocational opportunities and day activities in their local communities. My Department currently spends £8.25 million on Day Opportunities contracts and Trust commissioned services, benefiting 2,402 adults with Learning Disabilities each year.Health and Social Care Trusts either deliver Day Opportunities from within their own services or procure them from voluntary and community sector providers. For services commissioned to the community and voluntary sector, contracts are established between the providers and the Trust and are specific to the service required, varying in length and cost. Contracts are not managed collectively for the region for Day Opportunities as each opportunity is unique. Health and Social care Trusts will issue contractual opportunities in keeping with the needs of their population and within funding envelopes available. Contracts are managed in accordance with salient procurement processes and legislative requirements.Day services have been expanding regionally, offering a more diverse range of services in local communities. Opportunities include social farming, further education and supported employment. A recent example is within the Northern Trust where a new performing arts contract and a new social farming contract have been put in place to widen opportunities for service users.I recognise the importance of providing high quality Day Opportunities, and I am assured that is an area of focus within the draft Learning Disability Service Model. It is envisaged that the Learning Disability Service Model will result in further expansion of Day Opportunities and associated contractual opportunities.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans her Department has in place to support and promote locally-owned hospitality businesses to maintain Northern Ireland's unique identity.
answer_text: I recognise that our hospitality industry is not only an important part of our economy, providing significant employment and growth, but it is also a big part of our unique identity. Our hospitality is world renowned, and a leading factor in our tourism offering.		Both I, as Finance Minister, and my predecessor as Economy Minister, Conor Murphy, recently met with Hospitality Ulster to discuss the challenges the industry is facing. And in only my second day in my role as Minister for the Economy I had a constructive meeting with Belfast’s night-czar, Michael Stewart, where we discussed the opportunities and challenges of the night-time economy including for the hospitality industry.		The Autumn Budget announcement raised employers’ national insurance contributions and the level of national minimum and living wage. These all represent increased costs for hospitality businesses. These are reserved matters and hence are outside the control of the Executive. 		Calls have been made for us to use our rating system to compensate for some of the budget changes. However, it is not sustainable for us to offset the wider ramifications of decisions made in London on significant reserved taxation decisions through the local rating system. 		Our challenging financial position as an Executive makes any direct financial intervention to support businesses very unlikely.		When Minister of Finance, I engaged with the hospitality sector on the issue of VAT and made continued representations to the Treasury on the subject. I also commissioned research to better understand the cost of doing business that will look at the various challenges such as Employer National Insurance Contributions and increases to the national minimum and living wages, as well as other contributing factors.		The research is progressing and will go a long way to help us understand the impact on our hospitality businesses. The research is due to be completed by March 2025. It will help build the evidence base for making the case for the Treasury to act.		My colleague, the Minister of Finance, plans to highlight the adverse impacts of the taxation system with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the next Finance Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) later this month. I will work to support him and advocate alongside him where possible. We will continue to engage with the sector to help us in making persuasive representations to Treasury. 		There are several ways the Executive already provides support to the hospitality sector. Land and Property Services within the Department of Finance offer a wide range of rate support schemes worth a total of £275m to support business ratepayers, alongside offering alternative payment options to assist those with rating debt.		Support is also offered through the Small Business Rate Relief scheme, which benefits 65% of the local hospitality industry.		My Department, alongside Tourism NI, already provides a range of supports to hospitality and tourism busi

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of the number of independent hospitality businesses closing down, to detail any measures her Department is taking to support family-run businesses that are struggling against rising costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether her Department can provide targeted grants or financial relief for small hospitality businesses facing high energy costs and inflation-driven supplier price increases.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any consideration given to providing business rate relief, or other financial support, to small hospitality businesses struggling with increased costs due to a rise in National Insurance and the National Living Wage.
answer_text: I recognise that our hospitality industry is not only an important part of our economy, providing significant employment and growth, but it is also a big part of our unique identity. Our hospitality is world renowned, and a leading factor in our tourism offering.		Both I, as Finance Minister, and my predecessor as Economy Minister, Conor Murphy, recently met with Hospitality Ulster to discuss the challenges the industry is facing. And in only my second day in my role as Minister for the Economy I had a constructive meeting with Belfast’s night-czar, Michael Stewart, where we discussed the opportunities and challenges of the night-time economy including for the hospitality industry.		The Autumn Budget announcement raised employers’ national insurance contributions and the level of national minimum and living wage. These all represent increased costs for hospitality businesses. These are reserved matters and hence are outside the control of the Executive. 		Calls have been made for us to use our rating system to compensate for some of the budget changes. However, it is not sustainable for us to offset the wider ramifications of decisions made in London on significant reserved taxation decisions through the local rating system. 		Our challenging financial position as an Executive makes any direct financial intervention to support businesses very unlikely.		When Minister of Finance, I engaged with the hospitality sector on the issue of VAT and made continued representations to the Treasury on the subject. I also commissioned research to better understand the cost of doing business that will look at the various challenges such as Employer National Insurance Contributions and increases to the national minimum and living wages, as well as other contributing factors.		The research is progressing and will go a long way to help us understand the impact on our hospitality businesses. The research is due to be completed by March 2025. It will help build the evidence base for making the case for the Treasury to act.		My colleague, the Minister of Finance, plans to highlight the adverse impacts of the taxation system with the Chief Secretary to the Treasury at the next Finance Interministerial Standing Committee (F:ISC) later this month. I will work to support him and advocate alongside him where possible. We will continue to engage with the sector to help us in making persuasive representations to Treasury. 		There are several ways the Executive already provides support to the hospitality sector. Land and Property Services within the Department of Finance offer a wide range of rate support schemes worth a total of £275m to support business ratepayers, alongside offering alternative payment options to assist those with rating debt.		Support is also offered through the Small Business Rate Relief scheme, which benefits 65% of the local hospitality industry.		My Department, alongside Tourism NI, already provides a range of supports to hospitality and tourism busi

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister, following their statement to the Assembly on 27 January 2025, to detail any steps being taken to provide financial support to homeowners and businesses that have been denied insurance payouts for damage caused by Storm Éowyn.
answer_text: The Executive agreed at its meeting on 6 February to allocate £17.4m help departments deal with the costs associated with the impacts of Storm Éowyn.Compensation schemes, including the provision of goodwill payments, would be a matter for individual Executive Departments and relevant agencies to consider based on their remits and we will continue to keep this under review with the Executive Committee in the aftermath of Storm Éowyn.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to outline how tertiary education attainment rates in East Antrim compares to other constituencies.

To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 12966/22-27, to detail (i) when the multi-disciplinary steering group was established by his Department; and (ii) any progress made in addressing Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder as a result.
answer_text: i) Due to unprecedented demands as a result of planning for and responding to severe winter pressures and other crucial clinical commitments the Child Health Partnership has not had the opportunity to convene this important steering group.  The Partnership is meeting next week where this will be discussed by senior clinicians and an approach agreed. ii) Avoidant Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) will form part of a wider review of clinical pathways that are discussed and agreed by the Partnership.  This will be discussed at a meeting of the Partnership in the coming weeks where the clinical leads will decide, based on the range of clinical priorities facing the service, when this review will take place.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on when the National Joint Council pay award 2024-25 will be made.
answer_text: My Department has now approved the Housing Executive’s pay remits for 2024-25 and I understand the Housing Executive is moving to implement these with a likely date for pay of March.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment of the administration of the drug Librela to canines; and (ii) whether he is aware of any issues or severe side effects in canines who are administered this drug.
answer_text: Responsibility for matters relating to pharmacovigilance of veterinary medicines sits under the direction and control of the Secretary of State. The Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD), an agency of the Department of Environment, Food and Rural Affairs, is the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland. Queries related to the administration and safety of Librela or any other veterinary medicine should be directed to VMD.   Further information on the work of VMD is available at: https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/veterinary-medicines-directorate
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department oversees and approves the drugs that can be administered by veterinary practices.
answer_text: My Department does not oversee and approve the drugs that can be administered by veterinary practices.The Veterinary Medicines Directorate (VMD) are the Competent Authority for veterinary medicines in Northern Ireland.  VMD promote animal health and welfare by assuring the safety, quality and efficacy of veterinary medicines.  Further information on VMD is available at: https://www.gov.uk/government/organisations/veterinary-medicines-directorate/about
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the number of special educational needs occupational therapists that operate in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: If a child is assessed as having special educational needs, they may be involved with different specialists. An Occupational Therapist may be involved in terms of assessment, treatment and rehabilitation to children and young people with physical, co-ordination and processing problems.The Northern HSC Trust has the following SEN staffing establishment:Band 7: 2.3 Whole Time Equivalent (WTE)  in postBand 7 0.5 WTE Vacancy – has been advertised a number of times unsuccessfully Total Band 7 if fully staffed: 2.8WTEBand 6: 2.4WTE Band 5: 0.67 WTE in postBand 5 0.67 Vacancy – awaiting advertisement HSCNI as no match on regional wait listTotal Band 5 if fully staffed 1.34 WTE

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the recent storm and the disruption it has caused, to detail (i) any measures in place to support carers who require time off work to address emergency circumstances, including caring for dependents or loved ones; (ii) his assessment of whether the current special leave policy adequately accommodates such situations; and (iii) whether any adjustments or additional support is being considered to assist carers facing increased challenges during extreme weather events.
answer_text: Many of the issues faced by carers are multi factorial and cross sectorial and extend beyond the scope of my Department, including carers leave and special leave. Great Britain (GB) introduced Carers Leave Regulations on 6 April 2024.  Employees are entitled to one week’s unpaid leave per year if they provide or arrange care for someone with a long-term care need.  As employment law is a devolved matter any similar legislation for NI would require assembly consent.The Department for the Economy launched a consultation on the Good Jobs Employment Rights Bill in 2024 which included proposal for Carers rights to take leave.  My officials established a Cross Departmental Senior Officials Group to bring departments together to build recognition and strengthen cross government approaches for Carers issues.Staff across the Health and Social Care System are doing everything in their power to maintain services and community visits to support vulnerable people and their carers during this difficult time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice, pursuant to AQW 21048 22-27, (i) whether there is an official appeals process for organisations that are unsuccessful in applying for membership of the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue register; and (ii) whether her Department plans to periodically reassess service gaps in search and rescue operations.
answer_text: There is an appeals process available to those applicant organisations who have been unsuccessful in seeking NISAR Practitioners Group membership. This information is detailed on the supporting documentation at the back of the application form. Furthermore, when formally advising an applicant organisation if they have been unsuccessful, they are again made aware of the appeal process available to them.The NISAR Arrangements, through meetings of the NISAR Policy and Practitioners Groups will discuss, review, and assess the adequacy of services to ensure the coverage remains fit for purpose.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to provide a detailed timeframe for the introduction of the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: As I confirmed to the Member in my answer to AQW 20663/22-27, the process of obtaining confirmation that the Dilapidation Bill is within the legislative competence of the Assembly is taking longer than had originally been anticipated.  		The next stage will be to seek approval for the introduction of the Bill at the earliest available meeting of the Executive.   		Once Executive approval is received I will seek to have the Bill introduced, with the Speaker’s agreement, as soon as possible. 	Beyond that I am obviously unable to give definitive timescales for any subsequent stages of the Bill’s passage through the Assembly as this will be at the discretion of the Assembly Business Office.		However, I remain confident that the Bill will achieve Royal Assent later in the autumn.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the reasons why K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland were unsuccessful in their application to join the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue register.
answer_text: The decision to decline the application from K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland for membership in the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue Practitioners Group was based on the agreed criteria set out in the application process.  Based on this process, including an assessment of current service provisions, it was determined that there are already established canine search and rescue services in place, and no service gap in this area was identified that would necessitate additional resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland's unsuccessful application to join the Northern Ireland Search and Rescue register.
answer_text: I am satisfied that the decision regarding K9 Search and Rescue Northern Ireland’s application was made in accordance with the correct and established process based on an assessment against the agreed criteria outlined in the application process.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether there are any international best practices that his Department is considering adopting to enhance road safety for older drivers.
answer_text: You will be aware I launched the Road Safety Strategy to 2030 in September 2024 and the supporting 2024/25 Action Plan. The strategy is founded on the internationally recognised ‘Safe System’ principles and a review of international best practice was carried out during the development and consultation stages.  The Action Plan contains 10 Strategic Interventions and 35 in-year Actions.  The Strategic Interventions are identified as those actions that will have the biggest impact on Road Safety, longer term, and include a review of drink driving and drug driving legislation.  The in-year actions are led by my Department and Road Safety Partners including the PSNI, Fire and Rescue Service, Ambulance Service, DAERA and DoJ.  The Road Safety Strategy aims to improve road safety for everyone; while there are no specific actions regarding older people, the actions target road users in general, including older people, and in some cases vulnerable road users specifically, for example, ‘Share the Road to Zero’.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on the part-time 20mph speed limit outside schools.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been completed and is available on the Department’s website:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/review-part-time-20mph-speed-limits-schoolsThe review recommends that the Department should provide a third tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools, however this will be more modest in size and is dependent on the availability of funding.   The review report also concludes that a more targeted approach is needed to ensure that the correct safety intervention is selected and work updating our policy document is underway.  Funding for progressing the next tranche of part-time 20 mph speed limits at schools will be included within budget planning considerations for next financial year.During my time in office I would also like to see what else can be done to improve our attitudes to speeding,  not only for road safety purposes, but also to make places cleaner and greener and so as part of my consideration of the recently published review I have asked my officials to prepare a paper on 20mph speed limits in general. This will focus on schools, residential areas and places where there is a higher number of those walking, wheeling and cycling.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure older individuals have access to safe, reliable and affordable public transport if they choose to stop driving.
answer_text: My Department provides the free travel scheme for those aged over 60 which is administered by Translink. In addition, my Department also provide financial support to demand responsive transport services in both rural and urban areas.  These services, which aim to address social isolation by offering accessible and affordable transport, are available to those who have few transport options or cannot use mainstream public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his Department's assessment of (i) whether current legislation adequately addresses the safety needs of older drivers; and (ii) whether there is scope for further improvement.
answer_text: Under current legislation, drivers must renew their licences at the age of 70 and every three years thereafter. As part of that process, they are required to declare any medical condition or disability that could affect their ability to drive, including dementia, insulin treated diabetes, epilepsy and neurological or vision issues. Where a person indicates that they are suffering from a medical condition which may impair their ability to safely drive a motor vehicle, the Department may carry out medical investigations to assess fitness to drive.  These medical investigations may include, where appropriate, on the road driving assessments and/or cognitive function tests, undertaken by Disability Action on behalf of the Department. It is important to note that the requirement is on all drivers, and not just the elderly to report medical conditions that may impair their driving.Although there is no statutory requirement for older drivers, or indeed any driver, to have their practical driving skills re-assessed, if an individual driver is anxious or concerned about their driving ability or their confidence in dealing with today's traffic volumes and speeds, they have the option to take a driving assessment / course.  This would assess their driving ability and help address any concerns or anxieties they may have regarding their driving. I am supportive in principle of any initiatives that have the potential to improve driving standards and road safety for all road users.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any (i) tailored campaigns aimed at raising awareness among older drivers about when it might be time to stop driving; and (ii) guidelines or tools for families to help assess whether it is still safe for an older person to drive.
answer_text: (i) There are currently no tailored campaigns aimed at raising awareness among older drivers about when it might be time to stop driving, however advice and guidance is available on NiDirect. Research demonstrates that the main causation factors for collisions on our roads are: inattention/careless driving, drink driving or drug driving and speeding. Within the limited available budget, campaigns are targeted to address these causation factors and to protect vulnerable road users such as children, pedestrians and motorcyclists, as well as promoting lifesaving seatbelt wearing. (ii) DVA makes information on driving licences available on the NI Direct motoring pages. This includes advice to older drivers at https://www.nidirect.gov.uk/articles/older-drivers-deciding-when-stop-driving.  There is no age limit to driving, but drivers aged 70 years and over are required to renew their licences every three years. This provides DVA with the opportunity to assess older drivers' fitness to drive more frequently. To offset the inconvenience of the more frequent renewals, licences for persons aged 70 years and over are issued free of charge.As part of the renewal process, DVA will conduct medical investigations, which may include specialist driving assessments, if it appears that the licence applicant is suffering from a medical condition which could impair the person’s ability to drive safely.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any steps his Department is taking to ensure that public transport infrastructure in East Antrim is fully accessible for individuals with autism and other disabilities, including the provision of autism awareness training for public and private transport operators.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and have been advised as follows:Translink are committed to ensuring that services are inclusive and accessible for everyone network wide. Here are some key measures we have in place: Awareness Training: We provide comprehensive training for our staff, via disability representative organisations to increase awareness and understanding from a pan disability approach. This training equips Translink staff, particularly in a passenger facing role, to consider all customer needs and to be supportive. ASD Initiatives: We are supporters of the JAM Card which is a part of operational staff training. JAM stands for Just A Minute and lets front line staff know that someone with this card/lanyard may require more time to make transactions, may potentially have a communication barrier or has a hidden disability. We support Independent Travel Schemes which are programmes of work for many charities and social enterprises specifically for those in their community with a learning disability or ASD. This is where our station/depot staff accommodate site visits and talk to groups with the aim of supporting their independence to travel. We accept requests to talk at events when requested usually to support their Community Inclusion Programmes and increase confidence in parents of children with ASD or a learning disability. We offer one to one support when requested. We work with customers, carers and parents to tailor make a travel plan including planning a journey, ticketing support and exact service detail to trial a journey and gain confidence and comfort in using services. Sensory-Friendly Initiatives: We are continuously working to enhance the travel experience for individuals with ASD by implementing sensory-friendly measures, such as quieter spaces and sensory packs which are currently available at Belfast Grand Central Station. This was in conjunction with Sensory KidsNI who are based in Antrim. Co-production: We value feedback from our passengers and community partners to further improve all aspects of our services. We work closely with the Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC) who are a pans disability organisation specially advising on Transport in NI.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the timescale for the introduction of the Dilapidation Bill.
answer_text: The process of obtaining confirmation from legal advisers that the Bill is within the legislative competence of the Assembly has taken longer than had originally been anticipated.As soon as this has been received, I will bring the Bill to the Executive for approval to have it introduced to the Assembly so that we can scrutinise the legislation and see the benefits for communities of the Assembly passing it.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether his Department is aware that investigative surgeries, such as laparoscopy for suspected endometriosis, are often denied or delayed unless a patient has private healthcare; and (ii) how this disparity between private healthcare and NHS care will be addressed.
answer_text: Trust patients are added to the elective surgical waiting lists when surgery is required. Patients who are added to the waiting list must be clinically and socially ready for admission on the day of the decision to add the patient to the waiting list, i.e. the patient must be “fit, ready, and able” to come in for surgery. Waiting lists are managed according to clinical priority. Patients with the same clinical need will be treated in chronological order, starting with the patients who have waited the longest being seen first. (i) Patients who require investigative surgeries, such as laparoscopy for suspected endometriosis are added to elective waiting lists and managed accordingly therefore they are not denied or delayed treatment if they do not have private healthcare.  (ii) The Department of Health is unable to comment specifically regarding private healthcare providers, however work is continuing across all services within the HSC to ensure that capacity is being maximised in an aim to meet demands.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any steps his Department is taking to address delays and barriers women face in receiving timely and accurate diagnoses for conditions such as polycyctic ovary syndrome and endometriosis.
answer_text: Polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) is a common condition that affects how a woman's ovaries work, and the journey to diagnosis usually starts with the GP, who will perform a number of checks and tests, then if required the woman will be referred for a transvaginal ultrasound scan in hospital outpatients.At this point the treatment options would be correction of abnormal thyroid function with thyroxine, a reduction in BMI (sometimes using metformin) or treatment of diabetes and also consideration of hormone therapy ie. mini pill or some form of contraception, if appropriate.This journey for diagnosis is necessary to ensure that the woman is treated appropriately for her condition and symptoms.Patients who require investigative or treatment surgeries, such as laparoscopy for suspected endometriosis are added to the elective gynaecology waiting list and managed accordingly. Patients who are added to the waiting list must be clinically and socially ready for admission on the day of the decision to add the patient to the waiting list, i.e. the patient must be “fit, ready, and able” to come in for surgery. All waiting lists are managed according to clinical priority. Patients with the same clinical need will be treated in chronological order, starting with the patients who have waited the longest being seen first. As you may be aware, my Department commissioned the Getting It Right First Time (GIRFT) team to undertake an independent, external rapid review of gynaecology services in NI. The GIRFT report, Gynaecology across Northern Ireland, was published on 31 January 2024. It identifies 20 regional recommendations, designed to improve patient care and tackle waiting times for Gynaecology conditions.The Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust, PHA and GP representation, to oversee the ongoing service improvement and the implementation at a local and regional level. This includes considering the clinical pathways of women for assessment, diagnosis, treatment, and ongoing management for conditions such as PCOS and Endometriosis as well as overseeing the wider implementation of the GIRFT gynaecology report.It should be noted that the current financial position remains very challenging and as such not all of the service improvement officials identify may be able to proceed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department is aware of any case where a woman has been denied treatment for polycystic ovary syndrome or endometriosis due to their (i) weight; and (ii) marital status.
answer_text: I can advise that marital status would not be a reason for denying a woman treatment for polycystic ovary syndrome or endometriosis.  I am not aware of any woman being denied treatment on that basis.I am advised that on exceptional cases an anaesthetist can advise against undertaking surgery on a patient presenting with a High BMI due to anaesthetic risk but this discussion would be made at pre-assessment.  This would not apply solely to women presenting with polycystic ovary syndrome or endometriosis but to any surgery, where a High BMI would cause an anaesthetic risk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any work his Department is undertaking to (i) raise public and professional awareness of conditions like polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis; and (ii) provide support networks for affected women.
answer_text: (i)	Women’s health, including services for those suffering from conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, is a priority for me and my Department.In 2023, my Department commissioned the Getting it Right First Time Team (GIRFT) to undertake an independent, external rapid review of gynaecology services in Northern Ireland.  The aim of the GIRFT review was to identify areas where improvements could be made in the extensive waiting lists for gynaecology services, and to ensure that patients are treated as quickly as possible to ensure best possible outcomes.The report, which was published in January 2024, set out recommendations designed to maximise capacity, reduce waiting times, improve patient care and outcomes. A Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, has been established to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level.  Although work is at an early stage of development and will require additional funding, this will include mapping clinical pathways from presentation at GP services into secondary and tertiary care which will benefit both patients and clinicians. (ii)	In addition, my Department recognises that there is a clear need for improved public awareness, information and education on women’s health matters from an early age and across the life course.  As work on a Women’s Health Action Plan develops, we are working with the Public Health Agency to understand how best we can reach specific groups of women and girls to provide better awareness in a way that meets their needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether there are plans to introduce mandatory training for GPs and medical staff to better recognise and manage reproductive health conditions, such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, to ensure that women are not dismissed or misdiagnosed.
answer_text: The GP training program in NI currently delivers a complete training package that covers all aspects of the required GP Training curriculum as laid down by the Royal College of General Practitioners, this includes reproductive health conditions such as polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis.Training is delivered via a range of modalities including teaching sessions on woman’s health issues, group teaching and discussions during the day release programs as well as teaching delivered within GP training practices during the GPST2 and GPST3 years through tutorials, clinical encounter debriefs and case-based discussions; this is in addition to teaching in a hospital setting for those in obstetric and gynaecology placements.In addition to the training provided to GPs, my Department has established a Regional HSC Gynaecology Services Forum, with Trust and PHA representation, to oversee ongoing service improvement and implementation at a local and regional level – this includes considering the recommendations in the Getting it Right First Time report, Gynaecology across Northern Ireland.  GPs are represented on the Forum’s working groups and management of gynae presentations in primary care and the development of appropriate pathways to secondary care, including for those with polycystic ovary syndrome and endometriosis, will be part of this work.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme will continue beyond the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Within the draft Budget which is out for public consultation, I have been able to secure the funding necessary to allow the 15% subsidy as currently provided through the Northern Ireland Childcare Subsidy Scheme to continue into the next financial year. The funding secured in the draft Executive Budget will be finalised when we agree it as an Executive.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what progress has been made in implementing measures to combat violence against women and girls.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s long-term strategy for ensuring that Northern Ireland’s marine and freshwater ecosystems can deliver sustainable benefits for all stakeholders, including recreational anglers, commercial fishers and the wider public.
answer_text: UK is signatory to a number of international agreements on the sustainable management of the environment, many of which underpin our local strategies, policy and legislation in Northern Ireland to support profitable and sustainable fishing industries alongside a healthy and productive marine and aquatic environment.At national level and of direct interest to recreational anglers and commercial fishermen, the Fisheries Act 2020 provides the framework to manage our fisheries. The subsequent Joint Fisheries Statement sets out the policies for achieving national fisheries objectives and provides the basis for UK fisheries policy authorities, including my Department, to develop Fisheries Management Plans to help deliver our ambition for sustainable sea fisheries. These are under development for the Irish Sea and other UK waters.Fishery Management Plans were introduced for Lough Neagh and Lough Erne in May 2023 by the Department to sustainably manage our natural inland fishery resources while adding social and economic value.I have already brought forward Northern Ireland’s first Environmental Improvement Plan, agreed by the Northern Ireland Executive in September 2024, which sets a clear target for recovering and maintaining marine fish stocks to sustainable levels, but also seeks to deliver real improvements in the quality of the environment, including water quality.It is also my intention to bring the draft 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan to the Executive for approval in early 2025 that takes an integrated approach to the protection, improvement and sustainable use of the water environment.  In addition, I am bringing forward a Fisheries Bill which will modernise Northern Ireland’s fisheries policies and further support management of sustainable fisheries.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs whether his Department will commit to a review of the stocking policy at Carrickfergus Dams to ensure the sustainability of angling resources for future generations.
answer_text: A review of the Public Angling Estate (PAE) is currently underway, which will consider stocking policy for the PAE, including the Carrickfergus Dams.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps that will be taken to assess and address the social and recreational impact on East Antrim constituents caused by the non-stocking of Carrickfergus Dams, especially during school breaks and summer holidays.
answer_text: No decision has been made yet on stocking of the Public Angling Estate (PAE), which includes the Carrickfergus Dams, for the 2025 season. My Department continues to work with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a stocking protocol that would be compliant with the requirements of the Wildlife Order (Northern Ireland) 1985. A review of the PAE is also currently underway and social and recreational impacts will be assessed as part of that review.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail his Department’s strategy for addressing the critical depletion of sea angling stocks.
answer_text: The Joint Fisheries Statement (JFS) sets out the policies of the UK’s fisheries policy authorities for achieving or contributing to the achievement of the eight fisheries objectives of the Fisheries Act 2000 and sets out the ambition of the UK to continue delivering world class, sustainable management of fisheries in line with those objectives and how it will do so.In regard to sea angling stocks, the JFS states that the UK fisheries policy authorities will continue to work together, where possible, practicable and appropriate, to ensure recreational sea fishing is environmentally, socially and economically sustainable, and contributes to the achievement of the sustainability objective.  The UK fisheries policy authorities will aim, where possible, to take account of recreational sea fishing in wider fisheries management. This will continue to be underpinned by encouraging data collection on catches, economic impact, and species-specific data across the recreational sea fishing sector, and ensuring recreational interests are part of relevant stakeholder engagement processes. In doing so, this will contribute to the ecosystem and scientific evidence objectives in the Fisheries Act 2020.Aside from the JFS, key information to develop specific conservation management advice on shark, skate and ray species in Northern Ireland waters is being sought through my Department’s Elasmobranch Conservation Strategy.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the measures in place to ensure schools receive timely decisions and clear communication on Unavoidable Minor Works applications.
answer_text: When an Unavoidable Minor Works application is received, a designated officer will be identified who will be the school’s point of contact for all communications relating to the project. Communication is maintained with the school throughout the process, particularly at the key milestones of technical feasibility, business case approval, tender award and commencement of works on site.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the steps being taken to ensure that the condemned Home Economics facilities at Carrickfergus Grammar School are replaced or repaired as a matter of urgency.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that it received a structural engineer’s report on the modular unit housing the Home Economic facilities at Carrickfergus Grammar School on 9 January 2025. The report recommended that the mobile unit is condemned and replaced. The school authority has been informed that, as an interim measure, alternative accommodation requirements will need to be established within the footprint of the main school building.The EA is reviewing options for replacement Home Economics accommodation for the school.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when his Department’s impact assessment of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA 943 will be published.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Therefore, given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by an implementation plan. This will support the integration continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to upskill the workforce as required to support the adoption of diabetes healthcare technology across Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence Hybrid Closed Loop systems guidance states that the support of a trained multidisciplinary team experienced in insulin pumps and continuous glucose monitoring is required.  The upskilling of the workforce will be included in the development of a multi-year regional implementation plan, as clinicians will require time to train and implement service delivery safely and effectively.  This is similar to other nations, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) when he expects the final commissioning arrangements for diabetes healthcare technology to be finalised; and (ii) whether there is a timeline for stakeholders to be consulted on these plans.
answer_text: Following the endorsement of the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence TA943 as applicable to Northern Ireland, my Department has requested work to be undertaken to understand the implications.   Overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements, to both enable and support, are being considered. Funding is required for devices in addition to upskilling and recruiting the workforce needed to offer Hybrid Closed Loop safely, effectively and equitably across the region.  Therefore, given the scale of investment needed, financial requirements are also being considered.  Final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI will be made, supported by a regional implementation plan, however the timeframe is dependent on addition available resource. Stakeholders will be consulted in the development of the implementation plan. This will support the integration of continuous glucose monitoring technologies into diabetes care and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to outline the measures her Department has taken to improve the transparency and scope of information provided on stalking offences, particularly in relation to the time from offence reporting to court disposal.
answer_text: ORAL QUESTION No 9 - AQO 1325/22-27ANSWERMy Department is committed to providing information on the operation of the Protection from Stalking Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, in line with the requirements of Section 20 of the Act.A first report was published on 13 September 2024 and, on 11 December 2024, an Addendum to the first report, outlining additional information on processing times from reporting to court disposal for conviction cases of the stalking and threatening or abusive behaviour offences, was published. My officials continue to explore with justice partners, through the Stalking Implementation Working Group, how the information in future reports might be expanded.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether his Department is aware of the number of single let properties discharging sewage into Belfast Lough.
answer_text: The Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs is responsible for the regulation of wastewater discharges to waterways. My Department does not, therefore, hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the increase in violence against women, to detail any actions his Department has taken to improve access to safe housing for women fleeing violence.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Supporting People Programme helps vulnerable people live independently in the community, including women affected by domestic violence. The programme grant funds the delivery of short-term accommodation-based services, providing support for those in housing need and through short-term Floating Support services for those who remain at home or in a community setting. The aim of support is to help these women with needs specific to their situation make informed choices about their accommodation and enable them to maintain/sustain a home. Support can also include help to access other agencies and services. The SP Budget allocation (2024/25) for ‘women at risk of domestic violence’ short term refuge provision is £3.07m, funding 14 refuges with 138 units of support and £2.14m for Floating Support provision which delivers 9 services with a total of 1003 units of support.  The Housing Executive's Supporting People Strategic Plan and Covid Recovery Plan 2022-2025 considers the existing needs and emerging demands on housing support services.  An action aligned to Domestic Violence, while requiring additional funding and subject to DfC budget confirmation, is to develop new services and enhanced delivery for people at risk of domestic abuse.  Any proposals for services grant funded through the SP programme are required to come through the associated SP commissioning structures.HomelessnessAdditionally, the Housing Executive has engaged with a range of partners, including Women’s Aid, in the development of a Domestic Abuse Action Plan. The Action Plan was launched on Tuesday 10th December and contains a number of actions that will help women and children who are in households experiencing domestic abuse. Of particular importance is a commitment to review existing training for frontline staff and develop and deliver new training courses ranging from basis awareness of domestic abuse to identification of and response to domestic abuse within the workplace, tenant’s homes, Housing Executive hostels and during interviews for customers.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14052/22-27 and AQW 17748/22-27, when he will answer AQW 12965/22-27.
To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the success of the (i) Community Halls Pilot Programme; and (ii) Minor Works Programme, to detail whether there are any plans to reopen these schemes.
answer_text: In the financial year, 2016/17, my Department delivered a Community Halls Minor Works Pilot Programme as one scheme. The original Pilot was open to all types of community halls including Church, Scout, Masonic, Orange, Ancient Order of Hibernians and other not for profit halls and sports clubs who could demonstrate that their hall delivered benefits to the wider community.Previously, following my initial considerations of my budget priorities for the financial year 2025/26, I advised of my intention to reintroduce the Community Hall Scheme.I have asked my officials to consider how such a scheme may be developed and I hope to reach a decision on any potential Community Halls programme in the coming months.

answer_text: The list of schools from the East Antrim constituency that have benefitted from the special educational need (SEN) Capital Investment Programme and the associated expenditure is set out in the table below.
