question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to provide further detail on the (i) application process; and (ii) criteria for the Curriculum-led Capital Investment Programme.
answer_text: The application process for both the pilot project to provide outdoor covered sports spaces for primary schools and the programme for synthetic pitches and sports halls in the post-primary sector will be launched by my department in the coming weeks. The criteria for eligibility for funding for both projects will be included within the application process.Details on the application and assessment processes will be provided to schools when the application process is launched.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the status of the Thornfield House new build, including (i) the stage at which stage the development is at; and (ii) a projected timeline for completion.
answer_text: My department approved a business case, costs of circa. £5m, in July 2024 for a school enhancement project (SEP) at Thornfield house.  This was for the delivery of a 7-class extension, sports hall and some internal refurbishment works. In April 2024, as part of my statement on transforming capital investment to supporting children with special educational needs, I set aside the funding thresholds for the 10 SEPs at special schools and asked that the projects be adapted and expanded where possible to meet the longer-term needs of the schools. The Education Authority is therefore currently redesigning the SEP at Thornfield House in conjunction with the school.The current programme anticipates that construction will commence early 2026 with completion in mid-2027.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of pensioner households that are expected to receive the £100 fuel payment in the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: The member is referred to the response to AQW 19245 /22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the number of nursery and primary schools in the East Antrim constituency that will benefit from the announcement on funding for outdoor play equipment; and (ii) the amount of funding each will receive.
answer_text: I am providing £4 million of investment in play equipment and resources for outdoor learning.  Funding will be provided directly to every grant-aided pre-school, nursery and primary school to buy the equipment they need most.This will ensure every one of our youngest learners in Northern Ireland and within East Antrim will benefit from capital funding.The level of funding received will be dependent on pupil numbers and details of the scheme will be provided to schools and settings over coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any initiatives being taken to increase public awareness and community involvement in preserving Belfast Lough.
answer_text: The pressures throughout the Belfast Lough catchment are from wastewater and agriculture. Efforts throughout my Department are focused on reducing pressures on all our waterways, including Belfast Lough. Examples of initiatives being undertaken to increase public awareness and community involvement on our waterways include:The Water Quality Improvement Grant that aims to fund projects to deliver water quality improvements, help connect communities and bring about behavioural change.Partnership working between my Department, Department for Infrastructure, NI Water, Northern Ireland Environment Agency and District Councils to tackle the issue of misconnections by focusing on public awareness and preventative measures.Incentivising the farming community to deliver joint environmental measures, through schemes such as the Environmental Farming Scheme (EFS) and the Soil Nutrient Health Scheme.Live Here Love Here Campaign is a partnership to create more resilient and healthier communities through practical environmental action in their local area. The campaign is funded by my Department and run by Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful in partnership with nine of the eleven councils and the Northern Ireland Housing Executive.My Department also monitors bathing water quality at 26 locations and continually reports results for each site, both online and via our bathing water partners, throughout the bathing season to help inform communities. Four bathing sites are monitored specifically within Belfast Lough: Helen’s Bay, Crawfordsburn, Ballyholme and Groomsport.  Moreover, DAERA hosts a Better Beaches Forum in partnership with the councils, NI Water, Keep Northern Ireland Beautiful and others. The Forum focuses on improving water quality; improving beach cleanliness, facilities management and signage; and keeping the public and media better informed.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any investments being made to promote Belfast Lough as a destination for eco-tourism and recreational activities while protecting its natural beauty.
answer_text: One of the key objectives of my vision for the economy is to reduce carbon emissions and reach Net Zero by 2050. This is not just a legal requirement but a moral obligation to the wellbeing of our future generations.  The development and promotion of Belfast Lough as a tourism destination is challenging given the current poor level of water quality and also its role as a busy seaport.   I have met with Maritime Belfast Trust who are leading on the potential development of a Blueway along the Belfast Waterfront. Tourism NI continue to engage with them on the potential Blueway, which could offer trails running on and alongside Belfast Lough, supporting activities such as canoeing and cycling. Work has begun on a feasibility study to ascertain whether the development of a Blueway is possible and in line with the Blueways Ireland Accreditation criteria. This project will also support the Belfast Waterfront Promenade Framework, launched in December 2023, with an aspiration to balance community, nature and development, to deliver a world class waterfront along parts of Belfast Lough.  Tourism NI provided part funding and expertise to this Framework and they also sit on the Belfast Waterfront Promenade Task Group as a collaborative delivery partner. Tourism NI is also acting as Project Sponsor for the Gobbins Phase 2 City Deal project and is engaging with Mid and East Antrim (MEA) Borough Council on the Carrickfergus Regeneration & Tourism City Deal project, both situated along the shores of Belfast Lough.  Both projects have the ambition to promote best practice and deliver project construction in the most environmentally sensitive manner.  They are targeting excellence ratings for BREEAM (Building Research Establishment Environmental Assessment), which supports solutions to reduce carbon emissions to net zero, improve whole life performance, manage health and social impacts, boost circularity, resilience and biodiversity.The proposed designs for Gobbins Phase 2 will allow for enhanced viewpoints of the local wildlife.  Mid and East Antrim Borough Council have observed that previous installations of catch fences and netting to cliff faces have created additional habitat opportunities for nesting birds and the ambition is to further enhance this, while putting measures in place to prevent coastal erosion. Furthermore, both projects are currently engaging with Department for Infrastructure on active travel and looking at ways to encourage visitors to travel more sustainably using cycle networks, greenways and public transport.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, with the increase of incidents of extreme weather, how the infrastructure is being improved to prevent overflows and flooding in the Belfast Lough area.
answer_text: Through my Department and NI Water, investment continues to be made on improving drainage and wastewater infrastructure to manage flood risk and reduce unsatisfactory sewerage spills.  My Department’s £30m Belfast Tidal Flood Alleviation Scheme is nearing completion.  This involves the construction of approximately 8.5km of flood defences and will help protect the city against tidal flood risk by delivering enhanced protection for over 3,000 homes and businesses.  Through the Living With Water in Belfast Plan, NI Water has delivered sewerage network improvements to reduce the risk of out of sewer flooding, including the £8.5m Ravenhill Avenue Flood Alleviation Project.  NI Water is also currently on-site in the Carrickfergus, Greenisland and Seahill areas investing approximately £7.6m to resolve unsatisfactory spills from 20 combined sewer overflows.I remain committed to the Belfast Plan objectives to help protect against flooding, to enhance the environment and to grow the economy and will continue to make the case for additional funding to expedite its delivery. In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the preschools moving from part-time to full-time provision in the East Antrim constituency in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: As a result of work being undertaken in the current financial year, four pre-school settings in the East Antrim constituency have been approved to move from part-time to full-time provision from September 2025.  A further two are under consideration.  Details are provided below.I have approved (i) Acorn Integrated PS NU; (ii) Corran Integrated PS NU: (iii) Hollybank Pre-School Playgroup; and (iv) Sunnylands NS to transition to deliver the standardised pre-school education session length from September 2025.  Details of all settings approved thus far to transition have been published on the Department of Education website at Minister’s Decisions on Pre-School Settings Requesting Transition from September 2025 | Department of EducationIn addition, Alphabet Playgroup, Greenisland and Banana Bunch Playgroup, Whitehead have been included in Tranche Three of settings that have requested to transition from September 2025.  Consultation on this tranche of settings will close on 13th December and I expect to make decisions on these settings shortly thereafter.It will take a number of years for all pre-schools to transition to the standardised session length. The speed of transition will largely be dependent on the resources available from the Executive at each stage, as well as the circumstances of individual pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail (i) any measures in place to address the increasing levels of pollution in Belfast Lough; and (ii) how his Department is collaborating with local stakeholders to reduce this impact.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Environment Agency (NIEA) regulates sewage discharges to the water environment, including Belfast Lough, through the issue of Water Order Discharge Consents under the Water Order (as amended) 1999. The NIEA is engaged with NI Water on its programme to install storm overflow monitors, known as Event Duration Monitors (EDMs) which will improve monitoring at Combined Sewer Overflows and Emergency Overflows. DAERA also has a statutory obligation under the Urban Waste Water Treatment Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2007 to designate sensitive areas.  These are areas in need of protection through the provision of additional treatment at sewage treatment plants whose discharges impact the relevant waters. I have already indicated that, subject to the required Executive approval, I plan to designate the Inner Belfast Lough Shellfish Water Protected Area as a sensitive area under the 2007 Regulations.  This will assist in improving water quality in Inner Belfast Lough.  I have asked officials to progress this work as quickly as possible.My Department delivers the bathing water monitoring programme with the objective of protecting the public health of bathers.  This covers 4 identified sites in Belfast Lough at Helen’s Bay, Crawfordsburn, Ballyholme and Groomsport and a further candidate site at Brompton in Bangor.  The results of this monitoring are displayed on the NI Direct and DAERA web pages and are used to alert bathing water operators when appropriate.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any specific (i) policy measures; and (ii) funding allocations planned for 2024-25 to address the key challenges highlighted in the Story of Belfast Lough document.
answer_text: The water quality issues highlighted in NI Water’s ‘Story of Belfast Lough’ document have been known for some time and were one of the catalysts for the development of my Department’s Living With Water in Belfast Plan, which was published in November 2021 following Executive endorsement.  The Plan aims to address the increasing demands on the city’s wastewater and drainage network caused by population growth along with the more frequent extreme weather events resulting from climate change. In addition to improving the existing piped drainage systems, the Plan identifies the need for nature-based solutions to also be employed and recommends that policy proposals are brought forward to support implementation of sustainable drainage (SuDS) and natural flood management measures. To support this approach, I am bringing forward primary legislation in the form of a Water, Flooding, and Sustainable Drainage Bill which will include a power to make future regulations on the approval and maintenance of SuDS. In this financial year, I have provided NI Water with c£0.5billion funding and it will determine, through its prioritisation process, how much of this funding is allocated to Living With Water projects.  I will continue to make the case for additional funding for water and wastewater services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of places of worship that have been granted listed status in the last five years, broken down by (i) religious denomination; and (ii) constituency.
answer_text: My Department has a statutory role to list buildings of special architectural or historic interest under Section 80 of the Planning Act (NI) 2011. There have been 8 churches listed from 01/04/2019 to 04/12/2024, breakdown as below:Religious denomination & constituency4 Roman Catholic – 3 in Newry & Armagh, 1 in Belfast South1 Presbyterian  - Upper Bann1 Quaker – Newry & Armagh2 Church of Ireland – 1 in Belfast East, 1 in Belfast South

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will review its funding guidelines to prevent further allocations to bodies in breach of equality legislation.
answer_text: Funding provided to Sponsored Bodies by Departments is governed through adherence to Managing Public Money Northern Ireland (MPMNI), supported by Dear Accounting Officer letters and associated Management Statements and Financial Memoranda or Partnership Agreements.Department for Communities Sponsored Bodies are further subject to Delegated Authority & Approval Arrangements (DAAA). This document provides guidance highlighting when Department of Finance clearance is required. It also provides a supplementary approvals framework within the Department to ensure that all Departmental and ALB expenditure meets the Assembly’s expectations in terms of regularity, propriety and value for money.The DAAA is regularly reviewed and contains reference to financial redress in cases of maladministration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of properties currently listed on the Heritage at Risk Register, broken down by building type; (ii) the number of properties that have been added or removed from the register in each of the last five years; (iii) when the last comprehensive survey of at-risk buildings was carried out; and (iv) the findings of the last survey.
answer_text: On 31 March 2023, the last date for which official statistics were published on this issue, there were 1,037 buildings and structures on the Heritage at Risk Northern Ireland (HARNI) database. 		I have arranged for information on the breakdown by building type, to be placed in the Assembly Library. 		Data on the number of properties added or removed from the list is published in the NI Environmental Statistics Report, and is reproduced below for the five years to March 2023: 2018/192019/202020/212021/222022/23Number of buildings and monuments at risk5276207628921037Number of additions to the list1593142130145Number of buildings and monuments conserved1619101112				Heritage at Risk data is not compiled from a comprehensive survey. However, a statistical survey on the condition of all listed buildings, including those at risk, has just been completed. A previous survey was carried out in 2013/14. 		The findings of the 2013/14 survey are published on the department’s website at https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/baseline-survey-condition-listed-buildings. I hope to be able to release the 2024 condition report soon.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the steps his Department plans to take to prevent incidents involving vehicles colliding with the bridge at the North Road, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that Northern Ireland Rail (NIR) continue to work to prevent bridge strikes through a range of measures. NIR have installed collision protection beams at the bridge at North Road, Carrickfergus in 2009/2010. These protection beams are designed to absorb the vehicle impact while preserving the integrity of the bridge structure. Due to the effectiveness of the protection beams NIR have implemented special Operating Instructions at the North Road Bridge, which allows trains to pass in the event of a strike on the basis that the vehicle is not stuck under the bridge or on fire. These sacrificial beams limit the severity of damage to the structure in the event of a strike and decrease the duration for which North Road, Carrickfergus requires to be closed.In addition, vegetation growing on low bridges and the approaching signage on the railway network, through continual maintenance. Vegetation is removed from the bridge at the North Road, Carrickfergus and the approaching low headroom signage every six months.  Following a Low Headroom Bridge survey in 2022, a package of work on the road network associated with the bridge at the North Road, Carrickfergus was identified. This work includes replacing existing signs and adding additional signage is due to be completed by summer 2025.One of the best ways to ensure the safety of our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury on roads by 2050 is by changing road user behaviour.  If all of us who are drivers do not change our attitudes towards speeding and careless driving, our death toll, which has increased in the past year, will continue to increaseFor any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the number of incidents involving vehicles striking the bridge at the North Road, Carrickfergus, have occurred in each of the last five years.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and the frequency of bridge strikes in each of the last five years at North Road, Carrickfergus is outlined in the table below. 2019 2 2020 0 2021 1 2022 1 2023 1 2024 (to date) 6 For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding that has been invested into boxing in each of the last five years, broken down by constituency.
answer_text: Due to the volume of information to be provided I have arranged for the detail to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of the costs associated with vehicles colliding with the bridge at North Road, Carrickfergus, including; (i) repairs to infrastructure; and (ii) compensation or costs incurred by service providers such as Translink.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Translink.  I have sought the requested information and can confirm that the North Road Bridge in Carrickfergus is protected by independently constructed bridge strike protection beams, fitted to both sides of the structure. These beams are designed to reduce the likelihood of structural damage to the bridge and as a result, bridge strikes do not typically cause structural damage to the bridge at this location. However, on occasion damage to the ‘low bridge’ signage will occur to the extent that it requires replacement by DFI Roads as part of their overall responsibility for road traffic signage. However, in unique circumstances, like the incident that occurred on Saturday 30th November 2024 when a vehicle got stuck / wedged under the bridge, train services had to be suspended, and bus substitutions were required to transfer passengers. The direct cost for the bus substitutions relating to this incident was approximately £2,000 with other associated costs for lost revenue and staff time estimated to have cost £2,800. Translink work along with the PSNI to recover costs directly from offending parties where there is clear evidence of an offence and prosecute offenders when appropriate.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you should contact Translink directly at aqw.request@translink.co.uk.  Making direct contact with Translink will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of limited public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of legal proceedings brought by Monkstown boxer Daryl Clarke, to detail any actions he is taking to ensure the practices and procedures of the Ulster Boxing Council are compliant with the Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.
answer_text: I have sought the advice of the Chief Executive of Sport NI, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI is not a regulator or an arbitrator for the sports sector and has no powers of investigation or enforcement. The Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998 is enforced by the Equality Commission.“Sport NI has issued Promoting Fair Play in Sport (see https://www.sportni.net/wp-content/uploads/2013/09/Promotingfairplayinsport.pdf) in which ‘enforcement of the legislation’ states, “The employment provisions of the anti-discrimination legislation are enforced generally by way of complaint to an Industrial Tribunal, while the Fair Employment Tribunal deals specifically with employment issues relating to religious belief and/or political opinion, or hybrid cases involving either of these.”“Part of the settlement terms in this case are that Ulster Boxing Council agrees to liaise with the Equality Commission of Northern Ireland. It is our understanding that the Equality Commission is to conduct an equality review of governance, practices and procedures applicable to the Ulster Boxing Council to ensure they are compliant with the Fair Employment and Treatment (Northern Ireland) Order 1998.”In addition, I am advised that the Recognition Policy of the Sports Councils provides for review of a National Governing Body’s position in certain circumstances.  My officials have asked Sport NI to consider at the conclusion of the Equality Commission’s review whether the result of Mr Clarke’s case would be a circumstance to initiate a recognition review of the Ulster Boxing Council of the Irish Athletic Boxing Association.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any specific programs aimed at (i) preventing youth offending; and (ii) supporting rehabilitation in the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) is an Executive Agency of my Department and aims to make communities safer by helping children to stop offending. The Agency works with children aged 10-17 years who have offended or are at serious risk of offending.The Agency provides a range of services, often delivered in partnership with others, to help children to address their offending behaviour, divert them from crime, assist their integration into the community and to meet the needs of victims of youth crime.YJA’s Northern Area Team, based in Ballymena, covers the East Antrim constituency and works in partnership with a range of organisations throughout the constituency to prevent youth offending and support rehabilitation.  YJA’s Performance Impact Report 2023-24, published at the end of October, is available on the Department’s web-site and details YJA’s work and its impact.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what actions his Department is taking to address flooding risks in coastal areas of East Antrim.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any steps being taken to (i) address the shortage of community policing officers in the East Antrim constituency; and (ii) ensure visible policing in both urban and rural areas.
answer_text: As a political member of the Policing Board, the Member will be aware that the management of PSNI resources is an operational matter for the Chief Constable, who is directly accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  As I am committed to respecting the operational independence of both organisations, I would recommend that you direct your questions to Chief Constable.However, I must emphasise that my Department has been historically underfunded and I will continue to push for a better financial settlement for my Department, so that PSNI has adequate funding to stabilise and recover numbers back to a sustainable position.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of comments from the Construction Employers' Federation that the current funding model for NI Water is not fit for purpose, to detail any immediate steps his Department will take to secure sustainable and long-term funding for NI Water to address infrastructure needs.
answer_text: My Department has fully funded NI Water for the first three years of the current Price Control, to the level as deemed necessary by the Utility Regulator. During this financial year I have provided NI Water with a budget allocation of almost £0.5 billion, which is just under 40% of the total non-ring-fenced budget available for my entire Department.  Furthermore, from the recent October monitoring round, I have also provided NI Water with £32m, of which £19.5m is to fund targeted action to release wastewater constraints.   Going forward, I am progressing a three-pronged approach in (i) continuing to work with Executive colleagues to try and increase wastewater investment; (ii) reviewing the policy and legislation around developer contributions; and (iii) in securing Executive agreement for the Water, Flooding and Sustainable Drainage Bill to be introduced to the Assembly

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to outline what further support his Department can provide to schools to enhance sporting facilities.
answer_text: I refer to the oral statement I made on 3 December, in which I launched the Curriculum Investment Programme, with Physical Education being the key initial curricular area of focus over the next number of years.My Department will launch an application process for outdoor covered sports spaces for primary schools and for synthetic pitches and sports halls at post-primary schools over the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on her Department's introduction of a baby loss certificate scheme.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.My Department is currently working with the Office of the Legislative Counsel to draft the Deaths, Still-Births and Baby Loss Bill to enable a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme. This primary legislation will be the start of the process to establish a scheme here. We will continue to work with the Department of Health to specify the detail of this important initiative, to make sure that it meets the needs and expectations of the bereaved families, and to scope delivery options. My officials continue to progress this work at pace: A more detailed schedule will be made available when this legislation has been considered by the Assembly, including the secondary regulations which will set out the detail of the scheme

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission whether any consideration has been given to the cost of implementing measures to assist people with a mental or physical disability, within the new draft determination for constituency office expenses.
answer_text: Currently, under the provisions of the Assembly Members (Salaries and Expenses) Determination (Northern Ireland) 2016, Members are able to claim the costs incurred in making adjustments to the constituency office for persons with a disability, where those costs are not covered under the terms of the lease.  The costs may be claimed from either the Constituency Office Operating Expenses or Establishment Expenses categories of expenditure, as long as the usual admissibility criteria are fulfilled.  Members are also able to claim for Disability Expenses, which are those costs wholly and necessarily attributable to a disability a member has.  The new 2024 Determination will extend the current provision to allow Members to also make reasonable adjustments to their Constituency Office, which are not covered under the terms of the lease of that office, to accommodate a disabled member of staff or facilitate access for disabled members of the public.  The nature of the disability is not defined.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail any action her Department is taking to support male victims of domestic abuse.
answer_text: The new Domestic and Sexual Abuse Strategy recognises that domestic and sexual abuse can affect any individual, regardless of age, ethnicity, religion, gender, gender identity, sexual orientation or any form of disability. As such, the strategy is intended to be gender inclusive in that it should not discriminate based on gender, but has been developed to provide support, protection, and resources for everyone who is at risk, encompassing all individuals and needs.		The Strategy’s Support and Provision Pillar is to ensure that victims are supported, feel safe regardless of diversity of need and are guided into services quickly.  The Domestic and Sexual Abuse Helpline is open to all victims.  In addition, eligible victims can also avail of tailored help and support, specific to their needs,  from the domestic and sexual abuse advocacy service, regardless of gender.		My Department has also provided funding to the Commissioner-Designate for Victims of Crime to carry out a mapping exercise of service provision across Northern Ireland for victims of domestic and sexual violence.  The first phase of this exercise will include services available for males.		Furthermore, my Department is exploring with service providers how to reinforce support for males who are victims of domestic abuse. 		I have recently launched a small grant scheme and would hope this will encourage those organisations working with male victims to apply for funding through this.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure when NI Water staff will receive their agreed pay rise for 2023-24.
answer_text: I have approved the 2023/24 Pay Remit for the majority of NI Water staff, and I am seeking additional clarification on the governance arrangements for the consideration of pay awards for the  senior employee staff groups. DoF approval is now being sought on that basis. Should DoF approve the Pay Remit, it will be a matter for NI Water and Trade Unions to agree implementation of the pay rise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy how his Department will support small businesses struggling with rising costs and reduced consumer spending.
answer_text: I recognise the current challenges faced by small businesses, many of which are driven by international factors, and I have set out an ambitious economic plan which will help to improve resilience within the local economy.Invest NI support is tailored to business needs and includes advice, mentoring and finance. Examples of delivery to date include Invest NI interventions to make efficiency savings, address supply chain risks and, depending on business circumstances, provide financial assistance towards supply chain development. Repayable support is available through the NI Small Business Loan Fund, which can lend up to £100k to micro-businesses and SMEs. Support also exists to assist businesses to upskill and reskill staff, develop new business models and enhance leadership capability.To support delivery of my decarbonisation priority, I have also committed £40m to the Energy and Resource Efficiency Programme over the next five years, providing essential support for businesses to decarbonise and reduce costs, while creating economic opportunities in the green economy.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail any steps being taken to ensure that inheritance tax policies do not disproportionately affect farmers.
answer_text: Since the announcement in the Budget regarding the limitation of agricultural property relief and business property relief at a rate of 100% to a combined value of £1m I have been unequivocal in my conversations with the UK Government that the changes have caused great concern in the agricultural community. In the days after the Budget, I raised these concerns with the Secretary of State for Northern Ireland and urged that the matter be reconsidered. I have since met with the DEFRA Minister Daniel Zeichner MP and again emphasised the disproportionate impact that the changes will have on Northern Ireland farmers and referred him to a paper undertaken by my department which provides a high-level analysis of the potential impact of the tax changes for our local farmers. For your information, this paper is available at the NI Assembly Library (https://www.niassembly.gov.uk/assembly-business/research-and-information-service-raise/deposited-papers-2024/).  I have asked UK Government Ministers to engage with the farming community to respond to the concerns being raised and I will continue to do all I can, within my remit to support our farming community on this matter. I am pleased to say that a letter signed by the First Minister, Deputy First Minister, the Finance Minister and myself was sent to the Chancellor of the Exchequer on 18th November 2024 emphasising the disproportionate impact that the introduction of a £1 million threshold for APR will have on farming communities in Northern Ireland and urging her to reconsider this decision.As taxation policy is not devolved, the steps that my Department can undertake in response to this policy change are limited but I will do whatever I can to encourage the UK Government to take into account the concerns of the farming community and to look again at the potential impact and approach taken. In addition to the actions outlined, my Department’s new Farm Support and Development Programme contains a Farming for the Generations Scheme which is currently being rolled out as a pilot. The pilot scheme aims to raise awareness of the need for succession planning on farms supporting farm families through a three phased approach: planning for succession, developing the successor, and supporting the lead generation within the farm family. It will also link farmers without a family successor to new entrants to provide access to land and other resources.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to outline his Department's funding streams for community transport providers.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to outline what action his Department has taken to address the ongoing concerns of carers living in poverty.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether Larne Grammar School will be included in the Safer Routes to School initiative.
answer_text: A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the review report with my officials. I have asked for the report to be shared with the Infrastructure Committee, following which it will be made publicly available.Meanwhile concerns about driver behaviour such as speeding or other inconsiderate behaviour should be directed to the PSNI on their non-emergency 101 telephone number.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 15333/22-27, (i) for an update on the assessment of the area; and (ii) whether he intends to implement a 20mph speed limit at Islandmagee Primary School.
answer_text: At the request of Islandmagee Primary School a crossing survey was completed on Reid’s Road on 8 October this year. The crossing survey produced a score that was well below the minimum required to justify further assessment. A review of the existing signs and lines at the school was carried out in the same month. As a result of this review, new school warning signage and road markings will be provided at the location this financial year.A review of the initiative introducing part-time 20mph speed limits at schools has been undertaken and I recently discussed the review report with my officials.  I have asked for the report to be shared with the Infrastructure Committee, following which it will be made publicly available.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the extent to which he will engage with the community transport sector when developing the disability strategy.
answer_text: Work has begun to ensure that the Disability Strategy reflects the current needs of Deaf and disabled people alongside the Executive’s priorities. I can confirm that as part of this process, further engagement with stakeholders, including representatives from the community transport sector, will take place prior to its finalisation and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the anti-poverty strategy.
answer_text: I remain committed to developing effective and, just as importantly, sustainable solutions to help mitigate impacts, reduce the risk of falling into poverty and support people in exiting poverty through the implementation of a robust Anti-Poverty Strategy.I have written to Ministers of the other Departments and a Cross Departmental Working Group has been established. Senior officials across all Departments are engaging in work to identify key actions that will have a meaningful and lasting impact on socio-economic disadvantage in Northern Ireland.  I intend to present a paper on the Anti-Poverty Strategy to the Executive early in 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to commission a report into the social and economic benefits of marching bands in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: A research report demonstrating the social and economic benefits of marching bands in NI will be a valuable resource for my Department. I have therefore asked my Department’s research unit to progress such a report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to reduce the age for prostate cancer testing.
answer_text: Screening for prostate cancer is currently not recommended by the UK National Screening Committee (UK NSC). They last considered the evidence for screening for prostate cancer in 2020 and concluded that screening was not recommended.  This is because of the poor ability of the prostate specific antigen (PSA) test to predict cancer, which could lead to unnecessary tests that carry risks of potentially life-changing harm. Additionally, some prostate cancers may not produce elevated PSA levels, leading to false-negative results that provide false reassurance. Prostate cancer is rare in men under 50 but the risk increases with age. The UK NSC has commissioned a prostate cancer screening evidence review and cost effectiveness model, and it is expected to review this work during 2025. The UK NSC process will include a public consultation. The UK Health Departments, through the National Institute for Health and Care Research (NIHR), have invested in a significant programme of research to address the evidence gaps in this important area. A clinical trial (called the TRANSFORM trial), which will compare the most promising tests and provide evidence about the best way to screen for prostate cancer, is due to start in early 2025.My department will continue to be guided by the UK NSC’s recommendations on proposals for screening programmes. Anyone with symptoms which they believe may be related to the prostate gland should contact their GP.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the actions his Department has taken to improve collaboration between departments in relation to supporting community transport.
answer_text: I recently met with Minister Nesbitt (DoH) and Minister Muir (DAERA) on 9 September 2024 as part of the Cross Departmental Working Group, to explore what options are there to best meet the needs of users and the potential for community transport to support the work and objectives of other departments.  My officials are continuing to engage with colleagues in DoH and DAERA to explore potential options for community transport to support other departments.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 14052/22-27, when he will answer AQW 12965/22-27.
To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any plans his Department has to increase funding for training and apprenticeship programs tailored to the skills needed in East Antrim’s key industries.
answer_text: The ApprenticeshipsNI and Higher Level Apprenticeship programmes fund the off the job training element of an apprenticeship. The level of funding provided is determined by the specific apprenticeship undertaken and the associated level of qualification. The Department collaborates with employers, sector representatives and curriculum experts to ensure apprenticeships deliver on our skills needs. Both programmes are demand led with a wide range of opportunities available for people of all ages. My Department’s Assured Skills Training delivery is available across all areas and anyone who is eligible can apply. The programme can support growth in smaller companies in regional areas primarily through collaborative academies where a group of companies with vacancies identify common skills needs that can be successfully delivered through the academy model.  I would encourage all employers to make full use of these programmes to meet their local skills needs.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, further to his statement on the Integrated Advice Partnership Fund, to detail the amount of financial support the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council area will receive.
answer_text: I can confirm that the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council Area will receive £90,473.54 from the Integrated Advice Partnership Fund.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware that patients in the East Antrim area cannot access appointments for cervical screening; and (ii) what action his Department will take to ensure appointments will be urgently provided.
answer_text: I am not aware of any particular access issues in regards to patients in East Antrim accessing appointments for cervical screening.  SPPG officials have made contact with all 15 GP practices in the East Antrim area and none have reported any significant issues relating to accessing appointments for cervical screening. A small number of practices have advised availability of some clinics may be reduced to facilitate winter vaccination campaigns. Another practice had reduced capacity for a four-day period in October due to the installation of a laboratory system. However, none have advised that cervical screening clinics were not available.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions his Department will take to address ongoing unsafe parking at the Victoria Rise, Prince Andrew Way and Greenwood Park area, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: My department recently introduced new legislation for double yellow lines along the north side of Prince Andrew Way from its junction with Victoria Rise to its junction with Greenwood Park, along with corner restrictions at the latter junction. These new restrictions will be enforced by Traffic Attendants, who will undertake spot checks in the area.Inconsiderate parking causing obstruction of residents’ entrances, where double yellow lines are not present, should be reported to the PSNI using their 101 number.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline (i) how his Department allocates resources for healthcare in East Antrim relative to other regions; and (ii) what initiatives are being considered to address resource shortages specific to this area.
answer_text: (i)  My Department does not allocate resources to individual regions. In Health our primary goal is to direct available funding to the areas of greatest need and our priority in recent years has been to minimise the impact on front line services. Funding allocations across existing service level baselines in 2024-25 are directed towards those services where funding is required to meet inescapable commitments and those assessed to be under greatest pressure from unmet demand.The allocation process is that a resource budget is issued to Trusts and other ALBs based on their needs rather than to particular subsets of the region. The Trust’s allocations are allocated by the Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group (SPPG) and complemented by a service delivery plan (SDP) setting out the related activities which have been commissioned and are monitored on a monthly basis. Commissioners work with Trusts to ensure that services meet local needs and that the Trusts provide efficient, effective, good quality services as intended by the commissioner within the available funding envelope.(ii)  Through delivering efficiencies and reducing non-essential expenditure the Department is able to deliver more from the funding allocated which helps safeguard the funding of front-line services. As above, funding is directed to the areas of greatest need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 16117/22-27, for an update on when he intends to launch these investment programmes.
answer_text: As advised in my answer to AQW 16117/22-27, it is my intention to launch a series of targeted smaller scale investment programmes to support and enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools. One potential programme will be for sports facilities, and this could provide an opportunity for some post-primary schools to enhance their sports provision.The final details of the investment programme are being developed and I will provide further information in the coming weeks.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he is aware that patients in East Antrim are unable to access appointments for cervical screening; and (ii) to detail any action his Department will take to ensure appointments can be provided urgently.

To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) whether he is aware of two separate incidents of dogs passing away at Blackhead Path beach in recent weeks; and (ii) to detail the action his Department (a) has taken; and (b) will take to ensure no similar incidents occur in the future.
answer_text: I am aware of one incident of a dog death which was reported to my Department on 4 October 2024. I understand that the dog had entered the water on 1 October and had eaten something resembling apple cores. The dog subsequently died on 3 October.  No post-mortem was carried out on the pet.Officials from both NIEA and DAERA’s Marine and Fisheries Division have made site visits to the Blackhead Path to see if plants such as hemlock water dropwort, known to be toxic, are present at the location. No such plants were found.  It is not possible to guarantee that future such incidents will not occur. Dog owners need to remain vigilant to ensure their pets do not ingest unknown material.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he intends to reinstate the provision of an occupational therapist at Rosstulla Special School, Newtownabbey; and (ii) , in the absence of occupational therapist provision in Rosstulla Special School, what provision he will make available to ensure all children's needs are being met.
answer_text: Occupational Therapy services remain in place in Rosstulla, and have not been withdrawn.Universal training has been offered to all teachers, staff and parents within Rostulla school since the beginning of this school year. Northern Health and Social Care Trust operates an open referral system, through which children can be referred for Occupational Therapy.  Referrals are prioritised according to the Regional Prioritisation Guidelines for children with SEN.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of businesses that have applied to the Back in Business scheme in the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: I would refer the Member to the response provided for AQW 16925/22-27.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline any measures that are being taken to ensure there is greater transparency and accountability within the Executive Office.
answer_text: TEO operates under a Corporate Governance Framework that sets out management responsibilities and the actions that the Department takes to ensure that decisions are open and transparent. The Framework covers the principles of good corporate governance and explains the importance placed on policies, plans and review arrangements.  It prescribes the Department's systems of internal control and risk management, along with TEO's internal and external audit arrangements.  It is supported by the Departmental Board's operating framework and by the terms of reference of the Audit and Risk Assurance Committee, and the Major Business Case Committee.  Divisions and arm's-length bodies provide quarterly assurance statements, where senior managers confirm whether the Framework's requirements are being met.  		            The Framework is subject to an annual review to ensure that it is up to date and responsive to emerging issues.As part of its commitment to transparency, TEO publishes departmental board agendas and minutes of board meetings.In line with other NICS departments, TEO is committed to the principles outlined in DoF’s Open Data Strategy for Northern Ireland, and outlines details of relevant data for publication on the Open Data Northern Ireland website. The purpose of all procedures in place and measures being taken is to ensure that stakeholders have confidence in the decision-making processes and actions of TEO in the management of its activities, and to ensure that there is transparency and accountability within the Department.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of applications received for the Musical Instruments for Bands Programme for 2024; and (ii) the current budget for the scheme.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Arts Council of Northern Ireland.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“The Arts Council has received 140 applications to the Musical Instruments for Bands Programme. The current budget for the scheme is £250,000.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Arts Council directly. Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) when Agenda for Change staff will receive a pay rise for 2024-25; and (ii) any action he has taken to seek additional financial allocations to implement this pay rise.

To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of people charged for the offence of disclosing private sexual photographs and film with intent to cause distress under Section 51(1) of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 in each year since 2020, broken down by gender.
answer_text: The Department of Justice does not hold information on the number of people charged for offences by the PSNI.  The recording of information in relation to people charged for offences by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in the question to the PSNI. The Department hold information on numbers of convictions for offences as part of its datasets.  Information on the number of convictions at courts for offences relating to disclosing private sexual photographs and film with intent to cause distress, specified under Section 51(1) of the Justice Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 in the period from 2020 up to the end of 2023, the most recent year for which statistics are available, has been provided. Figures relate to initial disposal at courts.  Appeals are not included.2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at courts where there was a conviction for at least one of the offences specified.3. question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) for an update on the ongoing road works and the associated traffic congestion in (a) Belfast; and (b) East Antrim; (ii) to detail the steps being taken to minimise disruption for commuters and local residents; and (iii) to detail the measures in place to ensure these projects are completed within their scheduled time frame.
answer_text: I acknowledge that traffic congestion in Belfast has been a significant concern for road users over the past number of months.  There have been a number of factors which have contributed to this traffic congestion, including planned and emergency roadworks and also the impact and management of incidents and traffic collisions across the road network.The roadworks that are ongoing throughout Belfast and East Antrim characterise change.  The essential upgrading of our road, water, transportation and energy infrastructure is critical to the modernisation and progression of our economy, health, and education sectors, as are private industrial, commercial, retail and residential developments.  On completion these facilities often provide vital services to our citizens.Roadworks are carefully planned and programmed to minimise their impact on road users as much as possible.  However, given the scale and complexity of certain projects, some overlap is unavoidable and some congestion is inevitable.Whilst I understand that traffic delays are frustrating, I would encourage travellers to avoid traffic congestion by considering other sustainable and active modes of travel where possible such as public transport, using the park and ride sites or by walking or cycling.To minimise the impact of traffic congestion in the run up to Christmas, I recently announcement a pause of non-essential roadworks on key routes into Belfast, commencing on Monday 25th November.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) any actions being taken to reduce waiting times for diagnostic services, such as MRI and CT scans, at Antrim Area Hospital; and (ii) any investment that is planned to improve access to these critical services.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how his Department supports fathers who are obligated to pay child maintenance but are being refused access to their children; and (ii) the measures in place to ensure that the system is fair and addresses the rights and well-being of both parents and children in such circumstances.
answer_text: The Child Maintenance Service administers the statutory child maintenance scheme (known as the 2012 Scheme) in Northern Ireland. Parental access arrangements do not fall under the remit of the statutory 2012 Scheme. The Child Maintenance Service encourages parents to use the Department’s Signposting Directory which provides details of organisations who can support and advise on a range of family matters. The Child Maintenance Service operates within the relevant legislation. If a parent decides to apply for child maintenance under the 2012 scheme, the Child Maintenance Service will consider a range of information from both parents. The Child Maintenance Service can:resolve disagreements about parentage (i.e. where a person named as a non-resident parent in an application for child maintenance denies they are one of the child’s parents); attempt to locate the other parent if their whereabouts are unknown;calculate an amount of child maintenance to pay;arrange payments and take action if a parent does not pay the child maintenance; and  review child maintenance amounts annually or at the request of either parent.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the steps his Department is taking to address the long waiting times in the Emergency Department at Antrim Area Hospital, particularly during peak times and winter months.
answer_text: My Department has taken a number of steps to improve patient flow at the Antrim Area Hospital, as we recognise the potential risk of harm for patients who experience long waiting times in the Emergency Department (ED) at any time of year not limited to peak times or winter months.Following a Getting it Right First Time (GIRFT) review of Emergency Medicine in Northern Ireland, my Department has asked all Trusts to develop and implement preparedness plans to improve hospital flow, based around the three priority areas identified in the Review:Maximising ambulance capacity;Reducing time spent by patients in emergency departments awaiting clinical decision and next stage of care; and Timely hospital discharge for patients who are medically fit for discharge.The Northern HSC Trust (NHSCT) has been working with the Ambulance Service to put in place alternative pathways for patients to access a range of services as alternatives to attending an emergency department, where this is appropriate.  NHSCT are increasing Same Day Emergency Care (SDEC) services at the Antrim Hospital by introducing additional Respiratory and Cardiology SDEC pathways. Increasing the number of patients accessing alternative pathways to ED will lead to a reduction in ED attendances and ultimately a reduction in time spent by patients in ED awaiting clinical decision and next stage of care.The Northern Health and Social Care Trust has established a Control Room at Antrim Hospital to proactively manage the flow of patients through the hospital site and into community services; and a Discharge Coordination team is promoting earlier discharges and improved weekend discharge rates for patients who have been assessed as medically fit.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of classes that have had to close due to staff shortages in the 2024-25 academic year.
answer_text: The Department does not hold information on the number of classes which have had to close due to staff shortages in the 2024/25 academic year.  However, we can confirm that no exceptional closures have been approved by the Department in the 2024/25 academic year as a result of staff shortages.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the actions taken to address water quality in rivers, lakes, and loughs in East Antrim.
answer_text: I recognise that significant work is required to improve water quality and that this will require collective action across government, the public and private sectors and also wider society.I am pleased that the first Environmental Improvement Plan (EIP) for Northern Ireland has been published which lays out my commitment to improving water quality.The EIP contains the requirement to publish a third cycle River Basin Management Plan for the period 2021-2027 which contains a Programme of Measures to improve and protect waters across Northern Ireland. Examples of measures to improve water quality includes the current review of the Nutrients Action Programme, the Sustainable Use of Livestock Slurry and DAERA’s innovative Soil Nutrient Health Scheme. East Antrim is included in Zone 4 of the scheme with sampling scheduled for winter 2025/26. My focus is to complete the 3rd cycle River Basin Management Plan as soon as possible to help our water bodies to achieve “good” status or better so that improved water quality can underpin our future environmental and economic sustainability.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the policies and procedures in place in hospitals to ensure that patients discharged without transport are not left vulnerable in hospital grounds; and (ii) the measures in place to safeguard discharged patients and visitors from potential risks associated with disoriented individuals or others with ill intent on hospital premises.
answer_text: All HSC Trusts have provided the following response to the questions raised.Belfast TrustBelfast HSC Trust has a Discharge Policy and a Discharge Lounge Policy, both of which will encourage patients to arrange their own transport home on the day of discharge.  If this is not possible, then the staff caring for the patient will contact next of kin or other relatives to determine if they can arrange to take the patient home.  If this is not feasible, then staff can arrange a taxi to collect the patient and, if they do not have money for this, the Trust will pay for it, if appropriate.Dependent on care needs and feasibility, the Trust can arrange for the British Red Cross to accompany a patient home.  If the patient has other care needs, then they will request the Trust’s internal Transport Team to take the patient home and, if required, they would be escorted by one of Senior Nursing Assistants.  If further care is required, and the patient has mobility issues, then the Trust would utilise the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service.In summary, the Trust has multiple resources available to ensure a timely and safe discharge home for patients.With regard to measures to safeguard discharged patients and visitors/relatives, this is covered in the response to point (i).  Patients being discharged can be cared for in the Discharge Lounges, whilst awaiting transport home.Northern TrustNursing staff assess patients for transport at discharge. Vulnerable patients without transport are assisted with the booking of a taxi, the Trust bus service or via the ambulance service.The Northern HSC Trust employs Security Officers at its two acute sites who undertake regular patrols during contracted hours. They maintain open lines of communication between themselves and the Nurse-in-Charge. If the hospital is busy, the Security Officer will concentrate on being a presence in the Emergency Department and other vulnerable areas as required.Portering staff are also on hand to support nursing staff and security staff when dealing with any incidents.  Western TrustThe Western Trust have advised that it is ultimately the responsibility of the individual ward to arrange transport or ensure the patient has a means of safely getting home from hospital. An example of this is reflected in the Trust’s procedures for Ward 50 as detailed below:WHSCT Ward 50 (NWCC) Discharge Procedure Patients who will be discharged will remain on the ward or be accompanied to the Discharge lounge to await a nominated family member/ person to accompany them home. Where a patient has no suitable transport, Ward 50 nursing staff will arrange suitable transport via the Ambulance service or via the trust approved taxi company, when an ambulance is not available.   When a patient is transported home via Ambulance from Ward 50, they will not leave the ward until the Ambulance team accompanies them off the ward, following a handover from the nursing team. When a patient is transported via the WHSCT approved Ta

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in light of the rise in arson incidents in the East Antrim area, to detail the additional support that can be provided to the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service to enhance their educational outreach efforts.
answer_text: Over the five-year period, dated 01/04/2019 through 31/03/2024, NIFRS attended 1098 incidents in the East Antrim area that were determined to be deliberate fires.  During this period, there has been a downward trend of deliberate fires, with a 17% decrease from the baseline of 229 in the year 2019/20 and the figure of 192 in the year 2023/24.  Recognising there have been a number of high-profile incidents in the Greenisland area, NIFRS will continue to engage with the local community to deliver its prevention activities in an effort to drive down instances of deliberate fires. While the educational outreach work NIFRS has been delivering is having a positive impact on the number of deliberate fires, I welcome the continued support of elected representatives for NIFRS as they deliver the community safety messaging programmes and with the delivery of the Community Risk Management Plan.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on the (i) installation; and (ii) availability of electric vehicle charge points in Carrickfergus.
answer_text: The provision and operation of publicly accessible electric vehicle (EV) charge points is a commercially led operation. Responsibility for the maintenance and installation of those charge points rests solely with the Charge Point Operators (CPOs). In this commercially led market, my officials continue to engage with EV owners and provide advice to CPOs on operational matters. My Department does not hold records of the number of publicly available EV charge points in the North. The British Government publishes periodic updates by both Local Council area, and Parliamentary constituency which you may find helpful. These can be found in the link below. https://www.gov.uk/government/statistics/electric-vehicle-public-charging-infrastructure-statistics-july-2024Latest statistics show that there are 639 publicly accessible EV charge points in the North. This represents a growth of 64% in the total number of available EV charge points here since April 2023 and the number of rapid charge points, which provide significantly faster opportunity to charge, has grown by over 300% in that same period. Relevant CPOs are best placed to advise you of their plans for the installation of further charge points within your constituency and the position of their current installations.Local Councils have been able to avail of funding for the installation of charge points under the Office for Zero Emissions Vehicles (OZEV) ‘On Street Residential Charge Point Scheme’, which my Department has match funded. Derry City and Strabane District Council made an application under this scheme in 2021-22, on behalf of nine Local Councils to provide 127 charge points across those Council areas. Individual Councils are best placed to advise on the number of additional charge points this will provide in their area.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education how his Department is addressing educational inequalities in East Antrim, particularly in terms of access to digital resources and support for pupils from disadvantaged backgrounds.
answer_text: The RAISE programme will aim to improve outcomes for all children and young people, from cradle to career, ensuring every child is happy, learning and succeeding. The programme will be active in 15 localities across Northern Ireland, including areas within Antrim, Ballymena, Carrick and Newtownabbey. RAISE will offer the opportunity to target local concerns by providing resources to local stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to outline what action his Department plans to take to address road maintenance issues in East Antrim in the 2024-25 financial year.
To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to expand diabetes education programmes, such as the DESMOND or DAFNE programmes, to ensure they are more accessible to the wider population.
answer_text: One of the key themes within the drivers for change discussed in the Diabetes Strategic Framework is Supporting Self-management - Empowering People through Structured Diabetes Education.Within Northern Ireland, the regionally agreed suite of quality assured Structured Diabetes Education programmes are DAFNE, for people living with Type 1 diabetes; DESMOND for people living with type 2 diabetes and CHOICE for children and young people living with diabetes.Over the past two years, the NI Diabetes Network, in partnership with health care professionals, clinicians, People Living with Diabetes and Diabetes UK, have developed a Regional Structured Diabetes Education coordination service model.  Once implemented, this service model will provide equity of access, reduce variability and increase service delivery.  It will create efficiencies in process and time, better patient experience and improved patient education and outcomes across Northern Ireland.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the actions his Department is taking to protect the coastline and natural environment in East Antrim, particularly around (i) Carrickfergus; and (ii) Whitehead.
answer_text: The Environmental Improvement Plan for Northern Ireland sets out the actions my Department is taking to protect and improve the natural environment, and will guide specific action in local areas. The Outer Belfast Lough Special Protection Area and Area of Special Scientific Interest provide protection for coastal habitats and waterbirds in the Carrickfergus area. In the Whitehead area, the North Channel Special Area of Conservation provides protection for Harbour porpoise and the proposed East Coast Special Protection Area provides protection for seabirds.My Department has been undertaking surveys of the Northern Ireland coastline utilising methodologies such as LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging), high resolution orthophotography, satellite-derived bathymetry, and detailed analysis of Ordnance Survey maps, historical aerial photographs and geological datasets to improve understanding of potential impacts of climate change to the coastal environment. This evidence is publicly accessible on the Northern Ireland Coastal Observatory website and includes the Carrickfergus and Whitehead areas.My Department is also in the final stages of preparing a Marine Plan for Northern Ireland which will set the policy framework for sustainable development of the Northern Ireland marine area. The Marine Plan will sit alongside and interact with the terrestrial planning system, ensuring that coastal areas and activities taking place within them, are managed in line with the principles of Integrated Coastal Zone Management.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to support local initiatives aimed at addressing the growing issue of drug and alcohol misuse in East Antrim.
answer_text: One of the key priorities for my Department, and for the Executive, is to tackle the harm caused by alcohol and other drugs. Underscored by five population-level outcomes, the vision of our substance use strategy Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery is that people are supported in the prevention and reduction of the harm related to the use of alcohol and other drugs, and will be empowered to maintain recovery. A key action of Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery was for the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group to co-produce, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused strategic delivery plan, to replace the existing Alcohol & Drugs Services Commissioning Framework. Information on the range of services currently available in the Northern Trust area, including East Antrim, can be found at: https://drugsandalcoholni.info/the-ni-dacts/ndact/.However, we are keen to improve the access to quality services across Northern Ireland. To this end, the Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan 2024-28 has been co-developed following considerable stakeholder engagement and will shortly be launched. Two accelerated pieces of work are also due to report shortly – a Population Needs-Based Assessment of Substance Use and related services in the Western Health & Social Care Trust area, and an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland.It is the intention that the finalised Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan, and the associated recommendations from the Tier 4 Review (subject to public consultation) will inform all future funding decisions around substance use related services subject to additional funding being made available into the future.The Public Health Agency is also in the process of implementing a series of tender opportunities for the commissioning of Substance Use Services to be delivered across Northern Ireland – this tendering process will take place in a staged approach beginning in October 2024. Further information on upcoming Substance Use Service Tender Opportunities can be found on the PHA website at: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/contracts/news/pha-procurement-priorities-1718-and-1819.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail any plans in place to improve road infrastructure in East Antrim, particularly to reduce congestion on the A2 to ensure safer access to Carrickfergus and Larne.
answer_text: Officials are aware of the traffic congestion on the A2 Shore Road during peak traffic flows which is similar to many roads within the East Antrim area. A key aspect of my Department’s transport policy is to encourage the use of sustainable transport modes such as walking, cycling and public transport as an alternative to the private car and the active travel plans under development will help to provide the necessary infrastructure to promote this change.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to address the housing shortage in East Antrim, particularly in terms of affordable housing for young families.
answer_text: Within the East Antrim Parliamentary Constituency, 280 social housing units are currently under construction with a further 34 units planned to start this programme year 2024/25. Co-Ownership remains my Department’s primary delivery partner for intermediate housing. As it operates on a DIYSO (Do It Yourself Shared Ownership) basis, Co-Ownership does not develop housing, rather it provides customers with the opportunity to purchase a home that is suitable for them, whether new build or existing, anywhere in Northern Ireland.Most co-ownership customers are purchasing a home for the first time and predominantly come from the younger age groups. Co-Ownership customers require significantly lower, and in some cases no, deposit to obtain their mortgage, providing a route into homeownership for young families who cannot access the mortgage market without help.More widely, my Department has developed a draft Housing Supply Strategy that aims to provide a long-term framework for policies that can increase supply across all tenures. I am currently reviewing a final draft of the Strategy and intend to bring it to the Executive shortly.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will consider providing veterans with travel discounts, similar to the Veterans Railcard in England, Scotland and Wales.
answer_text: Under the NI Concessionary Fares Scheme, residents here in receipt of a War Disablement Pension or a Guaranteed Income Payment from the Service Personnel and Veterans Agency may apply for a War Disablement SmartPass, which allows the card holder free concessionary travel on public transport services within the North.  Similarly, everyone over the age of 60 is entitled to free travel on public transport services, by rail and road.Where a person does not qualify for discounts under the Concessionary Fares Scheme, Translink offers a range of discounts to all passengers travelling on the public transport network by rail and road in the north.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the progress made on the recommendations in the Northern Ireland Audit Office report, Injury on duty schemes for officers in the Police Service of Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Prison Service.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Audit Office report, ‘Injury on duty schemes for officers in the Police Service of Northern Ireland and the Northern Ireland Prison Service’ made four recommendations.My Department has consulted on potential changes to the Injury on Duty (IOD) scheme to reflect the recommendations of the NIAO report. This consultation ran from 26 March 2024 to 17 July 24.
