My officials are currently finalising the results of this consultation, and the consultation response will be circulated to all stakeholders in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any action his Department will take to (i) increase; and (ii) improve sporting provision at Carrickfergus Grammar School.
answer_text: Carrickfergus Grammar School was announced in May 2020 to advance in design under the second call of the School Enhancement Programme.  The proposal includes provision of a New Sports Centre to include a sports hall, changing facilities, fitness suite and Physical Education classroom.An Integrated Consultancy Team has been appointed to take the project through the design and construction phases and they have recently completed a Technical Feasibility Study which outlines options for the project.  A Business Case is currently being prepared.I understand the school has raised concerns about the newly laid shale hockey pitch and regrettably have not been able to use the pitch.  I am mindful that it is normal for a newly laid shale pitch to take some time to settle and further rolling has taken place to aid its natural settlement.  A DE official has visited the site to view the pitch and, in order to resolve the outstanding difficulties and further discuss the matter, a meeting has been arranged for Thursday 10 October between the Education Authority (EA), the school and the contractor. It is my intention to launch a series of targeted smaller scale investment programmes to support and enhance the delivery of the curriculum in schools. One potential programme will be for sports facilities, and this could provide an opportunity for the school to enhance its sports provision. Further detail on this will be provided in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the steps her Department is taking to dismantle organised crime groups and reduce their influence, especially in economically deprived areas.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward a range of work in relation to organised crime groups.The Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF) brings together law enforcement agencies, along with Government Departments, academia and the business community to combat organised crime.  It aims to provide strategic leadership and support collaborative, joined-up responses to the threat posed by organised crime.   Further information on their work can be found at: Organised Crime Task Force (octf.gov.uk) The Executive’s Programme on Paramilitarism, Criminality and Organised Crime (EPPOC), for which I am lead Minister, is currently investing £16 million a year in work to support individuals and communities that are experiencing harm as a result of the activities of organised crime groups. EPPOC’s main objectives are to keep people safe, by putting pressure on criminal groups; to increase community resilience by supporting communities to resist exploitation and harm; and to increase the protective factors around those who may be vulnerable to exploitation for many different reasons, including the fact that they live in deprived communities less resilient to paramilitary coercion and control. The Programme aims to tackle the harm caused by such groups in the here and now, as well as developing interventions aimed at breaking the cycle of violence in the future. It facilitates Departments, statutory bodies and the community and voluntary sector to work in partnership on an ambitious, innovative, multi-disciplinary programme of work that delivers around 100 interventions to systematically address the complex problem of paramilitarism. Illegal money-lending is one example of paramilitary activity that targets the most deprived communities. Programme initiatives such as the ‘Ending the Harm’ public awareness campaign on illegal money lending, (www.endingtheharm.com) have led the way in consciously highlighting the devastating impact that criminal gangs have on victims, families, communities, and wider society when at their most vulnerable. You will also be aware that I have recently introduced the Justice Bill. As part of this Bill, I plan to bring forward provisions to specifically tackle those who direct or participate in serious organised crime. I am hopeful that this legislation, the first of its kind in Northern Ireland, will show my commitment to further tackling organised criminality, those it harms and more effectively respond to this.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department plans to expand or improve teacher training on how to effectively (i) support children with attention deficit hyperactivity disorder; and (ii) manage behavioural challenges in the classroom.
answer_text: The Education Authority’s (EA) Behaviour Support and Provisions Service (BSPS) offers whole school, capacity building learning programmes for an identified area of development within the School Development Plan or through the Education Training Inspectorate’s (ETI) inspection process, linked to Social Behaviour and Emotional Wellbeing (SBEW) needs across all phases.The aim is to create a school culture where the strategies and resources provided through these programmes can lead to real and lasting success. The programmes of support and professional learning opportunities are immersed in the Public Health Agency’s ‘Take 5 Steps to Wellbeing’ framework and aim to effectively support the education workforce by establishing sustainable delivery of evidence-based, effective whole-school approaches across all nursery, primary, post-primary and specialist settings in Northern Ireland. Details of specific referral processes and support available to schools can be further accessed on the EA's website.	The post-primary BSPS provides placements through Education Other Than At School (EOTAS) for students in Initial Teacher Education (ITE), when requested, as part of the school placement module. The service works with the ITE providers to ensure these placements are promoted to student teachers. In addition, the service is currently scoping potential opportunities to deliver professional learning in areas specific to behaviour and SBEW needs to ITE students.As part of the End-to-End Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN), my Department is taking steps to increase the quality, consistency, and access arrangements for SEN-focussed professional learning for the teaching profession. This will include increasing the opportunities available to both teachers and the wider educational workforce to meaningfully participate in ongoing professional learning and development.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail the actions his Department plans to take to address (i) rural isolation; (ii) lack of access to services in rural areas; and (iii) declining employment opportunities in rural areas.
answer_text: My department’s Tackling Rural Poverty and Social Isolation (TRPSI) Framework focuses on three priority areas for intervention, namely Access Poverty, Financial Poverty and Social Isolation and has supported measures designed to address these priorities in rural areas since 2016.  Under the Framework, DAERA has invested approximately £54.58m to date, including £1.77m resource and £4.4m capital funding to deliver TRPSI initiatives in the current year. Ongoing initiatives include investment of £1.502m through the Rural Micro Capital Grants Scheme to over 900 voluntary and community sector organisations to purchase capital items to help address locally identified poverty or social isolation issues, further commitment to the Rural Support Charity through the issue of a contract of funding until 31 March 2027 and the Village Catalyst Programme which addresses rural dereliction and provides community facilities.As the TRPSI Framework has been in place for 8 years, my Department commissioned an independent review, which is examining how effectively it has been implemented.  The review is engaging with a range of stakeholders, including public authorities and rural community organisations in relation to addressing poverty and social isolation in rural areas.  This review is due to report back in early 2025 and will help inform DAERA’s future policy direction.  I welcome the recent publication of the Sub-Regional Economic Plan by the Department for the Economy (DfE), which has lead responsibility for the NI Executive’s Economic Strategy.  Addressing regional imbalance will help create more employment opportunities throughout our rural communities.The issues you have raised span across the remit of a number of departments, and I will continue to work with my Executive colleagues to seek to improve outcomes for our rural communities. I plan to consult next year on a new, evidence-based rural policy which will reflect that all departments have responsibility for exercising their functions in both rural and urban areas.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail any progress that has been made to implement baby loss certificates.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss. I want all parents and families who have suffered the devastating loss of a baby to feel that their grief is recognised.I met with the Health Minister in September to receive an update on progress from General Register Office officials in my Department who are working closely with the Department of Health officials on how the baby loss scheme work may be developed. I recently met with the Minister for Health again to mark Baby Loss Awareness Week. My officials and those from the Department of Health have met with managers from the Department of Health and Social Care in England and with the National Records of Scotland officials to learn more about how their schemes operate. This ongoing engagement will provide important lessons for the development of our scheme and work is ongoing to scope and assess options for the introduction of baby loss certificates here for parents here who have lost a baby during pregnancy up to 24 weeks. My Department will introduce new legislation to enable a Baby Loss Certificate Scheme and we will continue to work with the Department of Health on this important initiative.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail her Department's plans to reform the youth justice system to ensure young offenders are rehabilitated and removed from a cycle of re-offending.
answer_text: In March 2022, I approved the publication of a new Strategic Framework for Youth Justice, accompanied by a five-year Action Plan.  The Framework seeks to improve outcomes for children, families and victims across all stages of the youth justice system and focuses on actions which are within the gift of the Department and its agencies to deliver.   One of the most effective measures to prevent a cycle of reoffending is to divert children from the formal criminal justice system early and provide them with support aimed at meeting the underlying causes of offending behaviour.  Research has shown that the further a child is drawn into the system, the worse their outcomes can be, which is why a range of earlier stage diversion measures have been developed to support children and families and help prevent further offending.  We have also undertaken a public consultation on increasing the Minimum Age of Criminal Responsibility, requiring Executive approval, which could prevent children being drawn into the system in the first place.For those already in the justice system, key achievements include a new Model of Practice and an Enhanced Case Management Framework, alongside improvements in employability and mental health services for children, as these can be significant factors in reoffending if unaddressed.Work is ongoing to improve children’s experience of youth courts and the effectiveness of the subsequent disposals, informed by direct engagement with children and their families.  I intend to simplify the youth court sentencing framework, both for community and custodial sentences, to aid understanding and compliance.  Changes to children’s bail, remand and custody legislation will prevent unnecessary admissions to custody and, ensure that those in custody are held in child-appropriate facilities.  I am also proposing new, significantly reduced, rehabilitation periods to enable children to move on from offending more quickly.  We know that many issues sit outside the youth justice system and I will continue to work alongside Executive Ministers to lobby for necessary change.  In the interim, the focus of the Strategic Framework remains on reforming our youth justice system and preventing reoffending by children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 9534/22-27, what further update he can provide on the implementation of the Graduated Driver Licensing Monitoring Report recommendations.
answer_text: As I indicated in my previous response, the Graduated Driver Licensing Monitoring Report does not make recommendations on GDL. That said, the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act 2016 does provide the statutory framework for GDL. However, further subordinate legislation, dedicated funding and significant operational planning activity are required in advance of implementation. I have asked my officials to seek legal advice on GDL, which will help me determine the best way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for a breakdown of road resurfacing schemes (i) completed in the 2023-24 financial year; and (ii) planned for the 2024-25 financial year, for the East Antrim area.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme. Regrettably, for some time, the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of both completed resurfacing schemes for the 2023/24 financial year and the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year will be included within the Department’s Annual Report which will be presented to Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the current deposit requirements for each of the 20 Housing Authorities.
answer_text: Registered Housing Associations do not require a rent deposit from tenants accessing social housing. Some Housing Associations may request new tenants to make a payment in advance to cover rent and rates.  This is one of a number of measures that social housing landlords take to prevent tenants from falling into arrears at the start of their tenancy.  I have been assured that these deposits are not mandatory and will not preclude the allocation of a tenancy.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of Housing Associations currently charging social rents that exceed the 30 per cent threshold of those on the lowest Local Housing Allowance income.
answer_text: My Department does not hold this information.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice whether her Department has any plans to review family court outcomes to ensure that (i) fathers' rights are respected; and (ii) the best interests of the child include meaningful involvement from both parents.
answer_text: Decisions on applications made to the courts concerning arrangements for children following parental separation are matters for the independent judiciary, therefore I cannot become involved in the outcomes of individual cases.In relation to an application for child contact, while the legislation makes the child’s welfare the paramount consideration, this principle will be applied by the court with reference to case law and the evidence before it. Any legislative reform would be a matter for the Department of Finance, given their policy responsibility for private family law.It is usually in a child’s best interests if parents can mutually agree contact arrangements without having to apply to the court to determine them. That is why I have commissioned my officials to continue to work with colleagues in the Department of Health under the joint Private Family Law Early Intervention Action Plan to identify means of helping parents agree arrangements as early as possible, outside of the court environment. A number of tools under the Action Plan are being developed, some of which I hope to launch in the coming months, and potential next steps are also under consideration. This will hopefully assist in reducing acrimony and preventing disputes from escalating.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the programs and initiatives being implemented to address educational underachievement of working-class Protestant students.
answer_text: Since the publication of A Fair Start Report and Action Plan, approximately £16 million has been invested to date in addressing underachievement by implementing the report’s recommendations. In addition, £6.2 million is being invested in Early Years (standardisation of the pre-school day) and £4.5 million in Nurture Groups. Progress on A Fair Start can be found at A Fair Start | Department of Education (education-ni.gov.uk), this includes interventions such as the Horizons Programme, under Key Area 5 Maximizing Boys Potential. This 2-year developmental youth leadership programme for boys and girls has already seen over 400 young people engaged and completed vocational development.  Monkstown Boxing Club also receives A Fair Start funding to deliver its ‘In Your Corner’ programme, supporting young people at significant risk of disengaging from education. In North Belfast, the Full Service programme involving both “Model” post-primary schools has been operating since 2006 and is an integral part of how these two schools support their learners. The work involves support at transition from Year 7 to Year 8, support for attendance, emotional health and well-being, parental engagement, revision classes and so on.Greater Shankill Partnership Board has received funding since 2013/14 to support the West Belfast Community Project.  The aim of this project is to support the creation of a seamless education pathway for children and young people through a range of programmes and activities which creates wrap-around service to address the root causes of educational underachievement.Eastside Learning is also funded through the WRAP programme and with its focus on Early Years, many children and their families have been supported through the interventions delivered.  The WRAP programme has also supported the THRiVE programme in Monkstown and Rathcoole. Funding for this programme comes from the Executive’s programme for tackling paramilitary activity and organised crime/In addition, my department continues to provide each year, approximately £75 million for Targeting Social Need through the Common Funding Formula and £8.1 million through Extended Schools to support disadvantage children including working class Protestant children.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether he intends to work with the Minister of Health to improve access to direct payments and managed budgets to increase the options for those with learning difficulties to access increased support services.
answer_text: The Health Minister has policy responsibility for Direct Payments and Managed Budgets.  However, I understand that some organisations have suggested that there may be scope for these to be used as a part of a broader funding arrangement to enable school leavers with Special Educational Needs (SEN) to access post-school provision. My Department has a range of supports in place to enable students, including those with learning difficulties to access skills, education and training opportunities.  I am aware, however, that the current provision is not meeting the needs of all our young people.  I have therefore commissioned a review of the existing provision for young people with special educational needs. Progress on this important issue will require collaboration across Departments, including the Department of Health. It is possible that Managed Budgets and Direct Payments could be part of the solution to some of the issues being faced, and my officials will be examining this further. We will look to Department of Health as the policy lead and experts on those issues to guide us as we work through the project.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Disability Strategy.
answer_text: The development of a robust, sustainable and effective Disability Strategy is a key priority for my Department and I have taken time to consider the work taken forward to date. I will be writing to my Executive colleagues before the end of October to outline the next steps in developing the Disability Strategy. 		While the Department for Communities is leading on the development of the Strategy, final decisions on its content, publication and implementation will be subject to Executive agreement and public consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to increase awareness and improve services for adult ADHD, particularly for those who may have been undiagnosed during childhood.
answer_text: Although my Department is not currently planning any public awareness campaigns in relation to ADHD, officials are currently undertaking scoping work to commission a research exercise to inform future service provision for individuals impacted by ADHD. This work, which would cover both child and adult ADHD – and therefore be relevant to adults who did not receive a childhood diagnosis – would be expected to (a) establish current and projected demand for ADHD services, and (b) make recommendations as to what any future commissioned services might look like. Any future service provision the research may recommend would be subject to future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail (i) the current cycle lane strategy being implemented; and (ii) the assessments that are made before lanes are put in place in relation to the impact on visually impaired individuals.
answer_text: My Department is currently developing an Active Travel Delivery Plan for the North including an update to our policies and design guidance. This guidance is being developed based on current national and international best practice for the design of high-quality active travel infrastructure. I am committed to the delivery of inclusive pedestrian and cycling infrastructure to ensure people feel confident to use the network safely, freely, and comfortably. I am conscious that certain elements of active travel infrastructure can present challenges to some visually impaired people, particularly at interactions with pedestrian and cyclist. My officials have engaged with relevant stakeholders including The Inclusive Mobility and Transport Advisory Committee (IMTAC), Guide Dogs, and RNIB NI on the design of such elements and are committed to the continuation of this to establish a consistent approach appropriate to the site conditions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail (i) the number of people that receive direct payments, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust; and (ii) how this support is accessed.
To ask the Minister of Education to detail the number of schools in the controlled sector that use (i) astroturf pitches; and (ii) shale pitches for competitive sports.
answer_text: The controlled sector is operated and maintained by the Education Authority (EA). EA have advised that they do not hold the requested information. However, should a school feel that they require additional provision, provided they meet the requirements of the Building Handbook, they should complete a Minor Works application which will be considered by EA.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, in light of the new build Islandmagee Primary School, to detail any additional safety measures that will be installed to ensure safe crossing for pupils.
answer_text: Following the recent opening of Islandmagee Primary school, I have asked officials to undertake an assessment of the area. Unfortunately, due to limited staff resource and the competing requests for similar assessments, results may take some time.In the interim, any concerns about poor driver behaviour such as speeding or dangerous driving should be directed to the PSNI who have the powers of enforcement in that regard. They can be contacted on their non-emergency telephone number, 101.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe.  The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail any actions he will take to improve employment rights for substitute teachers.
answer_text: Teachers’ (including supply teachers’) terms & conditions are negotiated and agreed by the Teachers’ Negotiating Committee (TNC) which comprises representatives of teachers’ employers and sectoral bodies, the Department of Education and the five main teachers’ unions. Decisions are communicated to teachers and their employers via TNC Circulars.Through the TNC, and with limited resources, my officials are working with the employers of teachers to improve employment rights for supply teachers.A review of the use of supply teachers has been carried out through the TNC and the recommendations from this review are currently being considered.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail his Department's plans to address the lack of support for post-16 pupils with special educational needs in East Antrim.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) has advised that the Special Schools Area Planning Framework and the Framework for Specialist Provision in Mainstream set out the requirement for pathways of provision in special schools and specialist provision from ages 3-19, which includes Early Years and Post 16. The first strategic area plan for special schools and specialist provision, the Special Education Strategic Area Plan (2022-27), includes a key theme to 'increase parity of access for all to appropriate pathways.' Delivery of these priorities are included within Operational Plans which set out the workstreams across each Local Government District (LGD). The EA has submitted Operational Plan 2 (2024-26) to the department for consideration.The EA also advises that its current Pupil Support Services provide advice and guidance to those young people over 16 who are remaining in education, their families and schools

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the initiatives in place to support mental health in schools, particularly for adolescents.
answer_text: The Department of Health and the Department of Education collaborate to provide mental health support in schools through engagement and joint funding. In February 2021, the Department of Health and the Department of Education jointly launched the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  A copy of the Framework is available at:  https://www.education-ni.gov.uk/publications/children-young-peoples-emotional-health-and-wellbeing-education-framework-final-version The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach. The following initiatives specific to secondary schools have been piloted or rolled out since the publication of the Framework.Emotional Wellbeing in Schools (EWTS) is a multi-disciplinary health led team. The aim is to provide intervention at the earliest opportunity by ensuring schools are aware of appropriate pathways for support in their local area and within CAMHS. (commenced 2023/2024)School Nursing Pilot aims to deliver a range of innovative, transformational and pupil focused services. (commenced 2021/22)Text a Nurse is a confidential service aimed at young people to seek advice about sensitive health issues without a face-to-face appointment. (commenced 2021)Resilience in Education, Assisting Change to Happen (REACH) is delivered within the school environment by a professionally qualified youth work team and provides programmes based on the needs of pupils and schools under the themes of; Wellbeing for All, Early Support and Enhanced Support. (commenced 2021/22)Being Well Doing Well (BWDW) is a programme which aims to support schools (nursery, primary, post primary, special and EOTAS) to develop a Whole School Approach to Emotional Health and Wellbeing. (commenced 2023/24)

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the financial constraints placed on NHS staff who have to pay for car park usage.
answer_text: As a fair recognition of the hard work and dedication of HSC staff, all staff parking permits became free of charge from 12 May 2024.  There is insufficient capacity for all staff to be provided with a parking permit.To ensure that those staff with the greatest need are provided with a free on-site parking permit, the Department has requested that all HSC Trusts implement an assessment of demonstrable “need” for designated staff car parking spaces / permits. The assessment criteria takes into consideration individual circumstances, operational requirements, and the individual’s ability to access alternative and sustainable forms of transport.Trusts have a range of supports in place to help encourage a reduction in the demand for onsite staff car parking including free off-site staff parking spaces with free staff shuttle buses to and from work, cycle to work and car share schemes, cycle shelters and showering facilities onsite to encourage cycling, running or walking to work, free evening and weekend parking  passes for parking out of hours, as well as the provision of bespoke Personal Travel Plans for staff. Staff who park in visitor car parks at hospital sites will incur the relevant car parking charges.In line with the Hospital Parking Charges Act 2024, trusts are working at pace to introduce ANPR technology to enable free car parking for all users by May 2026.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Northern Ireland Football Fund.
answer_text: The Northern Ireland Football Fund will provide investment for football at all levels. It is a key priority for me, and I am committed to moving forward as quickly as possible and my officials are continuing to push forward at pace.I want to roll out the funding as quickly as possible and I expect the first projects, which will be taken forward under this Fund, will be identified by the end of this financial year. The Northern Ireland Football Fund will prioritise performance clubs first, but I am also moving forward with proposals to invest at grassroots level and in creation of a National Training Centre.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for his assessment of the affordability of social housing, particularly in new build developments.
answer_text: Housing Associations are the main developers of new build social housing. Decisions on rent levels in Housing Association properties are taken by each Housing Association’s Board and my Department has no power to impose rent levels on Associations.In terms of support for tenants, Housing Associations provide welfare advice, signposting to other support services and proactively work to identify those who are experiencing difficulty paying their rent in order to provide support to ensure that tenancies continue.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail any action he will take to ensure greater transparency between social housing providers and tenants in relation to a rise in rent and management fees.
answer_text: The process for setting rents across the NIHE & Housing Association sectors is separate and distinct. The NIHE is a Non-Departmental Public Body, created in statute. The Department for Communities oversees it, and I am accountable for it in the Assembly; a relationship also created in statute. As such, my Department has a role in approving requests for a rent increase sought by the NIHE annually. This process considers a wide range of factors, including inflation; projected running costs; spending requirements and tenant & landlord affordability. In my role of overseeing this process for the NIHE I will continue to ensure that NIHE tenants are kept informed regarding any uplift in rental charges.Conversely, there is no legislative provision for Departmental control of rent, rates or management fees imposed by Registered Housing Associations. However, Housing Regulation Branch (HRB) within my Department seeks assurance on housing associations’ compliance with standards contained in the Regulatory Framework. It requires RHAs to provide information on rent and other charge increases as part of the annual regulatory assessment process. Information on average rent increases by individual housing associations, and sector wide, is available via the Key Performance Reports which are published on the DfC website. A recent review of the framework, effective from 01 April 2024 will ensure a closer focus on the publication of information which enables tenants to hold landlords to account.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, given the importance of ensuring that all members of our community feel safe and understood when using public transport, particularly individuals with autism, to detail the steps his Department is taking to introduce or encourage autism-awareness training for taxi drivers.
answer_text: Taxi Driver Periodic Training (TDPT) is a mandatory training requirement for taxi drivers here, introduced in 2014 through powers in the Taxis Act (Northern Ireland) 2008 and the Taxi Drivers’ Licences Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2014.  The purpose of TDPT is to raise the standards of the taxi industry and to develop the current skills of taxi drivers.  Its benefits include improvements to road safety, a better understanding of the environmentally friendly driving techniques and to complement and enhance a taxi driver’s professionalism.All taxi drivers are required to complete 35 hours of TDPT during the validity of their five-year taxi driver licence, before they can renew their taxi driver licence.  The training must be delivered by a training provider authorised by the Department for this purpose, and at least 3.5 hours of TDPT must be Disability Awareness training.  The Driver and Vehicle Agency has developed a “Disability Equality Training Course” and a “Making Your Taxi Service Inclusive Course” as resources for training providers who deliver Disability Awareness training.   Their scope includes a range of topics related to understanding disability and the law and providing an inclusive and accessible service to people with disabilities, including those affected by autism.  The resources also suggest discussion points which can be supplemented by training providers.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how his Department intends to address the untreated weeds and footpaths in the East Antrim area.
answer_text: My Department undertakes routine weed control on the adopted road network, including footways, throughout the North.  Weed control is undertaken annually and normally results in weeds being sprayed once per year, with further treatments carried out, as required, following an assessment of the effectiveness of the initial action.I have been advised that, within the East Antrim constituency, an initial weed treatment commenced on 15 May 2024 and concluded by 29 July 2024.  Subsequently, a second treatment is currently underway, having commenced on 30 August 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his plans to ensure the staffing levels of healthcare professionals will meet current and future demands for services.

To ask the Minister for the Economy what his Department is doing to create sustainable job opportunities and support skills development to assist workers in deprived areas to adapt to the changing economic landscape.

To ask the Minister of Education, pursuant to AQW 13260/22-27, whether the Department will be in a position to collect the data required for the Prevalence of Autism in School Aged Children in Northern Ireland through the school census in October.
answer_text: The data used to produce the “Prevalence of Autism in School Aged Children in Northern Ireland” is sourced from the School Census. The 2024/25 school census data collection is scheduled to take place on 11 October 2024. The Department expects that this will be completed in full by all schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the Fundamental Review of Allocations.
answer_text: Work has been ongoing with the Fundamental Review of Allocations. Stage One was implemented on 31 January 2023, I gave approval to Stage Two in June 2024 and my officials are now working with the Housing Executive on the early stages of Stage Three.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on planned road resurfacing in the East Antrim area for the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention.Details of the planned list of resurfacing schemes for the 2024/25 financial year will be included within the Department’s Annual Report to Mid and East Antrim Borough Council in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the status of the dilapidation bill.
answer_text: It is my intention to introduce a Dilapidation bill to the Assembly in the autumn term of this current sitting, subject of course to the agreement of my Executive colleagues. 		The proposed Bill would provide councils with a modern and fit-for-purpose regime, including clear powers to tackle dilapidated buildings and neglected sites. Powers would be similar to those available to local authorities in other parts of the UK.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has any plans to introduce single occupancy rates relief.
answer_text: Our domestic rate support provision already includes a Lone Pensioner Allowance which provides a 20% discount to persons aged 70 or over who live alone – this was provided to nearly 40,000 people here last year at a cost of over £9 million to the Executive.I have no plans to introduce any single occupancy discount beyond Lone Pensioner Allowance provision. A Single Person Allowance policy was given detailed consideration by the Executive in 2007 and was ruled out at that time as such a widespread discount was difficult to justify on grounds of cost, need and vulnerability to fraud. These same considerations continue to apply today - in England alone for the period between 2020 to 2022 a total of 32,669 incorrect claims were detected for the Council Tax Single Person Discount, leading to £44.7m fraudulent or wrongly received payments. This was reported on within the 2022 National Fraud Initiative Report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail any action his Department will take to address the paediatric percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy surgery waiting list.
answer_text: The issue of access to percutaneous endoscopic gastrostomy (PEG) insertion for children and young people is an important issue as is the range of all surgical procedures that face children and young people in Northern Ireland.  This procedure is a complex procedure that requires specialised clinical and nursing input prior to, during and post procedure.  A Paediatric Surgeon is currently going through an urgent validation of Peg Tube waiting lists.  This process will identity which children are suitable to transfer to Alderhey Hospital in Liverpool for the procedure.  Clinical criteria, agreed by the specialist team, will be in place to determine this.Belfast Trust are also hoping to provide a waiting list initiative in October, to carry out additional sessions on Fridays, with patients being nursed in Knox ward in RBHSC over the weekend. Further discussions are underway with South Eastern Trust to provide the Belfast Trust paediatric surgeon a gastrostomy surgery list in the Ulster Hospital.  Belfast Trust Gastrostomy Specialist Nurses will provide training to staff in South Eastern Trust to help facilitate this.A further review of surgical work performed in RBHSC is underway and any work that can be redirected to District General Hospitals will be transferred.  This may free up capacity in RBHSC for Paediatric Surgeons to perform more gastrostomy surgery and other more complex surgery that can only be carried out in RBHSC.The Trust are also examining partnership working with Private Sector to use RBHSC capacity over the weekend to deliver Waiting List Initiatives in the Children’s Hospital and will be dependent on unscheduled care pressures. My Officials will continue to meet with BHSCT to discuss these potential solutions to examine their progress and success.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance whether her Department has any plans to introduce single occupancy rates relief.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer provided in AQW 13838/22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for an update on the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service pension scheme; (ii) why pension increases have been delayed; and (iii) to detail a time frame for addressing any payment issues.
answer_text: Historically the Northern Ireland Fire and Rescue Service (NIFRS) have processed the pension increase in July or August of each year, which included any arrears owed since the April increase was confirmed.  NIFRS has been undergoing a period of significant change with the pension’s administration and undertook a project to outsource to HSC Pensions’ Service.  The final stages of this project involved the transfer of the pension payroll to ensure that they would have a single, automated, end-to-end pension administration with improved resilience and support for their Firefighters and Pensioners. This will reduce the risk of human error from manual calculations of increases and arrears and provide a more efficient service for the member in the longer term.  The pensioner payroll migrated across in February 2024.There was a need to complete additional data cleansing and reconciliation as well as testing of the software application to ensure the pension increase was accurately implemented. Unfortunately there have been some technical issues with the software that has delayed the project and NIFRS have been working with the software supplier to resolve these issues and administer the increase. NIFRS administrator has advised them that the fixes required to the system have now been delivered to their test environment and they have commenced the rigorous testing required prior to moving and processing the increase in the live system.HSC Pension Service have advised they plan to apply the increase this month and issue and pay this along with any arrears due in payments scheduled for the 1st October 2024.  This will be conditional on the successful testing of the software and NIFRS are keeping Pensioners updated through the website.  It is NIFRS aim, once they have the process implemented, to process the pension increase much earlier than they have been doing historically but they unfortunately have to go through the process of a new system implementation to achieve this.Please be assured that NIFRS are doing all they can to implement the pension increase as quickly as possible and prevent any future delays.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what action her Department is taking to address an increase in attacks on places of worship.
To ask the Minister of Health when he will answer AQW 12965/22-27.

To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the actions his Department has taken in response to the Boxing Strategy, which was published by the predecessor Department; and (ii) how progress of this has been monitored.
answer_text: Neither my Department nor its predecessor, the Department of Culture Arts and Leisure (DCAL), have ever published a boxing strategy.  Responsibility for the development of a strategy for an individual sport rests with the Governing Body of that sport. I understand that the Irish Athletic Boxing Association (IABA), the Governing Body for Boxing, is currently in the process of designing a new Strategic Plan for 2024-28.To support previous IABA Strategic Plans, Sport NI’s Boxing Investment Programme delivered National Lottery funding of £3.27 million for boxing in Northern Ireland between 2012/13 and 2015/16 and was subject to National Lottery monitoring arrangements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) to detail the number of Sport NI organisations that have completed the Equality/Equity Standard: A Framework for Sport; and (ii) whether he plans to conduct a review into the impact of this.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI does not have any affiliate or arm’s length organisations. Therefore, no Sport NI organisations have completed the Equality/Equity Standard: A Framework for Sport.Those organisations who historically completed the Equality Standard are separately constituted organisations. Sport NI does not hold a record of those organisations that have completed the Equality/Equity Standard: A Framework for Sport.Please be advised that following a review and consultation with the sport and physical activity sector across the UK, the Equality Standard has been replaced by a new supportive programme, Moving to Inclusion https://movingtoinclusion.co.uk/.Sport NI launched Moving to Inclusion with its funded organisations in February 2024. Moving to Inclusion is currently optional and available data from it is very limited.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the amount of funding allocated by Sport NI between 2021 and 2024, broken down by constituency; and (ii) where this funding was allocated in East Antrim.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the amount of funding his Department and its arm's-length bodies have provided to amateur boxing clubs in each of the last five years, broken down by how much each club was awarded.
answer_text: My Department has not made any direct funding to boxing clubs over the last five years. However, I am aware that Sport NI has disbursed funding to boxing clubs over this period and this is an operational matter for the arm’s length body.For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.Due to the volume of information to be provided, I have arranged for the information requested to be placed in the Assembly Library.I can also advise that all funding information is available on the Government Funding Database. This website is publicly accessible and holds records of applications and funding to voluntary and community sector organisations including funding disbursed to the sports sector.  I have included the link for future convenience - https://govfunding.nics.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the funding currently available from his Department for boxing clubs; and (ii) whether he plans to make any additional funding available.
answer_text: You will be aware that there are budgetary pressures across all departments in the Northern Ireland Executive and this includes my Department for Communities. My Department has published an Equality Impact Assessment (EQIA) on Budget 2024-25, which launched on 11 June 2024 [See link EQIA] and I encourage all interested parties to respond.At this time only limited resources are available for capital funding and it is unlikely that additional funding will be available to invest in a new funding programme specifically for boxing. As this issue is also an operational matter for Sport NI, I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI have no programmes currently open for applications.Sport NI is planning a new Creating Opportunities funding programme. The investment programme offers match-funding support from Sport NI through the Crowdfunder platform and will be open to organisations including boxing clubs. Creating Opportunities is a programme designed to connect communities, enabling more people to take part in sport and physical activity. In particular, a focus of the investment is to create opportunities to ‘level the playing field’, so that accessibility and inclusion is felt and experienced by those who have been traditionally excluded from not only participating in sport but playing an active role within the club.  In addition, Sport NI has plans to operate a further Community Planning Investment Programme for this year. Applications to this programme will come from District Councils, but as in FY23-24, it is anticipated that clubs (and other organisations such as Governing Bodies; Community organisations) could be involved as delivery partners for projects.” For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the initiatives being implemented to integrate restorative justice practices in the criminal justice system.
answer_text: Restorative Justice has been at the core of the youth justice system in Northern Ireland for over two decades.  Based on this success, my intentions to embed Restorative Justice as an integral part of the adult criminal justice system were set out in the Department’s Adult Restorative Justice Strategy, published in March 2022.  My officials have been working with key partners to deliver on the commitments outlined in the Strategy’s five-year Action Plan.  As we enter year three of the Strategy, significant progress has already been made on a number of these actions, which has helped to establish a base on which future progress can be built.A key action for Year 1 of the Action Plan was to review and refresh the 2007 Protocol, which governs the use of restorative approaches in the criminal justice system.  Having appointed an independent panel of experts to conduct this review, I signed off on their report, which was published in February 2023, and the new agreed Protocol was published in July 2023. One of the key recommendations in the panel’s report was the appointment of a full-time Interim Protocol Lead (IPL) to progress the plans laid out in the new 2023 Protocol.  The IPL was appointed on 20 February 2024 and work on developing training standards and designing a new accreditation process is already well under way, with the help of the Restorative Justice Working Group and other key stakeholders. This work is the key to enabling the increased use of Restorative Justice as an integral part of the justice system and in the ongoing delivery of the Strategy’s Action Plan.   My officials have also been working alongside the PSNI to explore restorative diversionary approaches for adults.  A pilot scheme has been launched in North and West Belfast which incorporates a restorative element within Community Resolution Notices for certain low level offences for first time offenders.  If successful, the approach will be expanded to other areas and be used to inform the delivery of Restorative Justice across other stages of the criminal justice system.Progress made in implementing the Strategy and Action Plan is reported by my Department annually.  The first Annual Report was published in July 2023, with the second report currently being developed.  Delivery of Year 3 actions is being planned, alongside progressing ongoing actions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for her assessment of (i) the impact of educational and vocational training programs on the youth justice strategy; and (ii) reoffending rates by those engaged educational and vocational training.
answer_text: The Youth Justice Agency carries out two types of educational programmes.  The first is a schools programme, delivered by Agency staff, which focuses on prevention by raising awareness of offending behaviour and promoting better decision-making within partner schools.  The second is direct education and vocational learning provision within Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre.  During the 2023/24 financial year, within the YJA schools programme, staff delivered 168 workshops to over 5,000 children in 31 schools covering issues such as online safety, drugs & alcohol awareness and the impact of violence and offending.  YJA surveyed all participants and 77% of pupils felt the workshops would help them avoid offending.In Woodlands Juvenile Justice Centre, during the financial year 2023/24, 5535 lessons on core curriculum and essential skills and 1923 lessons on vocational subjects were delivered.  107 qualifications were gained by children in custody and 77% of children were linked to an education/training/employment placement in the community on discharge.The YJA schools programme is preventative and while the programme is strategically targeted to schools which draw pupils from areas where there is evidence of higher rates of offending or anti-social behaviour among children, it is working with a broad range of children, most of whom will not be known to the criminal justice system.  It is difficult to isolate the contribution of one component on reoffending and so there is no data available on the impact of education and vocational provision in Woodlands on reoffending rates.  However, it is recognised both locally and internationally that the provision of consistent, high quality educational and vocational learning support is an important element in supporting positive rehabilitation and community re-integration.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail his Department's long-term strategy to ensure consistent and reliable mobile telephone signal coverage for rural areas.
answer_text: Telecommunications policy is a reserved matter with the Department for Science, Innovation & Technology (DSIT) who set strategic direction and hold the policy responsibility on improvements to mobile connectivity. The Department for the Economy (DfE) maintains regular contact with DSIT on all telecommunications matters ensuring that all local issues are given appropriate consideration for any policy decisions taken regarding the improvement of mobile connectivity here.DfE understands the many challenges faced by our rural communities in terms of digital connectivity and indeed the issues faced by Mobile Network Operators (MNOs) when they attempt to roll out telecommunications infrastructure. My officials are involved in several ongoing initiatives which will help improve mobile connectivity here. The Shared Rural Network (SRN), is a project developed by the four MNOs (EE, Vodafone, VMO2 and Three) and the British Government. Locally, the SRN is expected to deliver 85% 4G coverage from all four MNOs and 98% coverage from at least one MNO.The SRN is expected to extend 4G coverage in the most cost-effective, efficient, timely and environmentally friendly way for consumers and businesses in these remote rural communities. My officials participate in the local SRN Working Group to provide a local perspective.More information about the SRN can be found at https://srn.org.uk.To help address wider challenges faced by the MNOs with regard to telecommunications infrastructure roll out, my officials led on the development of the published cross-sectoral Mobile Action Plan for Northern Ireland (MAP NI) and established the Northern Ireland Barrier Busting Taskforce (NIBBT), which aims to identify the main issues that impact on the roll-out of mobile technologies and provide solutions to remove barriers that prevent the fast and efficient deployment of mobile connectivity here. You can view the MAP NI implementation updates at https://www.economy-ni.gov.uk/articles/map-ni-implementation-progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether there are any specific projects or partnerships with telecom companies aimed at enhancing connectivity across rural East Antrim.
answer_text: Project Stratum is a broadband intervention scheme developed to improve connectivity for premises unable to access broadband services of 30 Mbps or greater, primarily across rural areas.  The contract to deliver Project Stratum was awarded to Fibrus Networks in November 2020. 91% of the 1,700 premises in the East Antrim constituency that are within scope of Project Stratum are now able to avail of a gigabit-capable broadband service under the scheme.  The remaining premises will be addressed by June 2025.My Department has completed a review of current and planned broadband coverage to identify where intervention under Project Gigabit, a programme to enable hard-to-reach communities to access gigabit-capable broadband, is needed. Pre-procurement meetings with companies interested in the project were held in May of this year, and an Outline Business Case for the project is being completed. I expect a procurement to commence later in the summer, aiming to award a contract for the delivery of gigabit-capable services by the end of the financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail when fisheries in East Antrim will be restocked.
answer_text: Until appropriate biosecurity protocols can be put in place in order to comply with the Wildlife Order (NI) 1985, the Department is unable to confirm when it will be in a position to commence restocking of the Department’s Public Angling Estate (PAE) waters.    	Officials are continuing to work with the Agri-Food and Biosciences Institute to develop a protocol that will prevent the transfer of zebra mussels and allow stocking of fish from Movanagher Fish Farm to the PAE waters.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy, in light of research conducted in 2013 which states that marching and Loyal Orders bands generate almost £55 million in economic and social benefits each year, whether he will be including unionist historical and cultural celebrations, such as the Royal Landing Parade in Carrickfergus, in his tourism action plan.
answer_text: The Tourism Action Plan will be co-designed by the Tourism Partnership Board (TPB). I anticipate that development of authentic tourism experiences which bring our unique culture and heritage to life will be a key topic for discussion and joint action for the TPB.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he will develop a register of autism-trained staff to better support individuals with autism.
answer_text: My Department intends to provide autism training across all of Health and Social Care (HSC), rather than a specialist group of individuals.  All HSC Trusts offer training and support to all staff in the effective support of those patients with communication challenges and barriers, including autism, when accessing services, whilst also supporting staff to be more aware.  HSC Trusts do take a service-wide approach to addressing the needs of those with Autism. This includes training for all staff, either run by individual Trusts, or commissioned from external organisations, as well as self-directed learning for those with a particular identified training need.Exploratory work is already underway to consider improvements to this package of training.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the funding of hybrid closed loop systems, to detail the process for a child moving from paediatric care to the adult diabetes clinic.
answer_text: In December 2023, the National Institute for Health and Care Excellence published final guidance on Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes.  My Department has formally endorsed the guidance as applicable to Northern Ireland, following review by the Department’s Medicines Policy Branch with service notification issued, 12th June 2024. Work is being undertaken to understand the implications of this decision in terms of overall provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support, including financial considerations given scale of investment needed. This will inform final decision-making and commissioning arrangements for NI across all patient cohorts and will be communicated to all stakeholders.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of the waiting list for an Occupational Therapist (OT) assessment for children, whether he will consider flexibility surrounding the current requirement for a Health and Social Care Trust OT service report for access to the Disabled Facilities Grants, by allowing a private OT report to be accepted.
answer_text: There are clear statutory obligations under the Housing Order 2003 Article 55 (3) for the Housing Executive to “consult with the relevant authority” when determining the works to be undertaken at a private property for disabled adaptations.  This relevant authority is the Health & Social Care Trusts.Occupational Therapists (OT’s) are nominated on behalf of the Health & Social Care Trusts to carry out assessments to determine the needs of disabled people, including their housing needs.  If an adaptation is required the OT will make a recommendation to the Housing Executive and, where appropriate, will inspect the adaptation when it is completed to ensure it is adequate for the client’s needs. This is a statutory responsibility of the Health & Social Care Trusts.Private OT’s cannot fulfil this statutory obligation as the legislation requires external and professional opinion by the relevant Health authority on whether the works are necessary and appropriate to meet the needs of the disabled occupant.Consequently, private OT reports cannot be accepted for access to Disabled Facilities Grants.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to provide an update on the actions taken by his Department since the Assembly passed the motion on the implementation of the Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, on 30 April 2024.
answer_text: My Department continues to deliver against the duties set out in the legislation, to develop, implement and monitor the Autism strategy.  The Autism Strategy 2023-2028 which was published, along with a two-year 2023-2025 Delivery Plan, on 13 December 2023 focuses on the implementation of the requirements of the Autism (Amendment) Act (NI) 2022.   I hope to be in a position to update the Assembly in relation to the appointment of an Autism Reviewer, in the coming weeks, following the conclusion of purdah. In terms of the implementation of the Strategy, my officials are working with delivery partners and across government departments to finalise a monitoring report for year one of implementation, which will be shared with the Assembly when it is complete.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the impact that the proposed changes in HM Treasury's consultation on the VAT Treatment of Private Hire Vehicles will have on the local taxi industry.
answer_text: I can advise the member that VAT falls outside the remit of my Department as a reserved matter and remains the responsibility of Treasury and Revenue and Customs.  However, you may wish to note that my officials have made the local taxi sector and the NI Consumer Council aware of the Consultation which launched 18 April and closes 8 August 2024. I have written to local taxi operators and drivers encouraging them to respond to the consultation. The potential VAT implications may affect each business differently, but I believe that a strong response from the sector will provide powerful evidence to negate the need for further tax burdens on the industry.I also intend to write to the Department for Transport to express my concern about the potential implications for our local taxi industry and its passengers. You may wish to note that the Consultation invites views on potential Treasury interventions to mitigate undue adverse effects of the Court Rulings in England and Wales. I would also take this opportunity to ask you to encourage any stakeholders you come into contact with to provide feedback to the Consultation. My officials can provide you with details of the link.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of his Department's announcement of the RAISE Programme, to provide an update on the design and implementation of this scheme.
answer_text: The RAISE Programme is a major new initiative to be delivered across Northern Ireland, in specific localities, to reduce educational disadvantage.This investment will support area-based partnership approaches over the next two years, and potentially extend further subject to evaluation.  It will enable delivery of a bespoke local Strategic Plan for each place, codesigned by schools and other settings, families/carers, and others with valuable roles to play in supporting children’s learning in communities across these localities.My officials have begun the engagement process with a series of workshops with the schools who service the pupils in the 15 identified localities. The next step will be engaging with the community in the localities and political representatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, in light of his comments in relation to developing Northern Ireland and America connections, how his Department will explore and promote the unique history of East Antrim.
answer_text: The 250th anniversary of the establishment of the United States of America provides for a unique opportunity to deliver a programme of cultural engagement that promotes the links between Northern Ireland and North America.My Department and its arms-length bodies will work with a wide range of partners including local councils to maximise the benefits of our close relationship with the US and to recognise the influence of the Ulster-Scots in shaping American society, including those from East Antrim.I have established a United States / Northern Ireland Cultural Working Group which involves key agencies here and in the United States to develop a collaborative programme of initiatives to promote events with the United States. I met with the group on 25 June to progress this important work and I was delighted that James Applegate, the US Consul General for Northern Ireland participated in the meeting and offered the US administrations support in taking for this work. I recognise the deep and important historical connections between East Antrim and the US including the ancestral home of Andrew Jackson, 7th President of the United States. and will ensure that it’s contribution will be included within the wider programme of cultural activities being developed by the US / NI Cultural Working Group.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will explore the digitalisation of the special educational needs placement process to improve the process for parents and carers.
answer_text: Ensuring that every child has access to an education placement which meets their needs is a key priority for my department.One of the workstreams of the End to End (E2E) Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN) is focused on SEN placements and area planning.  A review of the SEN placement process is ongoing and proposals are being developed to improve the process for children and their families.  This will include consideration of opportunities to streamline the SEN placements process.   Digitisation of the SEN placements process is not straightforward as children enter the SEN assessment process on an ongoing basis and are placed in accordance with their individually assessed needs, making it difficult to set application opening and closing dates, in a similar manner to the general schools admissions process.  However, this is something that will be explored jointly with the Education Authority (EA).  Implementation of any such proposals will require additional financial investment.There has been some progress on the digitalisation of the statutory assessment and review process for children with a statement of SEN.  EA Connect has been developed in the last two years to improve parental/carer engagement with their child’s annual review of their statement.  This development has allowed data to become more readily available to the EA to inform and confirm placements.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to reopen the Your School Your Club initiative to help promote community use of school sports facilities.
answer_text: I am fully committed to the Your School Your Club initiative and as such I have allocated funding to enable my Department to contribute towards the initiative this year, subject to the outcome of the current Equality Impact Consultation on the Budget.  On confirmation of the budget, the Your School Your Club Joint Working Group, which is led by my Department and includes representatives from Sport NI, Department of Education, Education Authority, local Councils and Department for Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs, will agree and commence the process to engage with schools to identify project proposals that can be delivered by March 2025.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the reform of the Housing Executive points system.
answer_text: Work is ongoing in relation to the Fundamental Review of Social Housing Allocations. I have approved Stage 2 and my officials are working with the Housing Executive to ensure the implementation of these proposals.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail the action his Department will take to increase and support the delivery of physical education in post-primary school settings.
answer_text: My Department recognises the importance of physical activity and the contribution it makes to the health and wellbeing of young people.  It is for that reason that Physical Education (PE) is a compulsory part of the statutory curriculum for all pupils at each key stage.  There is no statutory time prescribed for any subject, including PE, in post-primary schools; however, the Department recommends that schools should provide children and young people with at least two hours of good quality curricular PE each week. This contributes to the well-being and development of resilience, and allows for the progression of skills, knowledge and understanding in the range of activity areas that make up the PE curriculum.My Department also recognises the need to develop a sector-wide approach to strategic teacher workforce planning and is working closely with the Education Authority and other education partners to address this need. However, to date, post-primary PE has not been identified as a subject in which there is any significant shortage of suitably qualified teachers.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the provision of baby loss certificates.
answer_text: I am keen to see this scheme taken forward as quickly as possible to give bereaved families the opportunity to have formal recognition of their loss.Discussions are taking place regularly between the Department of Health and General Register Office officials as to how the baby loss scheme work may be developed. My officials and those from the Department of Health have met with managers from the Department of Health and Social Care who deliver the Baby Loss Certificate scheme in England. A meeting has also taken place with National Records of Scotland officials to investigate how the Scottish scheme operates.This ongoing engagement will provide important lessons for the development of a scheme here.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the report entitled An Investigation of Literacy Instruction and Policy in the United Kingdom and Ireland, published on 29 April 2024, whether he will review his Department’s advice on phonics instruction.
answer_text: 25 June 2024AQW 13486/22-27Cheryl Brownlee MLA has asked:To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the report entitled An Investigation of Literacy Instruction and Policy in the United Kingdom and Ireland, published on 29 April 2024, whether he will review his Department’s advice on phonics instruction.	ANSWEROfficials from my Department are currently considering the report.Paul Givan MLAMinister of Education

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how her Department is tackling organised crime.
answer_text: My Department is taking forward a range of work in relation to organised crime.  I will shortly publish an addendum work plan to the 2021 – 2024 Organised Crime Strategy, covering this financial year.  Delivery of actions under this, targeting organised crime objectives, is being taken forward in partnership with the Organised Crime Task Force (OCTF).  The OCTF brings together law enforcement agencies, along with Government Departments, academia and the business community to combat organised crime.  It provides strategic leadership and supports collaborative, joined-up responses to the threat posed by organised crime and the harm it causes.  The OCTF Strategy Group oversees a number of thematic subgroups with a focus on drugs; cybercrime; criminal finance; modern slavery and human trafficking; and intellectual property crime.  The  OCTF publishes an annual report to highlight the efforts and successes of partners who work together to tackle organised crime in Northern Ireland.  Further information on all aspects of the OCTF’s work can be found at:OCTF Annual Report and Threat Assessment 2022-2023 | Organised Crime Task ForceIn addition to disruption activities, operational partners ongoing work to recover criminal assets has enabled money confiscated by the courts, and allocated by my Department, to be distributed through the Assets Recovery Community Scheme (ARCS), to support initiatives aimed at preventing crime and reducing the fear of crime across local communities.Work is also underway on new Northern Ireland serious organised crime legislation and, through future Westminster legislation, it is hoped to bring forward a number of provisions locally on reform of confiscation powers to strip criminals of proceeds of crime and offences related to articles used in serious crime.I am also the lead Minister for the Executive’s cross-departmental programme to tackle organised criminality linked to paramilitarism (EPPOC), which is now in its second phase (2021-2025) and has evolved into a comprehensive and evidence-based approach to violence and harm reduction.  Significant and positive outcomes are being seen across multiple domains.  This includes major disruptions to criminal groups, increases in protective factors for those vulnerable to criminal exploitation and measurable reductions in risk, intent to engage in violence, and complex trauma. There is close collaboration between the EPPOC and the wider work of the Department of Justice to tackle organised crime.My Department continues to work closely with criminal justice partners across all aspects of our work.  The priority is to ensure that collective efforts are focused on the pursuit of criminals, using all available tools to seize the proceeds of crime and send a powerful message that crime does not pay.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how his Department plans to address mental health issues, especially among the younger population.
answer_text: The Mental Health Strategy, published in 2021, sets out a clear direction of travel to support and promote good mental health, provide early intervention to prevent serious mental illness, provide the right response when a person needs specialist help and support, as well as outlining how the system will work to implement these changes. The key aim of the Strategy is to ensure long-term improved outcomes for the mental health of the population. Work to implement the actions from the Strategy is ongoing.In relation to the younger population, the Strategy sets out a number of key actions to further improve the emotional wellbeing and mental health of our children and young people, from infancy through to older adolescents.  Actions 1 and 2 of the Strategy set out key commitments for promoting mental health through early intervention and prevention, with year-on-year actions covering a whole life approach, reaching from infancy to older age through the development of a three-year Early Intervention and Prevention Plan. One of the key actions within this plan is to ensure collective responsibility for promoting and supporting the emotional wellbeing of all our infants, children and young people.Actions 10 and 11 set out a need to increase the funding for CAMHS as well as ensuring the needs of vulnerable children and young people are met within CAMHS.
