An additional £2m was invested in CAMHS services through the Strategy in 2023/24 to help address pressures within inpatient and community CAMHS and work is continuing to develop longer term investment plans in recognition of the commitment set out in the Strategy to increase CAMHS funding to 10% of adult mental health funding.In addition to the Mental Health Strategy, in February 2021, the Department of Health and the Department of Education jointly launched the Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing in Education Framework.  The main emphasis of the Framework is to support educational settings to promote emotional health and wellbeing at a universal level, through a holistic, multi-disciplinary approach, building resilience to enable our children and young people to cope with challenges they may face in life and providing early and enhanced support for those who may be at risk or showing signs of needing further help. Services and projects that have been established under the Framework are available across primary and post-primary schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) to detail the response he has submitted, or will submit, to the HM Treasury consultation on VAT Treatment of Private Hire Vehicles; and (ii) for his assessment of the impact the proposed changes will have on the taxi industry.
answer_text: I have been awaiting the outcome of the British General Election and will now make representations to the relevant Department in London, making them aware of my opposition to the proposal to introduce VAT on Private Hire Vehicles.However, you may wish to note that my officials have made the local taxi sector and the NI Consumer Council aware of the Consultation which launched 18 April and closes 8 August 2024. I also intend to write to the Department for Transport to express my concern about the potential implications for our local taxi industry and its passengers. The Consultation invites views on potential Treasury interventions to mitigate undue adverse effects of the Court Rulings in England and Wales. I would also take this opportunity to ask you to encourage any stakeholders you come into contact with to provide feedback to the Consultation – link below.  www.gov.uk/government/consultations/consultation-on-the-vat-treatment-of-private-hire-vehicles.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions he will take to counteract any negative impact the proposed VAT Treatment of Private Hire Vehicles changes will have on the older population who rely on taxis for travel.
answer_text: I have wrote to the Rt Hon. Louise Haigh MP, Secretary of State for Transport, to comment on the significant concerns expressed locally by the taxi industry and place on record that I do not support any additional burdens that will potentially fall on taxi businesses here.

question_text: To ask the Assembly Commission to outline what work it will undertake to ensure there is a family friendly working environment for people who work in Parliament Buildings.
To ask the Minister of Health for an update on how he will ensure patients' information on encompass will be kept secure from cyber-attack.
answer_text: The encompass environment is subject to proactive 24/7 security monitoring and reporting to ensure that it is only accessed by known and suitably qualified HSCNI staff and from authorised equipment.  The overall security of the encompass solution is independently verified and quality assured by accredited third party vendors on a regular basis.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, given that the data used to produce the Prevalence of Autism in School Aged Children in Northern Ireland Report, sourced from the annual School Census and collated by his Department, has not been produced for the first time since 2011, what action his Department will take to insure this data is collated as a matter of urgency.
answer_text: The data used to produce the “Prevalence of Autism in School Aged Children in Northern Ireland” is sourced from the School Census.  The 2023/24 School Census data collection has been impacted by industrial action, due to Action Short of Strike. Although data has been received from all schools, for 25% of schools (primary, post primary and special schools) it has not been possible to process pupil level information as census information was submitted on paper.  For those schools who submitted their school census on paper, special education needs and medical condition information was not included.  Therefore, it has not been possible to provide autism data for all pupils attending schools in Northern Ireland.To collate this information would require the census data collection to be rerun. Many schools have started to migrate to a new management system (Edis) so the rerunning of the census is technically impossible at this time. Regrettably, it is not possible to undertake this work at this point in the year.  The information will be collected through the school census in October.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a schedule for upcoming planned maintenance of (i) roads; and (ii) grass verges in East Antrim.
answer_text: Routine road maintenance is generally carried out in a reactive manner, whereby defects can either be identified internally through regular highway inspections or reported externally.  If the defect meets the required threshold, in accordance with current policy, it will be taken forward for repair.Grass verges within East Antrim, and other constituencies, are maintained in accordance with my Department’s verge management policy.  The policy outlines the frequency of grass cutting on all categories of public road.  Further details of this policy can be found at:https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/publications/verge-management-grass-cutting-dem-19322

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will undertake a review of the current number of grass cuts per year on high traffic rural roads.
answer_text: I announced a new approach to verge management in October 2022 which seeks to increase the focus on environmental benefit, without compromising roads safety or the duty to deal with noxious weeds on Department maintained land. It also supports the actions needed to comply with the Climate Change Bill passed by the Assembly. Information on the Department’s grass cutting regime is available at: verge-management-(grass-cutting)-dem-193-22.pdf (infrastructure-ni.gov.uk) There are no plans to review the number of cuts per year on high traffic rural roads.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education to detail (i) the criteria; and (ii) the appeals process for exceptional circumstances in relation to funded preschool places.
answer_text: The remit of the Exceptional Circumstances Body (ECB) is limited to considering applications relating to post-primary school admissions only.  Parents may appeal to an Independent Appeal Tribunal during the pre-schools’ admission process in relation to a decision refusing their child’s admission to a statutory pre-school setting, only on the grounds that the Board of Governors, by or on whose behalf the decision was made, did not apply, or did not correctly apply, its published admissions criteria.  There is no other basis on which parents can submit an appeal.  Details on how parents can submit an appeal are published on the EA website following the issue of admissions decision letters.There is no formal appeals process for admissions to non-statutory pre-school settings.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for his assessment of the operational differences on travel between the Blind Smartpass and the Senior Smartpass.
answer_text: Senior (65+) SmartPasses are valid on cross border bus and rail services, and can also be used for free public transport journeys taken entirely within the Republic of Ireland on services run by participating operators.Registered Blind SmartPasses whilst valid on all participating services in the North  are  valid on point-to-point cross border bus and rail services but not on journeys made entirely within the South of Ireland.On presentation Senior SmartPass holders will be issued with a ticket and as stated in the terms and conditions for the scheme individuals are responsible for checking the ticket and raising any issues regarding the ticket to the driver. The Registered Blind SmartPass does not carry this requirement and as such no ticket is issued.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) for his assessment of current GP waiting times; and (ii) how he intends to make GP practices more accessible to the public.
answer_text: GP practices are under significant pressures at present as they deal with immediate demands and the consequences of wider service pressures.  GP services have been experiencing a sustained, high level of demand since before the COVID-19 pandemic.  Practices have therefore continued to adapt how services are delivered in order to make best use of available resources for everyone who is seeking to access the care that they need.   It is important that primary care continues to transform to meet growing patient demand.As independent contractors, GPs manage the day to day running of their practices, including the use of technology, telephony and management of their appointment system within the constraints of their available workforce.  Under the general practice telephone first consultations model, it allows patients to seek timely medical advice from their GP for both routine and urgent problems.  The GP determines the most appropriate approach to safely addressing the patient’s needs using their clinical expertise.  Where a face to face appointment is appropriate, that will be arranged.  Alternatively, the GP may decide, based on their clinical assessment, that a telephone consultation is appropriate or that the patient should be signposted or directed to other relevant services.   This approach has allowed practices to maintain the majority of GP services and has enabled GPs to appropriately manage, treat and support a greater number of people than would otherwise have been the case.  With the level of pressure on the service continuing to rise, the use of telephone consultations is an important part of how GPs can treat and support the large number of patients who need their services.Previously, significant funding has been made available to enable GPs to improve their telephony systems, improve accessibility and increase staffing levels to ensure telephony demand is managed effectively.Over the past number of years, there has been additional investment to build capacity in Primary Care through the roll out of Multi-disciplinary Teams (MDTs), and the introduction of new roles such as Advanced Nurse Practitioners, additional General Practice Nurses and General Practice Pharmacists; all of which are making a difference to the capacity of primary care services and how those services are delivered; and are contributing to improved patient outcomes.  My Department has recently agreed the 2024/25 General Medical Services contract with NI General Practitioners Committee.  These new contract arrangements will provide GPs with greater certainty over their income throughout the year, as well as reducing administrative burdens and associated costs to practices.  These settlements recognise the pivotal role played by general practice within out health and social care system and the Department’s commitments to taking measures to improve the sustainability of general practice provision.One of the key features of the 2024/25 contract is the Northern Ireland Co

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for an update on the Paediatric Autism Spectrum Disorder pathway.
answer_text: My officials have met with all Trusts to map current paediatric ASD referral/assessment pathways. Following this mapping exercise, officials within my Department are working with colleagues from Public Health Agency to develop a regionally consistent pathway that complies with NICE guidelines and take into consideration best practice, emerging evidence and the recognition that workforce planning is critical to meeting need. This work will be taken forward by the Joint Children’s Commissioning Group chaired by Deputy Directors from my Department and the PHA. In addition to this work, my Department has recently published the cross-departmental Autism Strategy 2023-2028 including an action plan for implementation.   The Autism Strategy 2023 – 2028 Delivery Plan 2023-2025 also includes an action to consult on and finalise a Children and Young People’s Emotional Health and Wellbeing Framework which will guide future development of services. The Framework will embrace a move towards understanding Autism within a broader neurodevelopmental approach and incorporate a regionally consistent single point of entry and diagnostic pathway.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice for a breakdown of the release process for those convicted under the Sexual Offences Act 2003.
answer_text: The Sexual Offences (NI) Order 2008 is the relevant legislation in Northern Ireland.  Those convicted under this Order are subject to the sentencing and subsequent release arrangements prescribed in either the Life Sentences (NI) Order 2001 or the Criminal Justice (NI) Order 2008.  The specific arrangements which apply to each case will depend on whether the sentencing court decides the offending should attract a life sentence, an indeterminate custodial sentence, an extended custodial sentence or a determinate custodial sentence.  The release arrangements for each type of sentence are as follows: A life sentence prisoner or a prisoner serving an indeterminate custodial sentence will only be released following a release direction from the Parole Commissioners.  A prisoner serving an extended custodial sentence may be released earlier than the release date specified by the sentencing judge but again this will only be after a release direction from the Parole Commissioners.  A prisoner serving a determinate custodial sentence will have their automatic release date determined at the time of sentencing.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for an update on the status of Carrickfergus Academy new build campus.
answer_text: The procurement of an Integrated Consultant Team for the project has commenced and the assessment of the Pre-Qualification Questionnaire element of the process has been undertaken.  It is estimated that a Call for Tender will issue to Integrated Supply Teams in July 2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 6792/22-27, what further work his Department has undertaken to make progress in addressing the needs of those suffering with Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder.
answer_text: Pursuant to AQW6792/22-27, officials from within my Department’s Strategic Planning and Performance Group have liaised with community and acute paediatricians regarding addressing the needs of those children presenting with ARFID. Clinicians felt that a multi-disciplinary approach is required to address future planning and treatment of the increasing incidences of this emerging issue.  This may include consideration of amending pathways and ensuring clinicians have the necessary skills and expertise to meet the need.The Department of Health will establish a multi-disciplinary steering group under the direction of the Child Health Partnership. This steering group will consider a range of clinical issues that affect children and young people and how we respond, regionally. Increased incidences of ARFID will be included in these discussions.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, pursuant to AQW 8873/22-27, what further work his Department has undertaken to increase (i) allowances paid to foster carers; and (ii) fees paid to those foster carers who care for children with complex needs.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the investments being made in transport infrastructure to improve connectivity (i) within Northern Ireland; and (ii) with the rest of the United Kingdom and Ireland.
answer_text: My decisions on indicative budget allocations, which are subject to equality considerations, include investment in transport infrastructure to improve connectivity across these islands.This includes an overall planned investment of £245m in the public transport network, which includes funding for the Coleraine - Derry Phase 3 project, which will allow Translink to progress the project as planned and seeks to ensure investment in public transport across the entire region. An element of funding for the Enterprise Replacement Programme in included which will provide the public transport investment required to improve connectivity across Ireland. Shared Island funding of €12.5m was announced in February 2024 to fund an additional 7 daily trips between Dublin and Belfast, for 3 years.In addition, overall planned investment in Major Roads Projects across, is £107m for 2024-25, which also contributes to the connectivity across these islands.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide an update on planned resurfacing works in East Antrim in the 2024-25 financial year.
answer_text: Having decided on indicative budget allocations, my officials can now consider the detailed programmes of work across the Department’s areas of responsibility, including resurfacing works within the East Antrim constituency.	Officials will then list all planned resurfacing schemes within my Department’s 2024/25 annual report to local councils.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance (i) how the £30 million from the Levelling Up Fund will be allocated; and (ii) whether organisations will be entitled to make applications for this funding.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer for AQW 12535-22-27.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) the number of visitors; (ii) the number of hire applications received; and (iii) the number of hire applications rejected, for each state care monument in the last three years.
answer_text: My Department is responsible for 187 State Care Monuments. Visitor numbers are recorded at six locations: Carrickfergus Castle, Derry’s Walls, Devenish Ecclesiastical site, Dunluce Castle, Ballycopeland Windmill and Enniskillen Castle.Data is captured on applications to use the State Care Monuments for a range of purposes including running events, filming and photography. Data on applications to hire locations is not specifically captured. I have arranged for the information on visitor numbers and applications over the last three years to be placed in the Assembly Library due to the volume of information provided (Annex A).

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether new procurement practices are being developed for the Education Authority.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is designated as education’s Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPE).  This means the EA is directly responsible for the procurement of goods, services and works on behalf of schools. The EA has been developing a new procurement strategy for its new maintenance and minor works contracts that will be based on defined geographical areas and will allow schools the greater freedom to engage locally approved suppliers.  In addition, the EA has undertaken a detailed review of schools’ tenant/ landlord responsibilities. As a result, the EA plans to introduce new arrangements, with the aim of providing greater flexibility for the delivery of Local Management of Schools (LMS) low-value maintenance works and cost certainty across the EA schools’ estate. The EA will also explore, where appropriate, the application of this type of delivery model more widely across the range of goods and services which it currently procures on behalf of schools.Finally, and in closing, I also understand that the Minister of Finance has commissioned a review of Public Procurement, including the Procurement Board, in line with recommendations made by the Northern Ireland Audit Office in its April 2023 Review of Public Procurement.  Both my Department and the EA look forward to contributing to this review.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department will provide an uplift in funding to the Supporting People Programme given the inflationary pressures on project delivery.
answer_text: In March 2024, I announced £3m of additional funding for the Supporting People Programme for the 2023/24 financial year. The 2024-25 budget awarded to my Department by the NI Executive is heavily constrained and difficult decisions were required to manage pressures and maintain service provision, to deliver a balanced budget for 2024-25.Notwithstanding the constraints, I have decided to provide a £4.8m increase in funding for the Supporting People Programme above the 2023-24 Final EQIA Budget position, to help ensure continued viability of the programme.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health (i) what consideration his Department has given to increasing the monthly allowance of glucose monitoring devices; and (ii) whether his Department will review the monthly allowance to improve access to these devices for users.
answer_text: There are many glucose monitoring systems available to support people living with diabetes. The most appropriate system for each individual is the responsibility of the HSC Trust and their clinicians to determine in consultation with the patient and/or the patient’s carer.  Some more complex Continuous Glucose Monitoring devices such as the Dexcom G6 CGM device, forms part of the Hybrid Closed Loop system but are not listed on the NI Drug tariff and are not routinely commissioned due to current financial pressures.In December 2023, NICE published guidance on Hybrid Closed Loop systems for Type 1 diabetes. This has been endorsed, following review, by the Department’s Medicines Policy Branch. Work is being undertaken to understand decision implications, including financial considerations given scale of investment needed and final decision regarding implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail (i) the number of vacancies in procurement roles in the Northern Ireland Civil Service, broken down by Department; and (ii) any actions that will be taken to address this.
answer_text: ANSWERThere are nine Centres of Procurement Expertise. Four of these Centres of Procurement Expertise (CoPEs) are within the Northern Ireland Civil Service. i) The total number of vacancies in the NICS CoPEs is 153. For completeness, the table below includes the number of vacancies in all nine CoPEs. The total number of vacancies in all CoPEs is 228. It should also be noted that a number of CoPEs will be responsible for client-side functions including engineering, design, contract specification, sourcing and management of projects/contracts, whereas some CoPEs’ primary responsibility will be related to sourcing the goods, services or works on behalf of their client. ii)  When a CoPE within an NICS Department identifies a vacancy to be filled and confirms that it is affordable, the request is referred to NICSHR and added to the recruitment caseload.  NICSHR will take forward the processes required to fill affordable vacancies from a range of suitable recruitment competitions or other resourcing solutions.Vacancies (as of May 24)Centre of Procurement Expertise (CoPEs)Buyers/ProcurementAdministrative Staff/Non-TechnicalTechnicalTotal No. VacanciesNICS CoPEs    CPD Supplies & Services Division (SSD)8109CPD Construction Division (CD)033134DoH Health Estates041014DfI Transport and Roads Asset Management1038396Wider Public Sector CoPEs    Education Authority (EA)Commercial Procurement Services103013Education Authority (EA)Project Managers Construction Delivery051924NI Water2002NI Housing Executive 6107Translink3003HSC Business Services Organisation Procurement and Logistics215026Total6025143228

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance to detail the number of businesses in the East Antrim constituency that (i) applied for Hardship Rates Relief; and (ii) were successful in their application, in the last five years, broken down by District Electoral Area.
answer_text: LPS does not hold this detail at District Electoral Area level. In the last five years, applications for Hardship Rate Relief have been received from businesses located in the following District Councils:			Causeway Coast and Glens:	10		Mid and East Antrim:		  3		Antrim and Newtownabbey:	20 		 (ii) 	There has been no award of Hardship Rate Relief made in any of the above named District Councils in the last five years.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister (i) for an update on Good Relations funding; and (ii) to provide a timescale on when Good Relations funding will be allocated.
answer_text: The assessment process for the Central Good Relations Fund 2024/25 has completed. All applicants will be updated on the status of their application once a budget allocation to the Fund is confirmed for 2024/25.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the planned road resurfacing work for the East Antrim constituency in (i) 2024; and (ii) 2025.
answer_text: Now that the Executive has agreed the 2024-25 Budget, my Department is considering how its funding will be allocated across its functions including road resurfacing. Once this is finalised my Roads Divisions will complete and publish their programmes. As part of my Department's annual report to councils, the programme for each council area will be delivered to the relevant Council at their meeting which will be scheduled in due course.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (i) for his assessment on the application of animal rights and policy in Mid and East Antrim Borough Council; (ii) how the performance and application of the legislation and policy by animal welfare officers is monitored and assessed; and (iii) whether his Department will provide a timeframe to strengthen animal welfare laws to counter increasing instances of animal cruelty.
answer_text: My Department’s animal welfare legislation applies across all of Northern Ireland and councils are responsible for the implementation and enforcement of the law, as it pertains to non-farmed animals.  Councils are best placed to determine how this function should be discharged to meet the specific requirements of their region and my department has no recourse to assess how this service is delivered. I have listened to the calls for reform of animal welfare in Northern Ireland and carefully considered what is needed to enhance protections for our animals. I am determined to bring forward animal welfare legislation; however, I am mindful of what can be delivered within the resources available to my department and the time remaining in this mandate. Nevertheless, I can confirm that, over the next 12 months, it is my intention to bring forward various pieces of legislation which will further protect Northern Ireland’s animals, including restrictions on the third-party sale of pups and kittens.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy (i) for her assessment of the impact the closure of the B90 Upper Road, Greenisland, will have on local businesses; (ii) what support (a) can be provided; and (b) has been offered to the businesses impacted.
answer_text: My Department has not carried out an impact assessment on the closure of B90 Upper Road, Greenisland to local businesses. The aim of the (DfI) resurfacing scheme is to improve the current road network that will ultimately benefit both residents and road users.No support has been provided by my Department to local businesses. DfI has ensured that access to premises has been maintained, albeit via a signed diversionary route that is in operation during the hours of the road closure.DfI officials have advised that they are keenly aware of the disruption caused by essential road works and thank road users for their continued patience while this (and any) infrastructure work is carried out.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy to detail any financial support available for the hospitality sector in light of the increasing costs businesses are facing.
answer_text: I understand the importance of the hospitality sector for both our tourism industry and the high street economy, with approximately 57,000 jobs in the accommodation and food service sector. Addressing the challenges of rising costs cuts across several areas of government, including the Department for Communities and local councils.  My Department is taking steps to support businesses through delivery of the Executive’s ‘Path to Net Zero Energy’ Strategy, a key objective being to ensure that all consumers have access to essential and affordable energy. Tourism NI provides a suite of advisory supports to help tourism and hospitality businesses reduce costs, improve productivity and be more sustainable, including Leaner and Greener, and Innovate Tourism.The level of VAT is also a specific issue, and I know that the Department of Finance has repeatedly raised with the Treasury the negative impacts on hospitality of high levels of VAT.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline what action his Department is taking to increase the availability of speech and language provision.
answer_text: I recognise the vitally important role that Speech and Language Therapists play in the management of complex speech, language, communication, and swallowing problems.The demand for speech and language therapy services exceeds capacity, so increasing the provision of the service is a key priority for my Department.  However, this will require further significant investment to increase the professional workforce. I have previously outlined my commitment to the recommendations of the Speech and Language Therapy Workforce Review 2019 – 2029, which envisages increasing the number of undergraduate training places commissioned each year above the current funded levels by 12 to reach the recommendation of 40. While I remain committed to this, budgetary pressure on my Department has made that unachievable for 2024/25.  I will continue to make the case for additional investment in training for Speech and Language therapists, including exploring the possibility of funding support either directly from other Departments or from the Executive’s Transformation monies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide an update on funding for the Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer.
To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to detail the action his Department will take to improve road safety at the Glenville Road and Shore Road junction, Newtownabbey.
answer_text: Departmental officials have reviewed the recent collision history and having consulted with PSNI Roads Policing Unit, intend to implement some immediate interventions to improve road safety at the Glenville Road and Shore Road junction.Initially, it is proposed to renew and extend the yellow box markings to provide some additional visibility. A proposal to prohibit the right turn movement out of Glenville Road and the U-turn movement at the signalised junction at Shore Road/Glenavna Road will then be taken forward.A proposal to signalise the junction will be progressed as resources permit, however, it will compete with other worthwhile schemes for inclusion in a future works programme.One of the best ways to ensure safety on our roads and fulfil our long-term goal of eliminating death and serious injury by 2050, is by changing road user behaviour. As road users we all have a personal responsibility to behave in a way that keeps ourselves and others safe. The sad reality is that if we do not change our own attitudes when we are using the roads, our death toll will continue to increase.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what action his Department will take to increase physical activity provision.
answer_text: Physical Education (PE) is a stand-alone, area of learning within the Northern Ireland Curriculum and is a statutory entitlement for every pupil from Foundation stage to key stage 4. The aim of the PE curriculum is to develop physically literate individuals who have the knowledge, skills, and confidence to enjoy a lifetime of healthy physical activity.Schools are the most important, but not the only, context for promoting healthy lifestyles in pupils. Pupils should have opportunities to take part in daily physical activities, either indoors or outdoors, such as physical play and regular planned sessions of PE.Although there is no statutory time requirement for PE in schools (and in fact primary legislation does not allow for this in respect of any subject), the Department recommends that schools should provide children and young people with at least two hours of good quality curricular PE each week. This contributes to the well-being and development of resilience; allowing for the progression of skills, knowledge and understanding in the range of activity areas that make up the PE curriculum. In 2021, my Department commissioned the Education and Training Inspectorate (ETI) to undertake a thematic evaluation of PE provision in primary schools. The purpose of the evaluation was to consider the nature and quality of the provision of PE, including best practice and barriers to children’s participation. The evaluation identified strengths including examples of best practice, barriers to participation and areas for improvement in the delivery of quality PE, including accommodation and resources.  The Department is currently working to address the findings of the ETI’s report.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether he plans to standardise accessibility to the Dexcom device in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Continuous glucose monitoring devices (CGMs) are used to support the management of diabetes. Dexcom, is one such device and is available in Northern Trust as it is listed on the NI Drug tariff. Some more complex CGM devices such as Dexcom 6, are not listed on the NI Drug tariff and not routinely commissioned due to current financial pressures.In December 2023, National Institute for Health and Care Excellence published final guidance on Hybrid closed loop systems for managing blood glucose levels in Type 1 diabetes.  Following guideline endorsement, my department is working to understand the implications across provision; priority groups; system costs; workforce and communication requirements to both enable and support to guide decision-making and commissioning.It is worth noting that NHS England has developed a phased implementation strategy over a 5-year period in response to the guidance, owing to the scale and scope of this recommendation. Significant additional investment will be required to support provision

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of homeless cases awaiting a suitable property in East Antrim, broken down by local council ward.
answer_text: The biannual Northern Ireland Homelessness Bulletin July - December 2023 was published on 14 March 2024 and contains information on a range of areas relating to homelessness and is available here: https://www.communities-ni.gov.uk/publications/northern-ireland-homelessness-bulletin-july-december-2023 The information you have requested is not available at ward level.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of wheelchair accessible Housing Executive properties in East Antrim, broken down by local council ward.
answer_text: This issue is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Work is ongoing to survey all Housing Executive properties across NI to classify them on an Accessible Housing Register. Regrettably in the absence of the full picture we are not in a position at this time to provide the requested data.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact the Housing Executive directly.  Making direct contact with them will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

answer_text: Due to the volume of detail required to answer AQW 11119/22-27, the information has been placed in the Assembly Library.

To ask the Minister of Health how many cases of solvent abuse for (i) adults; and (ii) teenagers, have been recorded in each of the last two years.
answer_text: Information on substance use is available from the Young Persons Behaviour and Attitudes Survey for children in school years 8 to 12. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to address the issue of teenagers smoking synthetic substances in vapes.
answer_text: Addressing the harm related to alcohol and other drugs is a key priority for my Department, which leads on the NI Executive’s 10-year Substance Use Strategy “Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery” (https://www.health-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/health/doh-substanceuse-strategy-2021-31.pdf). The Public Health Agency (PHA) works with community and voluntary organisations, local Councils, the PSNI, the education sector, and Health & Social Care partners to raise awareness of issues around young people vaping synthetic substances such as ‘spice’, including working with schools across Northern Ireland. The general health advice messaging around vaping is clear: young people and people who have never smoked should not vape. A pilot school-based stop smoking and vaping service is currently underway in the Belfast and South Eastern areas. Further information on the PHA’s awareness campaign along with a range of resources available can be accessed online at: https://www.publichealth.hscni.net/vaping.In 2022, my Department introduced regulations on prohibiting the sale, and proxy purchase, of nicotine inhaling products to under 18s. These regulations are enforced by District Council enforcement officers and apply to all types of nicotine containing e-cigarettes, including disposable devices. I have also indicated my desire for Northern Ireland to be included in the proposed UK-wide Tobacco & Vapes Bill. Subject to Assembly consent to NI’s inclusion, this Bill will provide additional regulation making powers which will help to tackle the growing problem of youth vaping. It should be noted, that should vapes contain illegal drugs, or substances which fall within the UK-wide Psychoactive Substances Act, then enforcement activity rests with the PSNI.In terms of addressing local community safety issues, including substance use amongst young people, Policing & Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs), funded by the Department of Justice (DoJ) and Northern Ireland Policing Board work in partnership with local communities and other key partners, including the voluntary and community sector. PCSPs are regularly involved in the delivery and facilitation of early intervention programmes and awareness raising initiatives in schools and amongst young people, highlighting the dangers of substance use as well as signposting to appropriate support services. DoJ also chairs the Community Safety Network, a multi-agency partnership forum, which brings together senior representatives from agencies across the public sector to discuss and address community safety issues of concern such as substance use, including via vaping, among children and young people.

answer_text: My Department allocated funding in 2021 for the development of Perinatal Mental Health Services in Northern Ireland, which included establishing  Community Perinatal Mental Health Teams in each Trust. The project was divided into two phases. Phase One prioritised a core staffing model for a Community Team, with phase two providing the enhanced service provision. Phase one of the project has now been completed.What date did each Trust’s Community Perinatal Mental Health Teams become operationalThe date below is when the Perinatal Mental Health Psychiatrists started with the teams and Medical Responsibility for the Clients was assumed. question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many patients have been waiting in excess of 18 months for an annual diabetes review, broken down by Health and Social Care Trust.

To ask the Minister for Infrastructure (i) whether he is aware of MOT Hub, an online system that searches for a MOT appointment and sells the booking at a cost; and (ii) what action his Department is taking to improve MOT booking availability.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) is committed to ensuring that all their customers have a fair and equal opportunity to book a test appointment. The DVA is aware that third-party applications attempt to access its booking system but, as they constantly monitor traffic attempting to reach the booking system, they can regularly update their security controls to block these types of applications.In each of the last two years the DVA conducted over 1.1 million vehicle tests which are the highest numbers ever recorded. This increase in capacity has been achieved through a range of measures including the recruitment of additional vehicle examiners, with the latest recruitment competition currently being progressed to fill vacancies across the DVA’s network of test centres. The DVA also use overtime to provide cover for leave and offer vehicle test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays when testing is not normally available.  In 2023, there were only seven days in the year when the DVA did not offer MOT appointments.  Disappointingly, in the region of 50,000 customers failed to attend a booked appointment in each of the last two years.The DVA appreciates that in some cases customers may not be able to have their vehicles tested before their current MOT certificate expires and has taken steps to ensure that motorists are still able to use their vehicles when they are unable to secure an MOT appointment before their current MOT expires.To further increase vehicle testing capacity, the DVA is investing in new test centres.  The construction of a new test centre at Hydebank, in Belfast, is nearing completion and is expected to open later this year.  The construction contract for a second new test centre at Mallusk has also been awarded and this new test centre is expected to open in 2025.  Once fully operational, these two new centres will provide the capacity to test over 200,000 additional vehicles per annum, which will help the DVA meet the future demand for this service.I have also asked officials to urgently consider a range of policy options including testing every two years and exemption certificates.  I will provide an update on these by the end of April.There are no simple fixes, and I will have to consider the impacts of these options carefully. I do not want to do anything that would add to the burden of hard-pressed households from increased insurance, nor reduce road safety when our current road death toll is already so high.Please be assured the DVA will continue to work extremely hard to increase its vehicle testing capacity and reduce waiting times.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he has plans to reform Key Stage 4 and 16+ qualifications.
answer_text: My officials are keeping abreast of planned and ongoing reforms nationally and internationally and will bring forward advice as required to ensure that qualifications on offer to schools in Northern Ireland remain up to date, comparable and portable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a timeline for the establishment of an Ulster Scots Strategy.
answer_text: Owing to the cross-cutting nature of the strategy, a Cross-Departmental Working Group is considering the Co-Design Group’s proposed action plan.  It will then be for Executive colleagues to agree next steps. A draft strategy will issue for public consultation following Executive agreement.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure to provide a costings report of street lighting repairs for 2023.
answer_text: My Department maintains expenditure records on a financial year basis.  Expenditure on Street Lighting repairs for 2022-23 was £3.142m.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health, to ensure compliance with Clause 3C of the Autism (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2022, when the independent reviewer post will be filled.
answer_text: I am aware of the responsibility to appoint an Independent Autism Reviewer for Northern Ireland and acknowledge the significant role this post will bring in advocating for autistic people and their families. I have therefore asked officials to provide advice on how this appointment may now be progressed so I can consider this more fully and in a timely manner.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure whether he will review the policy on the replacement of street lighting, given that areas which were previously well lit are being left in total darkness, leading to areas being unsafe for local residents to walk.
answer_text: When street lighting is deemed to have reached the end of its life and needs to be replaced, the area is assessed and new lighting is provided only in those areas that meet my Department’s policy for the provision of lighting.In residential areas, street lighting is provided on adopted pathways which provide access to the front entrances of properties, or where they are identified as through routes with no reasonable alternative lit route.  However, where a pathway is not adopted, or is identified as only providing access to the rear of properties, any existing lighting is removed and new lighting is not provided.This policy, which has been in place since 1993, ensures that my Department’s finite resources are directed at replacing lighting in those areas where maximum benefit to the public can be derived, particularly where road or infrastructure safety is concerned, whilst also seeking to minimise environmental impact and reduce future maintenance and energy costs.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education whether he plans to introduce mandatory autism training for teachers.
answer_text: I have no plans, at this stage, to introduce mandatory autism training for teachers as I am awaiting the recommendations arising from the End-to-End (E2E) Reviews of Special Educational Needs (SEN) and School Improvement.  Following receipt of the recommendations, I will review to ensure high quality SEN training, including autism training, is available to all teachers to enable them to be confident and capable to meet the needs of the children and young people in their classrooms.The Enhanced Autism Training Framework developed in response to the Northern Ireland Assembly motion, February 2020, in partnership by the Education Authority (EA) and Middletown Centre for Autism will continue to be delivered, reviewed and refreshed for the educational workforce including teachers and classroom assistants.The Enhanced Autism Training Framework takes a structured approach to professional development and is provided at various incremental levels, across a continuum of autism related need with content tailored for Initial Teacher Education, newly qualified teachers and for more experienced practitioners in Early Years, Primary and Post-Primary sectors.  Middletown Centre for Autism deliver a bespoke autism focussed training programme for Classroom Assistants.In setting out the Enhanced Autism Training Framework in this way, teachers and other education professionals have a clear opportunity to self-assess their professional development and learning needs in line with the department’s Teacher Professional Learning Strategy – ‘Learning Leaders’.  The Strategy signals a move away from ‘top-down’ and ‘one-size-fits-all’ approaches to Teacher Professional Learning, with teachers reflecting routinely on their own practice and assuming responsibility for their own professional learning and development, in conjunction with school leaders and based on the needs of pupils. The EA and Middletown Centre for Autism training is available to all teachers and the educational workforce via the C2K system and Middletown Centre for Autism training is also available directly via their website https://www.middletownautism.com/.The Higher Education Institutions (HEIs) in Northern Ireland deliver Initial Teacher Education (ITE) programmes that are aligned with, and prepare future teachers for the delivery of, the Northern Ireland Curriculum.  All ITE provision in Northern Ireland includes a range of mandatory and optional modules for student teachers with a focus on SEN.  It is for the HEIs to determine the precise content of these courses and the individual modules within them.  The HEIs continue their SEN support with a range of continuous professional development for (qualified) teachers in schools.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the waiting time for an annual diabetes review in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Diabetes affects nearly 112,000 people in NI, with the number growing. Northern Health and Social Care Trust, as the largest geographical Health and Social Care Trust, had a population of 27,830 (25.7%) Patients on Diabetes Mellitus Register in 2022.The majority of diabetes reviews within the Northern Health and Social Trust are taking place on an 18-month basis.  30% of patients receive a diabetes review approximately every 8 months, with 60% of patients receiving a diabetes review every 18 months, approximately. Services continue to recover post-Covid, with additional investment made to reduce waiting lists. Diabetes specialist multidisciplinary teams are delivering care, with patients in higher risk groups prioritised for review first. Despite this waiting lists remain long, with a constrained workforce.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs for an update on the proposed dilapidation bill.
answer_text: I refer the Member to the answer I gave to AQW 6803/22-27.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in light of the increase of children with special educational needs and disabilities (SEND), whether he intends to implement a mandatory SEND placement in third level teacher training.
answer_text: My Department and the Education Authority are currently carrying out an End-to-End Review of Special Educational Needs (SEN), through which steps are being taken to increase the quality, consistency and access arrangements of SEN-focused professional learning for the teaching profession.The Review will include consideration of the existing approach to the professional learning and development of student teachers during Initial Teacher Education (ITE) and engagement with the Higher Education Institutions (HEIs), who deliver ITE programmes in Northern Ireland, is due to take place shortly. Student teacher school placements will be one of the considerations in relation to the experience of SEN during ITE. It should be noted that all ITE provision in Northern Ireland includes a range of mandatory and optional modules for student teachers with a focus on SEN. The HEIs design their programmes to ensure, amongst other things, that new teachers achieve a set of professional competences identified by the General Teaching Council for Northern Ireland (GTCNI) in its "Teaching: The Reflective Profession" document. All aspects of ITE programmes can be mapped against one or more of these competences and SEN is provided with specific reference to competences 9, 15 and 21.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health when health visitors will receive their back pay.
answer_text: There are a number of processes which must be followed in order to implement any pay award. As a first step, and as required under Public Sector Pay Policy, the approval of the Department of Finance is required. Once that is received, my Department will issue a formal pay circular. The Business Services Organisation will then require a further period to make the necessary IT system changes in order to implement the pay award. It is therefore likely to be June at the earliest before any payments are made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail a timescale on when the agreed pay settlement with NHS staff will be implemented.
answer_text: Further to my answer to AQW 9649/22-27, approvals from the Department of Finance have been received in respect of the pay awards for hospital employed doctors and dentists and for staff employed under Agenda for Change terms and conditions. My officials issued formal pay circulars on 10 April 2024. The Business Services Organisation will now proceed to make the necessary IT system changes in order to implement the pay award.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to develop a regional strategy on adult ADHD assessment.
answer_text: The Department of Health currently has no plans to implement a specific regional strategy on adult ADHD.Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there are currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The Department is committed to working closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies, as well as a regional workshop to explore potential ways forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the ADHD adult assessment pilot scheme in the Northern Health and Social Care Trust.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.A number of factors prompted the Northern Trust to progress with a small, unfunded pilot for service users who have been referred to the Trust for Adult ADHD diagnosis and treatment, to be placed on a waiting list and then being offered an appointment with the Trust. Part of the Pilot process is detailed pre-screening to ensure assessments are offered, where there is a high likelihood of Adult ADHD. All patients on any waiting list remain under the care of their GP.This pilot approach has taken time to establish given the preparatory work required. Communication with individuals on the register commenced in December 2023, in chronological order of date of original referral.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what steps he will take to standardise the approach to ADHD medication in all Health and Social Care Trusts.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals, currently there are no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision of services between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the prevalence of adult ADHD is leading to individuals having to wait longer for assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.The Health and Social Care Trusts are committed to work closely and collaboratively with commissioners to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet the needs of patients with ADHD within Trust areas. This work would include the development of clinical pathways.Department of Health officials are regularly reviewing the situation regarding the commissioning of Adult ADHD services, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years. Any decision to commission Adult ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of budget availability.  Department of Health officials are now considering how best to progress the issue, in discussion with relevant stakeholders. Subject to future budget availability, potential next steps include the commissioning of research/analysis of adult ADHD demand to inform options for future commissioning strategies.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure, pursuant to AQW 6793/22-27, what further update he can provide on the implementation of the Graduated Driver Licensing Monitoring Report recommendations.
answer_text: The Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL) Monitoring Report presents overall statistics for collisions involving, and caused by, drivers and motorcyclists aged 17 to 23.  It does not make recommendations on GDL.  It provides a baseline so that when GDL is introduced we can monitor its impact.  I am not yet in a position to add substantively to my response to AQW 6793/22-27. As I indicated previously, I have asked my officials for further information on the GDL programme. I will use that information to take a decision on the way forward.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to outline his Department's plans to establish a regional autism information service.
answer_text: As part of the first-year delivery plan for the Autism Strategy my Department commissioned the HSC Leadership Centre to conduct extensive stakeholder engagement across Northern Ireland to inform proposals for the development of a regional Autism Information Service. It is vital that the provision of information is communicated and delivered in a way that is most beneficial to autistic people and their families and can also provide advice and support to those people who may be waiting for an autism assessment.The report conveying the findings of this engagement is due to be submitted to the Department shortly for consideration and this will enable decisions to be taken regarding how this work can be progressed.The development of the Autism Information Service will provide a readily accessible resource which can signpost to relevant advice and support.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education, in relation to the School Enhancement Programme funding awarded to Carrickfergus Grammar School in 2020, (i) when he anticipates this will reach business case stage; and (ii) what actions he will take to speed this process up.
answer_text: The Education Authority (EA) is managing the delivery of the School Enhancement Programme (SEP) project at Carrickfergus Grammar School. The project is in the final stages of the Technical Feasibility Stage, with the EA currently examining cost information to ensure that the report fully meets the project scope and the SEP funding thresholds.  When this exercise has concluded, the project business case will be completed.  Senior Management within the EA’s Major Capital Delivery Service are closely monitoring progress of the Technical Feasibility Report and will endeavour to ensure this is completed as quickly as possible.
