To ask the Minister of Health, in relation to the fees paid to foster carers who care for children with complex needs, what consideration has been given to (i) reviewing the fees, given they have not increased since 2020; and (ii) making the process for deciding the fees more transparent.
answer_text: The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis to determine an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. It is regrettable that the allowance rates payable to foster carers in Northern Ireland have now fallen behind the minimum allowance rates payable in other parts of the UK, however it is also important to note that the allowance rates in NI and those in other parts of the UK may not be directly comparable.Current rates for 2023-24 are available on the Department’s website at Foster care allowances update | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). A decision on the allowance rates payable for 2024-25, will be undertaken as soon as the budget for 2024-25 is known and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift in 2024/25 will be backdated to April 2024.The Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, identified payments to foster carers as a priority area for investment. Recognising the central role of foster carers as part of the team around the child, a dedicated workstream on Fostering has been established reporting to the Children’s Social Care Strategic Reform Programme.  In addition to the allowances referenced above, HSC Trusts also can provide fees for Foster placements.  Currently, Trusts have a variety of fee paid schemes in place to facilitate the placement of children with additional needs, who require specialised care which may prove challenging to secure. The aforementioned Fostering Workstream will be considering options for the development of a HSC wide Regional Framework for foster care fees in relation to the placement of children with more complex needs. This will have the potential to deliver a more regionally consistent approach to the level of remuneration to foster carers caring for these children, in addition to the payment of fostering allowances. Reviewing fostering fee levels will also include eligibility criteria, leading to greater transparency going forward.Financial implications of any future recommendations from the Fostering Workstream on revised fostering fees, will need to be considered within the context of the wider Department of Health budget at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department will take to ensure foster carers receive weekly allowances that provide parity with other regions in the United Kingdom.
answer_text: The Department reviews the allowances payable to foster carers on an annual basis to determine an annual uplift to apply to these allowances. The annual review considers rates of inflation, the rates of allowances payable in other UK jurisdictions, and what is affordable within the financial envelope available to the Department. It is regrettable that the allowance rates payable to foster carers in Northern Ireland have now fallen behind the minimum allowance rates payable in other parts of the UK, however it is also important to note that the allowance rates in NI and those in other parts of the UK may not be directly comparable.Current rates for 2023-24 are available on the Department’s website at Foster care allowances update | Department of Health (health-ni.gov.uk). A decision on the allowance rates payable for 2024-25, will be undertaken as soon as the budget for 2024-25 is known and foster carers will be notified of the outcome as soon as possible. Payment of any uplift in 2024/25 will be backdated to April 2024.The Independent Review of Children’s Social Care Services in Northern Ireland, published in June 2023, identified payments to foster carers as a priority area for investment. Recognising the central role of foster carers as part of the team around the child, a dedicated workstream on Fostering has been established reporting to the Children’s Social Care Strategic Reform Programme.  In addition to the allowances referenced above, HSC Trusts also can provide fees for Foster placements.  Currently, Trusts have a variety of fee paid schemes in place to facilitate the placement of children with additional needs, who require specialised care which may prove challenging to secure. The aforementioned Fostering Workstream will be considering options for the development of a HSC wide Regional Framework for foster care fees in relation to the placement of children with more complex needs. This will have the potential to deliver a more regionally consistent approach to the level of remuneration to foster carers caring for these children, in addition to the payment of fostering allowances. Reviewing fostering fee levels will also include eligibility criteria, leading to greater transparency going forward.Financial implications of any future recommendations from the Fostering Workstream on revised fostering fees, will need to be considered within the context of the wider Department of Health budget at that time.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what action his Department will take to reduce waiting times for those booking a practical driving test and retest.
answer_text: The Driver and Vehicle Agency (DVA) has steadily increased its driving test capacity by adopting a range of measures including the recruitment of additional driving examiners, use of overtime to rota off-shift dual role examiners to provide further capacity and cover for leave, they also offer driving test appointments on Sundays and bank holidays, where it is suitable to do so without compromising the integrity of the test. In 2022-2023, the DVA conducted 68,540 driving tests, which is the highest number of driving tests conducted since 2009. Almost 82% (56,132) of all driving tests in 2022-2023 were Category B (private car) tests. Disappointingly, 2,812 people failed to attend their test, which is the highest number on record. The DVA is in the process of recruiting new full time driving examiners who on successful completion of their training will be allocated to test centres from late March 2024 onwards. The DVA releases Category B (private car) driving test appointments five months in advance with all test centres set to release driving test appointments on the first working day of each month. Additional test slots will also be released as resources continue to become available.Due to the demand for services the advice to customers, is to book and pay for the earliest date available and check the booking system regularly for an earlier date as additional appointment slots are added when resources become available and when cancellations are made.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what action his Department will take to address unregulated aesthetics procedures.
answer_text: My Department continues to take decisive action to combat illegal promotion, supply or misuse of medicines and to alerting the public about the dangers of using unauthorised medicines outside of the regulated supply chain. The risk to public safety posed by the unlawful distribution and misuse of unauthorised medicinal products within the non-surgical cosmetic sector in Northern Ireland is a serious issue. Such breaches relating to Northern Ireland may be referred to My Department’s Medicines Regulatory Group (MRG) for investigation under the Human Medicines Regulations, 2012.MRG works to ensure continuing compliance with existing laws and guidance and where evidence suggests unlawful practice is occurring, enforcement actions are taken. Where a breach of the Regulations is identified MRG will consider a range of enforcement options proportionate to the circumstances. This includes securing compliance with the Regulations through the issuing of warning or advice, referral to professional bodies or prosecution.My Department remains vigilant and continues to work with key partner agencies to monitor the illicit marketplace and will continue to take effective action where necessary to protect public safety in Northern Ireland.Under the Independent Health Care Regulations (Northern Ireland) 2005, the Regulation and Quality Improvement Authority (RQIA) regulate clinics providing certain laser or intense pulsed light cosmetic treatments and providers are subject to certain requirements for example to undergo appropriate training.My Department is aware of the wider, changing health and social care service delivery environment, including in relation to cosmetic treatments. Prior to the COVID-19 pandemic, the Department had developed a new draft regulatory policy framework however further development work is required, including consultation on the draft policy.  The Department is currently operating within a constrained budget and is required to make decisions in relation to the work that can be delivered within current resources. In that context, work on the review of the regulation is currently paused to allow for other priority projects to progress.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure what actions his Department will take to ensure the completion of the new Bashford Park spine road, Carrickfergus.
answer_text: Responsibility for the construction and completion of the new Bashford Park Spine Road Carrickfergus rests with the developers of the adjacent land.  Governance for these works comes under planning permissions granted from Mid and East Antrim Borough Council.  My Department will ensure proper application and adherence to the street planning functions within the Private Streets Order which apply to the Bashford Park Spine Road.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy whether his Department will address the student finance inequalities facing long distance learners in Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Prior to my taking office, the higher education student support package for academic year 2024/25 had already been determined. I have asked my officials to update me on any work to date on a review of Higher Education Funding, as well as proposals to take it forward.  I will be engaging with my officials to consider the current support available and determine whether this can be improved.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice what engagement she has had with the Department of Education in respect of addressing the use of illegal drugs in schools.
answer_text: I have not had any specific engagement with the Department of Education on this issue. My Department engages with the education sector primarily through its work with the Education Authority (EA). The EA are designated members of Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs). PCSPs are engaged in tackling this concerning issue via community youth engagement projects, detached youth programmes, delivery of educational programmes and awareness raising campaigns on the personal risk to children and young people from substance use. The EA also sit on the multi-agency Community Safety Network, a multi-agency strategic partnership forum chaired by my Department, which brings together senior representatives from agencies across the public sector to discuss and address community safety issues including substance use among children and young people.The Youth Justice Agency (YJA) also engage with EA via Childrens Diversionary Forums, which aim to identify children who require additional support, share information, and link them and their families to appropriate support services. The Department of Education and the EA are delivery partners in the Executive’s Tackling Paramilitarism and Organised Crime Programme, which supports those who are vulnerable to all types of such harm, including children and young people.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education what commitments his Department can provide to Monkstown Boxing Club to retain their A Fair Start funding.
answer_text: I have recently given my approval to an interim 2024/25 budget. Correspondence will issue imminently to all relevant service providers in order to provide the reassurance they need.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs how the Minister intends to protect those dog breeds that fall under breed-specific legislation.
answer_text: Of the four breeds of dogs restricted under the Dangerous Dogs (Northern Ireland) Order 1991, only the Pitbull Terrier is present in Northern Ireland. The owners of these dogs can keep their dog as long as they comply with the conditions outlined in the legislation.  All dogs, including those that fall under breed-specific legislation, are protected under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011. Councils are responsible for enforcement and are able to take a range of action to improve the welfare of these animals, including providing advice, issuing Improvement Notices, taking animals into their possession, and initiating prosecution action if necessary.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs to detail current sanctions on those who mistreat animals.
answer_text: The welfare of all animals is protected under the Welfare of Animals Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.  The three bodies that are responsible for the enforcement of the 2011 Act are:Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs (DAERA) (farmed animals only)Councils (non-farmed animals, such as domestic pets and horses)Police Service of Northern Ireland (PSNI) (wildlife crime, animals on the road and other criminal activity such as dog fighting)Enforcement bodies can take proportionate enforcement action based on the extent and severity of the animal welfare breach that has taken place.  The maximum penalty available under the Act is five years imprisonment and/or an unlimited fine.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health whether his Department plans to allow younger teenagers access to a child ADHD assessment instead of an adult assessment.
answer_text: Provision of service for Attention Deficit Hyperactivity Disorder (ADHD) assessment and diagnosis has grown organically across Trusts in response to population demand and available expertise within Health & Social Care teams. Whilst a level of provision is available for ADHD referrals there is currently no specifically commissioned services. The Department is aware that this has led to inconsistencies in provision between Trusts and inconsistent pathways and service gaps. The limited capacity available in Trusts in terms of meeting demand and the level of prevalence of child and adult ADHD is leading to individuals waiting longer than assessment and support than described in NICE guidelines.Four of the five Trusts provide assessments to under 18’s. One of the Trusts focuses children’s assessments on the under 12’s and does not currently offer assessments to the 12-18 age group. The Department of Health are committed to work closely and collaboratively with Health and Social Care Trusts to develop innovative, cost effective, co-produced, quality, and evidenced based services that can meet NICE compliant pathways for ADHD diagnosis and support.Department of Health officials are considering how a future ADHD service might be delivered to meet needs, recognising that the demand for ADHD diagnosis has risen in recent years.  In line with the usual process for commissioning services, any decision to commission ADHD services in the future will be based on an assessment of the level of demand for services, and in the context of future budget availability.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what funding his Department can provide to Triathlon events.
answer_text: At this time there are no current programmes within my Department to apply to provide triathlon events. This issue is an operational matter for Sport NI.I have sought the requested information from the Chief Executive, who has advised me as follows:“Sport NI do not have any current programmes to which triathlon can apply. However, Sport NI is aware that Tourism NI provide events funding programmes and may have a suitable programme. Local authorities are another possible source of funding that Triathlon may wish to explore as there may be local opportunities depending on the event location.”For any future queries relating to operational matters, you may wish to contact Sport NI directly.  Making direct contact with the ALB will ensure that you receive the information in the shortest possible timeframe and make best use of public resources.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how much funding has been allocated from the Assets Recovery Community Scheme in East Antrim since its introduction in 2012.
answer_text: Since it was first launched the Asset Recovery Community Scheme, or ARCS, has awarded approximately £9 million to projects aimed at tackling crime and the fear of crime.  It funds valuable initiatives – mainly in community settings – that actively help victims, communities or the environment in support of a culture of lawfulness.  In January 2024, my Department announced an allocation of approximately £400,000 to £500,000 in each of the next three years.   182 applications have been received and are being considered, with funding for successful projects expected to commence in April 2024.  Five organisations from the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council area applied and decisions are pending.  In the previous Scheme, covering the financial years 2022/23 – 2023/24, three organisations from the council area applied for funding and one was eligible to receive £3,500, that is 100% of the amount applied for.  In 2021/22 one organisation in the council area was awarded almost £10,000 – or 90% of the funding applied for. A breakdown prior to this is not readily available.  In addition to projects operating in local areas a number of projects operate throughout Northern Ireland to benefit both urban and rural areas.  Some of these would include the Mid and East Antrim Borough Council area.ARCS sends a very visible message, both to criminals and to communities, that crime does not pay.  It not only funds projects that benefit communities, but it enhances confidence in the justice system.  By redistributing the value of criminal assets recovered through the Courts via confiscation orders, ARCS is not only helping to prevent crime and reduce the fear of crime but is also sending a strong message to those who seek to disregard the law and cause harm that their activities will not be tolerated. A range of interventions are supported through the Asset Recovery Community Scheme, including those working with young people to build skills and confidence; addressing anti-social behaviour and environmental crime; reducing re-offending; supporting victims of modern slavery, domestic abuse and hate crime; raising awareness of fraud and scams and organised crime as well as support for community safety initiatives.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail (i) how much funding was allocated by Sport NI between 2021 and 2023, broken down by constituency; and (ii) where this funding was allocated in East Antrim.
answer_text: The question relating to funding allocated by Sport NI is an operational matter for Sport NI.You should, therefore, seek the information requested directly from Sport NI and direct your question to Richard Archibald, Interim Chief Executive Officer at Assemblybusiness@sportni.net.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities what support his Department provides to improve accessibility in places of worship for those with a disability.
answer_text: My Department has no direct policy responsibility for improving accessibility in places of worship for those with a disability.  		Places of worship are required by law to comply with all relevant legislation to provide equality of opportunity and protection from discrimination for people with disabilities.  The Disability Discrimination Act 1995 imposes a duty on employers and service providers to make reasonable adjustments for disabled people to help them overcome barriers that they may face in accessing and using goods and services.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he intends to further fund the Access and Inclusion programme.
answer_text: My Department works in partnership annually with the disability sector, DAERA, local Councils and the NI Museums Council (NIMC) to deliver the Access and Inclusion programme. The aim of the programme is to promote a more inclusive society by improving accessibility at arts, cultural and active recreation venues for people with disabilities. My Department provided a capital budget of £414,000 and DAERA contributed £300,000 allowing 31 projects to be delivered in 2023/24. There is no scope to provide additional funding this financial year to increase the number of projects being delivered.	The Department’s capital budget for 2024/25 has not yet been confirmed.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether he will provide additional funding to the Supporting People programme.
answer_text: My Department and NIHE are engaging with the sector in these challenging times to seek ways to ensure the resilience of the Supporting People programme, to deliver value for money and to ensure the programme delivers real outcomes for service users – those most vulnerable in our society. The Executive will agree and set the 2024-25 Budget and once the outcome is known I will consider how best to allocate the Department’s budget against key priorities.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail the estimated waiting time for an ADHD assessment.
answer_text: There are currently no regionally commissioned services for adult ADHD and therefore no requirement for HSC Trusts to manage a dedicated and comparable ADHD waiting list. The information is therefore not available for adults.Please find Children’s ADHD waiting time information detailed below. Table 1. Number of children waiting for an ADHD assessment as at 28.02.2024*, by HSC Trust and waiting time bandsWaiting time BandsBelfastNorthernSouth Eastern*SouthernWestern*0-4 Weeks (0-28 days)61121365>4 to 8 Weeks (29-56 days)5667394814>8 to 13 weeks (57-91 days)6556384516> 13 to 18 weeks (92-126 days)49170344611> 18 to 26 weeks (127-182 days)70691717826> 26 to 39 weeks (183-273 days)10223715212836> 39 to 52 weeks (274-364 days)1241629112723> 52 weeks (365+ days)614102529124308Total Waiting1,1418641,075632439Source: HSC Trusts*Please note that the South Eastern Trust could only provide information as at 08.11.2023 as their latest position, and the Western HSC Trust provided information as at 29.02.2024.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what plans he has to introduce a baby loss certificate for those who have experienced the loss of a baby prior to 24 weeks in Northern Ireland, in line with England.
answer_text: I recognise the impact that pregnancy loss at any stage can have on parents and welcome the recent introduction of pre-24-week pregnancy loss certificates in England, as recommended by the Independent Pregnancy Loss Review commissioned by the Westminster Government. My Department will continue to engage with their counterparts in the Department of Health and Social Care in England as the new system is embedded there, with a view to understanding whether it might be possible to implement such a scheme in Northern Ireland, including any associated costs.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to outline what steps they intend to take to improve the application process for the Troubles Permanent Disablement Payment Scheme.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the NHS provision of insulin pumps for people with Type 1 diabetes.
To ask the Minister of Education whether his Department will carry out an assessment of problems surrounding (i) vaping; and (ii) drug misuse in schools.
answer_text: Current guidance places a statutory requirement on all schools in Northern Ireland to:Have a drugs policy and publish details in relation to the policy in their prospectus.Deliver drugs education to include legal and illegal substances. Inform the PSNI if they believe or suspect a pupil to be in possession of a controlled substance.My Department also provides guidance to schools to cover curriculum requirements, behavioural concerns, pastoral support and safeguarding which will incorporate vaping and drugs as appropriate.  My Department also assisted the Department of Health’s public consultation on smoking and vaping, ensuring this was issued to all schools to encourage input. Furthermore, schools have the flexibility to work directly with the Public Health Agency and other organisations to provide learning opportunities for their pupils around a wide range of health and indeed environmental issues attributed to vaping and drugs misuse.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Education for a breakdown of funding to each school sector in each of the last 5 years.
answer_text: The table below provides details of the funding allocated to each school sector in each of the last five years. School Sector2018-19 2019-20 2020-21 2021-22 2022-23 £m£m£m£m£mControlled  463.3491.4566.8605.2602.2Maintained  447.8470.4540.4576.0565.6Grant Maintained Integrated 66.370.883.087.987.7Voluntary Grammar 216.5227.9265.0269.5268.4TOTAL 1,193.91,260.51,455.21,538.61,523.9Notes:The figures include funding allocated to schools in accordance with the Common Funding Scheme (Common Funding Formula, Contingency, Dual Site funding, etc.) and earmarked funding.2020-21 Includes £51.8m of additional funding allocated to schools to mitigate the impact of Covid-19.2021-22 Includes £69m of additional funding allocated to schools to mitigate the impact of Covid-19.2022-23 Includes £23m of additional funding allocated to schools to mitigate the impact of Covid-19.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice to detail the number of (i) charges; and (ii) convictions in cases of rape, in each of the last five years.
answer_text: The recording of information on persons arrested and charged by police is a matter for the Chief Constable, who is accountable to the Northern Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Northern Ireland Policing Board.The Police Service of Northern Ireland maintain their own statistics on persons arrested and charged by them. You may, therefore, wish to direct any query relating to the issues raised in part (i) of the question to the PSNI.In relation to part (ii) of the question, information on the number of convictions for rape has been provided for cases that were dealt with in the period 2018 to 2022, the most recent five years for which information is available.Convictions at courts for rape offences, 2019 – 2023YearConvictions2019                         18 2020                         13 2021                         19 2022                         21 2023                         29 Note:1. Figures relate to initial disposal at court only.  Results of appeals are not included. 2. Figures relate to cases dealt with at Crown Court and at magistrates’ courts.3. Figures relate to convictions for substantive rape offences only.4. Figures relate to defendants who were convicted of at least one offence of rape during the period reported.5. Figures for 2023 are provisional and have not been validated.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure for an update on changes to (i) driver licencing; and (ii) the practical driving test as suggested as part of the Graduated Driving Licensing Framework in the Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (NI) 2016.
answer_text: The Road Traffic (Amendment) Act (Northern Ireland) 2016 provides the statutory framework for the introduction of Graduated Driver Licensing (GDL). Following extensive consultation, a suite of subordinate legislation is being developed to take forward the GDL proposals, which will introduce:•	A Mandatory Minimum Learning Period of 6 months;•	Removing the 45mph speed restriction on learner and newly qualified drivers and enabling learning and driving on the motorway;•	A Programme of Training and learner Logbook;•	Passenger restrictions for newly qualified drivers;•	A new ‘R’ plate (post-test) and an increase in the restricted period.It is recognised that the cornerstone of any improvement in driving standards is the learning process and the Mandatory Minimum Learning Period, the Programme of Training and the use of a Logbook are aimed at changing the current mind-set when considering or learning how to drive.  For this approach to be effective, it must be supported by a form of assessment that covers the full range of knowledge, understanding, skills and behaviours required to drive safely.To achieve this, it has been necessary to review the current practical driving test to ensure that it complements GDL.  For example, a new improved practical driving test will include testing learner drivers up to posted speeds.  It will also increase the amount of time given over to Independent Driving and introduce using Satellite Navigation Systems during the test.I have asked my officials for an update on the GDL programme, and the potential timeline for full implementation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health what support and treatment his Department can offer to those suffering from Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder.
answer_text: Avoidant/Restrictive Food Intake Disorder (ARFID) is a complex eating disorder presentation that is recognised as a mental health condition. Previously ARFID was known by lots of different names and some young people were diagnosed with feeding disorder of infancy or early childhood. ARFID is characterised by a pattern of eating that avoids certain foods or food groups entirely and/or is restricted in quantity.Due to the varied forms ARFID may take it can look very different from person to person. Support and treatment for children and young people with ARFID begins in primary care with a referral to the GP. This referral should seek to ensure there are no other medical conditions that are making it hard for the child or young person to eat. Community Paediatricians also provide support within eating and neurodevelopmental clinics for children presenting with ARFID symptoms. Some young people diagnosed with ARFID would be seen in CAMHS but only when they have a co-morbid mental health condition.From an adult perspective regionally, none of the Health Trusts in NI are commissioned to treat ARFID. All Trusts work within the NICE guidelines for Treatment for Eating Disorders. ARFID is excluded from these guidelines and it is currently not included in the Regional Care Pathway for the Treatment of Eating Disorders. However, in some cases if the reason for diagnosis is associated with fear and anxiety other than weight/shape concerns (such as fear of choking), the client may be treated in other Mental Health teams such as Adult Psychological Therapy Services.The Royal College of Psychiatrists acknowledge that more research is needed to understand how ARFID should be treated as underlying causes vary from individual to individual. My Department will continue to work with Health and Social Care Trusts and professional bodies to better understand how these needs can be met through future service delivery models/pathways.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5540/22-27, what measures he will take to ensure adequate funding is provided to marching bands through the Arts Council NI Instruments for Bands scheme to meet demand.
answer_text: The Musical Instruments Programme is a competitive funding programme and the number of awards is limited by the amount of funding available. In 2023/24 the budget for the whole programme was set at £400k and half of that was awarded to the Musical Instruments for Bands strand. The Programme has been popular, with each of its three strands being oversubscribed in 2023/24.  To bolster the important contribution that the programme makes, I am pleased to advise that I have been able to allocate an additional £100k to the £400k budget for the current financial year.  	The Budget for 2024-25 has not been settled. Future funding of this programme, along with all the other valuable calls for investment, will be considered in the light of decisions on the budget.

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the Skill Up initiative.
answer_text: The Skill Up initiative has supported over 22,000 people since its introduction in 2021, offering flexible learning opportunities through online courses in partnership with local colleges and universities. Skill up has been particularly beneficial for those starting with no or low qualifications, and in increasing access for people who face barriers to learning, for example due to caring responsibilities.		Skill Up is currently being evaluated and I will consider the need for any further programmes in the context of that evaluation and as part of the upcoming Budget exercise.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities, pursuant to AQW 5540/22-27, what action his Department will take to address the demand for the oversubscribed Arts Council NI musical instruments scheme.
answer_text: The Musical Instruments Programme is a competitive funding programme and the number of awards is limited by the amount of funding available. In 2023/24 the budget for the whole programme was set at £400k and half of that was awarded through the Bands scheme.To bolster the important contribution that the programme makes, I am pleased to advise that I was able to allocate an additional £100k to the £400k budget for the 23/24 financial year.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the number of people on the social housing waiting list in East Antrim.
answer_text: The number of people on the social housing waiting lost in East Antrim is an operational matter for the Housing Executive. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Housing Executive at its dedicated MLA response team, which can be contacted at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Justice how she will address the projected number of police officers to fall to under 6000 by 2025.
answer_text: Police officer numbers are a matter for the Chief Constable who is accountable to the Northen Ireland Policing Board.  I am committed to respecting the operational independence of the Chief Constable and the role of the Policing Board.  You may therefore wish to address your question to the Chief Constable.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities whether his Department has a strategy to address the lack of sporting facilities in East Antrim.
answer_text: In 2022 my Department published the Sport and Physical Activity Strategy for Northern Ireland – ‘Active Living’.  This Strategy was co-designed following consultation with other Government Departments, the sports sector and local Councils.  One of the key themes of this strategy is to ensure that everyone has access to “inclusive, shared, safe, welcoming and high quality indoor and outdoor sport and physical activity infrastructure”.Under Article 10 of the Recreation and Youth Order (Northern Ireland) 1986 the statutory responsibility for the provision of providing adequate local facilities for recreational and physical activities lies with the local district council. In respect of the East Antrim constituency this responsibility would therefore rest with Antrim & Newtownabbey, Mid & East Antrim Borough Councils and Causeway Coast and Glens Borough Councils.  Sport NI, an arm’s length body of my Department, is currently undertaking an infrastructure review of sporting and physical activity facilities across Northern Ireland. When completed the results of this survey will be shared with the sector and will help identify areas where provision of facilities can be improved.  In addition, Sport NI aspire to ensure that its capital budgets are available to all sporting organisations throughout Northern Ireland with open and competitive challenge funds.  Sport NI welcome applications from organisations from the East Antrim constituency for its future programmes.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail the undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive in the East Antrim constituency.
answer_text: The undeveloped land owned by the Housing Executive in the East Antrim constituency is an operational matter for the Northern Ireland Housing Executive. 		You should therefore direct your question to the Northern Ireland Housing Executive at public.affairsunit@nihe.gov.uk

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to detail his plans to ensure the sustainability and growth of Carrickfergus Castle as a heritage and tourism attraction.
answer_text: My Department has invested £1.8 million since 2018 in the Carrickfergus Castle Roof, Grand Battery and East Sea Tower, to help conserve this important State Care Monument for the community and for future generations. The Castle, along with the Carrickfergus Town Walls and Gasworks are at the heart of the £42 million heritage-led Belfast Region City Deal investment in Carrickfergus. The City Deal investment will deliver more jobs and wellbeing benefits for residents, as well as benefitting local economies by attracting visitors and increasing dwell-time and spend.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Infrastructure how many roads require (i) resurfacing; and (ii) partial resurfacing in East Antrim.
answer_text: Resurfacing schemes are funded through my Department’s Structural Maintenance Programme; regrettably, for some time the available investment has not kept pace with what is needed to maintain all of our roads in a reasonable condition.  While there are many roads that would benefit from investment, due to limited budget resources, resurfacing work is taken forward through the prioritisation of those deemed most in need for intervention. I am very aware of the long-term underfunding from which our road network has suffered. Therefore, I have recently announced the immediate investment of an additional £1m in road maintenance to begin to address the growing backlog of pothole repairs. This much needed funding will be used between now and the end of the financial year to target areas of particular need with small scale resurfacing schemes designed to repair areas that have suffered the most and where localised repairs are less effective.I have also allocated over £8m of additional funding to structural maintenance to allow further large scale resurfacing schemes to be delivered. This represents a significant investment in improving the structural integrity of our road network. The 2023/24 resurfacing programme for the Mid And East Antrim Borough Council area was presented to Council members and can be found at https://www.infrastructure-ni.gov.uk/sites/default/files/publications/infrastructure/annual-report-to-mid-and-east-antrim-council-2023.PDFThe resurfacing programme along with other programmes for 2024/25 will be prepared when budgets are confirmed and will be presented to Council in due course.The importance of a well maintained road network cannot be understated but this requires investment. I will continue to seek the support of Executive colleagues to enable this to happen.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Finance for an update on the City Deal for the Mid and East Antrim Council area.
answer_text: Mid and East Antrim Borough Council is Lead Council on three projects within the Belfast Region City Deal (BRCD).The Carrickfergus Regeneration project is currently within the detailed design phase which is progressing as expected.In regard to the Gobbins Phase Two and the i4C Centre at St. Patricks Barracks projects, BRCD are currently developing the Contract for Funding in collaboration with the Department for Economy, which when signed, will allow the funding to flow to those projects.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities for an update on the review of the Caravans Act (Northern Ireland) 2011.
answer_text: The 2021 Review of the Caravans Act completed with the publication of a Summary Report with recommendations on 28 March 2023.  The Act cuts across the remit of my Department, the Department for the Economy and the Department for Infrastructure, and the onus is on each to take forward those recommendations which align to their responsibilities. Those recommendations which come under the remit of my Department continue to be progressed.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health to detail his Department's plans to reduce ambulance waiting times.
To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the Regional Insulin Pumps Service for people with Type 1 diabetes.
answer_text: The Regional Pumps Service specification was co-produced with People Living with Diabetes (PLWD), clinicians and health care professionals across all HSCTs. BHSCT lead this approach to insulin pump administration for NI. This is delivered by a regional multi-disciplinary team that aligns regional and local provision across the pump pathway. In 2021, an average of 12-13% of People Living with Type 1 Diabetes (PLWT1D) in NI were in receipt of a pump. NICE anticipate 15-20% of PLWT1D would be eligible for pump therapy. Significant waiting lists across HSCTs meant variable provision and inequity across NI. Implementation of this regional service specification over a 3-year period will ensure that an average of 14% adults are assessed and managed with equity of access to insulin pump therapy as appropriate within this period. In Year 1, the service has focused on rebalance across the region to support the ambition of equity.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the sufficiency of the provision of services for residential drug and alcohol treatment.
answer_text: Under the Northern Ireland Executive's 10-year Substance Use Strategy Preventing Harm, Empowering Recovery which I launched in September 2021, the Public Health Agency and the Strategic Planning & Performance Group have co-produced, across all tiers of service, a new outcomes-focused strategic service delivery plan, to replace the current Alcohol & Drug Services Commissioning Framework. This new Substance Use Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan will: •	ensure that the population of NI have access to a continuum of service with clear pathways and step up/step down provision;•	ensure equal access to community detoxification for alcohol and other drugs across NI;•	ensure that all services are delivered in line with the UK-wide “Drug Misuse and Dependence: Guidelines on Clinical Management” and NICE Guidelines; •	provide support to address the wider physical, mental health, and wellbeing needs of those in treatment, including housing, education, employment, personal finance, healthcare e.g. should be supported to stop smoking and address other physical health conditions; and•	recognise the importance of co-production and strengthen joint working between the community/voluntary sector, service users and peers, and the Health & Social Care Sector.As a priority action within Preventing Harm Empowering Recovery, an Independent Review of Inpatient Tier 4 Detoxification & Residential Rehabilitation Services across Northern Ireland is due to publish its report shortly. This will provide an overview of current service provision and will help set the future direction for Tier 4 services across NI based on best practice and learning from across the UK and Ireland. The recommendations from this forthcoming report will help to inform the Strategic Commissioning & Implementation Plan currently being finalised, and it is the intention that all future funding decisions around substance use related services will be based on this new commissioning framework. The full delivery of the strategy will be dependent on additional funding being made available into the future. In the meantime, a full range of substance use services remain available across all five Health & Social Care Trust areas, details of which can be found online at:  https://services.drugsandalcoholni.info/.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health how many ambulance call-outs in 2023 were drug and alcohol related.
answer_text: Information on how many ambulance call-outs in 2023 were drug and alcohol related was not held centrally but has been provided by the Northern Ireland Ambulance Service (NIAS), in Table 1 overleaf.NIAS advise that drug and alcohol related calls usually fall under the call category "OVERDOSE / POISONING (Ingestion)".  The number of Overdose/Poisoning calls is for the 2023 period and includes alcohol/drug related calls that were not specifically in the ‘Overdose/Poisoning’ category by adding key words "Drug" OR "Alcohol" to the Problem/Reason for call field. This will show alcohol/drug related calls that fell under a different category, e.g. Falls, Psychotic episodes etc. The total calls sourced includes those with any mention of overdose, drug or alcohol. A number of these may not be associated with illicit drugs as accidental overdoses can occur with prescribed medication. Likewise, calls related to alcohol may simply mention alcohol during the call-taking process rather than alcohol being the sole reason for requesting an ambulance. This figure could also be artificially low as there may be a number of calls which are only found out to be related to drugs or alcohol once a crew attends, which may have been presented as a fall, collapse or unconscious patient.Table 1: Total Number of Overdose, Drug or Alcohol Calls with a Response (1st January 2023 – 31st December 2023) Count of Calls7,603Source: NIAS, C3 Command and Control SystemTable 2 overleaf provides more information on the criteria used by NIAS to determine the count of Overdose calls reported in Table 1. Table 2: Criteria Used by NIAS to Determine Count of Overdose Calls									Data Inclusions and Exclusions:Includes all Calls with a Pickup Primary Care Trust (HSCT).Calls excluded are those with a Call Stopped Reason of "Call Entered in Error", "Call for Information Only", "Done by another Service", "Duplicate Call" or "Test Call".Overdose calls are taken to be those which fit any of the following criteria: Chief Complaint/Symptom Group is equal to "OVERDOSE / POISONING (Ingestion)"; Despatch Code Description is equal to "OVERDOSE / POISONING  (Ingestion)"; What's the Problem/Reason for Call contains one of the following - "overdose", "odose", "* OD *", "* O/D *", "* O'D *", "o'dose", "o/dose", "O/D'd", "O/D.", "-O/D", "OD *", "O/D *", "O'D *", "* OD", "* O/D", "* O'D" where * is used as a wildcard.What's the Problem/Reason for Call contains "Drug" OR contains "Alcohol".The data provided is based on information obtained at the time of the 999 call, and since the caller may not know all the relevant details, this data may be under-reporting.			NIAS have also included the following disclaimer:“Please use this data in a cautionary manner, as there may be manual data entry errors contained within.”

question_text: To ask the Minister for the Economy for his assessment of the impact that Loyal Order and marching bands' parades have on the local economy.
answer_text: My Department has not assessed the impact that Loyal Order and marching bands' parades have on the local economy.

question_text: To ask the First Minister and deputy First Minister to provide an update on the implementation of the High Street Task Force report Delivering a 21st Century High Street.
To ask the Minister of Health (i) whether he will allocate the funding required to sustain the present bed capacity at NI Children's Hospice; and (ii) when such funding could be made available.
To ask the Minister of Justice (i) how many attacks there have been on Orange Halls, each year since 2017; and (ii) what plans she has to reduce this number.
answer_text: I recognise the effect that attacks on symbolic buildings such as Orange Halls can have, including the distress and the disruption caused by the resulting damage.The numbers of attacks on Orange and Apprentice Boys Halls from 2017 to date is detailed in ANNEX A. The PSNI has advised that the data is published on the PSNI website periodically. A PSNI control strategy for attacks on symbolic buildings is in place across all Districts.  The police continue to work in partnership with the Orange Order and other community organisations in an effort to prevent such attacks and, where they occur, to exploit all evidential opportunities to bring those responsible to justice.My Department, in partnership with the Northern Ireland Policing Board, provides funding to Policing and Community Safety Partnerships (PCSPs) to work in partnership with local communities and other key partners, including local statutory bodies/agencies and the voluntary and community sector, to develop appropriate actions to deal with, and reduce, the impact of issues such as Anti-Social Behaviour (ASB) and hate incidents. Although there are no projects being delivered which are specific to Orange Halls, PCSPs act swiftly to address local issues as and when they arise. Actions taken by PCSPs to address ASB include: provision of community safety warden services; diversionary and community engagement projects; detached youth programmes; delivery of educational programmes; awareness raising campaigns to prevent ASB; and graffiti removal. PCSPs are also included in delivering educational projects to address hate crime issues including sectarianism.	My Department chairs a multi-agency cross Departmental Community Safety Network to ensure a collaborative response to multi-faceted community safety issues.		DOJ is engaged in cross departmental work in tackling the enabling factors of sectarian hate crime, aimed at creating more cohesive communities by addressing intolerance and hate.	I would encourage anyone concerned about attacks on Orange Halls to access guidance from the PSNI’s Crime Prevention Officers (CPOs).  The CPOs can provide advice on security management in respect of buildings such as Orange Halls to help prevent any incidents in the future.  ANNEX AAttacks on Orange and Apprentice Boys Halls 2017 – 2023PSNI data on attacks on symbolic buildings is compiled centrally with Orange Halls grouped alongside Apprentice Boys Halls and relate to crimes where a sectarian motivation has been recorded, where the premises is the intended target of the attack, and where the premises is an Orange Hall or Apprentice Boys Hall. Financial year Number of attacks on Orange Halls / Apprentice Boys Halls2017/18 112018/19 102019/20 142020/21 142021/22 122022/23 192023/24* 4** For the period 1st April 2023 – 30th September 2023.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities (i) for an update on the rollout of the Sub Regional Stadia Programme for Soccer; (ii) whether he will seek additional funding for the scheme in line with inflation and rising costs of materials; and (iii) whether there will be a fresh consultation exercise for clubs to bid for funding.
answer_text: The Sub-Regional Stadia Programme for Football is an important flagship Executive commitment and I recognise the valuable contribution it can deliver for football and wider society.My officials are working with a range of stakeholders to develop proposals and I have asked my them to present me with proposals and options for consideration as a matter of priority. I understand the budget allocated in 2011 is no longer sufficient to meet identified needs. I intend to discuss this with Executive colleagues and the UK Sports Minister at the earliest opportunity.You may be aware that a consultation on the Programme took place in 2015. Once the new proposals are finalised consideration will be given to the need for a fresh consultation.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Health for his assessment of the autism under-fours referral pathway; and whether he plans on making this pathway accessible throughout Northern Ireland.
answer_text: Children under 4 access assessment for developmental delay through primary care and community paediatric services. Where neurodevelopmental concerns are raised children will be referred to Trust services for assessment. ASD Assessments for younger children including those under 4 will include a Community Paediatrician as part of the multi-disciplinary assessment.Officials in my department are currently reviewing the paediatric ASD pathway. Officials are meeting with Trusts on a monthly basis as part of this review and to develop pathways that are consistently and equally accessible across the region.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown (i) of the number of applicants who applied for the Arts Council for Northern Ireland’s musical instruments scheme, broken down by council area; and (ii) the amount awarded to each council area.
answer_text: Applications and awards to the Musical Instruments Funding Programme are operational matters for the Arts Council of Northern Ireland. You should, therefore, direct your question to the Chief Executive of the Arts Council of Northern Ireland at chief@artscouncil-ni.org.

question_text: To ask the Minister for Communities to provide a breakdown (i) of the number of applicants who applied for the Arts Council for Northern Ireland’s musical instruments scheme; and (ii) how many were successful.
answer_text: There was a total of 475 applications to the Arts Council of Northern Ireland’s 2023/24 Musical Instruments Programme.  A total of 296 of these applications met the eligibility conditions of the programme, and of these 43 were successful.

question_text: To ask the Minister of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs when the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme will open.
answer_text: Officials are at an advanced stage of finalising a Business Case for the Rural Micro Capital Grant Scheme. Subject to my consideration and budget availability I anticipate being in a position to make an announcement regarding the Scheme early in the 2024/2025 financial year.
department: Department of Agriculture, Environment and Rural Affairs
